# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term 'limiting error' in measurement systems. How is it different from relative error?**

**Model Answer:**
Limiting error is the maximum deviation of an indicated value from the true value, expressed as a percentage of the true value or full-scale reading. It defines the **worst-case bound** within which the measured value is guaranteed to lie.

Relative error is the **ratio** of absolute error to the true value, i.e., $\varepsilon_{r} = \varepsilon_a / A_{\text{true}}$. It is dimensionless and is preferred when comparing the precision of measurements of different magnitudes. Limiting error, by contrast, is usually stated as a specification by the instrument manufacturer and indicates the **maximum guaranteed precision** under specified operating conditions.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Why does the error in a difference quantity often become very large? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
The relative error in a difference $y = x_1 - x_2$ is $\varepsilon_{ry} = (\varepsilon_{x_1} + \varepsilon_{x_2}) / (x_1 - x_2)$. When the two measured values $x_1$ and $x_2$ are nearly equal, the denominator becomes very small, while the absolute errors in the numerator remain unchanged. This causes the relative error to **balloon** to very large values.

**Example:** Measuring $x_1 = 100.0$ V and $x_2 = 99.5$ V, each with an absolute error of $\pm 0.1$ V. The difference is $0.5$ V, but the absolute error is $\pm 0.2$ V, giving a relative error of $40\%$. This phenomenon is especially relevant in **null-balance instruments** and **differential measurements**.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule**: Each Part B question has internal choice. Students must answer EITHER (a) OR (b) from the same module-set.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 1, Internal Choice 1]**

**(a)** The resistance of a wire is given by $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$, where $\rho$ is the resistivity, $L$ is the length, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area ($A = \pi d^2 / 4$). The nominal values and their limiting errors are:
- $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \ \Omega \cdot m$, $\varepsilon_{r\rho} = \pm 1\%$
- $L = 2.00$ m, $\varepsilon_{rL} = \pm 0.5\%$
- $d = 0.50$ mm, $\varepsilon_{rd} = \pm 0.2\%$

**Calculate the nominal resistance and the maximum relative and absolute errors in $R$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Express $R$ in terms of the given variables:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi d^2 / 4} = \frac{4 \rho L}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln 4 + \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln \pi - 2 \ln d$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate both sides:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - 2 \cdot \frac{dd}{d}$$

**Step 4:** Take the absolute values to obtain the limiting relative error:

$$\varepsilon_{rR} = \varepsilon_{r\rho} + \varepsilon_{rL} + 2 \cdot \varepsilon_{rd}$$

$$\varepsilon_{rR} = 1\% + 0.5\% + 2(0.2\%) = 1.5\% + 0.4\% = 1.9\%$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the nominal value of $R$:

$$R = \frac{4 \times (1.72 \times 10^{-8}) \times 2.00}{\pi \times (0.50 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$R = \frac{13.76 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 0.25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{13.76 \times 10^{-8}}{0.7854 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = 17.52 \times 10^{-2} = 0.1752 \ \Omega$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the absolute error:

$$\varepsilon_{aR} = 0.019 \times 0.1752 = 0.00333 \ \Omega$$

**Final Answer:** $R = 0.1752 \ \Omega \pm 1.9\% = 0.1752 \pm 0.0033 \ \Omega$ (i.e., between $0.1719 \ \Omega$ and $0.1785 \ \Omega$).

---

**(b)** A power is computed using the formula $P = V I \cos \phi$, where the measurements and their limiting errors are:
- $V = 200$ V, $\varepsilon_{rV} = \pm 1\%$
- $I = 5$ A, $\varepsilon_{rI} = \pm 1.5\%$
- $\cos \phi = 0.8$, $\varepsilon_{r\cos\phi} = \pm 2\%$

**Calculate the nominal value of $P$ and the limiting error in $P$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the nominal value of $P$:

$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos \phi = 200 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 800 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of $P$:

$$\ln P = \ln V + \ln I + \ln(\cos \phi)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dI}{I} + \frac{d(\cos \phi)}{\cos \phi}$$

**Step 4:** Apply limiting error (absolute values):

$$\varepsilon_{rP} = \varepsilon_{rV} + \varepsilon_{rI} + \varepsilon_{r\cos\phi} = 1\% + 1.5\% + 2\% = 4.5\%$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the absolute error:

$$\varepsilon_{aP} = 0.045 \times 800 = 36 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:** $P = 800 \pm 4.5\% = 800 \pm 36$ W, i.e., $P$ lies between **764 W and 836 W**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1, Internal Choice 2]**

**(a)** The inductance of a coil is given by $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l}$. The following data are given:
- $N = 500$ turns, $\varepsilon_{rN} = \pm 0.1\%$
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m (exact constant)
- $\mu_r = 800$, $\varepsilon_{r\mu_r} = \pm 0.5\%$
- $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $\varepsilon_{rA} = \pm 1\%$
- $l = 0.25$ m, $\varepsilon_{rl} = \pm 0.5\%$

**Determine the nominal inductance and the percentage limiting error.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute nominal $L$:

$$L = \frac{(500)^2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 800 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.25}$$

$$L = \frac{250000 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 800 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.25}$$

$$L = \frac{250000 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4}{0.25}$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$N^2 = 250000$$
$$\mu_0 \mu_r = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 800 = 3200\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.00531 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Numerator = $250000 \times 1.00531 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$
= $250000 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-7}$
= $125.66 \times 10^{-3}$
= $0.12566$

Denominator = $0.25$

$$L = 0.12566 / 0.25 = 0.5027 \text{ H} \approx 502.7 \text{ mH}$$

**Step 2:** Take natural log:

$$\ln L = 2 \ln N + \ln \mu_0 + \ln \mu_r + \ln A - \ln l$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate:

$$\frac{dL}{L} = 2 \frac{dN}{N} + \frac{d\mu_r}{\mu_r} + \frac{dA}{A} - \frac{dl}{l}$$

**Step 4:** Take absolute values:

$$\varepsilon_{rL} = 2 \varepsilon_{rN} + \varepsilon_{r\mu_r} + \varepsilon_{rA} + \varepsilon_{rl}$$

$$\varepsilon_{rL} = 2(0.1\%) + 0.5\% + 1\% + 0.5\% = 0.2\% + 0.5\% + 1\% + 0.5\% = 2.2\%$$

**Final Answer:** $L = 0.5027$ H $\pm 2.2\%$, so $L$ lies between $0.4916$ H and $0.5137$ H.

---

**(b)** Two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ are connected in series, and the total resistance is $R_s = R_1 + R_2$. Their nominal values and tolerances are:
- $R_1 = 100 \ \Omega \pm 5\%$
- $R_2 = 200 \ \Omega \pm 5\%$

**Calculate the nominal series resistance, the absolute limiting error, and the relative error.** If the same resistors are connected in **parallel**, calculate the new nominal value and relative error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Series case:**

**Step 1:** Nominal value: $R_s = 100 + 200 = 300 \ \Omega$

**Step 2:** Absolute errors: $\varepsilon_{R_1} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5 \ \Omega$, $\varepsilon_{R_2} = 0.05 \times 200 = 10 \ \Omega$

**Step 3:** Absolute limiting error (sum rule):

$$\varepsilon_{Rs} = \varepsilon_{R_1} + \varepsilon_{R_2} = 5 + 10 = 15 \ \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Relative error:

$$\varepsilon_{rRs} = 15 / 300 = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**Parallel case:**

**Step 1:** Nominal value: $R_p = (R_1 \cdot R_2) / (R_1 + R_2) = (100 \times 200) / 300 = 66.67 \ \Omega$

**Step 2:** For product and quotient, relative errors add:

$$\varepsilon_{rR_p} = \varepsilon_{rR_1} + \varepsilon_{rR_2} + \varepsilon_{r(R_1+R_2)} = 5\% + 5\% + 5\% = 15\%$$

**Step 3:** Absolute error:

$$\varepsilon_{aR_p} = 0.15 \times 66.67 = 10 \ \Omega$$

**Final Answer:** Series: $R_s = 300 \ \Omega \pm 5\%$; Parallel: $R_p = 66.67 \ \Omega \pm 15\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Differentiation**: When differentiating $\ln(x_1 - x_2)$, students often forget the minus sign or drop absolute values prematurely. This costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Square the Exponent of $d$**: In the resistance formula, the area involves $d^2$, so the relative error in $d$ must be **multiplied by 2**. Missing this costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing Absolute and Relative Error**: Always state clearly which form your final answer is in. Mixing units (% vs. $\Omega$) without proper labeling loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the Absolute Value in the Difference Step**: For $y = x_1 - x_2$, the absolute error is still the *sum* of the absolute errors of $x_1$ and $x_2$, not the difference. Many students write $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_{x_1} - \varepsilon_{x_2}$, which is **wrong** for limiting error.
> 5. **Not Specifying the Result as a Range**: Always conclude with "value lies between X and Y" to demonstrate full understanding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Propagation of Errors**

- **Limiting error** is the **maximum possible error**, not a typical error. It assumes worst-case alignment of all individual errors.
- **Sum/Difference Rule**: Absolute errors ADD; relative error requires division by the result.
- **Product/Quotient Rule**: Relative errors ADD directly (no need to differentiate if you remember the rule).
- **Power Rule**: Relative error of $x^n$ is $\vert n \vert$ times relative error of $x$.
- **General Mixed Expression** $y = \dfrac{x_1^a x_2^b}{x_3^c}$: $\varepsilon_{ry} = a \varepsilon_{rx_1} + b \varepsilon_{rx_2} + c \varepsilon_{rx_3}$.
- **Logarithmic Differentiation** is the master technique — always take $\ln$ of both sides first for any product/quotient/power expression.
- **Difference Trap**: When two nearly-equal values are subtracted, relative error **blows up**. Watch for this in **null-type** measurements.
- **Statistical (RSS) method** uses square root of sum of squares — used when errors are random and independent, not the default in KTU.
- Always report final answer as a **range** (min–max) and clearly state whether the error is **absolute** or **relative** (percentage).
- **Constants** like $\pi$ and $\mu_0$ are exact, so their errors are zero and they drop out of the error analysis.
- KTU typically gives 1 Part A (3 marks) and 1 Part B choice (14 marks) on this topic per exam cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Expression of Uncertainty – The Language of Measurement

Every measurement is imperfect. The deviation between the *measured value* and the *true value* is captured by the **Expression of Uncertainty**. The five foundational terms listed below form the vocabulary of every instrument engineer and are tested almost every semester in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Terminology** – Examiner's eye always scans for these five terms in any measurement question. Memorize the definitions word-for-word.

| Term | Formal Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of the measured value to the **true (actual) value**. | A voltmeter reading **100.2 V** for a **100 V** supply is highly **accurate**. |
| **Precision** | Closeness of two or more measurements to **each other** (reproducibility). | A clock that consistently gains **+2 seconds/day** is **precise** but not accurate. |
| **Sensitivity** | Ratio of the **change in output (response)** to the **change in input (stimulus)**. | $S = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta x}$ – deflection per unit quantity. |
| **Resolution** | The **smallest change** in the input that produces a detectable change in output. | A **4½-digit DMM** has a resolution of **0.0001 V** on the 20 V range. |
| **Loading Effect** | The disturbance introduced by the instrument itself on the circuit under test. | A low-impedance voltmeter draws current and **drags down** the actual voltage. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Bullseye Analogy)** – Imagine throwing darts at a dartboard.
> - **Accuracy** = darts hit near the bullseye (true value).
> - **Precision** = darts cluster tightly together, even if far from the bullseye.
> - **Sensitivity** = how far the dart moves on the board for every small wrist adjustment.
> - **Resolution** = the smallest dartboard gap you can visually distinguish.
> - **Loading Effect** = your hand nudging the dartboard while aiming.

## 1.2 Classification of Instruments

Instruments are broadly classified into two branches:

1. **Primary Instruments** – Instruments that directly give the value of the measurand (e.g., a ruler, a beam balance). They are the absolute reference.
2. **Secondary Instruments** – Instruments whose calibration is referenced against a primary standard. They form **90 %** of engineering practice and are subdivided into three classes:

| Class | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Indicating Instruments** | Display the **instantaneous** magnitude using a pointer on a calibrated scale. | Ammeter, Voltmeter, Galvanometer |
| **Integrating Instruments** | **Totalize / accumulate** a quantity over a period of time. | Energy meter (kWh meter), Ampere-hour meter |
| **Recording Instruments** | Provide a **continuous trace** of the measurand vs. time on paper / digital storage. | Strip-chart recorder, X-t plotter |

## 1.3 Operating Forces in Electromechanical Instruments

For the pointer to settle correctly, three torques must act on the moving system simultaneously:

- **Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)** – Produced by the measurand. It is *proportional to the input quantity* and is the *cause* of pointer movement.
- **Controlling Torque ($T_c$)** – Opposes deflection. It is *proportional to the angular displacement* of the pointer (like a spring). Without this, the pointer would slam to the maximum.
- **Damping Torque ($T_{damp}$)** – Opposes the *rate of change of deflection* to prevent oscillation. It does **not** exist at the final rest position.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Balancing Act** – At steady state: $T_d = T_c$, and $T_{damp} = 0$. The pointer comes to rest at the true reading.

## 1.4 Electromechanical Indicating Instruments – Overview

- **Suspension Galvanometer** – A ultra-sensitive PMMC-type instrument used for detecting/measuring *very small currents* (microamperes), with the coil suspended by a fine metallic ribbon that supplies current and provides control.
- **PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil)** – The workhorse for **DC measurements**. It uses a permanent magnet, a moving coil, hair-springs, and a pointer. Its torque is linear with current: $T_d \propto I$.
- **DC Ammeter & Voltmeter** – PMMC movements with **shunt resistors** (ammeter) or **multiplier resistors** (voltmeter) to extend range.
- **Ohmmeter** – A PMMC movement with an internal battery that measures **resistance** directly.
- **Multimeter** – A combination instrument switching between voltmeter, ammeter, and ohmmeter modes via a rotary selector.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Terms of Measurement – Deeper Dive

### 2.1.1 Accuracy vs Precision – Why Both Matter

A measurement can be **accurate but imprecise** (shooting scatter close to the bullseye), or **precise but inaccurate** (tight cluster far from bullseye). A *good* instrument is both.

Quantitatively, accuracy is expressed using three error forms:

- **Absolute Error:** $\epsilon_a = \vert A_m - A_t \vert$
- **Relative Error:** $\epsilon_r = \dfrac{\epsilon_a}{\vert A_t \vert}$
- **Percentage Error:** $\% \epsilon = \dfrac{\epsilon_a}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$

where $A_m$ = measured value, $A_t$ = true value.

### 2.1.2 Sensitivity, Resolution, and Loading Effect

- **Static Sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}} = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta x}$
- **Resolution** depends on *scale subdivision* (analog) or *number of bits* (digital).
- **Loading Effect** arises when the instrument's input impedance disturbs the circuit. For a voltmeter measuring a source voltage $V_s$ with source resistance $R_s$ through a meter of resistance $R_m$:

$$V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_m}{R_s + R_m}$$

- **Loading Error** $V_s - V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_m}$

The condition for **minimum loading** is $R_m \gg R_s$ (for voltmeters) and $R_m \ll R_s$ (for ammeters).

## 2.2 Operating Torques – Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)

Produced by electromagnetic or electrostatic forces on the moving element. For common instrument types:

- PMMC: $T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$
- Moving Iron: $T_d \propto I^2$
- Electrodynamic: $T_d \propto I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos \phi$

### 2.2.2 Controlling Torque ($T_c$)

Two principal methods:

- **Spring Control (most common):** $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$, where $K_s$ = spring constant (Nm/rad).
- **Gravity Control:** $T_c = K_g \cdot \sin\theta$ – used only for *panel-mount* horizontally-mounted instruments.

### 2.2.3 Damping Torque ($T_{damp}$)

Three damping techniques exist:

| Damping Type | Governing Relation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Friction** | $T_{damp} \propto d\theta/dt$ | PMMC instruments |
| **Fluid Friction** | $T_{damp} \propto (d\theta/dt)^n$ | Heavy industrial meters |
| **Eddy Current** | $T_{damp} \propto d\theta/dt$ | Most modern instruments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight** – When asked *"Which damping is most efficient?"*, the answer is **Eddy Current Damping** because it is frictionless, reliable, and provides linear damping torque.

## 2.3 PMMC Mechanism – The Heart of DC Measurements

The PMMC movement is built around:
1. A **strong permanent magnet** (Alnico / Alcomax) producing radial flux density $B$.
2. A **rectangular coil** of $N$ turns wound on a light aluminium former, area $A$.
3. **Pivot and jewel bearings** (or taut suspension) supporting the coil.
4. Two **control springs** (spiral) providing $T_c$.
5. A **pointer** attached to the coil, moving over a calibrated scale.

### Equilibrium Equation

At steady state, $T_d = T_c$:

$$N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K_s \cdot \theta$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta = \dfrac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K_s} \cdot I\;}$$

The deflection $\theta$ is **directly proportional** to current $I$ – yielding a **uniform linear scale**.

## 2.4 DC Ammeter, Voltmeter, Ohmmeter – Design Equations

### Ammeter (Range Extension by Shunt)

A low resistance **shunt** $R_{sh}$ placed in parallel with the PMMC movement (resistance $R_m$, full-scale current $I_m$) extends range to $I$. The multiplier ratio $m = I / I_m$, and:

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

### Voltmeter (Range Extension by Series Multiplier)

A high resistance **multiplier** $R_s$ in series with the meter extends voltage range to $V$:

$$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m$$

### Series Ohmmeter

A battery $V_b$, current-limiting resistor $R_1$, and the meter in series form the simplest ohmmeter. The **half-scale resistance** $R_h$ satisfies:

$$R_h = R_m + R_1 = \dfrac{V_b}{I_{fs}}$$

At any unknown resistance $R_x$:

$$R_x = R_h \cdot \dfrac{I_{fs} - I}{I}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet** – Memorize this table; these equations appear in **almost every Module-1 Part-B question**.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $\epsilon_a = \vert A_m - A_t \vert$ | same as measurand |
| 2 | Percentage Error | $\% \epsilon = \dfrac{\epsilon_a}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Static Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta x}$ | rad/unit |
| 4 | Loading Voltage | $V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_m}{R_s + R_m}$ | V |
| 5 | PMMC Deflecting Torque | $T_d = N B I A$ | Nm |
| 6 | PMMC Deflection | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K_s} \cdot I$ | rad |
| 7 | Spring Controlling Torque | $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$ | Nm |
| 8 | Damping Torque (Eddy Current) | $T_{damp} = D \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Nm |
| 9 | Ammeter Shunt | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$ | Ω |
| 10 | Voltmeter Multiplier | $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m$ | Ω |
| 11 | Ohmmeter Half-scale | $R_h = \dfrac{V_b}{I_{fs}}$ | Ω |
| 12 | Voltmeter Sensitivity | $S_v = \dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{R}{V}$ where $R = R_m + R_s$ | Ω/V |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PMMC Ammeters** are the building blocks of every analog control panel, battery tester, and laboratory DMM (before ADC).
- **Voltmeter Sensitivity (Ω/V)** is the gold standard for comparing voltmeters; higher Ω/V means lower loading.
- **Ohmmeters** form the basis of insulation testers, continuity testers, and **megohmmeters** for high-resistance measurements.
- **Loading Effect** dictates why electronic (FET-input) voltmeters with $R_{in} > 10^{10} \, \Omega$ replaced passive analog voltmeters in research labs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PMMC Deflection Equation

### Problem Statement

A PMMC instrument has a moving coil of $N$ turns, effective coil area $A$, placed in a uniform radial magnetic flux density $B$. The coil is restrained by springs of combined constant $K_s$. Derive the equation of steady-state deflection.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.**

When a conductor of length $l$ carries current $I$ in flux density $B$, the force is:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

**Step 2 — Total force on the coil.**

For $N$ turns, the total length perpendicular to flux is $2 \cdot l$ per turn (two active sides of the rectangular coil). Hence:

$$F_{total} = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot (2 l_{side})$$

**Step 3 — Deflecting torque equals force × perpendicular distance.**

The effective area is $A$. With two active sides each at the radial edges, the torque arm equals $w/2$ each, giving total:

$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Controlling torque from springs.**

A spiral spring provides torque proportional to angular deflection:

$$T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium condition at steady state.**

At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$:

$$N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K_s \cdot \theta$$

**Step 6 — Solve for deflection $\theta$.**

$$\boxed{\;\theta = \left(\dfrac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K_s}\right) \cdot I\;}$$

The factor $\dfrac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K_s}$ is the **current sensitivity** (rad/A) of the PMMC instrument.

### Physical Interpretation

- Doubling turns $N$ doubles sensitivity.
- Doubling area $A$ doubles sensitivity.
- Doubling spring constant $K_s$ halves sensitivity (the spring becomes stiffer).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ammeter Shunt Resistance

### Problem Statement

A PMMC movement with internal resistance $R_m$ and full-scale current $I_m$ must be extended to measure a maximum current $I$. Derive the required shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the multiplier ratio.**

$$m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the meter–shunt node.**

Current splits such that the meter carries $I_m$ and the shunt carries $(I - I_m)$.

**Step 3 — Voltage equality across the parallel combination.**

$$I_m \cdot R_m = (I - I_m) \cdot R_{sh}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_{sh}$.**

$$R_{sh} = \dfrac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m} = \dfrac{R_m}{\frac{I}{I_m} - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression.**

$$\boxed{\;R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltmeter Multiplier Resistance

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Full-scale meter current for voltage $V$.**

For a voltmeter measuring $V$, the total circuit resistance must satisfy:

$$V = I_m \cdot (R_m + R_s)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for series multiplier.**

$$\boxed{\;R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m\;}$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity of voltmeter.**

$$S_v = \dfrac{1}{I_m} = \dfrac{R_m + R_s}{V} \quad [\Omega / V]$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Series Ohmmeter – Unknown Resistance

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Circuit model.**

A series ohmmeter has an internal EMF $V_b$, current-limiting resistor $R_1$, and meter (resistance $R_m$, full-scale current $I_{fs}$) all in series with the unknown resistance $R_x$ across the external terminals.

**Step 2 — Full-scale condition with terminals shorted ($R_x = 0$).**

$$I_{fs} = \dfrac{V_b}{R_1 + R_m}$$

This defines the **half-scale resistance**:

$$R_h = R_1 + R_m = \dfrac{V_b}{I_{fs}}$$

**Step 3 — General current with $R_x$ inserted.**

$$I = \dfrac{V_b}{R_1 + R_m + R_x} = \dfrac{V_b}{R_h + R_x}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_x$.**

$$R_x + R_h = \dfrac{V_b}{I} = R_h \cdot \dfrac{I_{fs}}{I}$$

$$R_x = R_h \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{fs}}{I} - 1\right) = R_h \cdot \dfrac{I_{fs} - I}{I}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression.**

$$\boxed{\;R_x = R_h \cdot \dfrac{I_{fs} - I}{I}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick** – When $I = I_{fs}/2$, we get $R_x = R_h$. This is why $R_h$ is called *half-scale resistance* and the scale is marked with $R_x$ values that *decrease* as the pointer moves toward full-scale.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Loading Error in Voltmeter Measurement

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Thevenin equivalent of source.**

Source: open-circuit voltage $V_s$, internal resistance $R_s$. Meter: resistance $R_m$.

**Step 2 — Voltage divider across meter terminals.**

$$V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_m}{R_s + R_m}$$

**Step 3 — Loading error.**

$$\epsilon_{loading} = V_s - V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_m}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage loading error.**

$$\% \epsilon_{loading} = \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_m} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Condition for negligible loading.**

For $\% \epsilon_{loading} \to 0$:

$$\boxed{\;R_m \gg R_s\;}$$

This is why voltmeters are designed with **maximum possible input resistance**.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Instrument Type | Deflecting Torque | Scale Type | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| PMMC Ammeter | $T_d = N B I A$ | Uniform (Linear) | DC current |
| PMMC Voltmeter | $T_d = N B I A$, $I = V/R_{total}$ | Uniform | DC voltage |
| Series Ohmmeter | $T_d = N B I A$, $I = V_b/(R_h + R_x)$ | **Reversed** (high to low) | Resistance (mid range) |
| Suspension Galvanometer | $T_d = N B I A$ | Uniform | Microcurrents (µA, nA) |
| Multimeter | All above (switched) | Multiple | Universal lab tool |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Instruments – Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Measuring Instruments] --> nodeB[Primary Instruments]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Secondary Instruments]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Absolute Reference Standard e.g. Beam Balance]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Indicating Instruments]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Integrating Instruments]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Recording Instruments]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[PMMC Ammeter Voltmeter]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Moving Iron Meter]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Galvanometer]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Energy Meter kWh]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Ampere Hour Meter]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Strip Chart Recorder]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[X Y Plotter]
```

## 4.2 Operating Forces Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP[Measurand Input e.g. Current I] --> node1[Deflecting Torque Td]
    node1 --> node2[Pointer Movement theta]
    node2 --> node3[Controlling Torque Tc from Springs]
    node3 --> node4[Steady State when Td equals Tc]
    node2 --> node5[Damping Torque Tdamp]
    node5 --> node6[Opposes rate of change of theta]
    node4 --> node7[Final Deflection on Calibrated Scale]
    node6 --> node7
```

## 4.3 PMMC Mechanism Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodePM[Permanent Magnet Radial Field B] --> nodeC[Moving Coil N turns Area A]
    nodeI[Current I enters via upper spring] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeT[Deflecting Torque Td equals N B I A]
    nodeT --> nodeM[Coil rotates by angle theta]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Pointer deflects on calibrated scale]
    nodeM --> nodeCS[Lower Control Spring provides Tc equals Ks theta]
    nodeCS --> nodeEQ[Equilibrium Td equals Tc]
    nodeEQ --> nodeR[Pointer Reads Measured Value]
```

## 4.4 DC Ammeter with Shunt – Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS[Source Current I to be measured] --> nodeSH[Shunt Resistor Rsh]
    nodeS --> nodeM[PMMC Meter Resistance Rm Full Scale Im]
    nodeSH --> nodeK[KCL at node Im plus Ish equals I]
    nodeM --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeR[Computed Rsh equals Rm divided by m minus 1]
    nodeR --> nodeDisp[Pointer indicates measured I]
```

## 4.5 DC Voltmeter with Multiplier – Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeV[Source Voltage V to be measured] --> nodeRS[Series Multiplier Rs]
    nodeRS --> nodeM[PMMC Meter Rm Full Scale Im]
    nodeM --> nodeK[Ohm Law V equals Im times Rm plus Rs]
    nodeK --> nodeRSol[Computed Rs equals V divided by Im minus Rm]
    nodeRSol --> nodeDisp[Pointer indicates measured V]
```

## 4.6 Series Ohmmeter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeB[Battery EMF Vb internal resistance negligible] --> nodeR1[Current Limiting Resistor R1]
    nodeR1 --> nodeM[PMMC Meter Resistance Rm Full Scale Ifs]
    nodeM --> nodeT[Test Terminals for Unknown Rx]
    nodeT --> nodeL[Loop completes through Rx]
    nodeK[Half Scale Resistance Rh equals Vb divided by Ifs] --> nodeRX[Unknown Rx equals Rh times Ifs minus I divided by I]
    nodeRX --> nodeDisp[Pointer deflects opposite direction scale marks Rx]
```

## 4.7 Suspension Galvanometer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeMS[Metallic Suspension Ribbon provides control torque and current path] --> nodeC[Taut Suspension Coil N turns very small area A]
    nodeI[Unknown Micro Current I enters via ribbon] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeT[Deflecting Torque Td equals N B I A very sensitive]
    nodeT --> nodeM[Mirror mounted on coil rotates by theta]
    nodeM --> nodeL[Light Beam reflects from mirror scale Lamp]
    nodeL --> nodeD[Deflection detected on graduated scale]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the following with one example each:**
**(i) Accuracy (ii) Precision (iii) Sensitivity**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of a measured value to the *actual (true) value* of the quantity being measured. Example: A voltmeter reading **100.05 V** when the true EMF is **100 V** has an error of only **0.05 %**, indicating high accuracy.
- **Precision** is the degree of closeness of *two or more measurements* to each other, irrespective of their closeness to the true value. Example: Five successive readings of a voltage source are **99.8, 99.9, 99.8, 99.9, 99.8 V** – these are highly precise though possibly biased low.
- **Sensitivity** of an instrument is defined as the *ratio of change in output* (deflection) to the *change in input* (measurand). Example: A voltmeter whose pointer deflects by **1° per volt** has a sensitivity of **1°/V**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A voltmeter reads 48.7 V when the true voltage is 50.0 V. Calculate the absolute error, relative error, and percentage error.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Absolute error.**

$$\epsilon_a = \vert A_m - A_t \vert = \vert 48.7 - 50.0 \vert = 1.3 \, V$$

**Step 2 — Relative error.**

$$\epsilon_r = \dfrac{\epsilon_a}{\vert A_t \vert} = \dfrac{1.3}{50.0} = 0.026$$

**Step 3 — Percentage error.**

$$\% \epsilon = 0.026 \times 100 = 2.6 \, \%$$

---

## 5.2 Part B – Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **PMMC instrument** with a neat functional diagram. Derive the equation for the **steady-state deflection** of the moving coil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMMC voltmeter has the following specifications: coil turns $N = 80$, coil area $A = 4 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$, flux density $B = 0.15 \, T$, spring constant $K_s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, Nm/rad$. The voltmeter is connected across a 75 V source through a multiplier resistor of $4950 \, \Omega$. Calculate: **(i)** the current through the coil, **(ii)** the deflecting torque, and **(iii)** the pointer deflection in degrees. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q.A(a)

**Construction of PMMC (4 marks):**

The Permanent Magnet Moving Coil instrument consists of:
1. **Permanent Magnet** – A high-grade magnet (Alnico / Alcomax) shaped to provide a *uniform radial magnetic field* in the air gap.
2. **Moving Coil** – A rectangular coil of $N$ turns of fine copper wire wound on a light aluminium former.
3. **Pivot and Jewel Bearings** – Support the spindle; or *taut suspension* in sensitive types.
4. **Control Springs** – Two spiral springs (top and bottom) provide both the *controlling torque* and the *current path*.
5. **Pointer and Scale** – A lightweight aluminium pointer attached to the spindle moves over a calibrated mirror scale.

**Working Principle and Derivation (3 marks):**

When current $I$ flows through the coil placed in radial flux density $B$, each turn experiences a force $F = BIl$. For $N$ turns over area $A$, the **deflecting torque** is:

$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$

The **controlling torque** from the springs is $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$. At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$:

$$N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K_s \cdot \theta$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta = \dfrac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K_s} \cdot I\;}$$

This gives a **uniform linear scale** since $\theta \propto I$.

---

### Model Answer for Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Current through the coil.**

Total resistance = $R_m + R_s$. Assuming $R_m \approx 50 \, \Omega$ (typical PMMC), total = $50 + 4950 = 5000 \, \Omega$.

$$I = \dfrac{V}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{75}{5000} = 0.015 \, A = 15 \, mA$$

**Step 2 — Deflecting torque.**

$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$

$$T_d = 80 \times 0.15 \times 0.015 \times (4 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$T_d = 80 \times 0.15 \times 0.015 \times 0.0004$$

$$T_d = 7.2 \times 10^{-5} \, Nm$$

**Step 3 — Pointer deflection.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{T_d}{K_s} = \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 14.4 \, rad$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 14.4 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 825 \, \text{degrees} \;\; (\text{multi-turn coil})$$

In practice, the deflection is read directly from the scale calibrated for **0–75 V**.

---

## 5.3 Part B – Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **working of a series-type ohmmeter**. Derive the expression for the **unknown resistance $R_x$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMMC ammeter has internal resistance $R_m = 100 \, \Omega$ and full-scale current $I_m = 1 \, mA$. Design a **multi-range shunt** to measure currents of **10 mA, 100 mA, and 1 A**. Compute the required shunt resistances for each range. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q.B(a)

**Series Ohmmeter – Construction & Working (4 marks):**

A series ohmmeter consists of:
- A **dry cell battery** (EMF = $V_b$, typically 1.5–9 V).
- A **current-limiting resistor** $R_1$.
- A **PMMC movement** (resistance $R_m$, full-scale $I_{fs}$).
- **Two external test terminals** to connect the unknown resistance $R_x$.

All elements are in **series**. When the test terminals are **shorted** ($R_x = 0$), the meter must indicate **full-scale deflection** (zero ohms). The pointer deflection decreases as $R_x$ increases.

**Derivation of $R_x$ (3 marks):**

With terminals shorted:

$$I_{fs} = \dfrac{V_b}{R_1 + R_m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_h = R_1 + R_m = \dfrac{V_b}{I_{fs}}$$

$R_h$ is called the **half-scale resistance**.

When $R_x$ is connected, current becomes:

$$I = \dfrac{V_b}{R_h + R_x}$$

Solving for $R_x$:

$$\boxed{\;R_x = R_h \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{fs}}{I} - 1\right)\;}$$

This gives a *reverse* scale where full-scale = 0 Ω and zero deflection = ∞ Ω, with half-scale = $R_h$.

---

### Model Answer for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Multiplier ratios.**

For each range $I$, define $m = I / I_m$:

- $m_1 = 10 / 1 = 10$
- $m_2 = 100 / 1 = 100$
- $m_3 = 1000 / 1 = 1000$

**Step 2 — Ayrton shunt calculation.**

For multi-range, an **Ayrton (Universal) shunt** is used. The total shunt chain is chosen first, then tapped.

Pick total chain resistance $R_{sh,total}$ such that the meter reads full-scale when *all* of the chain is in parallel. A convenient choice: $R_{sh,total} = R_m / 9 \approx 11.11 \, \Omega$.

For tap at $k \, \Omega$ above ground:

**For 10 mA range ($m_1 = 10$):**

$$R_{sh1} = \dfrac{R_m}{m_1 - 1} = \dfrac{100}{9} \approx 11.11 \, \Omega$$

**For 100 mA range ($m_2 = 100$):**

$$R_{sh2} = \dfrac{R_m}{m_2 - 1} = \dfrac{100}{99} \approx 1.0101 \, \Omega$$

**For 1 A range ($m_3 = 1000$):**

$$R_{sh3} = \dfrac{R_m}{m_3 - 1} = \dfrac{100}{999} \approx 0.1001 \, \Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1"** in $R_{sh} = R_m/(m-1)$ – yields incorrect value, loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing ammeter and voltmeter formulas** – Ammeter uses *shunt* (low resistance, parallel); Voltmeter uses *multiplier* (high resistance, series). Confusing these loses **3 marks**.
> 3. **Neglecting units** – Always state $\Omega$, $mA$, $Nm$, $rad/s$. Unit mistakes attract **negative marking**.
> 4. **Skipping half-scale explanation** for ohmmeters – Examiners specifically test whether you know why the scale reads "half-scale = $R_h$". Missing this loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Linear vs Square Law** – In PMMC, $T_d \propto I$ (linear); in Moving Iron, $T_d \propto I^2$ (square). Mixing them up loses **3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 1**

- **Accuracy** = closeness to *true* value. **Precision** = closeness *among* readings.
- **Static Sensitivity** $S = \Delta\theta / \Delta x$. **Resolution** = smallest detectable input change.
- **Loading Effect** can be minimized by $R_m \gg R_s$ for voltmeters.
- **Indicating** instruments give *instantaneous* values; **Integrating** instruments totalize; **Recording** instruments produce continuous traces.
- Three operating torques: **Deflecting** ($T_d$), **Controlling** ($T_c$), **Damping** ($T_{damp}$).
- At equilibrium: $T_d = T_c$, $T_{damp} = 0$.
- **Spring control** is universal; **Gravity control** is for horizontally-mounted meters.
- **Eddy current damping** is the most efficient and commonly used.
- **PMMC** equation: $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K_s} \cdot I$ → uniform linear scale.
- **Ammeter shunt:** $R_{sh} = R_m / (m - 1)$.
- **Voltmeter multiplier:** $R_s = V / I_m - R_m$.
- **Voltmeter sensitivity** is rated in **Ω/V**; higher is better.
- **Series Ohmmeter** uses $R_x = R_h \cdot (I_{fs}/I - 1)$; scale is *reversed*.
- **Suspension Galvanometer** uses a taut ribbon instead of pivots/jewels for ultra-sensitive microcurrent detection.
- **Multimeter** combines ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter via a rotary selector switch and precision shunt/multiplier network.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Indicating Instruments - Dynamometer, Watt-Hour Meter, Power Factor Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** AC Indicating Instruments - Dynamometer, Watt-Hour Meter, Power Factor Meter # AC Indicating Instruments: Dynamometer, Watt-Hour Meter & Power Factor Meter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are AC Indicating Instruments? An **AC Indicating Instrument** is a measurement device that directly displays the instantaneous or integrated magnitude of an alternating electrical quantity (voltage, current, power, energy, or power factor) using a mechanical pointer deflection system against a calibrated scale. Unlike DC instruments that rely on magnetic effects of steady current, AC instruments must overcome the bidirectional nature of alternating current to produce a **unidirectional (average) deflecting torque**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (PCBMT403 Module 1):** Students must understand construction, working principle, torque equation, phasor diagrams, and sources of error for Dynamometer-type instruments, Induction-type Watt-Hour Meters, and Power Factor Meters. Numericals based on meter constants and torque equations are **high-frequency** in End Semester Examinations. ### 1.2 The Dynamometer Instrument — Core Definition The **Electrodynamometer (or Dynamometer)** is an AC/DC indicating instrument whose operating torque is produced by the **electrodynamic interaction** between two coils — a pair of **fixed (current) coils** carrying the load current and a movable **(potential or voltage) coil** carrying a current proportional to the circuit voltage. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** *"A dynamometer type instrument is one in which the operating field is produced by the current flowing in the instrument itself, in contrast to permanent-magnet moving-coil instruments where the field is fixed. Hence the deflecting torque is proportional to the product of the two coil currents."* **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine two bar magnets. If you hold one fixed and bring another near it, they will either attract or repel based on their relative orientation. Now imagine both "magnets" are actually coils whose strengths (magnetization) depend on the currents you push through them. The Dynamometer does exactly this in miniature — the **stronger** the currents in both coils and the **more aligned** (in phase) they are, the larger the mechanical force twisting the spindle. This is why Dynamometers naturally measure **true RMS power** in AC circuits, making them indispensable for laboratory-grade wattmeters. ### 1.3 The Watt-Hour Meter — Core Definition A **Watt-Hour Meter** (or **Energy Meter**) is an integrating instrument that measures the **total electrical energy** consumed by a load over a period of time. In AC systems, the universally employed construction is the **Induction-Type Watt-Hour Meter**, based on the principle of the two-phase induction motor (Ferraris Principle). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Distinction:** A *Wattmeter* indicates instantaneous power ($P$, in watts). A *Watt-Hour Meter* integrates power over time to yield energy ($E = \int P \, dt$, in kWh). The standard domestic electricity billing unit in India is the **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** = 3.6 × 10⁶ Joules. **Conceptual Analogy:** A Watt-Hour Meter is to electricity what an **odometer** is to a car. The car speedometer shows instantaneous speed (analogous to a wattmeter), but the odometer adds up every kilometer driven (analogous to the watt-hour meter counting revolutions of an aluminum disc). Each disc revolution is proportional to a fixed quantity of energy consumed — this fixed quantity is called the **Meter Constant (Kh)**. ### 1.4 The Power Factor Meter — Core Definition A **Power Factor Meter** is a special-purpose indicating instrument that displays the **phase angle** ($\phi$) between the alternating voltage and current of a load, expressed as $\cos\phi$ on its calibrated scale. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Electrodynamic (Dynamometer-type) Power Factor Meter** for single-phase and the **Rotating-Field / Two-Deflecting-Coil Type** for three-phase systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Reality:** A Power Factor Meter has **NO control springs** and **NO damping chambers**. The deflecting position is set purely by the equilibrium of two opposing torques produced by two coils at right angles, with the pointer coming to rest where these torques are exactly equal and opposite. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a Power Factor Meter as a tug-of-war between two teams (Coil 1 and Coil 2) pulling on a rope at right angles. Coil 1 pulls harder when current and voltage are in phase (resistive load, pf = 1). Coil 2 pulls harder when there is a 90° phase shift (purely reactive load, pf = 0). Where the pointer settles reveals the "balance point" between resistive and reactive behaviors — that's the power factor. ### 1.5 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Deflecting Torque | $T_d$ | N·m | Driving torque on spindle | | Controlling Torque | $T_c$ | N·m | Spring-restoring torque | | Meter Constant | $K_h$ | **rev/kWh** | Energy meter calibration | | Disc Speed | $N$ | rev/kWh | Revolutions per unit energy | | Phase Angle | $\phi$ | degrees or radians | Power factor = $\cos\phi$ | | Reference Frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** (India) | Disc design parameter | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Dynamometer Instrument — Operational Logic The Dynamometer instrument follows the principle of **electromagnetic force between two current-carrying coils**. Let us break down the operational sequence: **Step 1 — Magnetic Field Generation:** The fixed coil (current coil) carries the line current $I_1$ and establishes a magnetic flux density $B_1$ at the location of the movable coil. Since $I_1$ is AC, $B_1$ is also alternating at the supply frequency $f$. **Step 2 — Force on Movable Coil:** The movable coil (potential coil) carries current $I_2$ proportional to the line voltage. The instantaneous force on a conductor of length $l$ in field $B_1$ is $F = B_1 l I_2$. This produces an instantaneous torque: $$T_{inst} = K \cdot B_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ where $M$ is the mutual inductance between the coils and $\theta$ is the deflection angle. **Step 3 — Averaging for AC (CRITICAL STEP):** Because the meter cannot follow 50 Hz oscillations mechanically, the pointer responds to the **average deflecting torque** over one cycle: $$T_d \propto \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T i_1(t) \cdot i_2(t) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \, dt$$ Assuming sinusoidal steady-state currents $i_1 = I_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$ and $i_2 = I_{2m} \sin(\omega t + \phi)$: $$T_d \propto I_{1,rms} \cdot I_{2,rms} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ **Step 4 — Why $\cos\phi$ Appears:** The product of two sinusoidal quantities averaged over a cycle always yields the cosine of their phase difference. This is the **mathematical signature** of true power measurement. **Step 5 — Deflection Equation:** At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$ (spring torque). Since $T_c \propto \theta$: $$\theta = K_1 \cdot I_{1,rms} \cdot I_{2,rms} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ For an **air-core dynamometer**, $M$ varies linearly with $\theta$, making the scale **non-uniform (crowded near the center)**. For an **iron-core dynamometer**, $M$ is shaped to compensate, yielding a more uniform scale. ### 2.2 Dynamometer as Wattmeter, Voltmeter, Ammeter > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multiplier Configuration Rule:** Connecting the voltage coil in series with a high resistance and across the load converts the dynamometer into a **wattmeter**. Removing the series resistance makes it a **voltmeter**. Bypassing the fixed coil with a low-resistance shunt converts it into an **ammeter**. ### 2.3 Induction-Type Watt-Hour Meter — Operational Logic The Induction Watt-Hour Meter follows the **Ferraris Principle (1885)**: **Step 1 — Two Coils, Two Fluxes:** - **Current Coil (CC):** Few turns of heavy wire, connected in series with the load. Carries line current $I$ and produces flux $\phi_I \propto I$. - **Voltage Coil (VC):** Many turns of fine wire, connected across the supply. Carries current $I_v$ lagging voltage by ~90° (due to high inductance). Produces flux $\phi_V \propto V$. **Step 2 — Two Alternating Fluxes Link an Aluminum Disc:** Two fluxes $\phi_V$ and $\phi_I$ pass through the aluminum disc at two different points, inducing **eddy currents** in the disc by Faraday's Law. **Step 3 — Eddy Currents × Fluxes = Driving Torque:** Each eddy current interacts with the *other* flux to produce a force on the disc. The average torque is: $$T_d \propto \phi_V \cdot \phi_I \cdot \sin(\beta)$$ where $\beta$ is the angle between the two flux phasors. With proper design, $\beta = 90° - \phi$ (where $\phi$ is the load phase angle), giving: $$T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = P \text{ (instantaneous power)}$$ **Step 4 — Braking Torque (Permanent Magnet):** A permanent magnet produces a constant flux $\phi_B$ through the disc. Eddy currents induced by disc motion react with $\phi_B$ to produce a **braking torque**: $$T_b \propto N \cdot \phi_B^2$$ where $N$ is the disc speed in rev/s. **Step 5 — Steady-State Energy Equation:** At equilibrium ($T_d = T_b$): $$P \cdot K_1 = N \cdot K_2 \cdot \phi_B^2$$ $$N = \frac{K_1 \cdot P}{K_2 \cdot \phi_B^2} = K \cdot P$$ Integrating over time: $$\text{Total Revolutions} = \int N \, dt = K \int P \, dt = K \cdot E$$ Therefore: $$E = \frac{N}{K} = N \cdot K_h$$ where $K_h = 1/K$ is the **Meter Constant** (rev/kWh). ### 2.4 Power Factor Meter — Operational Logic The **Electrodynamic Power Factor Meter** has two crossed coils (90° apart) on the same spindle: **Step 1 — Coil Currents:** - **Coil A** (reference): Carries current $I_A \propto I$ (in phase with line current). - **Coil B** (quadrature): Connected through a non-inductive resistor such that $I_B \propto V$ and is in phase with the supply voltage. **Step 2 — Field Phase Relationship:** - Coil A produces field $B_A \propto I \cdot \sin(\omega t)$. - Coil B produces field $B_B \propto V \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. **Step 3 — Torque Balance (No Spring!):** The two coils produce opposing torques whose **average values** depend on the deflection $\theta$: $$T_1 = K_1 \cdot I \cdot V \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\phi$$ $$T_2 = K_2 \cdot I \cdot V \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi$$ At equilibrium $T_1 = T_2$: $$\theta = \tan^{-1}(\tan\phi) = \phi$$ **Step 4 — For Three-Phase Meter:** For balanced 3-phase systems, due to the 120° displacement between line voltages: $$\theta = \phi \quad \text{(directly)}$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Instrument | Key Formula | Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dynamometer Torque | $T_d \propto I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | $I_1, I_2, \phi, M, \theta$ | N·m | | 2 | Dynamometer as Wattmeter | $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = P$ | $V, I, \phi$ | N·m | | 3 | Watt-Hour Driving Torque | $T_d = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | $K_1$ = constant | N·m | | 4 | Watt-Hour Braking Torque | $T_b = K_2 \cdot N \cdot \phi_B^2$ | $N$ = disc speed (rev/s) | N·m | | 5 | Energy from Revolutions | $E = N_{total} \cdot K_h$ | $N_{total}$ = total rev | kWh | | 6 | Meter Constant | $K_h = \dfrac{N_{total}}{E}$ | — | rev/kWh | | 7 | Single-Phase PF Meter | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(\tan\phi)$ | — | radians | | 8 | Three-Phase PF Meter | $\theta = \phi$ | — | radians | | 9 | Creeping Error Adjust | Two holes diametrically on disc | — | — | | 10 | Disc Speed Relation | $N = K \cdot P$ | $P$ = power (W) | rev/s | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Dynamometer wattmeters are the gold standard for laboratory power measurement (accuracy class 0.1 – 0.5). Induction watt-hour meters are ubiquitous in domestic metering across India (typically accuracy class 2.0). Power factor meters are essential in industrial load surveys to determine penalty/surcharge zones in electricity tariffs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Average Deflecting Torque in a Dynamometer Instrument We begin with the instantaneous electromagnetic force between two current-carrying coils. The instantaneous current in coil 1 is $i_1(t)$ and in coil 2 is $i_2(t)$. **Step 1 — Starting Equation (Instantaneous Torque):** The instantaneous deflecting torque on the movable coil is given by the change in stored magnetic energy with respect to angular displacement: $$T_{inst}(t) = i_1(t) \cdot i_2(t) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ where $M$ is the mutual inductance between the fixed and movable coils, and $\theta$ is the deflection angle from the geometric reference position. **Step 2 — Assumption of Sinusoidal Steady State:** Let the two currents be sinusoidal at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ with a phase difference $\phi$ between them: $$i_1(t) = I_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$$ $$i_2(t) = I_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ (The negative sign convention for $\phi$ depends on whether coil 2 lags or leads coil 1; the final result is symmetric.) **Step 3 — Substitute into Instantaneous Torque:** $$T_{inst}(t) = I_{1m} \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \phi) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Trigonometric Identity:** Using the product-to-sum identity: $$\sin A \cdot \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$$ We obtain: $$T_{inst}(t) = \frac{I_{1m} I_{2m}}{2} \left[\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\right] \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ **Step 5 — Average Over One Cycle:** The pointer cannot follow the high-frequency $2\omega$ component. Averaging over one complete cycle $T = 2\pi/\omega$: $$T_d = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T T_{inst}(t) \, dt$$ The integral of $\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ over a complete cycle is zero. Hence: $$T_d = \frac{I_{1m} I_{2m}}{2} \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ **Step 6 — Convert Peak to RMS:** Since $I_{1,rms} = I_{1m}/\sqrt{2}$ and $I_{2,rms} = I_{2m}/\sqrt{2}$: $$T_d = I_{1,rms} \cdot I_{2,rms} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$ $$\boxed{T_d = K \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}}$$ where $K$ is the dimensional constant and $I_1, I_2$ are RMS values. **This is the master equation for all dynamometer-type instruments.** ### 3.2 Derivation: Watt-Hour Meter Speed vs. Power **Step 1 — Driving Torque:** The two alternating fluxes from the current coil ($\phi_I \propto I$) and the voltage coil ($\phi_V \propto V$) link the aluminum disc. Eddy currents are induced in the disc that react with the orthogonal flux to produce a torque: $$T_d = K_1 \cdot \phi_V \cdot \phi_I \cdot \sin\beta$$ where $\beta$ is the angle between $\phi_V$ and $\phi_I$ in space-time. With proper phase adjustment using shading bands, $\beta = (90° - \phi)$. **Step 2 — Substitute Phase Adjustment:** $$T_d = K_1 \cdot \phi_V \cdot \phi_I \cdot \sin(90° - \phi) = K_1 \cdot \phi_V \cdot \phi_I \cdot \cos\phi$$ Since $\phi_V \propto V$ and $\phi_I \propto I$: $$T_d = K_2 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_2 \cdot P$$ **Step 3 — Braking Torque from Permanent Magnet:** The aluminum disc rotates with angular velocity $\omega_d = 2\pi N$ (where $N$ = rev/s). Eddy currents induced in the disc by the permanent magnet flux $\phi_B$ react with $\phi_B$ itself to produce a retarding torque: $$T_b = K_3 \cdot \phi_B^2 \cdot N$$ **Step 4 — Steady-State Equilibrium:** At constant speed, $T_d = T_b$: $$K_2 \cdot P = K_3 \cdot \phi_B^2 \cdot N$$ Solving for $N$: $$N = \frac{K_2}{K_3 \cdot \phi_B^2} \cdot P$$ Define $K = \dfrac{K_2}{K_3 \cdot \phi_B^2}$: $$\boxed{N = K \cdot P}$$ **Step 5 — Energy Integration:** Energy consumed over a time interval $T_0$: $$E = \int_0^{T_0} P \, dt = \int_0^{T_0} \frac{N}{K} \, dt = \frac{1}{K} \int_0^{T_0} N \, dt = \frac{N_{total}}{K}$$ Define the **Meter Constant** $K_h = 1/K$: $$\boxed{E = N_{total} \cdot K_h \quad \text{(kWh)}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Single-Phase Electrodynamic Power Factor Meter **Step 1 — Coil Geometry:** Two identical coils A and B are mounted rigidly on the same spindle, crossed at 90°. There is no control spring. **Step 2 — Coil Currents:** - Coil A is connected in series with a high non-inductive resistance such that $I_A \propto V$ and **lags V by a small angle $\alpha$** (slightly inductive). - Coil B is connected in series with a high inductance such that $I_B$ is in **phase quadrature** with the supply voltage — practically achieved by using a non-inductive resistor in series. Actually, the standard textbook construction is: - Coil A current: $I_A$ in phase with the line current $I$ (reference field). - Coil B current: $I_B$ in phase with the supply voltage $V$. **Step 3 — Torque Expressions:** Coil A produces a torque proportional to $I_A \cdot I_B \cdot \sin\theta$, while Coil B produces a torque proportional to $I_A \cdot I_B \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi$. The two opposing torques are: $$T_1 = K_1 \cdot I \cdot V \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\phi$$ $$T_2 = K_2 \cdot I \cdot V \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi$$ **Step 4 — Equilibrium:** For identical coils, $K_1 = K_2 = K$. At steady deflection: $$T_1 = T_2$$ $$K \cdot I \cdot V \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\phi = K \cdot I \cdot V \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi$$ **Step 5 — Simplify:** $$\sin\theta \cdot \cos\phi = \cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi$$ $$\tan\theta = \tan\phi$$ $$\boxed{\theta = \phi}$$ The deflection is **independent of $V$ and $I$**, depending only on the phase angle. This is a unique property of the PF meter — it reads the phase angle correctly regardless of load magnitude. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern **Problem:** A single-phase energy meter has a meter constant $K_h = 600$ rev/kWh. During a test, the disc makes 100 revolutions in 2 minutes at a load of 5 kW. Calculate the percentage error. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Actual Energy Consumed:** $$E_{actual} = P \times t = 5 \text{ kW} \times \frac{2}{60} \text{ h} = \frac{5}{30} = 0.1667 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2 — Energy Recorded by the Meter:** $$E_{recorded} = N_{total} \times K_h = 100 \text{ rev} \times \frac{1}{600} \text{ kWh/rev} = 0.1667 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3 — Percentage Error:** $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{E_{recorded} - E_{actual}}{E_{actual}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.1667 - 0.1667}{0.1667} \times 100 = 0\%$$ The meter is accurate in this test condition. **Variant Numerical:** If the meter records 105 revolutions instead of 100 in the same conditions: $$E_{recorded} = \frac{105}{600} = 0.175 \text{ kWh}$$ $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.175 - 0.1667}{0.1667} \times 100 = +5\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Mixing up the meter constant — some textbooks define $K_h$ as rev/kWh while others as Wh/rev. Always check the units in the numerator and denominator before substituting. --- ## 4.
