> - **Switching Element:** Historically **electromechanical chopper** (now obsolete); modern designs use **JFET/MOSFET analog switches** or **Gilbert Cell multipliers**.
> - **Output Polarity Convention:** A reference convention of **+ for in-phase** and **− for 180° out-of-phase** is universally adopted.
> - **Noise Rejection:** PSRs achieve **>60 dB** of out-of-band noise rejection when properly locked to a known reference frequency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Chopping (Switching) Type PSR

The most common implementation is the **switching-type PSR** (also called the **chopper-type PSR**). It works on the principle of **synchronous multiplication followed by low-pass filtering**.

### 2.1.1 The Block Diagram Logic

The system consists of three cascaded stages:

1. **Input Signal** $v_s(t)$ — the unknown AC signal under measurement.
2. **Electronic Switch (Chopper)** — controlled by a **reference square wave** $v_r(t)$ of the same fundamental frequency $f_0$ as $v_s(t)$.
3. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — extracts the DC (average) component of the chopped waveform.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Operation

Let the input be:
$$v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

and the reference be a **unit-amplitude square wave** of the same frequency:
$$v_r(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \le \omega t < \pi \\ -1, & \pi \le \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

The output of the chopper is:
$$v_o(t) = v_s(t) \cdot v_r(t)$$

This product is a **full-wave rectified version** when $\theta = 0$ (in-phase), and a **full-wave inverted version** when $\theta = 180°$ (out-of-phase).

### 2.1.3 The Fourier Series of a Square Wave

A unit-amplitude symmetric square wave of angular frequency $\omega$ can be expanded as:
$$v_r(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega t)$$

Substituting into the multiplier output:
$$v_o(t) = v_s(t) \cdot v_r(t) = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(\omega t + \theta) \sin(n\omega t)}{n}$$

Applying the trigonometric identity $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$ and noting that the LPF removes all AC components (frequencies $\ge \omega$), the surviving DC term is:

$$V_{o,\text{DC}} = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical PSR output equation** — it is the single most important formula in this entire topic. The KTU 2024 examiner will expect you to either derive it, apply it, or plot the $\cos(\theta)$ phase response.

### 2.2 The Three Canonical Phase Cases

| Case | Phase Angle $\theta$ | Physical Meaning | Output DC Polarity | Output DC Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | $0°$ (in-phase) | Signal and reference rise/fall together | **Positive (Maximum)** | $\frac{2V_s}{\pi}$ |
| **Case 2** | $90°$ (quadrature) | Signal leads/lags reference by quarter cycle | **Zero (Null)** | $0$ |
| **Case 3** | $180°$ (anti-phase) | Signal is mirror image of reference | **Negative (Maximum)** | $-\frac{2V_s}{\pi}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Null at 90° is the key instrument feature.** It is this null behavior that allows PSRs to act as **zero-detectors in AC bridges** — when the bridge is balanced, the input signal is exactly $90°$ to the reference, and the PSR output is zero. This is fundamentally more precise than amplitude-only detection.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input signal | $v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta)$ | Volts, $V_s$ = peak amplitude |
| 2 | Reference square wave (Fourier) | $v_r(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n \text{ odd}} \frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$ | Dimensionless (normalized) |
| 3 | PSR DC output (general) | $V_{o,\text{DC}} = \dfrac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$ | Volts |
| 4 | Output at $\theta = 0°$ | $V_o = \dfrac{2 V_s}{\pi}$ | Maximum positive DC |
| 5 | Output at $\theta = 90°$ | $V_o = 0$ | Null condition |
| 6 | Output at $\theta = 180°$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{2 V_s}{\pi}$ | Maximum negative DC |
| 7 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{2}{\pi}$ | V/V (slope at $\theta=0$) |
| 8 | Phase sensitivity | $\dfrac{dV_o}{d\theta} \vert_{\theta=0} = 0$ | Maximum sensitivity at $\theta = \pm 90°$ for phase detection |
| 9 | Scale factor for sinusoidal reference (instead of square) | $V_{o,\text{DC}} = \dfrac{V_s V_r}{2}\cos(\theta)$ | $V_r$ = peak of reference |
| 10 | Bandwidth of LPF | $f_c \ll f_0$ | Rejects all harmonics of $f_0$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a KTU examiner tests this topic:** The PSR is the *heart* of every precision AC measuring instrument. The following applications are routinely asked in the 14-mark questions:
>
> 1. **AC Wheatstone / Maxwell / Hay / Schering Bridges** — Used as the null detector instead of a vibration galvanometer. The PSR provides a stable, drift-free DC null reading.
> 2. **Vector Voltmeter** — Measures both magnitude and phase of an AC voltage simultaneously.
> 3. **Lock-In Amplifier** — Extracts signals at nanovolt levels buried in microvolts of noise; the PSR is the first demodulation stage.
> 4. **LCR Meter (Automatic Bridge)** — Modern digital LCR meters use the PSR principle to compute R, L, and C in real time.
> 5. **Phase Sequence Indicator** and **Power Factor Meter**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION OF THE PSR OUTPUT EQUATION

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 3. Every step is shown explicitly. Follow each line carefully.

## 3.1 Setup and Assumptions

Let:
$$v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

This is the input AC signal with peak amplitude $V_s$ and unknown phase $\theta$ relative to the reference.

Let the reference $v_r(t)$ be a symmetric square wave of the same fundamental frequency $\omega$ and unit amplitude:
$$v_r(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -1, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

with period $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$.

## 3.2 Fourier Expansion of the Reference

A unit-amplitude square wave (odd symmetry, zero average) has the well-known Fourier series:
$$v_r(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \cdots \right]$$

In compact summation form (sum over odd $n$ only):
$$v_r(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega t)$$

**Reasoning:** The series contains only odd harmonics because a symmetric square wave has half-wave symmetry: $v_r(t + T/2) = -v_r(t)$.

## 3.3 Multiplication Stage

The switching element multiplies the input by the reference. The output of the multiplier is:
$$v_o(t) = v_s(t) \cdot v_r(t)$$

Substituting:
$$v_o(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega t)$$

Factoring out the constants:
$$v_o(t) = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(\omega t + \theta) \sin(n\omega t)$$

## 3.4 Trigonometric Identity Application

Using the product-to-sum identity:
$$\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \cos(A - B) - \cos(A + B) \right]$$

with $A = \omega t + \theta$ and $B = n\omega t$:

$$\sin(\omega t + \theta)\sin(n\omega t) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \cos\bigl((\omega t + \theta) - n\omega t\bigr) - \cos\bigl((\omega t + \theta) + n\omega t\bigr) \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \cos\bigl((1-n)\omega t + \theta\bigr) - \cos\bigl((1+n)\omega t + \theta\bigr) \right]$$

Substituting back:
$$v_o(t) = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2n} \left[ \cos\bigl((1-n)\omega t + \theta\bigr) - \cos\bigl((1+n)\omega t + \theta\bigr) \right]$$

$$v_o(t) = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \left[ \cos\bigl((1-n)\omega t + \theta\bigr) - \cos\bigl((1+n)\omega t + \theta\bigr) \right]$$

## 3.5 The Critical Term ($n = 1$)

The $n = 1$ term is special because it produces a **DC (zero-frequency) component**:

When $n = 1$, the first cosine argument becomes:
$$(1 - 1)\omega t + \theta = 0 \cdot \omega t + \theta = \theta$$

Therefore the first part of the $n=1$ term gives:
$$\frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cos(\theta) = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$$

This is a **true constant** (independent of $t$) — it is the **DC component** we seek.

## 3.6 Effect of the Low-Pass Filter

All other terms in the summation have arguments of the form $(1 \pm n)\omega t$ with $n \ge 1$, meaning they oscillate at frequencies $\omega, 2\omega, 3\omega, 4\omega, \dots$ (the fundamental and all harmonics).

A well-designed low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c \ll f_0 = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$ removes **all** these AC components and passes only the DC term.

Therefore, the filtered PSR output is:
$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)}$$

## 3.7 Verification at the Three Canonical Phase Values

**Case 1 — In-phase ($\theta = 0°$):**
$$V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(0°) = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cdot 1 = +\frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.6366\, V_s$$

This is the **maximum positive DC output** — physically, the full-wave rectification of $V_s$ gives a DC average of $\frac{2 V_s}{\pi}$, which matches ordinary full-wave rectifier behaviour.

**Case 2 — Quadrature ($\theta = 90°$):**
$$V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(90°) = 0$$

This is the **null condition** — used as the null detector in AC bridges.

**Case 3 — Anti-phase ($\theta = 180°$):**
$$V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(180°) = -\frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \approx -0.6366\, V_s$$

The **polarity reverses**, demonstrating phase sensitivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** The peak input amplitude $V_s$ is preserved up to the constant scale factor $\frac{2}{\pi}$. The cosine dependence is purely a function of phase, which is exactly the behaviour we want from a "phase-sensitive" device.

## 3.8 Sinusoidal Reference Variant (Brief Derivation)

If instead of a square wave, the reference is a sinusoid $v_r(t) = V_r \sin(\omega t)$, the multiplier output is:
$$v_o(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta) \cdot V_r \sin(\omega t)$$

$$= V_s V_r \cdot \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(\theta) - \cos(2\omega t + \theta)\bigr]$$

After low-pass filtering, the second term vanishes and:
$$V_o = \frac{V_s V_r}{2} \cos(\theta)$$

This is the **multiplier-type PSR** output equation — note the extra factor of $V_r/2$ compared to the switching-type version (where $V_r$ is implicitly 1 for a square wave's fundamental component $\frac{4}{\pi}$... actually, the scaling differs, but both have the $\cos(\theta)$ dependence which is the universal signature of a PSR).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phase Sensitive Rectifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input AC Signal Vs sine omega t plus theta] --> C[Electronic Chopper Switch]
    B[Reference Square Wave Vr same frequency] --> C
    C --> D[Multiplier Output V multiplied by Vr]
    D --> E[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    E --> F[DC Output Vo proportional to cos theta]
    F --> G[Indicating Meter or ADC]
    
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style D fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style F fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825
    style G fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F
```

**Reading the flow:**
- The **blue input** is the unknown AC signal under test.
- The **orange reference** is a fixed, locally-generated square wave of the same frequency.
- The **purple block** is the chopper/switch — this is the phase-discriminating element.
- The **red stage** is the multiplied waveform containing the desired DC term plus AC harmonics.
- The **green LPF** strips the harmonics and passes only the DC.
- The **yellow DC output** is read by a meter or ADC; its *sign* and *magnitude* jointly encode the input's phase and amplitude.

## 4.2 Decision Flow — How a PSR Reacts to Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Input Vs sine omega t plus theta arrives] --> Q{Is theta between minus 90 and plus 90 degrees?}
    Q -- YES --> R[Output is POSITIVE DC]
    Q -- NO --> S[Output is NEGATIVE DC]
    R --> T[Indicate positive meter deflection]
    S --> U[Indicate negative meter deflection]
    T --> V[For theta equals zero: MAX positive equals 2 Vs over pi]
    U --> W[For theta equals 180: MAX negative equals minus 2 Vs over pi]
    V --> X[For theta equals 90: NULL equals 0 V]
    W --> X
    X --> Y[Use null for bridge balance detection]
```

## 4.3 Signal Path Within an AC Bridge Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[AC Source oscillator] --> Brg[AC Bridge circuit]
    Brg --> Det[Phase Sensitive Detector PSR]
    Ref[Reference from same AC source] --> Det
    Det --> Amp[DC Amplifier]
    Amp --> Out[Indicating Meter]
    
    style Src fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD
    style Brg fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00
    style Det fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457
    style Ref fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F
    style Amp fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#4527A0
    style Out fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#212121
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Insight:** The reference signal in an AC bridge PSR is **derived from the same oscillator** that energises the bridge. This guarantees identical frequency and a fixed phase relationship, allowing the PSR to act as a phase-sensitive null detector with extreme sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define a Phase Sensitive Rectifier. How is it different from an ordinary rectifier?**

**Model Answer:**
A Phase Sensitive Rectifier (PSR) is a rectifier that produces a DC output whose **magnitude is proportional to the in-phase component** of the input AC signal and whose **polarity depends on the phase relationship** between the input and a reference signal of the same frequency.

| Feature | Ordinary Rectifier | Phase Sensitive Rectifier |
|---|---|---|
| Phase awareness | Insensitive to phase | Sensitive to phase |
| Output polarity | Always positive | Positive, zero, or negative |
| Reference signal | Not required | Mandatory (same frequency) |
| Primary use | Power supplies, DC extraction | Bridge null detection, vector measurement |

**(3 marks — definition 1, difference 1, table-based justification 1)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is the significance of the 90° null condition in a PSR-based AC bridge detector?**

**Model Answer:**
When the phase angle between the input and reference is exactly $90°$, the PSR output is **zero**. This null occurs at the **balance condition of an AC bridge**, allowing the operator to detect balance with extreme precision. Unlike a vibration galvanometer (which only shows amplitude minimum), a PSR null is **true zero** — meaning both the in-phase and quadrature components vanish simultaneously, providing a sharp, unambiguous, and **drift-free** balance indication. This is the primary reason modern bridges universally employ PSR-based detectors.

**(3 marks — null condition 1, balance significance 1, advantage over galvanometer 1)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Phase Sensitive Rectifier (Switching/Chopper Type). Derive the output equation and discuss its application in an AC bridge as a null detector.**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Block Diagram (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The switching-type PSR consists of three main blocks: an electronic chopper, a low-pass filter, and an indicating meter, all synchronised to a reference square wave.

**Operation:**
1. The **input AC signal** $v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta)$ and the **reference square wave** $v_r(t)$ of the same fundamental frequency are applied to the chopper.
2. During the **positive half-cycle** of the reference, the chopper passes the input signal directly.
3. During the **negative half-cycle** of the reference, the chopper **inverts the input signal** (multiplies by $-1$).
4. The resulting waveform is a **phase-corrected full-wave rectified signal** that contains a DC component proportional to $\cos(\theta)$ plus higher harmonics.
5. The **low-pass filter** removes all AC harmonics, leaving only the DC.
6. The DC output is given by $V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$.

**Three working cases:**
- $\theta = 0°$: Output is **maximum positive** ($+2V_s/\pi$).
- $\theta = 90°$: Output is **zero** (null).
- $\theta = 180°$: Output is **maximum negative** ($-2V_s/\pi$).

**(7 marks: Block diagram 2, operation steps 3, three cases table 2)**

#### Part (b) — Derivation and AC Bridge Application (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation** (key steps):

Starting from $v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta)$ and the Fourier expansion of the unit square wave:
$$v_r(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega t)$$

The chopper output:
$$v_o(t) = v_s(t) \cdot v_r(t) = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(\omega t + \theta) \sin(n\omega t)}{n}$$

Using $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$, the $n=1$ term yields a DC component:
$$V_{o,\text{DC}} = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$$

The LPF removes all $(1 \pm n)\omega$ harmonic terms, leaving this DC as the final output.

**Application in AC Bridge:**

In AC bridges (Maxwell, Hay, Schering, Wien), the detector must distinguish between a true balance and a false minimum caused by harmonics. A conventional AC detector (vibration galvanometer, headphones) often shows an ambiguous minimum.

When a PSR is used:
- The **reference is taken from the same oscillator** that drives the bridge.
- At balance, the bridge output is exactly $90°$ to the excitation (this is a property of the bridge topology).
- The PSR therefore reads **exactly zero** at balance — a **true, sharp, drift-free null**.
- The polarity reversal around balance tells the operator which way to adjust the standard arm.

**Advantages over conventional detectors:**
1. Sharper, unambiguous null.
2. DC output is easy to amplify and read.
3. Insensitive to frequency drift (works as long as the reference tracks the source).
4. Can be automated with servo-driven bridge balancing.

**(7 marks: Derivation 4, application explanation 3)**

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q: Explain the multiplier-type Phase Sensitive Rectifier. Compare it with the switching (chopper) type. Derive its output equation when a sinusoidal reference is used.**

#### Part (a) — Multiplier-Type PSR and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **multiplier-type PSR** uses an electronic **four-quadrant analog multiplier** (e.g., AD633, MC1496 Gilbert cell) as the phase-discriminating element, with a **sinusoidal reference**.

**Setup:**
- Input: $v_s(t) = V_s \sin(\omega t + \theta)$
- Reference: $v_r(t) = V_r \sin(\omega t)$
- Multiplier output: $v_o(t) = v_s(t) \cdot v_r(t) = V_s V_r \sin(\omega t + \theta) \sin(\omega t)$

**Derivation:**

Using the identity $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:
$$v_o(t) = \frac{V_s V_r}{2} \bigl[\cos(\theta) - \cos(2\omega t + \theta)\bigr]$$

After LPF (which removes the $2\omega$ term):
$$V_o = \frac{V_s V_r}{2} \cos(\theta)$$

**Three cases:**
- $\theta = 0°$: $V_o = \frac{V_s V_r}{2}$ (maximum positive)
- $\theta = 90°$: $V_o = 0$ (null)
- $\theta = 180°$: $V_o = -\frac{V_s V_r}{2}$ (maximum negative)

**(7 marks: Block description 2, derivation 4, three cases 1)**

#### Part (b) — Comparison with Switching-Type PSR (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Switching (Chopper) Type | Multiplier Type |
|---|---|---|
| Reference signal | Square wave (preferred) | Sinusoidal |
| Phase-discriminating element | Electronic switch (FET/MOSFET) | Four-quadrant analog multiplier |
| Output equation | $V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$ | $V_o = \frac{V_s V_r}{2} \cos(\theta)$ |
| Harmonics generated internally | Yes (odd harmonics) | No (only DC and 2$\omega$) |
| LPF requirement | Must reject up to highest harmonic | Easier (only 2$\omega$ to reject) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by chopper speed | Limited by multiplier bandwidth |
| Accuracy | Moderate | High (better linearity) |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher (precision multiplier IC) |
| Typical application | AC bridge null detectors, general instrumentation | Vector voltmeters, lock-in amplifiers, spectrum analysers |

**Key qualitative differences:**

1. The **multiplier type** is mathematically cleaner — no harmonic content, simpler LPF design.
2. The **switching type** is **cheaper and more rugged**, and works with simpler drive electronics.
3. The multiplier type's output amplitude depends on $V_r$ (the reference amplitude), giving an **extra degree of gain control**. The switching type's output is fixed by the $\frac{2}{\pi}$ geometric factor.
4. For very **high-frequency applications**, the switching type is limited by the chopper's switching time, while the multiplier can work up to several hundred MHz (Gilbert cell limiters).

**(7 marks: Comparison table 4, qualitative discussion 3)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{2}{\pi}$ factor:** Many students write the DC output as $V_s \cos(\theta)$. This is wrong. The correct expression is $\frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$ for a square-wave-referenced switching PSR. Marks are specifically awarded for the correct constant.
> 2. **Confusing switching-type with multiplier-type formulas:** Do not mix $V_o = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi}\cos(\theta)$ (switching) with $V_o = \frac{V_s V_r}{2}\cos(\theta)$ (multiplier with sinusoidal reference) in the same derivation.
> 3. **Skipping the LPF justification:** The derivation must explicitly show *which terms are removed by the LPF*. Students who jump from the product to the final answer lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning the reference signal source:** When describing AC bridge applications, you MUST state that the reference is taken from the same oscillator driving the bridge. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 5. **Sign convention errors:** Always state the convention (e.g., in-phase gives positive output) before working the problem. A sign error in the cos(180°) case is a common -1 mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Pin this to your wall the night before the exam:**

- [ ] **PSR = Rectifier + Phase Awareness** — it is an ordinary rectifier with a reference signal added to retain phase polarity.
- [ ] **Master Equation (Square Reference):** $V_o = \dfrac{2 V_s}{\pi} \cos(\theta)$
- [ ] **Master Equation (Sinusoidal Reference):** $V_o = \dfrac{V_s V_r}{2} \cos(\theta)$
- [ ] **Three Phase Rules:** $0° \Rightarrow +V_{\max}$, $90° \Rightarrow 0$ (NULL), $180° \Rightarrow -V_{\max}$
- [ ] **The Null is Gold** — used in AC bridges for ultra-precise balance detection.
- [ ] **Reference Must Match Frequency** — both signals at the same $\omega$, ideally with the reference derived from the same source.
- [ ] **LPF is Mandatory** — to remove all $(1 \pm n)\omega$ harmonic terms.
- [ ] **Fourier Trick** — every square-wave PSR derivation starts with $v_r(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n \text{ odd}} \frac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$.
- [ ] **Identity to memorise:** $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$.
- [ ] **Two Architectures:** Switching (chopper, cheap) vs. Multiplier (clean, accurate) — know both, know the difference.
- [ ] **Applications to list in any answer:** AC bridges, vector voltmeter, lock-in amplifier, LCR meter, phase meter.
- [ ] **Sign Convention:** In-phase = +ve output, Anti-phase = -ve output. State this in every numerical answer.
- [ ] **Numerical Trap:** $\frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.6366$ — use the exact form in derivations, the decimal only in final answers.
- [ ] **Frequency Limit:** Chopper-type limited to ~100 kHz; multiplier-type to ~500 MHz (Gilbert cell limit).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phase Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Phase Detectors — Core Definition & Intuitive Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phase Detector (PD)** is a non-linear signal processing circuit or transducer that compares two input signals of the **same frequency** and produces an output voltage (or current) that is a function of the **instantaneous phase difference** ($\phi$) between them. It is the central error-detection element of every **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** and is widely used in frequency synthesis, motor-speed control, FM demodulation, and precision instrumentation.

Mathematically, for two coherent sinusoidal inputs:

$$v_1(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1)$$

$$v_2(t) = V_2 \sin(\omega t + \phi_2)$$

The phase detector produces an output proportional to the phase error:

$$\theta_e = \phi_1 - \phi_2 = \Delta \phi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A phase detector is essentially a multiplier followed by a low-pass filter. Its **transfer characteristic** (output voltage vs. phase difference) defines whether the loop will lock and how stable it will be.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two Metronomes 🎵**
> Imagine two musicians tapping their feet to the same beat. If they are perfectly synchronized, the *time gap* (phase) between their taps is **zero**. A phase detector is like a stopwatch measuring the exact time gap between every foot-tap of Musician A and Musician B. If Musician A taps too early, the stopwatch records a *positive* error; if too late, a *negative* error. The stopwatch's display (the output voltage) tells the orchestra's conductor (the **VCO**) whether to speed up or slow down Musician B so both stay in perfect rhythm. This closed-loop dance is exactly how a PLL works — and the phase detector is the stopwatch.

## 1.3 Classification of Phase Detectors

| Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Analog / Multiplier** | Four-quadrant multiplier (e.g., AD633, MC1496) | Coherent demodulators, instrumentation |
| **Digital Switching** | XOR gate, Edge-triggered JK flip-flop | Basic digital PLLs, frequency multipliers |
| **Sequential / Charge-Pump** | Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) with charge pump | Modern high-performance PLLs, frequency synthesizers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Multiplier-as-Phase-Detector

A phase detector's heart is a **four-quadrant analog multiplier** whose inputs are the two signals being compared. The product contains a DC term proportional to the phase difference plus a high-frequency term removed by a low-pass filter.

Let the two inputs be:

$$v_1(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1)$$

$$v_2(t) = V_2 \cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$$

The multiplier output is:

$$v_{PD}(t) = K_m \, v_1(t) \cdot v_2(t)$$

where $K_m$ is the multiplier gain constant in **V⁻¹**. Expanding the product using the trigonometric identity:

$$\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}\big[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)\big]$$

$$v_{PD}(t) = K_m V_1 V_2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\big[\sin(2\omega t + \phi_1 + \phi_2) + \sin(\phi_1 - \phi_2)\big]$$

The $2\omega$ (sum frequency) component is rejected by the low-pass filter, leaving the desired DC output:

$$\boxed{\,v_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}\,\sin(\Delta\phi)\,}$$

$$v_d = K_d \,\sin(\Delta\phi) \quad \text{where} \quad K_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}$$

For small phase errors ($\Delta\phi < 30°$), $\sin(\Delta\phi) \approx \Delta\phi$, giving the linearized detector:

$$v_d \approx K_d \, \Delta\phi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The gain constant $K_d$ has units of V/rad.** It is the slope of the phase-detector characteristic at the lock point and is the single most important parameter used in PLL loop-gain calculations.

## 2.2 The Four-Quadrant Analog Multiplier Architecture

A **four-quadrant multiplier** operates in all four quadrants of its input plane — both inputs can be of any polarity, and the output reflects the signed product. The Gilbert Cell (translinear multiplier) is the most common monolithic implementation.

**Operating Quadrants:**

| $v_1$ sign | $v_2$ sign | Output sign | Quadrant |
|:----------:|:----------:|:-----------:|:--------:|
| + | + | + | I |
| + | − | − | II |
| − | − | + | III |
| − | + | − | IV |

> [!NOTE]
> A **two-quadrant multiplier** accepts inputs of only one polarity but with full output swing. The four-quadrant version is required for true AC-coupled phase detection because both sinusoidal inputs cross zero.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$ | Nonlinear PD characteristic | V |
| 2 | $v_d \approx K_d \, \Delta\phi$ | Linearized (small-signal) form | V |
| 3 | $K_d = \dfrac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}$ | Detector gain constant | V/rad |
| 4 | $v_{out} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{2}\cos(\phi_2 - \phi_1)$ | Output when inputs are sine–cosine | V |
| 5 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | LPF cut-off (must be << signal freq.) | Hz |
| 6 | $K_{PFD} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2\pi}$ | Phase-Frequency Detector gain | V/rad |
| 7 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{OH} \, t_p}{T/2}$ | Average output of XOR PD (square waves) | V |
| 8 | $\Delta\phi_{max} = \pi$ (XOR), $2\pi$ (PFD) | Maximum lock range | rad |
| 9 | $\theta_{lock} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ | XOR lock point (square wave inputs) | rad |
| 10 | $i_{CP} = I_p \, \dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi}$ | Charge-pump current in PFD | A |

> [!WARNING]
> **Always state the units of $K_d$ explicitly** (V/rad) in your KTU exam answers. Examiners award marks for the correct dimensional form, and forgetting the units is one of the most common reasons for partial credit loss.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Frequency Synthesizers** — PLLs in mobile phones, Wi-Fi routers, and GPS receivers use PDs to lock VCOs to ultra-stable crystal references.
- **Motor Speed Control** — Optical/encoder-based PDs regulate BLDC and stepper motors to within 0.01% of commanded RPM.
- **FM Demodulation** — In an FM-PLL receiver, the PD output directly reproduces the modulating audio signal.
- **Instrumentation & Measurement** — Lock-in amplifiers and laser interferometers use PDs to extract signals buried 100+ dB below noise.
- **Carrier Recovery in PSK/QAM** — Digital modems recover carrier phase for coherent demodulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Multiplier-Type Phase Detector Output

**Given:** Two coherent sinusoidal inputs
$$v_1(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1)$$
$$v_2(t) = V_2 \cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$$

**Step 1 — Write the multiplier output:**

$$v_{PD}(t) = K_m \, v_1(t) \cdot v_2(t)$$

This is direct: the multiplier produces the instantaneous product of the two inputs scaled by its gain constant $K_m$ (units V⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Substitute the input expressions:**

$$v_{PD}(t) = K_m \cdot V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1) \cdot V_2 \cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$$

$$v_{PD}(t) = K_m V_1 V_2 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1)\cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product-to-sum trigonometric identity:**

We use:
$$\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}\big[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)\big]$$

with $A = \omega t + \phi_1$ and $B = \omega t + \phi_2$.

$$v_{PD}(t) = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}\big[\sin\!\big((\omega t + \phi_1) + (\omega t + \phi_2)\big) + \sin\!\big((\omega t + \phi_1) - (\omega t + \phi_2)\big)\big]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the two sine arguments:**

$$A + B = 2\omega t + \phi_1 + \phi_2$$
$$A - B = \phi_1 - \phi_2 = \Delta\phi$$

Therefore:
$$v_{PD}(t) = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}\big[\sin(2\omega t + \phi_1 + \phi_2) + \sin(\Delta\phi)\big]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Low-Pass Filter (LPF):**

The first term $\sin(2\omega t + \phi_1 + \phi_2)$ is a sinusoid at **twice the carrier frequency** $2\omega$. The LPF cutoff $f_c$ is designed to be well below $2\omega$ (typically $f_c \ll 2f$ by a factor of 10 or more), so this term is completely attenuated to zero.

The second term $\sin(\Delta\phi)$ is a **DC component** (since $\Delta\phi$ is constant for coherent signals of the same frequency) and passes unattenuated.

**Step 6 — Final DC output voltage:**

$$\boxed{\,v_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}\sin(\Delta\phi) = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)\,}$$

where $K_d = \dfrac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}$ is the **phase-detector gain constant** with units of V/rad.

**Step 7 — Small-signal linearization:**

Using the Taylor expansion $\sin(x) = x - \dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \dots$, for small $\Delta\phi$:

$$v_d \approx K_d \,\Delta\phi \quad \text{(valid for } \vert \Delta\phi \vert \leq 0.5 \text{ rad)}$$

This linear form is what makes the PLL behave as a linear control system in lock condition.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — XOR Phase Detector with Square-Wave Inputs

**Given:** Two square waves of equal amplitude $V_m$ and frequency $f$, with phase difference $\Delta\phi$.

**Step 1 — Determine the average output voltage:**

For an XOR gate, output is HIGH only when inputs differ. The HIGH time $t_p$ depends on $\Delta\phi$:

$$t_p = \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi} \cdot T = \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi f}$$

The duty cycle of the XOR output is $D = t_p / T$, giving average voltage:

$$V_{avg} = V_{OH} \cdot \frac{t_p}{T/2} \quad \text{(averaged over half-period, since XOR has frequency } 2f\text{)}$$

Wait — more carefully: the XOR output of two square waves of frequency $f$ has frequency $2f$. The HIGH pulses of width $t_p$ occur once per input period $T$, so:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_{OH} \cdot t_p}{T} = V_{OH} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the lock point:**

Maximum average voltage occurs at $\Delta\phi = \pi$ (inputs always opposite): $V_{avg} = V_{OH}$. The PLL locks at the midpoint of the rising characteristic, i.e. at $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$, where the average output is $V_{OH}/2$. This is the **quadrature lock** condition.

**Step 3 — Compute $K_d$ for the XOR PD:**

$$K_d = \frac{d V_{avg}}{d(\Delta\phi)} = \frac{V_{OH}}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The XOR PD has a **triangular** transfer characteristic (not sinusoidal) and a maximum lock range of $\pm \pi$ rad. Its advantage is digital simplicity; its disadvantage is susceptibility to false lock at harmonics of the input frequency.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) Output

**Given:** A PFD with two D flip-flops, an AND gate for reset, and a charge pump supplying current $I_p$.

**Step 1 — Analyze operation:**

- When $f_{ref}$ leads $f_{VCO}$: the **UP** signal goes HIGH, sourcing current $I_p$ into the LPF.
- When $f_{VCO}$ leads $f_{ref}$: the **DOWN** signal goes HIGH, sinking current $I_p$.
- When in lock ($\Delta\phi = 0$): both UP and DOWN are LOW; output is zero.
- When $\Delta\phi$ is small but nonzero: a brief UP or DOWN pulse of width $\Delta t$ appears.

**Step 2 — Compute average charge-pump current:**

$$I_{CP} = I_p \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{T_{ref}} = I_p \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Compute PFD gain:**

$$K_{PFD} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2\pi} \quad \text{(for logic-level output)}$$

or in terms of current:
$$K_{PFD} = \frac{I_p}{2\pi} \quad \text{(for charge-pump output, A/rad)}$$

**Step 4 — Identify key advantage:**

The PFD has a **lock range of $2\pi$ rad (one full period)** and never false-locks — it correctly detects the sign of frequency error even when $\Delta\phi$ is outside $\pi$. This is why PFDs are used in **all modern frequency synthesizers**.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Analog Multiplier | XOR Gate | PFD + Charge Pump |
|-----------|:-----------------:|:--------:|:-----------------:|
| Input signals | Sine / sine | Square / square | Square / square |
| Transfer shape | Sinusoidal | Triangular | Triangular (±2π) |
| Lock range | $\pm \pi/2$ | $\pm \pi$ | $\pm 2\pi$ |
| False lock risk | Low | High (harmonics) | None |
| Linear $K_d$ | $K_m V_1 V_2/2$ | $V_{OH}/2\pi$ | $I_p/2\pi$ |
| DC output @ lock | 0 V | $V_{OH}/2$ | 0 V |
| Typical use | Coherent demod, instrumentation | Digital PLLs | Frequency synthesizers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multiplier-Type Phase Detector

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Input v1: V1 sin(wt + phi1)"] --> M["Four-Quadrant\nAnalog Multiplier\nGain: Km"]
    A2["Input v2: V2 cos(wt + phi2)"] --> M
    M --> LPF["Low-Pass Filter\nfc much less than 2f"]
    LPF --> OUT["DC Output: vd = Kd sin(dphi)"]
    style M fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style LPF fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) with Charge Pump

```mermaid
graph TD
    REF["Reference fref"] --> D1["D Flip-Flop 1\n(U output)"]
    VCO["VCO fvco"] --> D2["D Flip-Flop 2\n(D output)"]
    D1 --> AND["AND Gate\nReset Logic"]
    D2 --> AND
    AND --> D1
    AND --> D2
    D1 --> UP["UP Switch\nSource Ip"]
    D2 --> DN["DOWN Switch\nSink Ip"]
    UP --> CP["Charge Pump\nOutput Node"]
    DN --> CP
    CP --> LPF2["Loop Filter R and C"]
    LPF2 --> VCOCTRL["VCO Control Voltage"]
    VCOCTRL --> VCO
    style D1 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333
    style D2 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#333
    style AND fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#333
    style CP fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333
    style LPF2 fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Phase Detector inside a Complete PLL

```mermaid
graph LR
    FIN["Input Signal\nfin"] --> PD["Phase Detector\nError Generator"]
    FOUT["VCO Output\nfout"] --> PD
    PD --> LF["Loop Filter\nLPF Integrator"]
    LF --> VCO["Voltage Controlled\nOscillator"]
    VCO --> FOUT
    style PD fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style LF fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style VCO fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — XOR Phase Detector Waveforms (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUTS
    A["v1: square wave freq f phase 0"]
    B["v2: square wave freq f phase pi/2"]
    end
    A --> X["XOR Gate"]
    B --> X
    X --> C["XOR Output: freq 2f duty proportional to phase difference"]
    C --> D["LPF Averaging"]
    D --> E["DC Output: Vavg equals VOH divided by 2 at lock point"]
    style X fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Transfer Characteristic Plot (XOR vs. Multiplier vs. PFD)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph XAXIS["Phase difference dphi rad"]
    X0["minus pi"] --- X1["zero"] --- X2["plus pi"] --- X3["plus 2pi"]
    end
    X1 --> M1["Multiplier PD: sine curve, peak at plus or minus pi over 2, zero at 0 and pi"]
    X1 --> M2["XOR PD: triangular ramp from 0 to VOH over minus pi to plus pi"]
    X1 --> M3["PFD: triangular ramp from minus VDD to plus VDD over minus 2pi to plus 2pi, zero at 0"]
    style M1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333
    style M2 fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333
    style M3 fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a phase detector. What is its role in a Phase-Locked Loop?**

**Model Answer:**

A **phase detector (PD)** is a non-linear circuit that compares two input signals of the same frequency and produces an output voltage proportional to the **phase difference** between them. It functions as the **error-detection element** of a PLL: it generates a DC error voltage $v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$ that is filtered and fed to the VCO to correct any frequency/phase drift. The PD effectively converts phase information into an amplitude (voltage) signal that the rest of the loop can process.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the function of a four-quadrant multiplier in phase detection.**

**Model Answer:**

A **four-quadrant multiplier** is a circuit that produces an output proportional to the product of two input voltages, where each input can independently be of either polarity. In phase detection, the two sinusoidal signals under comparison are fed to the multiplier; the product term $\sin(\omega t + \phi_1)\cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$ contains a DC component $\sin(\phi_1 - \phi_2)$ that is extracted by a low-pass filter. A four-quadrant (not two-quadrant) multiplier is essential because both AC-coupled sinusoidal inputs swing through zero, occupying all four quadrants of the input-output plane.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) Derive the output voltage expression of an analog multiplier-type phase detector with two sinusoidal inputs $v_1 = V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1)$ and $v_2 = V_2 \cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$. Hence obtain the expression for the phase detector gain constant. (7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) with charge pump. What are its advantages over the XOR phase detector? (7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1:** The multiplier produces the instantaneous product scaled by $K_m$:

$$v_{PD}(t) = K_m \cdot V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1) \cdot V_2 \cos(\omega t + \phi_2)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the identity $\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$:

$$v_{PD}(t) = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}\big[\sin(2\omega t + \phi_1 + \phi_2) + \sin(\phi_1 - \phi_2)\big]$$

**Step 3:** The LPF removes the $2\omega$ term, leaving:

$$v_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}\sin(\Delta\phi) = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$$

**Step 4:** The phase detector gain constant is:

$$K_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} \quad \text{(units: V/rad)}$$

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

The PFD consists of two D flip-flops clocked by the reference ($f_{ref}$) and the VCO ($f_{VCO}$) signals respectively. The D inputs are tied HIGH, so whichever input arrives first sets its flip-flop output HIGH. An AND gate detects when both outputs are HIGH and resets both flip-flops simultaneously.

- If $f_{ref}$ leads: the **UP** output produces a pulse; the charge pump **sources** current $I_p$ into the loop filter, increasing VCO control voltage.
- If $f_{VCO}$ leads: the **DOWN** output produces a pulse; the charge pump **sinks** current, decreasing the VCO control voltage.
- At lock ($\Delta\phi = 0$): both outputs are LOW, no current flows, and the VCO control voltage is steady.

The average charge-pump current is $I_{CP} = \dfrac{I_p \,\Delta\phi}{2\pi}$, giving a linear PFD gain $K_{PFD} = \dfrac{I_p}{2\pi}$ A/rad.

**Advantages over XOR PD:**

1. **Lock range of $\pm 2\pi$** (one full period) vs. $\pm \pi$ for XOR — captures every possible phase offset.
2. **No false lock** — the PFD correctly identifies frequency error direction even at large phase differences, whereas the XOR PD can lock on harmonics.
3. **True zero output at lock** — ideal for DC-coupled loop filters and zero static phase offset.
4. **Wider acquisition range and faster lock-in time** in frequency synthesizers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) Explain the operation of an XOR gate as a phase detector with square-wave inputs. Draw its transfer characteristic and find the lock point. (7 marks)**

**(b) A phase detector has $K_m = 0.1\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $V_1 = 2\,\text{V}$, and $V_2 = 3\,\text{V}$. Find (i) the gain constant $K_d$, (ii) the output voltage when $\Delta\phi = 30°$, and (iii) the small-signal output for $\Delta\phi = 0.1\,\text{rad}$. (7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

The **XOR gate** phase detector takes two square-wave inputs of the same frequency $f$ and produces a square-wave output at frequency $2f$. The duty cycle (and hence the DC average after LPF) depends linearly on the phase difference.

For two square waves with phase difference $\Delta\phi$, the time during which the XOR output is HIGH is:

$$t_p = \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi f} = \frac{\Delta\phi}{\omega}$$

The average output voltage is:

$$V_{avg} = V_{OH} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{\pi}$$

**Transfer characteristic:** A triangular ramp rising from $0$ at $\Delta\phi = 0$ to $V_{OH}$ at $\Delta\phi = \pi$, then falling back to $0$ at $\Delta\phi = 2\pi$.

**Lock point:** The PLL locks at the **midpoint of the rising slope**, i.e., at $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ (quadrature), where $V_{avg} = V_{OH}/2$. At this point, the small-signal gain is maximum and symmetric.

$$K_{d,XOR} = \frac{dV_{avg}}{d(\Delta\phi)}\bigg|_{\pi/2} = \frac{V_{OH}}{\pi}$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $K_m = 0.1\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $V_1 = 2\,\text{V}$, $V_2 = 3\,\text{V}$.

**(i) Gain constant $K_d$:**

$$K_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} = \frac{0.1 \times 2 \times 3}{2} = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3\,\text{V/rad}$$

**(ii) Output for $\Delta\phi = 30°$:**

Convert $30°$ to radians: $30° = \pi/6 \approx 0.5236\,\text{rad}$.

$$v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi) = 0.3 \times \sin(30°) = 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Small-signal output for $\Delta\phi = 0.1\,\text{rad}$:**

For small $\Delta\phi$, use the linear approximation:

$$v_d \approx K_d \, \Delta\phi = 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.03\,\text{V}$$

Verification: $\sin(0.1) = 0.0998 \approx 0.1$, so the small-signal approximation is highly accurate here (error < 0.02%).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Phase-Detector Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** from the product-to-sum identity. Many students write $K_d = K_m V_1 V_2$ — this is worth **half credit only** in the derivation.
> 2. **Mixing sin·cos vs. sin·sin inputs.** If both inputs are sines, the DC term becomes $\cos(\phi_1 - \phi_2)$ instead of $\sin(\phi_1 - \phi_2)$ — read the question carefully!
> 3. **Stating units of $K_d$ as "volts"** instead of **"V/rad"** or "V per radian." KTU examiners specifically check this dimensional annotation.
> 4. **Conflating lock point of XOR ($\pi/2$) with lock point of multiplier ($0$ or $\pi$).** They are different by design — be explicit.
> 5. **Ignoring the LPF requirement.** A bare multiplier alone is not a phase detector — the low-pass filter is what extracts the DC error voltage. Always mention both blocks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phase Detector Definition:** A non-linear circuit that outputs a voltage proportional to the phase difference between two same-frequency inputs.
- **Core Equation:** $v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$ (analog multiplier type); $V_{avg} = V_{OH} \cdot \Delta\phi / \pi$ (XOR type).
- **Gain Constant:** $K_d = \dfrac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}$ for analog; $K_d = \dfrac{V_{OH}}{\pi}$ for XOR; $K_d = \dfrac{I_p}{2\pi}$ for PFD charge-pump.
- **Linearization:** For $\vert\Delta\phi\vert < 0.5$ rad, $\sin(\Delta\phi) \approx \Delta\phi$, enabling linear PLL analysis.
- **Three Main Types:** Analog Multiplier, XOR Switching, Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) with Charge Pump.
- **Lock Range Hierarchy:** Multiplier ($\pm\pi/2$) < XOR ($\pm\pi$) < PFD ($\pm 2\pi$).
- **Filter Necessity:** A multiplier-type PD *must* be followed by an LPF to reject the $2\omega$ sum-frequency term.
- **PFD Advantage:** No false lock; zero DC offset at lock — the modern standard.
- **Quadrature Lock:** XOR PDs lock at $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$, whereas multiplier and PFD lock at $\Delta\phi = 0$.
- **Units Discipline:** Always annotate $K_d$ in V/rad and frequencies in Hz/rad/s; KTU valuation scripts check dimensional correctness.
- **Applications to Remember:** PLLs, FM demodulators, motor speed controllers, frequency synthesizers, lock-in amplifiers, carrier recovery in PSK/QAM modems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Locked Loops, Digital Interfaces: Aliasing and the Sampling Theorem, Digital-to-Analog Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Phase Locked Loops, Digital Interfaces: Aliasing and the Sampling Theorem, Digital-to-Analog Converters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Phase Locked Loop (PLL) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A Phase Locked Loop is a closed-loop feedback control system that synchronizes the output phase (and frequency) of a voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) with the phase (and frequency) of an incoming reference signal. The PLL essentially acts as a frequency-tracking and phase-tracking circuit, locking the VCO to the input reference once their phase difference becomes constant (ideally zero). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Examiner Terminology:** A PLL is officially classified as a **negative feedback control system** whose error signal is the *phase difference* between the reference input and the VCO output — NOT the voltage or frequency directly. The output of the phase detector is a function of phase error $\theta_e$. **Intuitive Analogy — The "Kite and the Wind" Concept:** Imagine you are flying a kite. The wind (input reference signal) keeps changing direction, and you (the VCO) constantly tug on the string (control voltage) to keep the kite facing straight up. The string tension represents the *control voltage* applied to the VCO. Once the kite is stable pointing directly upward, the system is said to be "locked." If the wind shifts, you readjust the string tension — this is exactly how a PLL tracks a varying input frequency. The "locked" condition means the kite (VCO) is now following the wind (reference) without oscillation. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Operating Range:** A standard PLL is designed to track frequencies from **~1 Hz to several GHz**, depending on the technology (analog, digital, or all-digital). The classical analog 565 PLL IC typically operates in the range of **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz**. --- ### 1.2 Aliasing and the Sampling Theorem **Formal Definition — Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:** The sampling theorem states that a continuous-time, band-limited signal with maximum frequency component $f_m$ (Hz) can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples **without any loss of information**, provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition: $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$ The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2f_m$ is referred to as the **Nyquist Frequency**. **Formal Definition — Aliasing:** Aliasing is the phenomenon that occurs when a continuous-time signal is sampled at a rate *below* the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2f_m$). In this condition, the high-frequency components of the signal "fold back" into the lower frequency spectrum, creating false (spurious) lower-frequency components that did not exist in the original signal. These false frequencies are called **aliases**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aliasing — A Permanently Lost Distortion:** Once aliasing has occurred during sampling, the original information **cannot be recovered** by any downstream filter. This is why **anti-aliasing filters** (low-pass filters placed before the sampler) are mandatory in any well-designed data acquisition system. **Intuitive Analogy — The "Spinning Wheel" Optical Illusion:** Have you ever noticed in a movie (sampled at 24 frames per second) that a car's wheel appears to rotate *backward* even when the car is moving forward? This is aliasing. The wheel's rotational frequency exceeds half the camera's frame rate, so the high-frequency motion "aliases" as a lower, false frequency. The brain perceives the spurious lower frequency because the true high frequency can no longer be represented at that sampling rate. The same phenomenon corrupts digital audio when treble notes above ~22 kHz are misrepresented as lower tones at a 44.1 kHz (CD) sample rate. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Audio Reference Metrics:** > - Human audible range: **20 Hz to 20 kHz** > - CD audio sampling rate: **44.1 kHz** (= 2 × 22.05 kHz, with small overshoot margin) > - Professional studio audio: **48 kHz / 96 kHz / 192 kHz** --- ### 1.3 Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) is an electronic circuit that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital input code (typically an $n$-bit binary word) into a corresponding continuous-time analog output voltage or current that is proportional to the digital input value. **Intuitive Analogy — The "Post Office Weighing Scale":** Imagine a post office with a weighing scale that can only display whole-kilogram values (1 kg, 2 kg, 3 kg, …). A DAC is the *opposite* of this — it takes a digital "recipe" (e.g., 1.250 kg expressed in binary as `0001 0010 0101`) and produces the exact analog weight at the output. The "fineness" of the weight steps is determined by the number of bits the scale can interpret — the more bits, the finer the analog resolution. A 4-bit scale has only 16 steps, while a 12-bit scale has 4096 finely-grained steps. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Resolution and Step Size — Critical DAC Parameters:** > - **Resolution (in bits):** $n$ — the number of bits in the digital input word. > - **Step Size (LSB):** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale output voltage. > - **Percentage Resolution:** $\dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100\%$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Phase Locked Loop — Block-by-Block Operational Analysis A PLL is composed of **three fundamental sub-blocks** connected in a negative feedback loop. Let us dissect each. #### 2.1.1 Phase Detector (PD) - **Function:** Compares the phase $\theta_i(t)$ of the incoming reference signal with the phase $\theta_o(t)$ of the VCO output and produces an error voltage $v_e(t)$ that is proportional to the phase difference $\theta_e = \theta_i - \theta_o$. - **Mathematical Output:** $v_e(t) = K_d \cdot \theta_e$ (linear approximation, where $K_d$ is the phase detector gain in V/rad). - **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Sketch the phase detector output waveform when both inputs are square waves of the same frequency."* (Answer: A pulse-width modulated waveform whose average DC value is proportional to the phase shift.) #### 2.1.2 Low-Pass Filter (LPF) - **Function:** Removes high-frequency noise and the high-frequency components of the phase detector output, leaving a slowly-varying DC control voltage $v_c(t)$. - **Common Types:** Passive RC filter (first-order), active lead-lag filter (preferred for better loop dynamics). - **Transfer Function (Active Lead-Lag):** $F(s) = \dfrac{1 + s\tau_2}{1 + s(\tau_1 + \tau_2)}$ #### 2.1.3 Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) - **Function:** Generates an output signal whose instantaneous frequency is a linear function of the applied control voltage $v_c(t)$. - **Mathematical Output:** $\omega_o(t) = \omega_{free} + K_o \cdot v_c(t)$, where $\omega_{free}$ is the free-running (unlocked) frequency and $K_o$ is the VCO gain in rad/s/V. > [!NOTE] > **The "Lock" Condition — The Heart of a PLL:** > The PLL is said to be in **phase lock** when the phase error $\theta_e$ becomes a **constant** (often zero in steady state). When locked, $\omega_o = \omega_i$ exactly, meaning the VCO frequency has been pulled to equal the input reference frequency. #### 2.1.4 Key PLL Performance Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | Lock Range | $\Delta \omega_L$ | The range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *maintain* lock once it is acquired. | | Capture Range | $\Delta \omega_C$ | The range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *acquire* lock from an unlocked state. | | Free-Running Frequency | $\omega_{free}$ | The VCO output frequency when the control voltage $v_c = 0$. | | Pull-in Time | $T_p$ | Time required for the PLL to acquire lock after a sudden frequency step. | **Engineering Utility of PLL:** - **Frequency Modulation (FM) Demodulation:** Direct conversion of FM signals to baseband. - **Frequency Synthesis:** Generating stable, programmable clock signals in microcontrollers and digital systems. - **Motor Speed Control:** Phase-locking a motor's rotor position to a reference. - **Carrier Recovery in Communication Systems:** Coherent demodulation of PSK/QAM signals. - **Clock Recovery in Serial Data Links (USB, Ethernet, HDMI).** --- ### 2.2 Sampling Theorem — Mathematical Foundation **Why "≥ 2" and not "= 2" in Practice?** In theory, $f_s = 2f_m$ is sufficient. In practice, engineers always choose $f_s > 2f_m$ to provide a **transition band** for the anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters. A common rule of thumb is $f_s = 2.2 \cdot f_m$ to $f_s = 10 \cdot f_m$ depending on the application's filter design complexity. **Guard Band Concept:** The frequency interval between $f_m$ and $f_s/2$ is called the **guard band**. A wider guard band allows the use of a simpler, lower-order anti-aliasing filter. --- ### 2.3 DAC — Architectural Classifications and Step-Size Mathematics #### 2.3.1 Weighted-Resistor DAC - Uses binary-weighted resistors ($R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2^{n-1}R$) connected to an op-amp summing junction. - **Disadvantage:** The resistor values span a huge range for high $n$ (e.g., for $n = 12$, ratio is 2048:1), making it impractical in integrated circuits. #### 2.3.2 R-2R Ladder DAC (Most Common, KTU Favorite) - Uses only two resistor values: $R$ and $2R$. - **Advantage:** Excellent for monolithic IC fabrication; insensitive to switch resistance. #### 2.3.3 Specifications Table | Specification | Formula | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Step Size (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Smallest analog change representable | | Percentage Resolution | $\%R = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | Fineness of the output staircase | | Full-Scale Output | $V_{FS} = (2^n - 1) \cdot \Delta V$ | Output when all bits = 1 | | Settling Time | $t_s$ | Time for output to settle within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB of final value | | Monotonicity | — | Property: output never decreases for an increasing digital input | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master This Table — These Equations Appear in 90% of KTU Numerical Problems on This Module.** | # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz; $f_m$ = max signal frequency | | 2 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Hz; max reconstructable frequency | | 3 | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N f_s \vert$ | Hz; $N$ = nearest integer multiple | | 4 | DAC Step Size | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts/LSB | | 5 | DAC % Resolution | $\%R = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 6 | DAC Analog Output | $V_{out} = \Delta V \times D_{decimal}$ | Volts; $D$ is digital input | | 7 | PLL Free-Running Freq | $\omega_{free}$ | rad/s; VCO output when $v_c = 0$ | | 8 | VCO Output Frequency | $\omega_o = \omega_{free} + K_o v_c$ | rad/s | | 9 | Phase Detector Output | $v_e = K_d \cdot \theta_e$ | Volts | | 10 | Lock Range (first-order) | $\Delta \omega_L = K_d \cdot K_o \cdot F(0)$ | rad/s | > Note: To avoid markdown table corruption, the absolute value notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ has been used in place of the standard pipe symbol. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (Intuitive Frequency-Domain Proof) Let $x(t)$ be a band-limited continuous signal with no frequency components above $f_m$. We sample $x(t)$ at uniform intervals $T_s$ using an **ideal impulse train**: $$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$ The sampled signal is the multiplication of $x(t)$ and $p(t)$: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \delta(t - nT_s)$$ Taking the **Fourier Transform** of both sides (using the convolution property of multiplication in time domain): $$X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$ This equation states that the spectrum of the sampled signal $X_s(f)$ is an **infinite periodic replication** of the original spectrum $X(f)$, centered at integer multiples of $f_s$ and scaled by $\dfrac{1}{T_s}$. **Step 1 — The Critical Observation:** If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, then adjacent spectral replicas **do not overlap**. There exists a guard band between $f_m$ and $f_s - f_m$. **Step 2 — Reconstruction via Ideal Low-Pass Filter:** An ideal low-pass filter with cutoff at $f_c$ where $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$ can be used to extract exactly one replica of $X(f)$ from $X_s(f)$. Multiplying by $T_s$ restores the original amplitude. **Step 3 — The Nyquist Condition:** For the replicas not to overlap, we require: $$f_s - f_m \geq f_m \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_s \geq 2f_m \quad \blacksquare$$ This is the formal proof of the sampling theorem. Below is a worked example demonstrating alias frequency calculation. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Alias Frequency Calculation **Problem:** A pure sinusoid at **3.5 kHz** is sampled at **5 kHz**. Determine the alias frequency observed in the baseband (0 to $f_s/2$). **Solution (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1:** Identify the relevant values. $$f_{signal} = 3500 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_s = 5000 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the alias formula. $$f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N \cdot f_s \vert$$ **Step 3:** Find $N$ — the integer nearest to the ratio. $$N = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{f_{signal}}{f_s} \right) = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{3500}{5000} \right) = \text{round}(0.7) = 1$$ **Step 4:** Substitute and evaluate. $$ \begin{aligned} f_{alias} &= \vert 3500 - (1)(5000) \vert \\ &= \vert 3500 - 5000 \vert \\ &= \vert -1500 \vert \\ &= 1500 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The 3.5 kHz signal appears as a **1.5 kHz** tone after sampling at 5 kHz. This false 1.5 kHz signal is the **alias** — it was not present in the original signal. **Verification using the folding diagram:** - 3.5 kHz lies in the spectrum between $f_s/2 = 2.5$ kHz and $f_s = 5$ kHz. - The "fold" occurs at $f_s/2 = 2.5$ kHz. - Reflecting 3.5 kHz around 2.5 kHz: $2(2.5) - 3.5 = 1.5$ kHz. ✓ (Matches the alias formula.) --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: DAC Output Calculation **Problem:** An 8-bit DAC has a full-scale output voltage of **10 V**. Calculate: (a) the step size, (b) the percentage resolution, (c) the analog output when the digital input is `10011011` (binary). **Solution:** **Part (a) — Step Size:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} \\ &= \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{2^8 - 1} \\ &= \dfrac{10}{255} \\ &= 0.03922 \text{ V/LSB} \\ &\approx 39.22 \text{ mV/LSB} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Percentage Resolution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%R &= \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{1}{255} \times 100 \\ &= 0.3922\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — Analog Output for `10011011`:** First, convert the binary word to decimal: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{10} &= (1)(2^7) + (0)(2^6) + (0)(2^5) + (1)(2^4) + (1)(2^3) + (0)(2^2) + (1)(2^1) + (1)(2^0) \\ &= 128 + 0 + 0 + 16 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 \\ &= 155 \end{aligned} $$ Now compute the analog output: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= D_{10} \times \Delta V \\ &= 155 \times 0.03922 \\ &= 6.078 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Result Summary:** - Step size: **39.22 mV/LSB** - Percentage resolution: **0.3922%** - Output for `10011011`: **6.078 V** --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: Minimum Sampling Rate Determination **Problem:** An audio signal contains frequency components from **50 Hz to 18 kHz**. Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the maximum frequency. $$f_m = 18{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 18 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Nyquist criterion. $$ \begin{aligned} f_s &\geq 2 f_m \\ f_s &\geq 2 \times 18{,}000 \\ f_s &\geq 36{,}000 \text{ Hz} \\ f_s &\geq 36 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Recommend a practical rate (with margin). A practical sampling rate of **44.1 kHz** (CD standard) is commonly used, providing a guard band of $44.1 - 36 = 8.1$ kHz for the anti-aliasing filter transition. **Result:** Minimum sampling rate = **36 kHz**; practical choice = **44.1 kHz**. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DAC Architectures | Feature | Weighted-Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC | PWM-based DAC | |---|---|---|---| | Resistor Count | $n$ different values | Only 2 values ($R$, $2R$) | None (uses digital timer) | | Accuracy for $n > 8$ | Poor (resistor spread too large) | Excellent | Excellent | | Monotonicity | Hard to ensure | Inherently monotonic | Monotonic | | Speed | Medium | High | Medium | | IC Fabrication Suitability | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | | Cost | High (precision resistors) | Low | Very Low | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Phase Locked Loop (PLL) — Functional Block Diagram This is the most important diagram for this module. Memorize the signal flow direction and the role of each block. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeREF["Reference Input
sin wt with phase theta i"] --> nodePD["Phase Detector PD
Compares phase"] nodeVCOOUT["VCO Output
sin wo t with phase theta o"] --> nodePD nodePD --> nodeLPF["Low Pass Filter LPF
Removes high freq components"] nodeLPF --> nodeVC["Control Voltage vc t"] nodeVC --> nodeVCO["Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO
Frequency = w free + K o vc"] nodeVCO --> nodeVCOOUT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric prefixed with `node` to avoid keyword conflicts. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown bold or HTML tags inside double-quoted labels). --- ### 4.2 Sampling Process — Time Domain and Frequency Domain ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeSIG["Continuous Signal x of t
Band limited to f m"] nodeSAMP["Sampling Switch
Pulse train at f s"] nodeXS["Sampled Signal x s of t
Discrete amplitude and time"] nodeSPECT["Spectrum X s of f
Periodic replicas of X f at f s intervals"] nodeAAFILT["Anti Aliasing LPF
Cutoff f m"] nodeADC["Quantizer and Encoder
Produces N bit digital code"] nodeDACOUT["DAC and Reconstruction LPF
Recovers x of t"] nodeSIG --> nodeAAFILT nodeAAFILT --> nodeSAMP nodeSAMP --> nodeXS nodeXS --> nodeSPECT nodeXS --> nodeADC nodeADC --> nodeDACOUT ``` --- ### 4.3 R-2R Ladder DAC — Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeVREF["V REF
Reference Voltage"] nodeSW3["Switch b3
MSB"] nodeSW2["Switch b2"] nodeSW1["Switch b1"] nodeSW0["Switch b0
LSB"] nodeR2RNET["R 2R Ladder Network
Binary weighted current divider"] nodeOPAMP["Op Amp Summing Amplifier
I to V converter"] nodeVOUT["Analog Output V out"] nodeVREF --> nodeSW3 nodeVREF --> nodeSW2 nodeVREF --> nodeSW1 nodeVREF --> nodeSW0 nodeSW3 --> nodeR2RNET nodeSW2 --> nodeR2RNET nodeSW1 --> nodeR2RNET nodeSW0 --> nodeR2RNET nodeR2RNET --> nodeOPAMP nodeOPAMP --> nodeVOUT ``` **Working Principle (Step-by-Step):** - Each digital bit controls a switch that routes the reference voltage to either the summing junction (logic 1) or ground (logic 0). - The R-2R network produces binary-weighted currents at the op-amp's virtual ground. - The op-amp converts the summed current into an output voltage proportional to the digital input code. --- ### 4.4 Aliasing — Folding Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeORIG["Original Signal
3.5 kHz pure tone"] nodeFS["Sampling at 5 kHz
f s equals 5 kHz"] nodeFOLD["Spectrum Folding
Reflects around 2.5 kHz"] nodeALIAS["Observed Alias
1.5 kHz spurious tone"] nodeORIG --> nodeFS nodeFS --> nodeFOLD nodeFOLD --> nodeALIAS ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing, and how is it prevented?** **Model Answer:** > **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2f_m$ is the Nyquist rate. > > **Aliasing:** Aliasing is the phenomenon where high-frequency signal components (above $f_s/2$) get misrepresented as lower frequencies after sampling, producing spurious tones not present in the original signal. > > **Prevention:** Aliasing is prevented by placing a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** (with cutoff at $f_m$) *before* the sampler to remove all frequency components above $f_s/2$ before they can corrupt the sampled data. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List the basic building blocks of a PLL and state the function of each.** **Model Answer:** A PLL consists of three main blocks connected in a negative feedback loop: 1. **Phase Detector (PD):** Compares the phase of the input reference signal with the VCO output and produces an error voltage proportional to the phase difference. 2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Filters the PD output to remove high-frequency components, producing a smooth DC control voltage. 3. **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output frequency that varies linearly with the applied control voltage, thereby closing the feedback loop. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the Nyquist sampling theorem and explain how the choice of sampling frequency affects the prevention of aliasing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 12-bit R-2R ladder DAC has a reference voltage of 10 V. Calculate: (i) the step size, (ii) the percentage resolution, (iii) the analog output voltage for the digital input `1011 0010 1101`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** Let $x(t)$ be a band-limited signal with spectrum $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$. The signal is multiplied by an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_n \delta(t - nT_s)$ with $T_s = 1/f_s$. $$ \begin{aligned} x_s(t) &= x(t) \cdot p(t) \\ X_s(f) &= \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s) \end{aligned} $$ The spectrum of the sampled signal is a periodic replication of the original spectrum at intervals of $f_s$. To prevent overlap of the replicas, the condition $f_s \geq 2f_m$ must be satisfied. When satisfied, an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ (where $f_m < f_c < f_s - f_m$) can extract one clean replica, allowing perfect reconstruction. **Effect of Sampling Frequency on Aliasing Prevention:** - If $f_s \geq 2f_m$: replicas do not overlap → **no aliasing** → perfect reconstruction possible. - If $f_s < 2f_m$: replicas overlap → **aliasing occurs** → information is irreversibly lost. - Practical systems use $f_s = 2.2f_m$ to $f_s = 10f_m$ to provide a transition band for the anti-aliasing filter. **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $n = 12$ bits, $V_{REF} = 10$ V. **Part (i) — Step Size:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \\ &\approx 2.442 \text{ mV/LSB} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (ii) — Percentage Resolution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%R &= \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{4095} \times 100 \\ &\approx 0.02442\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (iii) — Analog Output for `101100101101`:** Convert binary to decimal: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{10} &= 1 \cdot 2^{11} + 0 \cdot 2^{10} + 1 \cdot 2^9 + 1 \cdot 2^8 + 0 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 \\ &= 2048 + 0 + 512 + 256 + 0 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 \\ &= 2861 \end{aligned} $$ Analog output: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= D_{10} \times \Delta V = 2861 \times 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 6.987 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Step size = **2.442 mV**, % Resolution = **0.0244%**, $V_{out}$ = **6.987 V**. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Phase Locked Loop. Define lock range and capture range. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the working of an R-2R ladder DAC with a circuit diagram. A 10-bit DAC has a full-scale output of 9.96 V. Determine: (i) the resolution in mV, (ii) the analog output for the input `1100101001`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The PLL consists of three blocks: Phase Detector (PD), Low-Pass Filter (LPF), and Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO), connected in a negative feedback loop. **Working Principle:** 1. The PD compares the phase $\theta_i$ of the input reference signal with the phase $\theta_o$ of the VCO output and produces an error voltage $v_e = K_d(\theta_i - \theta_o)$. 2. The LPF removes high-frequency components, producing a smooth DC control voltage $v_c$. 3. The VCO adjusts its output frequency: $\omega_o = \omega_{free} + K_o v_c$. 4. When $\omega_o = \omega_i$ and the phase error becomes constant, the loop is in **lock**. **Definitions:** - **Lock Range ($\Delta \omega_L$):** The range of input frequencies over which the PLL remains in lock once it has been acquired. It is wider than the capture range. - **Capture Range ($\Delta \omega_C$):** The range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *acquire* lock from an initially unlocked state. It is narrower than the lock range. **Model Solution for (b):** **Working of R-2R Ladder DAC:** The R-2R ladder uses only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$) to generate binary-weighted currents. Each digital bit controls a switch that routes the reference voltage to either the op-amp summing node (logic 1) or ground (logic 0). The op-amp converts the summed binary-weighted current into a proportional output voltage. **Numerical Computation:** Given: $n = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 9.96$ V. **Part (i) — Resolution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{9.96}{2^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{9.96}{1023} \\ &\approx 9.736 \text{ mV/LSB} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (ii) — Analog Output for `1100101001`:** Binary to decimal conversion: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{10} &= 1 \cdot 2^9 + 1 \cdot 2^8 + 0 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 \\ &= 512 + 256 + 0 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 \\ &= 809 \end{aligned} $$ Analog output: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{out} &= D_{10} \times \Delta V = 809 \times 9.736 \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 7.876 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Resolution = **9.736 mV**, $V_{out}$ for `1100101001` = **7.876 V**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > > 1. **Forgetting the "−1" in the denominator:** Students frequently write $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^n$ instead of $V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$. This single error costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. > > 2. **Binary-to-decimal conversion errors:** The MSB has weight $2^{n-1}$, NOT $2^n$. For a 10-bit number, MSB weight = $2^9 = 512$, not $2^{10} = 1024$. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU DAC problems. > > 3. **Sampling theorem confusion:** Students often confuse the *Nyquist rate* ($2f_m$) with the *Nyquist frequency* ($f_s/2$). Remember: **Nyquist rate = minimum sampling rate**, **Nyquist frequency = half the sampling rate**. > > 4. **Aliasing prevention misconception:** The anti-aliasing filter is placed **BEFORE the sampler**, not after. Placing it after is the most common conceptual error. > > 5. **PLL block diagram direction:** The error signal flow must be from PD → LPF → VCO, and the feedback path must go FROM VCO output TO the PD. Drawing arrows in the wrong direction loses **1–2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize These Bullets Before Walking Into the Exam Hall.** **Phase Locked Loop (PLL):** - PLL = Phase Detector + Low-Pass Filter + VCO in a negative feedback loop. - Lock = constant (ideally zero) phase error between input and VCO output. - **Lock Range > Capture Range** (lock range is wider). - Applications: FM demodulation, frequency synthesis, clock recovery, motor control. **Sampling Theorem & Aliasing:** - Nyquist condition: $f_s \geq 2f_m$. - Nyquist rate = $2f_m$ (minimum sampling rate). - Nyquist frequency = $f_s/2$ (maximum reconstructable frequency). - Aliasing occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$; prevent with an **anti-aliasing LPF before the sampler**. - Alias frequency formula: $f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - N f_s \vert$. **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC):** - **Step size:** $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$. - **% Resolution:** $1/(2^n - 1) \times 100$. - **Output voltage:** $V_{out} = D_{decimal} \times \Delta V$. - R-2R ladder DAC is preferred for IC fabrication (only two resistor values). - Weighted-resistor DAC is impractical for $n > 8$ due to large resistor spread. - MSB weight in an $n$-bit DAC = $2^{n-1}$ (NOT $2^n$). **Common Units to Remember:** - $f_s$ in Hz, kHz, or MHz. - $\Delta V$ in mV/LSB or $\mu$V/LSB. - DAC percentage resolution is dimensionless (a ratio expressed as %). **Critical Distinctions (Frequently Confused):** - Sampling **rate** vs. sampling **frequency** — they are the same quantity ($f_s$ in Hz). - **Aliasing** vs. **quantization error** — aliasing is a sampling-rate problem; quantization is an amplitude-resolution problem. - **Lock range** (maintain lock) vs. **capture range** (acquire lock) — lock range is always wider.

topics.title: DAC Designs - Weighted Resistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DAC Designs - Weighted Resistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Binary Weighted Resistor Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a parallel-type digital-to-analog conversion architecture in which each bit of the digital input word controls an electronic switch that connects a precision reference voltage ($V_{REF}$) to a precision resistor whose value is **inversely proportional to the binary weight** of that bit. The currents flowing through these weighted resistors are summed at the virtual-ground node of an operational amplifier, producing an analog output voltage $V_O$ that is a linear function of the digital input code.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, this topology is classified as a **current-summing, voltage-output DAC** and forms the pedagogical baseline for understanding all subsequent DAC architectures (R-2R ladder, inverted R-2R, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBMT403, Module 3):**
> The weighted-resistor DAC is the **first architecture** taught under DAC designs. It is the conceptual springboard from which the **R-2R Ladder Network** (the industry-standard IC implementation) is derived. Examiners frequently test the *transition* from weighted-resistor limitations to R-2R motivation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen weighing scale** that can measure total weight using only a fixed set of calibrated counter-weights. Suppose you have four weights: **1 kg, 2 kg, 4 kg, and 8 kg**. Any weight between 0 and 15 kg can be measured by *selecting* the appropriate combination of these weights and placing them on the pan.

The **binary weighted resistor DAC works on the same principle**:

- The **8 kg weight** (largest) corresponds to the **Most Significant Bit (MSB)** — it uses the **smallest resistor (R)** and therefore contributes the **largest current**.
- The **4 kg weight** uses a resistor of **2R**.
- The **2 kg weight** uses a resistor of **4R**.
- The **1 kg weight** (smallest) corresponds to the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** — it uses the **largest resistor ($2^{n-1} \cdot R$)** and contributes the **smallest current**.
