<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the advantages of an R-2R ladder DAC over a weighted resistor DAC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **weighted resistor DAC** uses resistors of values R, 2R, 4R, 8R, ..., 2<sup>n−1</sup>R. For an n-bit DAC, this requires **n different resistor values** with extreme ratios (e.g., for a 12-bit DAC, the ratio of MSB to LSB resistor is 2048:1).

**Disadvantages of weighted resistor DAC:**
1. **Wide range of resistor values** makes integrated circuit (IC) fabrication difficult.
2. **Manufacturing tolerance** is hard to maintain for high-value resistors.
3. **Temperature drift** differs between resistors, causing linearity errors.

**Advantages of R-2R ladder DAC (1.5 marks):**
1. **Only two resistor values** are needed: R and 2R — making IC fabrication simple and economical.
2. **Easy to manufacture** with high precision and matched tolerance.
3. **Better temperature tracking** since all resistors are of similar values.
4. **Easier to extend to higher bit counts** (e.g., 12-bit, 16-bit) without redesigning the entire resistor network.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is a Tracking ADC? Why is it unsuitable for high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Tracking ADC** (also called a **Counter-Type ADC** or **Servo ADC**) is a type of ADC that uses an **up/down counter** whose output drives a DAC. A comparator continuously compares the analog input with the DAC output and forces the counter to count up or down until V<sub>DAC</sub> matches V<sub>IN</sub> within ±1 LSB. (1.5 marks)

**Why unsuitable for high-frequency applications (1.5 marks):**
1. The counter must step through **every intermediate code** for a large change in input. For an n-bit ADC, a full-scale jump requires **2<sup>n</sup> − 1 clock cycles**.
2. For a 12-bit, 1 MHz Tracking ADC, the worst-case conversion time is **4095 μs ≈ 4 ms**, corresponding to a maximum sampling rate of only **~250 Hz** — far too slow for audio or RF signals.
3. Tracking ADCs are best suited for **slowly varying DC signals** (temperature, pressure, weight) where the input changes by only a few LSBs between samples.

---

## 📕 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4-bit R-2R DAC** has a reference voltage of **V<sub>REF</sub> = 10 V**. Calculate the analog output voltage for the digital inputs: **(i) 0101**, **(ii) 1100**, **(iii) 1111**. Also determine the **resolution** of the DAC. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working of 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**
The 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC consists of:
- 4 SPDT analog switches (S3, S2, S1, S0) corresponding to bits b3 (MSB), b2, b1, b0 (LSB).
- A resistor network with 4 series resistors of value R and 4 shunt resistors of value 2R arranged in a ladder.
- An op-amp in inverting configuration with feedback resistor R<sub>f</sub> = R acting as a current-to-voltage converter.

Each switch connects the bottom of its 2R resistor to either V<sub>REF</sub> (bit = 1) or GND (bit = 0). The top of the 2R resistors are tied to the ladder nodes.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
At any node, the current splits into two equal halves because the equivalent resistance looking right is always R (since 2R || 2R = R, and adding series R gives 2R at the next node). This produces **binary-weighted currents**:
- Switch S3 (MSB): $I_3 = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2R}$
- Switch S2: $I_2 = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{4R}$
- Switch S1: $I_1 = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{8R}$
- Switch S0 (LSB): $I_0 = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{16R}$

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$V_{OUT} = -R_f \cdot I_{total} = -R \cdot \left[ b_3 \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} + b_2 \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{4R} + b_1 \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{8R} + b_0 \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{16R} \right]$$

$$V_{OUT} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{16} \cdot \left[ 8b_3 + 4b_2 + 2b_1 + b_0 \right] = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^4} \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the decimal equivalent of the 4-bit input.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** V<sub>REF</sub> = 10 V, n = 4 bits.

**Formula:**

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D = \frac{10}{16} \cdot D = 0.625 \cdot D \text{ V}$$

**(i) Input = 0101** (D = 4 + 1 = 5):

$$V_{OUT} = 0.625 \times 5 = 3.125 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Input = 1100** (D = 8 + 4 = 12):

$$V_{OUT} = 0.625 \times 12 = 7.5 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Input = 1111** (D = 15):

$$V_{OUT} = 0.625 \times 15 = 9.375 \text{ V}$$

**Resolution:**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625 \text{ V}$$

This is the smallest change in output voltage per 1-LSB change in digital input.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a Tracking ADC** with the help of a block diagram. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **8-bit Tracking ADC** operates with a clock frequency of **500 kHz**. The analog input makes a step change from **0 V to 3.0 V**, where V<sub>REF</sub> = 5 V. Calculate: **(i)** the worst-case conversion time, **(ii)** the average conversion time, **(iii)** the time taken to first reach within 1 LSB of 3.0 V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Tracking ADC Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The Tracking ADC consists of:
- A **Comparator** with two inputs: V<sub>IN</sub> (analog input) and V<sub>DAC</sub> (DAC feedback).
- An **Up/Down Binary Counter** with clock input.
- A **DAC** that converts the counter output to an analog voltage.
- A **Clock generator**.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Initially, the counter is reset to 0, so V<sub>DAC</sub> = 0.
2. If V<sub>IN</sub> > V<sub>DAC</sub>, the comparator output is HIGH, and the counter **increments** by 1 with each clock pulse.
3. As the counter counts up, V<sub>DAC</sub> rises in a staircase pattern (in steps of 1 LSB).
4. The moment V<sub>DAC</sub> exceeds V<sub>IN</sub>, the comparator output goes LOW, and the counter starts **decrementing**.
5. The counter output then oscillates between two adjacent codes, with V<sub>DAC</sub> hovering within ±1 LSB of V<sub>IN</sub>. This is the **tracking band**.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Simple hardware; no complex successive approximation register.
- Good for **slowly varying signals** — no need to start a fresh conversion each time.
- Lower power consumption compared to flash ADCs.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Very **slow for large step changes** (worst-case = 2<sup>n</sup> − 1 clocks).
- Not suitable for high-speed or multiplexed applications.
- Output oscillates by ±1 LSB around V<sub>IN</sub>, introducing tracking noise.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** n = 8 bits, f<sub>clk</sub> = 500 kHz, V<sub>REF</sub> = 5 V, step change from 0 V to 3.0 V.

**Clock period:**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^3} = 2 \, \mu s$$

**(i) Worst-case conversion time:**

$$T_{conv(max)} = (2^8 - 1) \cdot T_{clk} = 255 \times 2 \, \mu s = 510 \, \mu s$$

**(ii) Average conversion time:**

$$T_{conv(avg)} = 2^{n-1} \cdot T_{clk} = 128 \times 2 \, \mu s = 256 \, \mu s$$

**(iii) Time to first reach within 1 LSB of 3.0 V:**

The LSB voltage is:

$$\text{1 LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V}$$

Target decimal code:

$$D_{target} = \left\lfloor \frac{3.0 \times 256}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 153.6 \right\rfloor = 153$$

Number of clock pulses needed from 0:

$$N = 153 \text{ pulses}$$

Time taken:

$$T = 153 \times 2 \, \mu s = 306 \, \mu s$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing R-2R with Weighted Resistor DAC:** Many students mistakenly write the formula for a weighted resistor DAC ($V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n - 1}$) when asked about R-2R. **Remember:** For R-2R, the formula uses $2^n$ in the denominator, **not $2^n - 1$**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Sign in Inverting Configuration:** R-2R DACs in standard I-to-V op-amp configuration give a **negative** output. Students who write $+V_{OUT}$ lose 1 mark unless specified otherwise.
>
> 3. **Tracking ADC: Confusing Worst-Case with Average:** A common pitfall is using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ for the worst-case counter cycles. The counter goes from **0 to (2<sup>n</sup> − 1)**, so the count is $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** KTU examiners **strictly deduct marks** for missing units (V, μs, kHz). Always include units in the final answer.
>
> 5. **Mixing Up R-2R with M-DAC or Inverted R-2R:** If the question specifies a different topology, the formula may differ. Always draw the circuit first before applying the formula.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing the Block Diagram:** For Tracking ADC questions, **always include the block diagram** — it carries 2-3 marks even if your explanation is incomplete.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **R-2R Ladder DAC** uses only **two resistor values: R and 2R**, making it ideal for IC fabrication.
- The output voltage of an n-bit R-2R DAC is: $V_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D$ (inverting) or with a positive sign (non-inverting).
- The **MSB contributes V<sub>REF</sub>/2**, and each subsequent bit contributes **half** of the previous bit's contribution.
- The **Tracking ADC** uses an **up/down counter** and continuously tracks the input, settling within ±1 LSB.
- **Worst-case conversion time** of Tracking ADC = $(2^n - 1) \times T_{clk}$.
- **Best-case conversion time** = $1 \times T_{clk}$ (only 1 LSB change).
- Tracking ADCs are **slow for large signal changes** but excellent for **slowly varying signals** (e.g., temperature, strain, pressure).
- The **equivalent resistance** at any R-2R node looking to the right is always **R** — this is the key property enabling binary weighting.
- R-2R DACs are preferred over weighted resistor DACs in modern ICs because of **ease of manufacturing** and **better linearity**.
- For KTU exams, always draw **circuit/block diagrams** and **state assumptions** clearly in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Successive Approximation ADC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Successive Approximation ADC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is a widely used type of analog-to-digital converter that employs a binary search algorithm to convert an analog input voltage into a corresponding digital code in **n clock cycles**, where *n* is the resolution (number of bits) of the converter. It is classified as a *medium-speed, medium-accuracy* ADC and is the workhorse of modern data acquisition systems, microcontrollers, and instrumentation front-ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Verbatim**
> *A Successive Approximation ADC is a closed-loop converter that compares the unknown analog input against the output of an internal Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC), whose digital input is progressively adjusted by a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) until the DAC output converges to the input within half an LSB. The final SAR contents are the digital equivalent of the analog input.*

The architecture is fundamentally a **feedback system** combining:
- A **Comparator** (analog decision element)
- A **DAC** (digital-to-analog reference generator)
- A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** (control logic & storage)
- **Clock and Control Logic** (sequencing)

## Conceptual Analogy — The Guessing Game

Imagine a student is asked to guess a number between **0 and 100** chosen by the teacher, with the only feedback being *"higher"* or *"lower"*. The smartest strategy is **not** linear guessing (1, 2, 3, ...). Instead, the student first guesses **50** (half). If told *higher*, the next guess is **75** (midway between 50 and 100). If told *lower*, the next guess is **62 or 63**. This **binary search** approach guarantees the answer in at most **7 guesses** (since 2⁷ = 128 ≥ 100).

The SAR ADC operates **exactly like this game**:
- The unknown is the **analog input voltage $V_{in}$**.
- The student is the **SAR + DAC** combination.
- The "higher/lower" feedback is the **comparator output**.
- The guesses are made with **bit weights of powers of two**, starting from the **MSB**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often frame SAR questions as "explain the conversion process for a given $V_{in}$." A clear binary-search mental model prevents the student from getting lost in the bit-by-bit toggling.

## Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution ($n$) | 8, 10, 12, 16, 18, 20 bits | Determines quantization step |
| Conversion Time | $n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Independent of input magnitude |
| Throughput | $f_{clk}/n$ | Determines sampling rate |
| Reference Voltage | **2.5 V, 5 V, 10 V** | Sets full-scale range |

> [!TIP]
> The fact that conversion time is **constant and equal to $n$ clock cycles** (independent of $V_{in}$) is the *single most important feature* that distinguishes SAR ADCs from integrating and flash ADCs in KTU answer keys.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The SAR ADC performs a **binary search** through all possible digital codes. The operational flow is:

1. **Start of Conversion (SOC)**: The SAR is cleared to all zeros. The MSB is set to '1', producing a trial code of $1000\ldots0$ (half-scale).
2. **DAC Conversion**: The trial code is fed to the internal DAC, producing an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
3. **Comparison**: The comparator evaluates $\Delta V = V_{in} - V_{DAC}$.
   - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: the trial bit is **kept** (1). The MSB decision is final.
   - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$: the trial bit is **reset** (0). The MSB is too large.
4. **Next Bit Trial**: The next lower bit is set to '1' (keeping all previously decided bits). Steps 2–3 repeat.
5. **Termination**: After the LSB is tested, the SAR contains the digital equivalent. The End-of-Conversion (EOC) signal goes high.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind binary search:** Each comparison *halves* the uncertainty range. After $k$ decisions, the remaining uncertainty is $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{k+1}}$. After $n$ decisions, it is at most **$\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} = 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB}$**, which is the inherent quantization error.

## Block Architecture

The SAR ADC is built as a **closed feedback loop**:

$$V_{in} \;\longrightarrow\; [\text{Comparator}] \;\longleftrightarrow\; [\text{SAR + Logic}] \;\longrightarrow\; [\text{DAC}] \;\longrightarrow\; V_{DAC} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Comparator}$$

The comparator does *not* need to be high-speed because only **one bit-decision per clock cycle** is made. This is why SAR ADCs achieve high resolution (16–20 bits) with moderate clock rates.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}}$ | Weight of least significant bit | Volts; defines resolution step |
| $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{2^{n} - 1}{2^{n}} \approx V_{REF}$ | Full-scale input range | For $n \geq 8$, $V_{FS} \approx V_{REF}$ |
| $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Number of discrete steps | Unitless; sometimes called $2^{n}$ levels |
| $T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Total conversion time | Seconds; **constant** for all inputs |
| $f_{sample,max} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{n}$ | Maximum sampling rate | Samples per second |
| $e_{q,max} = \pm 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB}$ | Maximum quantization error | Volts |
| $V_{DAC}(D) = V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_{i} \cdot 2^{i-n}$ | DAC output for digital code $D$ | Volts; $b_i \in \{0,1\}$ |
| $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76 \;\text{dB}$ | Theoretical signal-to-noise ratio | dB; for ideal N-bit ADC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students write $V_{FS} = V_{REF}$ and $\text{Resolution} = 2^{n}$. The technically correct expressions are $V_{FS} = V_{REF}\cdot(1 - 2^{-n})$ and $\text{Number of codes} = 2^{n}$. The error margin is **$\pm 1$ LSB**, which can cost 1 mark in Part A.

## Real-World Utility

SAR ADCs dominate wherever **moderate-to-high resolution (12–20 bits) and medium speed (100 kSPS – 10 MSPS)** are needed:
- **Microcontrollers**: Almost all ARM Cortex-M and AVR MCUs embed 12-bit SAR ADCs.
- **Digital Multimeters (DMMs)**: 16–24 bit SAR ADCs are standard.
- **Industrial Process Control**: Temperature, pressure, and strain-gauge signal chains.
- **Audio (mid-tier)**: 16-bit SAR ADCs at 44.1 kSPS for consumer audio.
- **Medical Instrumentation**: Patient monitors, ECG front-ends, infusion pumps.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU, if a question lists *pros and cons*, always mention: **"Speed is independent of input magnitude, fixed at $n$ clock cycles"** as the *primary* advantage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Example — Full 8-Bit SAR Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the highest-weightage KTU problem type.** Practice tracing each clock cycle.

**Given:**
- Resolution: $n = 8$ bits
- Reference voltage: $V_{REF} = 10\;\text{V}$
- Analog input: $V_{in} = 6.8\;\text{V}$

**Required:** Trace the SAR conversion and compute the final digital code and quantization error.

### Step 1 — Compute the LSB

The weight of the LSB is the smallest voltage step the ADC can resolve:

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10\;\text{V}}{2^{8}} = \frac{10}{256}\;\text{V}$$

$$\text{LSB} = 0.0390625\;\text{V} \approx 39.06\;\text{mV}$$

### Step 2 — Bit-by-Bit Decision Trace

The SAR starts with code $= 0000\,0000$ and tests bits from MSB ($b_7$) to LSB ($b_0$).

**Clock Cycle 1 — Test MSB ($b_7 = 1$):**

$$V_{DAC} = 1 \cdot 2^{7} \cdot \text{LSB} = 128 \cdot 0.0390625 = 5.0\;\text{V}$$

Since $V_{in} = 6.8\;\text{V} > V_{DAC} = 5.0\;\text{V}$, the comparator outputs HIGH → **bit 7 is KEPT = 1**.

**Clock Cycle 2 — Test $b_6$:**

$$V_{DAC} = (1\cdot 2^{7} + 1 \cdot 2^{6}) \cdot \text{LSB} = 192 \cdot 0.0390625 = 7.5\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} < 7.5\;\text{V}$, comparator LOW → **bit 6 is RESET = 0**.

**Clock Cycle 3 — Test $b_5$:**

$$V_{DAC} = (128 + 0 + 32) \cdot 0.0390625 = 160 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.25\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} > 6.25\;\text{V}$, comparator HIGH → **bit 5 is KEPT = 1**.

**Clock Cycle 4 — Test $b_4$:**

$$V_{DAC} = (128 + 0 + 32 + 16) \cdot 0.0390625 = 176 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.875\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} < 6.875\;\text{V}$, comparator LOW → **bit 4 is RESET = 0**.

**Clock Cycle 5 — Test $b_3$:**

$$V_{DAC} = (128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8) \cdot 0.0390625 = 168 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.5625\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} > 6.5625\;\text{V}$, comparator HIGH → **bit 3 is KEPT = 1**.

**Clock Cycle 6 — Test $b_2$:**

$$V_{DAC} = (128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4) \cdot 0.0390625 = 172 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.71875\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} > 6.71875\;\text{V}$, comparator HIGH → **bit 2 is KEPT = 1**.

**Clock Cycle 7 — Test $b_1$:**

$$V_{DAC} = (128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 2) \cdot 0.0390625 = 174 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.796875\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} > 6.796875\;\text{V}$, comparator HIGH → **bit 1 is KEPT = 1**.

**Clock Cycle 8 — Test LSB ($b_0$):**

$$V_{DAC} = (128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1) \cdot 0.0390625 = 175 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.8359375\;\text{V}$$

Since $6.8\;\text{V} < 6.8359375\;\text{V}$, comparator LOW → **bit 0 is RESET = 0**.

### Step 3 — Final Code and Error

The final SAR contents are:

$$D = 1010\,1110_{2}$$

Converting back to verify:

$$V_{out} = (1\cdot 128 + 0\cdot 64 + 1\cdot 32 + 0\cdot 16 + 1\cdot 8 + 1\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 2 + 0\cdot 1) \cdot 0.0390625$$

$$V_{out} = 174 \cdot 0.0390625 = 6.796875\;\text{V}$$

The **quantization error** is:

$$e_{q} = V_{in} - V_{out} = 6.8 - 6.796875 = 0.003125\;\text{V}$$

Since $\vert e_{q} \vert = 0.003125\;\text{V} < 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB} = 0.01953125\;\text{V}$, the result is within the theoretical limit. ✓

## Analytical Matrix — Full Conversion Trace Table

| Clock | Bit Tested | Trial Code | $V_{DAC}$ (V) | $V_{in}$ vs $V_{DAC}$ | Decision | Final Bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $b_7$ | $1000\,0000$ | 5.0000 | 6.8 > 5.0 | KEEP | 1 |
| 2 | $b_6$ | $1100\,0000$ | 7.5000 | 6.8 < 7.5 | RESET | 0 |
| 3 | $b_5$ | $1010\,0000$ | 6.2500 | 6.8 > 6.25 | KEEP | 1 |
| 4 | $b_4$ | $1011\,0000$ | 6.8750 | 6.8 < 6.875 | RESET | 0 |
| 5 | $b_3$ | $1010\,1000$ | 6.5625 | 6.8 > 6.5625 | KEEP | 1 |
| 6 | $b_2$ | $1010\,1100$ | 6.7188 | 6.8 > 6.7188 | KEEP | 1 |
| 7 | $b_1$ | $1010\,1110$ | 6.7969 | 6.8 > 6.7969 | KEEP | 1 |
| 8 | $b_0$ | $1010\,1111$ | 6.8359 | 6.8 < 6.8359 | RESET | 0 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice how the $V_{DAC}$ values **bracket** $V_{in} = 6.8$ V on both sides (6.7969 below, 6.8359 above). The final code 10101110 produces the closest DAC output **without exceeding** $V_{in}$. This is the hallmark of mid-tread quantization.

## Derivation of SNR (Bonus for 14-mark questions)

For an ideal $n$-bit ADC processing a full-scale sine wave, the **quantization noise** can be modeled as a uniform random variable in $[-0.5 \cdot \text{LSB}, +0.5 \cdot \text{LSB}]$. Its RMS value is:

$$V_{n,rms} = \frac{\text{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}\sqrt{12}}$$

The signal RMS for a full-scale sine is:

$$V_{s,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

The Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio is:

$$\text{SQNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{s,rms}}{V_{n,rms}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2^{n}\sqrt{12}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

$$\text{SQNR} = 20 \log_{10}(2^{n}) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{12}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right) = 6.02\,n + 1.76\;\text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this:** **"6 dB per bit, plus 1.76"** — examiners often award 2 marks just for stating this formula correctly in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 — Functional Block Diagram of SAR ADC

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **closed-loop architecture** of the SAR ADC and the direction of analog/digital signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Vin Analog Input"] --> nodeB["Comparator + or -"]
    nodeC["Vref Reference"] --> nodeD["DAC R-2R Ladder"]
    nodeD -- "Analog Feedback" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "Decision HIGH or LOW" --> nodeE["Successive Approx Register SAR"]
    nodeE -- "N-bit Digital Code" --> nodeD
    nodeE -- "Hold register" --> nodeF["Output Latch N bits"]
    nodeG["Clock and SOC"] --> nodeE
    nodeE -- "EOC Signal" --> nodeH["Host Processor"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH -- "Read N-bit code" --> nodeI["Digital Output"]
```

**Signal flow interpretation:**
- **$V_{in}$** enters the comparator's non-inverting input.
- The **SAR** drives the **DAC** with a trial code.
- The **DAC** produces $V_{DAC}$ which is fed back to the comparator's inverting input.
- The **clock** sequences the SAR one bit at a time.
- After **$n$ clock cycles**, the SAR contents are latched to the **output register**, and **EOC** flags completion.

## 4.2 — State-Machine Flow of One Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["IDLE State"] -->|"SOC Pulse"| nodeB["Clear SAR to 0000 0000"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Set MSB to 1 Trial"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["DAC generates VDAC"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Comparator V_in vs V_DAC"}
    nodeE -->|"V_in greater"| nodeF["Keep bit = 1"]
    nodeE -->|"V_in smaller"| nodeG["Reset bit = 0"]
    nodeF --> nodeH{"All N bits tested?"}
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|"No"| nodeI["Shift to next lower bit"]
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeH -->|"Yes"| nodeJ["Latch SAR to Output Register"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Assert EOC High"]
    nodeK --> nodeA
```

## 4.3 — Binary Search Visualization (Convergence Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["0 to 10 V Full Range"] --> nodeB["0 to 5 V Lower Half KEPT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["5 to 7.5 V Lower Quartile KEPT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["6.25 to 7.5 V Test at 6.875 V RESET"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["6.25 to 6.875 V Lower Octave KEPT"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Converges to 6.796 V at Clock 7"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Final Window 6.797 to 6.836 V Done"]
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, drawing **even a small binary search tree** showing how the input range halves each cycle earns full marks for "explain the working principle." It demonstrates conceptual clarity without verbose text.

## 4.4 — Timing Diagram Summary

| Signal | Cycle 1 | Cycle 2 | Cycle 3 | $\ldots$ | Cycle 8 | Post-Conv |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $b_7$ | **1** (test) | 1 (final) | 1 | $\ldots$ | 1 | 1 |
| $b_6$ | 0 | **0** (test) | 0 | $\ldots$ | 0 | 0 |
| $b_5$ | 0 | 0 | **1** (test) | $\ldots$ | 1 | 1 |
| $\vdots$ | | | | | | |
| $b_0$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | $\ldots$ | **0** (test) | 0 |
| EOC | 0 | 0 | 0 | $\ldots$ | 0 | **1** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded values** denote the *active* decision cycle for that bit. All other bits are *final* (latched) or *untouched*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List the main components of a Successive Approximation ADC and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

The SAR ADC consists of four main blocks:
1. **Comparator**: Compares $V_{in}$ with $V_{DAC}$ and outputs a logic HIGH or LOW indicating which is larger.
2. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**: Generates the analog trial voltage $V_{DAC}$ from the SAR's digital code. Usually an R-2R ladder or weighted-resistor network.
3. **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)**: The heart of the ADC. Holds the trial code, performs the binary search, and latches the final output.
4. **Clock and Control Logic**: Sequentially steps the SAR through the $n$ bit-tests, generates SOC/EOC handshake signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip (1 mark each):** Awarded for naming the block + stating its function. Vague statements like *"it does conversion"* get zero.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is the conversion time of a SAR ADC constant and independent of the input voltage?*

**Model Answer:**

In a SAR ADC, the conversion always proceeds through **all $n$ bits sequentially**, from MSB to LSB, with exactly **one clock cycle per bit**. Whether the comparator decides to keep or reset a bit, the next clock cycle *always* tests the next lower bit. There is no early-termination or variable-length logic.

Therefore, the total conversion time is:

$$T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}$$

This depends *only* on the resolution $n$ and clock period $T_{clk}$ — **never on the magnitude of $V_{in}$**. This property makes SAR ADCs deterministic and easy to interface with fixed-rate DSPs and microcontrollers.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | Internal Choice Pair]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a Successive Approximation ADC and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 10-bit SAR ADC has a reference voltage $V_{REF} = 5.12\;\text{V}$. An analog input $V_{in} = 3.00\;\text{V}$ is applied. Trace the conversion cycle-by-cycle, showing the trial code, DAC output, and final decision for each bit. Compute the final digital output and the quantization error. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

Refer to the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The diagram must show the closed loop: $V_{in}$ → Comparator ← $V_{DAC}$ from DAC ← SAR ← Control ← Clock.

**Block Functions (5 marks, 1 mark each):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Comparator** | Performs the bit-decision by comparing $V_{in}$ with $V_{DAC}$. Its 1-bit output drives the SAR. |
| **DAC (R-2R or Weighted)** | Converts the SAR's digital trial code into the analog feedback voltage $V_{DAC} = V_{REF} \cdot D / 2^{n}$. |
| **SAR + Control Logic** | Sequentially tests each bit from MSB to LSB, latches the comparator's decision, and holds the final code. |
| **Clock Generator** | Provides the timing reference; one bit-decision per clock edge. |
| **Output Latch & EOC** | Holds the final code for external readout and asserts End-of-Conversion when the conversion is complete. |

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 10-bit Conversion Trace (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute LSB and LSB-half (1 mark):**

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5.12}{1024} = 0.005\;\text{V} = 5\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Bit-by-bit trace (5 marks, deduct 1 per error):**

| Clock | Bit | Trial Code | $V_{DAC}$ (V) | $V_{in}$ vs $V_{DAC}$ | Decision | Final Bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $b_9$ | $10\,0000\,0000$ | 2.560 | 3.00 > 2.560 | KEEP | 1 |
| 2 | $b_8$ | $11\,0000\,0000$ | 3.840 | 3.00 < 3.840 | RESET | 0 |
| 3 | $b_7$ | $10\,1000\,0000$ | 3.200 | 3.00 < 3.200 | RESET | 0 |
| 4 | $b_6$ | $10\,0100\,0000$ | 2.880 | 3.00 > 2.880 | KEEP | 1 |
| 5 | $b_5$ | $10\,0110\,0000$ | 3.040 | 3.00 < 3.040 | RESET | 0 |
| 6 | $b_4$ | $10\,0101\,0000$ | 2.960 | 3.00 > 2.960 | KEEP | 1 |
| 7 | $b_3$ | $10\,0101\,1000$ | 3.000 | 3.00 = 3.000 | KEEP | 1 |
| 8 | $b_2$ | $10\,0101\,1100$ | 3.020 | 3.00 < 3.020 | RESET | 0 |
| 9 | $b_1$ | $10\,0101\,1010$ | 3.010 | 3.00 < 3.010 | RESET | 0 |
| 10 | $b_0$ | $10\,0101\,1001$ | 3.005 | 3.00 < 3.005 | RESET | 0 |

**Step 3 — Final code and error (1 mark):**

$$D = 10\,0101\,1000_{2} = 600_{10}$$

$$V_{out} = 600 \cdot 0.005 = 3.000\;\text{V}$$

$$e_{q} = V_{in} - V_{out} = 3.000 - 3.000 = 0\;\text{V}$$

**Verification (in 0.5 LSB bound):**

$$0.5 \cdot \text{LSB} = 0.0025\;\text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert e_{q} \vert = 0 \leq 0.0025\;\text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** For Cycle 7, both "keep" and "reset" are *technically* acceptable (tie case). The standard convention is "**keep**" the bit if $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$ — but students should mention this convention explicitly to avoid losing 0.5 marks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 3 | Internal Choice Pair]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a 4-bit Successive Approximation ADC. Show the conversion process for an analog input of $V_{in} = 9.2\;\text{V}$ with $V_{REF} = 10\;\text{V}$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the performance of SAR ADC with Flash ADC and Dual-Slope ADC in terms of speed, resolution, cost, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 4-bit SAR Working (7 marks)

**Sketch (2 marks):** Refer to the block diagram from Section 4.1.

**LSB computation (1 mark):**

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625\;\text{V}$$

**Conversion trace (3 marks):**

| Clock | Bit | Trial Code | $V_{DAC}$ (V) | Compare | Decision | Final Bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $b_3$ | $1000$ | 5.000 | 9.2 > 5.0 | KEEP | 1 |
| 2 | $b_2$ | $1100$ | 7.500 | 9.2 > 7.5 | KEEP | 1 |
| 3 | $b_1$ | $1110$ | 8.750 | 9.2 > 8.75 | KEEP | 1 |
| 4 | $b_0$ | $1111$ | 9.375 | 9.2 < 9.375 | RESET | 0 |

**Final output (1 mark):**

$$D = 1110_{2} = 14_{10}$$

$$V_{out} = 14 \cdot 0.625 = 8.75\;\text{V}$$

$$e_{q} = 9.2 - 8.75 = 0.45\;\text{V} \quad (\text{slightly less than } 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB} = 0.3125\;\text{V — within tolerance ✓})$$

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | **SAR ADC** | **Flash ADC** | **Dual-Slope ADC** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Medium ($n$ clocks) | Very fast (1 clock) | Slow ($2^{n+1}$ clocks) |
| **Resolution** | Medium-High (8–20 bit) | Low (4–8 bit) | Very High (16–24 bit) |
| **Conversion Time** | Fixed, $n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Constant, 1 $T_{clk}$ | Variable, depends on $V_{in}$ |
| **Hardware Cost** | Moderate (DAC + comparator) | Very high ($2^{n}-1$ comparators) | Low (op-amp + integrator) |
| **Noise Rejection** | Poor (no integration) | Poor | Excellent (integrates over time) |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | Very high (many comparators) | Low |
| **Typical Use** | MCUs, DMMs, data loggers | Oscilloscopes, video, radar | DMMs, precision weighing, industrial sensors |
| **Architecture Type** | Feedback (closed-loop) | Direct (parallel) | Integrating (indirect) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The **dual-slope ADC's conversion time is *variable* and proportional to $V_{in}$**, whereas SAR's is *constant*. This is the most-tested contrast question in KTU Module 3.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear the SAR at SOC:** If a student starts conversion with a stale code from the previous cycle, the result is wrong by $\pm 1$ LSB. Deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{FS}$ with $V_{REF}$:** Use $V_{REF}$ in your calculations; $V_{FS}$ is the *maximum code's* output. Mixing them costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the comparator direction statement:** Always write *"if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, keep; else reset"* at the top of your trace. Without it, examiners may deduct 0.5 marks for "incomplete methodology."
> 4. **Mis-stating conversion time:** Many students write "$n + 1$ clocks" or "$n - 1$ clocks." The exact answer is $n$ clock cycles (one per bit) — no offset, no scaling.
> 5. **Forgetting the quantization error verification:** Always end with $\vert e_{q} \vert \leq 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB}$ verification. A correct conversion *without* the verification gets 6.5/7 instead of 7/7.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The SAR ADC uses a **binary search** algorithm through all $2^{n}$ possible digital codes.
- ✅ It is a **closed-loop / feedback** architecture: Comparator ← DAC ← SAR.
- ✅ The **internal DAC** is essential; it can be **R-2R ladder** or **weighted-resistor** type.
- ✅ **Conversion time is constant** at $T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}$, independent of $V_{in}$.
- ✅ **Quantization step (LSB)** is $\text{LSB} = V_{REF} / 2^{n}$.
- ✅ Maximum **quantization error** is $\pm 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB}$.
- ✅ Bit decisions go strictly from **MSB → LSB** (never reverse).
- ✅ Decision rule: **KEEP the trial bit if $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$, RESET otherwise.**
- ✅ Ideal **SQNR** is $6.02\,n + 1.76\;\text{dB}$ — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ SAR ADCs dominate **medium-resolution, medium-speed** applications (12–20 bit, 100 kSPS – 10 MSPS).
- ✅ Major **advantages**: fixed conversion time, high resolution, low power, single comparator.
- ✅ Major **disadvantages**: requires a precision DAC, susceptible to noise (no integration), and has a sample-and-hold on the input.
- ✅ **Versus Flash ADC**: SAR is slower but far more area- and power-efficient at $n > 6$.
- ✅ **Versus Dual-Slope**: SAR is faster but Dual-Slope has superior noise rejection and linearity.
- ✅ Trace tables in KTU answers **must show all 4 columns**: trial code → $V_{DAC}$ → comparison → decision. Missing any column loses 0.5 marks.
- ✅ After every conversion, **verify** the result by converting the digital code back to analog and checking the quantization error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrating Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrating Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Integrating Converter** is an electronic signal conditioning circuit that produces an output signal whose instantaneous amplitude is proportional to the **time integral (or continuous summation)** of the input signal over a defined time window. In the context of instrumentation, integrating converters are fundamental building blocks used to:
- Convert a voltage into a time-averaged digital value (e.g., dual-slope ADC).
- Convert a voltage into a proportional frequency (Voltage-to-Frequency Converter, VFC).
- Convert frequency into an analog voltage (Frequency-to-Voltage Converter, FVC).
- Amplify the charge output from piezoelectric sensors (Charge Amplifier).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Integrating converters fall under the family of **Analog Signal Conditioning Circuits**. They are preferred in noisy industrial environments because integration inherently averages out random noise, yielding superior Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) compared to instantaneous (instantaneous) converters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of an integrating converter like a **rain gauge**:
- Rain (input signal, e.g., voltage) falls continuously into a cylindrical bucket.
- The water level (output) is not just the *current* rainfall rate — it is the **cumulative total** of everything that has fallen over time.
- If the bucket is emptied at regular intervals, the water level measured just before emptying represents the **average rainfall rate** during that interval.

In the electrical domain, the "bucket" is a **capacitor** that accumulates electric charge, and the "emptying action" is the controlled discharge cycle. The mathematical equivalent is the integral operation:

$$V_{out}(t) \propto \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

### 1.3 Key Sub-Topics Covered

1. **Op-Amp Based Active Integrator**
2. **Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (VFC)** — using 555 timer or op-amp
3. **Frequency-to-Voltage Converter (FVC)**
4. **Dual-Slope Integrating ADC**
5. **Charge Amplifier (Piezoelectric Signal Conditioning)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - Capacitance unit: **Farad (F)**
> - Frequency unit: **Hertz (Hz)**
> - Time constant: **τ = RC (seconds)**
> - Typical integration capacitor range: **1 nF to 10 µF**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Op-Amp Based Active Integrator (Miller Integrator)

The active integrator uses an op-amp with a capacitor in the feedback path and a resistor at the input. It is the most fundamental integrating converter.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
- The input resistor $R$ converts input voltage $V_{in}$ into a current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R$.
- The op-amp virtual ground at the inverting input forces this entire current to flow into the feedback capacitor $C$.
- The capacitor integrates this current: $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$.
- The non-inverting input is grounded, establishing the virtual ground at the inverting terminal.

**Practical Modification — Lossy Integrator:**
- A high-value resistor $R_f$ is placed in parallel with the capacitor to prevent **DC saturation** due to op-amp input offset voltage and bias currents.
- This converts the ideal (pure) integrator into a **first-order low-pass filter** with a lower cutoff frequency $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Integration Matters in Instrumentation:**
> Integration is the dual operation of differentiation. Many physical sensors (e.g., accelerometers) output a signal proportional to **acceleration**, but we need **velocity** or **displacement**. Since velocity is the integral of acceleration, an integrator directly converts the sensor output into a physically meaningful variable.

### 2.2 Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (VFC)

A VFC produces an output **pulse train** whose frequency is directly proportional to the input DC voltage. The dual-slope ADC, charge balancing ADC, and many digital voltmeters rely on VFCs.

**Operating Principle (using op-amp + 555 timer hybrid):**
- The input voltage $V_{in}$ charges a capacitor through a resistor.
- When the capacitor voltage reaches a precise threshold (set by a comparator referencing a stable voltage), the comparator triggers a **monostable multivibrator** (often built around a 555 timer).
- The monostable discharges the capacitor with a **fixed-width, fixed-amplitude current pulse** — independent of $V_{in}$.
- The repetition rate (frequency $f_{out}$) of these discharge pulses is therefore **linearly proportional to $V_{in}$**.

**Why VFCs are excellent for noisy environments:**
The output is a **frequency**, which is a digital quantity immune to amplitude noise, line voltage drops, and ground-loop interference. The pulses can be transmitted over long cables (4–20 mA loops in industry) without degradation.

### 2.3 Frequency-to-Voltage Converter (FVC)

FVC is the inverse of VFC. A common implementation uses a **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** or a **pulse-averaging low-pass filter**:
- A train of fixed-amplitude, fixed-width pulses arrives at frequency $f_{in}$.
- Each pulse delivers a fixed charge $Q = C \cdot V_{ref}$ to a capacitor via a transistor switch.
- The capacitor's average voltage is $V_{out} = Q \cdot f_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot C \cdot R \cdot f_{in}$.

### 2.4 Dual-Slope Integrating ADC

This is the **gold standard** for high-resolution digital voltmeters (DVMs) in laboratory and industrial settings.

**Two-Phase Operation:**

**Phase 1 — RUN-UP (Signal Integration):**
- The unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ is connected to the integrator for a **fixed time $T_1$**.
- The integrator output ramps up (or down) to: $V_{A} = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$.

**Phase 2 — RUN-DOWN (Reference Integration):**
- A **reference voltage of opposite polarity $V_{ref}$** is switched to the integrator input.
- The integrator output ramps back toward zero.
- The time $T_2$ required to return to zero is measured by a digital counter.
- The relationship: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$.

**Key Advantages:**
- **Noise Rejection:** The integration period $T_1$ is set to an integer multiple of the power-line period (e.g., **100 ms for 50 Hz** or **83.33 ms for 60 Hz**), completely rejecting mains-frequency interference.
- **Accuracy independent of $R$, $C$:** Both $R$ and $C$ cancel out in the final ratio — long-term drift is eliminated.
- The final count $N$ represents $V_{in}$ directly.

### 2.5 Charge Amplifier

Used for **piezoelectric sensors** which produce a high-impedance charge output (not a voltage). The charge amplifier is essentially an integrator that converts charge $Q$ into an output voltage.

**Configuration:** A charge-to-voltage conversion capacitor $C_f$ is placed in the feedback path of an op-amp. A high-value feedback resistor $R_f$ provides a DC path to prevent saturation.

**Output:** $V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{\int i \, dt}{C_f}$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Charge amplifiers are indispensable in vibration analysis, force measurement, and acoustic emission testing where piezoelectric transducers (PZT, PVDF films) are used. Without integration, the signal from these sensors cannot be processed by standard voltage-input data acquisition systems.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Converter Type** | **Input Quantity** | **Output Quantity** | **Governing Equation** | **Key Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Op-Amp Integrator | $V_{in}$ (V) | $V_{out}$ (V) | $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$ | DC saturation if no $R_f$ |
| Lossy Integrator | $V_{in}$ (V) | $V_{out}$ (V) | $\tau = (R \parallel R_f) \cdot C$ | Lower cutoff $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ |
| Voltage-to-Frequency (VFC) | $V_{in}$ (V) | $f_{out}$ (Hz) | $f_{out} = k \cdot V_{in}$ | Linearity error typically $\lt 0.01\%$ |
| Frequency-to-Voltage (FVC) | $f_{in}$ (Hz) | $V_{out}$ (V) | $V_{out} = k' \cdot f_{in}$ | Pulse width must be $<< \frac{1}{f_{in}}$ |
| Dual-Slope ADC | $V_{in}$ (V) | Digital count $N$ | $N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot N_{full}$ | $T_1 = N_{full} \cdot T_{clock}$ |
| Charge Amplifier | Charge $Q$ (C) | $V_{out}$ (V) | $V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$ | Open-loop gain $A \to \infty$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial Process Control:** 4–20 mA current loops use VFCs to transmit sensor data over long distances in chemical plants.
- **Medical Electronics:** ECG / EEG signal conditioning uses integrators for baseline drift removal and noise rejection.
- **Digital Weighing Scales:** Dual-slope ADCs in load cell readouts provide high accuracy with excellent 50 Hz / 60 Hz mains noise rejection.
- **Vibration Monitoring:** Charge amplifiers interface with piezoelectric accelerometers in structural health monitoring of bridges, turbines, and aircraft engines.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Op-Amp Integrator Output

**Given Circuit:** Inverting op-amp with input resistor $R$ and feedback capacitor $C$. Non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step 1: Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground, $V^- = 0$ V).**

The current through the input resistor is:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 2: Since the op-amp input draws no current, all of $I_{in}$ flows through the capacitor.**

The current into the capacitor (toward the output side) is $I_C$. The capacitor voltage relation is:

$$I_C = C \cdot \frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V^- = 0$ and rearrange.**

$$I_C = C \cdot \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Equate $I_{in}$ to $I_C$ (current continuity at the inverting node).**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to isolate $V_{out}$.**

$$dV_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \cdot V_{in} \cdot dt$$

**Step 6: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$\int_{0}^{t} dV_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 7: Apply the limits (assuming initial output $V_{out}(0) = 0$).**

$$V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 8: Final Result.**

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates **inversion** of the integrated input. This is a direct consequence of the inverting topology.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lossy Integrator Transfer Function

**Circuit Modification:** Add a feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with the feedback capacitor $C$.

**Step 1: The feedback impedance is the parallel combination.**

$$Z_f = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{sC}}{R_f + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C}$$

**Step 2: Apply the inverting amplifier gain formula in the s-domain.**

$$\frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{Z_f}{R} = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sR_f C}$$

**Step 3: Identify the form as a first-order low-pass filter.**

The pole frequency is at:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$$

**Step 4: Identify the DC gain (low-frequency limit, $s \to 0$).**

$$A_{DC} = -\frac{R_f}{R}$$

**Step 5: Identify the high-frequency behavior ($s \gg \frac{1}{R_f C}$).**

At high frequencies, $Z_f \approx \frac{1}{sC}$, so the gain becomes:

$$\frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} \approx -\frac{1}{sRC}$$

This is the **ideal integrator** behavior restored at high frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The lossy integrator behaves as a **pure integrator for $f \gg f_p$** and as a **proportional amplifier for $f \ll f_p$**. This prevents DC saturation while preserving integration in the signal band of interest.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Dual-Slope ADC Conversion Equation

**Step 1: Run-Up Phase.**

Input voltage $V_{in}$ applied for fixed time $T_1$. The integrator output at the end of run-up is:

$$V_{A} = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{T_1} V_{in} \, dt = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

(Assuming $V_{in}$ is approximately constant over $T_1$, which is the standard case in DVM design.)

**Step 2: Run-Down Phase.**

Reference voltage $-V_{ref}$ is applied. The output returns to zero. Let $T_2$ be the run-down time:

$$0 = V_{A} - \frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{T_2} (-V_{ref}) \, dt$$

**Step 3: Simplify the run-down equation.**

$$0 = V_{A} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $V_A$ from Step 1.**

$$0 = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 5: Cancel the common factor $\frac{1}{RC}$.**

$$0 = -V_{in} \cdot T_1 + V_{ref} \cdot T_2$$

**Step 6: Solve for $V_{in}$.**

$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 7: Express in terms of digital counts.**

A clock of period $T_{clk}$ counts during $T_2$. Let $N$ be the count. Then $T_2 = N \cdot T_{clk}$. Also $T_1 = N_{full} \cdot T_{clk}$ where $N_{full}$ is the count corresponding to full-scale:

$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{N}{N_{full}}$$

**Step 8: The digital output is directly proportional to $V_{in}$.**

$$\boxed{N = N_{full} \cdot \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical observation:** The clock period $T_{clk}$, the resistor $R$, and the capacitor $C$ **all cancel out**. This is why dual-slope ADCs achieve excellent long-term accuracy and are immune to component drift.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Voltage-to-Frequency Converter Output

**Step 1: Charge accumulation during ON-time.**

Consider a constant input voltage $V_{in}$ driving a current $I_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$ into integrating capacitor $C$. The voltage across the capacitor after time $t$ is:

$$V_C(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} \frac{V_{in}}{R} \, d\tau = \frac{V_{in} \cdot t}{RC}$$

**Step 2: Comparator threshold condition.**

A comparator triggers the monostable when $V_C$ reaches the threshold voltage $V_{th}$:

$$V_{th} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot T_{charge}}{RC}$$

**Step 3: Monostable discharges the capacitor in a fixed time $T_{dis}$.**

This discharge time is **independent of $V_{in}$** (set by an internal RC of the 555 timer):

$$T_{dis} = k_1 \cdot R_{int} C_{int}$$

**Step 4: The period of one cycle is the sum.**

$$T = T_{charge} + T_{dis} = \frac{V_{th} \cdot RC}{V_{in}} + T_{dis}$$

**Step 5: The output frequency is $f_{out} = 1/T$.**

For $T_{dis} \ll T_{charge}$ (the design goal for linear VFCs):

$$f_{out} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{V_{th} \cdot RC}$$

**Step 6: Final linear relationship.**

$$\boxed{f_{out} \approx k \cdot V_{in}, \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{1}{V_{th} \cdot RC}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration:** In practice, the constant $k$ is determined by trimming the threshold $V_{th}$ using precision voltage references (e.g., bandgap references like LM4040) and low-drift resistors.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Charge Amplifier Output

**Step 1: Piezoelectric sensor model.**

The piezoelectric transducer is modeled as a **charge source $Q$** in parallel with the sensor's own capacitance $C_p$ and leakage resistance $R_p$.

**Step 2: Apply KCL at the inverting input of the op-amp (virtual ground).**

The charge source delivers $Q$ coulombs. This charge flows into the feedback capacitor $C_f$:

$$Q = C_f \cdot (V^- - V_{out})$$

**Step 3: With virtual ground $V^- = 0$:**

$$Q = C_f \cdot (0 - V_{out}) = -C_f \cdot V_{out}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$$

**Step 5: If the input is a time-varying current $i(t)$ from a current-output sensor:**

$$Q(t) = \int i(t) \, dt$$

**Step 6: Therefore:**

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{C_f} \int i(t) \, dt$$

This is **explicit integration** of the input current.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** The output voltage is independent of the cable capacitance between the sensor and the amplifier. This is a major advantage over voltage-mode amplifiers, which suffer from cable motion artifacts in industrial installations.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Dual-Slope ADC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input V_in] --> B[Analog Switch S1]
    Vref[Precision Reference V_ref] --> C[Analog Switch S2]
    B --> D[Integrator Op-amp with RC]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Zero Crossing Comparator]
    E --> F[AND Gate]
    CLK[Clock Oscillator] --> F
    F --> G[Binary Counter N]
    G --> H[Output Latch and Display]
    CTRL[Control Logic FSM] --> B
    CTRL --> C
    CTRL --> F
    CTRL --> G
```

**Description of Signal Flow:**

1. The **Control Logic FSM** (Finite State Machine) orchestrates the two phases.
2. **Phase 1 (Run-Up):** Switch S1 closes, S2 opens. The integrator ramps for a fixed time $T_1$ set by the counter reaching $N_{full}$.
3. **Phase 2 (Run-Down):** S1 opens, S2 closes. The reference voltage of opposite polarity is applied. The comparator output enables the AND gate, allowing clock pulses into the counter.
4. When the integrator output returns to zero, the comparator disables the AND gate. The final count $N$ is proportional to $V_{in}$.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Voltage-to-Frequency Converter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Voltage V_in] --> B[Input Resistor R]
    B --> C[Integrating Capacitor C]
    C --> D[Comparator with Reference V_th]
    D --> E[Monostable Multivibrator 555]
    E --> F[Discharge Transistor Switch]
    F --> C
    E --> G[Output Frequency f_out as Pulse Train]
    style A fill:#ffe6e6
    style G fill:#e6ffe6
    style D fill:#fff2cc
    style E fill:#cce6ff
```

**Description of Signal Flow:**

1. The input voltage $V_{in}$ charges capacitor $C$ through resistor $R$.
2. When $V_C$ crosses the comparator threshold $V_{th}$, the monostable fires.
3. The monostable generates a fixed-width pulse that turns ON the discharge transistor.
4. The capacitor is rapidly discharged, the monostable resets, and the cycle repeats.
5. The output pulse train frequency $f_{out}$ is linearly proportional to $V_{in}$.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Charge Amplifier Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Piezoelectric Sensor Charge Source Q] --> B[Sensor Cable]
    B --> C[Inverting Input of Op-amp]
    G[Ground] --> D[Non-inverting Input of Op-amp]
    C --> E[Op-amp High Gain Amplifier A_OL]
    E --> F[Feedback Capacitor C_f and Resistor R_f]
    F --> C
    E --> G2[Output Voltage V_out proportional to Q]
    style A fill:#fff2cc
    style E fill:#cce6ff
    style G2 fill:#e6ffe6
```

**Description of Signal Flow:**

1. The piezoelectric sensor generates a charge $Q$ proportional to applied force or acceleration.
2. The charge is conveyed through the cable to the inverting input of the op-amp.
3. The op-amp's virtual ground absorbs the charge, converting it to a voltage across the feedback capacitor $C_f$.
4. The feedback resistor $R_f$ provides a DC path to prevent op-amp saturation due to input bias currents.
5. The output is a low-impedance voltage signal $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$ suitable for data acquisition.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Frequency-to-Voltage Converter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Frequency f_in as Pulse Train] --> B[Differentiator or One-shot Pulse Shaper]
    B --> C[Transistor Switch Driven by Fixed-width Pulses]
    C --> D[Integrating Capacitor C]
    C --> E[Reference Voltage V_ref]
    D --> F[Low-pass Filter Op-amp Buffer]
    F --> G[Output DC Voltage V_out]
    style A fill:#e6f2ff
    style G fill:#e6ffe6
    style C fill:#fff2cc
```

**Description of Signal Flow:**

1. The input frequency $f_{in}$ is shaped into uniform-amplitude, uniform-width pulses.
2. Each pulse delivers a precisely defined charge $Q = V_{ref} \cdot C$ to the integrating capacitor.
3. The low-pass filter smooths the resulting staircase voltage into a clean DC level.
4. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is directly proportional to $f_{in}$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define an integrating converter. List any two advantages of a dual-slope ADC over a flash-type ADC.*

**Model Answer:**

An **integrating converter** is an electronic circuit that produces an output signal proportional to the **time integral of the input signal** over a defined window. Examples include op-amp integrators, voltage-to-frequency converters, and dual-slope ADCs.

**Two advantages of dual-slope ADC over flash-type ADC:**

1. **Superior noise rejection:** The fixed integration time $T_1$ can be chosen to be an integer multiple of the power-line period (e.g., 100 ms for 50 Hz), providing nearly 100% rejection of mains-frequency interference. Flash ADCs have no such property and are highly susceptible to noise.
2. **Component-insensitive accuracy:** The final digital output depends only on the ratio $T_2/T_1$ and the reference voltage $V_{ref}$. The integrating resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ cancel out of the equation, eliminating long-term drift errors. Flash ADCs require precisely matched resistor ladders, which are expensive and drift over time.

Additionally, dual-slope ADCs are far simpler and cheaper to implement for high resolution (16+ bits), whereas flash ADCs require $2^N - 1$ comparators for $N$-bit resolution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the function of a charge amplifier. Why is it preferred for piezoelectric sensors?*

**Model Answer:**

A **charge amplifier** is an op-amp based integrator that converts the **charge output** of a piezoelectric sensor into a proportional low-impedance voltage signal. The output voltage is given by:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$$

where $Q$ is the input charge and $C_f$ is the feedback capacitor.

**Why it is preferred for piezoelectric sensors:**

1. **Independence from cable capacitance:** The op-amp's virtual ground holds the input cable at 0 V, so the cable capacitance does not appear in the transfer function. This eliminates motion artifacts and allows long cables to be used between sensor and amplifier.
2. **High input impedance and low output impedance:** The amplifier accepts the high-impedance charge signal from the sensor and provides a low-impedance voltage output suitable for data acquisition systems.
3. **Direct charge-to-voltage conversion:** The output is directly proportional to the applied force or acceleration, making signal interpretation straightforward.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the output voltage expression for an **op-amp based active integrator**. Discuss the role of the feedback resistor in practical integrators. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of operation of a **dual-slope integrating ADC** with a block diagram. Derive the expression relating the input voltage to the digital output count, and highlight its key advantage in noisy industrial environments. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** An inverting op-amp with input resistor $R$ connected to $V_{in}$, feedback capacitor $C$ between the output and the inverting input, and the non-inverting input grounded.

**Derivation:**

Applying **KCL at the inverting node** $V^-$ which is at virtual ground ($V^- = 0$):

The current through $R$ is:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

This current flows entirely into the capacitor (op-amp input draws no current):

$$I_C = C \cdot \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

Equating $I_{in} = I_C$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

$$dV_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC} V_{in} \, dt$$

Integrating from 0 to $t$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

**Role of Feedback Resistor $R_f$:**

In a practical integrator, a high-value resistor $R_f$ is placed in parallel with the capacitor $C$. This converts the **ideal integrator** into a **lossy integrator** with the following effects:

1. **Prevents DC saturation:** Op-amp input offset voltage and bias currents, when integrated indefinitely, would drive the output to the supply rail. The resistor $R_f$ provides a discharge path that limits the DC gain to $-R_f/R$.
2. **Establishes a low-frequency cutoff:** The transfer function becomes a first-order low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$.
3. **Trade-off:** $R_f$ must be large enough (typically $R_f \geq 10 R$) to ensure the integration behavior is preserved over the signal bandwidth.

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the dual-slope ADC architecture.

**Principle of Operation:**

The dual-slope ADC operates in two distinct phases controlled by a finite state machine:

**Phase 1 — Run-Up (Signal Integration):**

The unknown input voltage $V_{in}$ is connected to the integrator input for a **fixed time period $T_1$**, typically determined by counting a predetermined number $N_{full}$ of clock pulses. During this phase, the integrator output ramps linearly:

$$V_{A} = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

**Phase 2 — Run-Down (Reference Integration):**

A precision reference voltage $V_{ref}$ of **opposite polarity** is switched to the integrator. The integrator output ramps back toward zero. A digital counter measures the time $T_2$ required to reach zero by counting clock pulses:

$$T_2 = N \cdot T_{clk}$$

**Conversion Equation:**

From charge conservation, setting the total integrator output change to zero:

$$V_{in} \cdot T_1 = V_{ref} \cdot T_2$$

$$N = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot N_{full}$$

**Key Advantage in Noisy Industrial Environments:**

The integration time $T_1$ is deliberately chosen to be an **integer multiple of the power-line period**:
- For 50 Hz systems: $T_1 = 100$ ms (or multiples thereof).
- For 60 Hz systems: $T_1 = 83.33$ ms (or multiples thereof).

A sinusoidal interference at the line frequency averages to **exactly zero** over a complete number of cycles during $T_1$. This provides approximately **40 dB of normal-mode noise rejection** at 50 Hz, making dual-slope ADCs the standard for industrial digital voltmeters, weighing scales, and process instrumentation where high noise immunity is essential.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the working principle of a **Voltage-to-Frequency Converter (VFC)**. Derive the relationship between input voltage and output frequency. List two industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **charge amplifier** for piezoelectric transducers. Derive the output expression and explain why it is insensitive to cable capacitance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Working Principle:**

A VFC converts a DC or slowly varying input voltage $V_{in}$ into a pulse train whose **frequency $f_{out}$** is directly proportional to $V_{in}$. The typical implementation uses an op-amp integrator followed by a comparator and a monostable multivibrator (often built around a 555 timer).

**Operating Cycle:**

1. The integrator capacitor $C$ charges through resistor $R$ with current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R$.
2. When the capacitor voltage reaches a **threshold $V_{th}$** set by a precision reference, the comparator triggers the monostable.
3. The monostable generates a **fixed-width, fixed-amplitude discharge pulse** that rapidly resets the integrator output.
4. The cycle repeats continuously. The repetition rate is the output frequency $f_{out}$.

**Derivation of the Frequency Equation:**

Charge time $T_{charge}$ until the capacitor reaches $V_{th}$:

$$V_{th} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot T_{charge}}{RC} \implies T_{charge} = \frac{V_{th} \cdot RC}{V_{in}}$$

Total period $T = T_{charge} + T_{dis}$, where $T_{dis}$ is the fixed discharge time:

$$T = \frac{V_{th} \cdot RC}{V_{in}} + T_{dis}$$

For linear VFC design with $T_{dis} \ll T_{charge}$:

$$f_{out} = \frac{1}{T} \approx \frac{V_{in}}{V_{th} \cdot RC} = k \cdot V_{in}$$

**Two Industrial Applications:**

1. **4–20 mA Current Loop Transmission:** VFCs convert sensor signals to frequency-modulated pulses for noise-immune transmission over long cables in chemical plants and oil refineries.
2. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion:** VFCs are the front end of integrating ADCs that require high noise rejection in industrial environments.
3. **(Bonus) Motor Speed Control:** Frequency output can directly drive frequency-to-voltage converters for closed-loop control.

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Circuit Diagram:** An op-amp with the piezoelectric sensor connected to the inverting input (with sensor's intrinsic capacitance $C_p$ and leakage $R_p$ shown as parallel elements). A feedback capacitor $C_f$ (with parallel high-value resistor $R_f$) connects the output to the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Derivation of Output Expression:**

The piezoelectric sensor delivers charge $Q$. The inverting input is at virtual ground ($V^- = 0$ V) because the op-amp has high open-loop gain. The charge on the feedback capacitor is:

$$Q = C_f \cdot (V^- - V_{out}) = C_f \cdot (0 - V_{out}) = -C_f \cdot V_{out}$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$$

For a time-varying input current $i(t)$ from the sensor:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{C_f} \int i(t) \, dt$$

**Why It is Insensitive to Cable Capacitance:**

The op-amp's virtual ground at the inverting input forces the cable shield and inner conductor to remain at **0 V at all times**, regardless of cable length. Since the voltage across the cable capacitance is held at zero, no charge is stored on the cable capacitance, and therefore **no charge is lost** to the cable.

Mathematically, the charge delivered by the sensor is independent of the cable capacitance $C_{cable}$ because:

- Charge on the cable: $Q_{cable} = C_{cable} \cdot V_{cable} = C_{cable} \cdot 0 = 0$
- All sensor charge $Q_{sensor}$ flows into $C_f$.

This is in stark contrast to a **voltage-mode amplifier**, where the cable capacitance appears in parallel with the sensor capacitance and forms a capacitive voltage divider, making the output signal dependent on cable length and motion. The charge amplifier's cable-insensitive property makes it the **only practical choice** for industrial installations with long or moving cables.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in Integrator Output:** Forgetting the **negative sign** in $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in} \, dt$. KTU examiners specifically test this. Always state the sign explicitly and link it to the inverting topology.
> 
> 2. **Omitting Initial Condition $V_{out}(0)$:** When deriving the integrator, do not assume the output starts at zero unless the problem states so. Show the general form: $V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in} \, dt + V_{out}(0)$.
> 
> 3. **Confusing VFC and FVC Transfer Directions:** A common trap: "VFC output is voltage." Wrong. **VFC** output is **frequency**. **FVC** output is **voltage**. Always state which quantity is the input and which is the output.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the Role of $R_f$ in Practical Integrators:** KTU 2024 scheme questions explicitly ask about the difference between ideal and practical integrators. Always discuss the **saturation problem** and how $R_f$ resolves it via the lossy integrator transfer function.
> 
> 5. **Not Explaining Noise Rejection in Dual-Slope ADC:** Stating the formula alone is insufficient. Always explicitly mention that $T_1$ is set to a multiple of the **power-line period** and explain the averaging principle that gives noise rejection.
> 
> 6. **Mixing Up Charge Amplifier Transfer Function:** Some students write $V_{out} = -Q \cdot C_f$ instead of $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$. The units alone should flag this: charge is in coulombs, capacitance in farads, and dividing gives volts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Active Integrator Core Equation:** $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in} \, dt$. Negative sign comes from the inverting op-amp topology.
- **Virtual Ground Principle:** The inverting input is held at 0 V by negative feedback, which is what makes the integrator linear and well-defined.
- **DC Saturation Problem:** Ideal integrators drift to saturation due to op-amp offsets. Solution: add a high-value feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$.
- **Lossy Integrator Cutoff:** $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$. Design so that $f_L$ is well below the lowest signal frequency of interest.
- **VFC Output Quantity:** Frequency (Hz). Formula: $f_{out} = k \cdot V_{in}$.
- **FVC Output Quantity:** DC Voltage (V). Formula: $V_{out} = k' \cdot f_{in}$.
- **Dual-Slope ADC Final Equation:** $N = N_{full} \cdot \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$. Notice $R$, $C$, and $T_{clk}$ all cancel.
- **Dual-Slope Noise Rejection:** Set $T_1$ to an integer multiple of the mains period (100 ms for 50 Hz, 83.33 ms for 60 Hz) to achieve near-perfect rejection of power-line interference.
- **Charge Amplifier Output:** $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$. Independent of cable capacitance due to virtual ground at the input.
- **Piezoelectric Sensor:** Always modeled as a **charge source** (not a voltage source). The op-amp must have very high input impedance and very low input bias current (JFET or MOSFET input op-amps like TL071, OPA129 are preferred).
- **Industrial Application Hierarchy:** VFCs are used for transmission, dual-slope ADCs for high-resolution DC measurement, and charge amplifiers for vibration / force measurement with piezoelectric sensors.
- **Units Discipline:** Integration of voltage (V) over time (s), divided by $RC$ (s), yields volts — dimensional check confirms correctness.
- **Practical Component Values:** $R$ in the range of 10 kΩ to 1 MΩ; $C$ in the range of 1 nF to 10 µF; $R_f$ typically 10× to 100× the value of $R$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flash Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flash Converter** (also termed a **Parallel Analog-to-Digital Converter** or **Direct Conversion ADC**) is the highest-speed architecture for analog-to-digital conversion. It performs quantization in a **single comparator decision step** by employing a parallel array of $2^N - 1$ voltage comparators that simultaneously compare the unknown input analog signal against $2^N - 1$ equally spaced reference voltages generated by a precision resistor ladder network. The comparator outputs form a **thermometer code** which is then converted to a standard binary representation by a priority encoder circuit.

For an **N-bit Flash ADC**:
- Number of comparators required = $2^N - 1$
- Number of precision resistors required = $2^N$
- Conversion time = 1 clock cycle (effectively, the propagation delay of the comparators + encoder)
- The Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage = $\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Flash converters are categorized under **high-speed ADC architectures** in Module 3 (Data Converters). They are fundamentally different from successive approximation and dual-slope converters because they trade **circuit complexity and power consumption for raw conversion speed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Terminology Note:** The KTU 2024 PCBMT403 syllabus officially uses the term **"Flash Type ADC"** in Module 3. The terms *Flash*, *Parallel*, and *Direct* ADC are interchangeable in literature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **height measurement scenario** where 7 students are standing in a line, and we want to determine the exact height of an unknown person as quickly as possible.

- **Slow Method (Successive Approximation):** We would ask "Are you taller than 4 feet?" then "Are you taller than 5 feet?" then refine. This is slow because it takes multiple sequential questions.
- **Fast Method (Flash Conversion):** We pre-arrange **7 horizontal bars** at heights of 1 ft, 2 ft, 3 ft, 4 ft, 5 ft, 6 ft, and 7 ft. We push the unknown person against all 7 bars **simultaneously**. Every bar shorter than the person gets pushed up (giving a "1"), and every bar taller stays down (giving a "0"). The pattern of "pushed up" bars (e.g., 1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0) is the **thermometer code**, which is then translated to a binary number (4 feet).

**Key Insight:** Flash conversion is **massively parallel** — every comparison happens at the exact same instant, just like every bar in our analogy is checked simultaneously. This is why flash ADCs are the **fastest** ADCs in existence, at the cost of needing an exponentially growing number of comparators.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world connection:** Flash ADCs are the heart of **digital storage oscilloscopes (DSOs)**, **radar signal processing**, **high-speed data acquisition in particle physics experiments**, and **satellite communication receivers** — anywhere the analog signal changes too fast for slower ADCs to keep up.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Flash Converter Design

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Voltage | $V_{REF}$ | **2.5 V to 5.0 V** (typical IC supply) |
| Full-Scale Input Range | $V_{FS}$ | **0 V to $V_{REF}$** |
| Resolution (N) | bits | **6 to 8 bits** (practical IC limit) |
| Number of Resistors | $2^N$ | **64 to 256** for 6 to 8-bit |
| Number of Comparators | $2^N - 1$ | **63 to 255** for 6 to 8-bit |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s$ | **100 MSPS to 10 GSPS** |
| Comparator Propagation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | **10 ps to 1 ns** |
| Power Dissipation | $P$ | **100 mW to 500 mW** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard ICs:** Examples of commercial flash ADCs include the **Analog Devices AD9215** (10-bit, 105 MSPS), **Texas Instruments ADC08B2000** (8-bit, 2 GSPS), and the historic **CA3308** (8-bit, 15 MSPS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

A Flash ADC contains **three fundamental sub-systems** working in perfect synchronization during one clock cycle:

**Stage 1 — The Precision Resistor Ladder (Voltage Divider Network)**
- A series chain of **$2^N$ identical, high-precision resistors** connected between the reference voltage $V_{REF}$ (top) and ground (bottom).
- The current flowing through the ladder is $I = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N \cdot R}$ (Ohm's Law).
- Each tap point produces a unique reference voltage, separated from the next by exactly one **LSB voltage** = $\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$.

**Stage 2 — The Comparator Bank (Parallel Decision Array)**
- $2^N - 1$ high-speed voltage comparators.
- The **non-inverting input (+)** of every comparator is tied to the **analog input voltage $V_{in}$**.
- The **inverting input (−)** of the k-th comparator (counted from the bottom, $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^N - 1$) is tied to the k-th tap on the resistor ladder.
- The reference voltage for comparator $k$ is: $V_{ref,k} = k \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$
- Decision rule: Comparator output $C_k = \mathbf{1}$ (HIGH) if $V_{in} > V_{ref,k}$, else $C_k = \mathbf{0}$ (LOW).

**Stage 3 — The Priority Encoder (Thermometer-to-Binary Translator)**
- Accepts the $2^N - 1$ comparator outputs as a "thermometer code" (a contiguous run of 1s at the bottom transitioning to 0s at the top).
- Generates an N-bit binary word representing the position of the transition.
- Critical requirement: must be **fast** to not bottleneck the comparator stage.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

**Why so many comparators?**
The fundamental tradeoff in ADC design is **speed vs. hardware**. By brute-forcing all possible voltage decisions in parallel, we eliminate the iterative comparison loop that slows down SAR ADCs. We pay with **$2^N$ silicon area and power** for an N-bit converter.

**Why a resistor ladder?**
- A string of equal resistors is the **simplest, most linear, and most monotonic** way to generate $2^N$ equally-spaced reference voltages.
- The ladder is a **passive component** — it does not need a clock, settling time, or active drive, ensuring the reference voltages are rock-stable.

**Why a priority encoder?**
The thermometer code is **not a standard binary format** that downstream digital systems understand. The priority encoder is the digital translator that converts the spatial "height" information into a numerical "code".

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Comparators | $N_c = 2^N - 1$ | Hardware cost for N-bit resolution |
| Number of Resistors | $N_R = 2^N$ | In the precision reference ladder |
| LSB Voltage (Resolution) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$ | Smallest distinguishable voltage step |
| Reference Tap Voltage (k-th) | $V_{ref,k} = k \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$ | Voltage at comparator k's inverting input |
| Ladder Current | $I_{ladder} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N \cdot R}$ | DC current through resistor string |
| Full-Scale Range | $V_{FS} = V_{REF}$ | Maximum input span (0 to $V_{REF}$) |
| Quantization Error (max) | $E_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N+1}}$ | Inherent conversion inaccuracy |
| Input Capacitance | $C_{in} = (2^N - 1) \cdot C_{comp}$ | Limits analog bandwidth |
| Conversion Time | $T_c = t_{pd,comp} + t_{pd,enc}$ | Sum of comparator + encoder delays |
| SNR (theoretical) | $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio for ideal N-bit ADC |
| Effective Number of Bits | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Real-world resolution |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flash ADCs are **indispensable** in any application where the analog signal has frequency components exceeding the capability of slower ADCs:

1. **Digital Oscilloscopes (DSOs):** Modern high-end oscilloscopes use banks of interleaved flash or SAR ADCs at multi-GSPS rates to capture GHz-bandwidth signals.
2. **Radar and LIDAR Systems:** Time-of-flight measurements demand sub-nanosecond timing resolution, requiring flash or equivalent-speed conversion.
3. **High-Speed Communication:** 100 Gbps+ optical communication systems use flash ADCs at the receiver front-end for signal equalization.
4. **Medical Imaging (PET/SPECT):** Particle detectors produce nanosecond-scale pulses that need ultra-fast digitization.
5. **Digital Receiver Front-Ends:** Software-defined radio (SDR) systems for electronic warfare and 5G base stations.
6. **Particle Physics Experiments:** Large Hadron Collider experiments digitize detector signals at 40 MSPS to 5 GSPS rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In the KTU 2024 PCBMT403 syllabus, the **comparator count formula $2^N - 1$** and the **LSB formula $V_{REF}/2^N$** are the **two most frequently tested equations** for the Flash ADC topic. Memorize these without fail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Comparators Required for N-bit Flash ADC

**Problem Statement:** Derive the relationship between the number of comparators and the bit-resolution of a Flash ADC.

**Step 1 — Establish the resolution requirement:**
An N-bit binary code has $2^N$ distinct values, ranging from $0$ to $2^N - 1$.

$$
\text{Total discrete output codes} = 2^N
$$

**Step 2 — Map output codes to input voltage levels:**
