For correct quantization, the ADC must distinguish between $2^N$ different input voltage intervals. Each interval corresponds to one output code. The boundaries of these intervals occur at the $2^N - 1$ internal tap voltages on the resistor ladder (excluding 0 V and $V_{REF}$).

$$
\text{Number of decision thresholds required} = 2^N - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the parallel architecture rule:**
Since the Flash ADC makes every decision simultaneously, it requires **one dedicated comparator per decision threshold**.

$$
\boxed{N_c = 2^N - 1}
$$

**Numerical Verification:** For a 3-bit Flash ADC: $N_c = 2^3 - 1 = 7$ comparators. For an 8-bit Flash ADC: $N_c = 2^8 - 1 = 255$ comparators. This **exponential growth** is the fundamental hardware bottleneck that limits practical Flash ADCs to 6–8 bits.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — LSB (Step Size) Voltage and Tap Voltages of the Resistor Ladder

**Problem Statement:** A 3-bit Flash ADC uses a resistor ladder with $V_{REF} = 8\text{ V}$ and $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. Determine the LSB voltage, all tap voltages, and the ladder current.

**Step 1 — Compute LSB voltage (resolution):**
The full-scale range $V_{FS} = V_{REF} = 8\text{ V}$ is divided into $2^N = 2^3 = 8$ equal intervals.

$$
\Delta V = \text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N} = \dfrac{8}{2^3} = \dfrac{8}{8} = 1 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ladder current:**
The ladder contains $2^N = 8$ resistors in series, total resistance $8R = 8 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$$
I_{ladder} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N \cdot R} = \dfrac{8}{8 \times 1000} = 1 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each tap voltage (counting from ground, $k = 1$ to $2^N - 1 = 7$):**

$$
V_{ref,k} = k \cdot \Delta V = k \cdot 1 \text{ V}
$$

| Tap $k$ | Reference Voltage $V_{ref,k}$ | Comparator $C_k$ Output State (for $V_{in} = 5.2 \text{ V}$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 V | HIGH (1) — since 5.2 > 1 |
| 2 | 2 V | HIGH (1) — since 5.2 > 2 |
| 3 | 3 V | HIGH (1) — since 5.2 > 3 |
| 4 | 4 V | HIGH (1) — since 5.2 > 4 |
| 5 | **5 V** | **HIGH (1)** — since 5.2 > 5 |
| 6 | 6 V | LOW (0) — since 5.2 < 6 |
| 7 | 7 V | LOW (0) — since 5.2 < 7 |

**Step 4 — Read the thermometer code:**
Comparator outputs (from top to bottom): $C_7 C_6 C_5 C_4 C_3 C_2 C_1 = 0\ 0\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1$

**Step 5 — Determine the binary output via priority encoder:**
The transition from 1 to 0 occurs **between $C_5$ and $C_6$**. The priority encoder identifies the highest-order "1" output, which is $C_5$, and outputs the corresponding binary code.

$$
\text{Binary output} = 101_2 = 5_{10}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify by linear interpolation:**
Theoretical expected code for $V_{in} = 5.2 \text{ V}$: $\text{Code} = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{5.2}{1}\right) = 5$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Quantization Error

**Problem Statement:** Derive the maximum quantization error for an ideal N-bit Flash ADC with reference voltage $V_{REF}$.

**Step 1 — Define quantization error:**
Quantization error is the difference between the actual analog input and the digitally reconstructed output value.

$$
E_q = V_{in} - V_{out,digital}
$$

**Step 2 — Bound the error:**
For an ideal ADC, the reconstructed output $V_{out,digital}$ will be within $\pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ of the actual input $V_{in}$, since the input can fall anywhere within a single LSB interval.

$$
-\dfrac{\Delta V}{2} \leq E_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the LSB definition:**

$$
\boxed{\vert E_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N+1}}}
$$

**Numerical Verification:** For $V_{REF} = 8 \text{ V}$ and $N = 3$ bits:

$$
\vert E_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{8}{2^{3+1}} = \dfrac{8}{16} = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

This is half of the LSB size of 1 V, which is consistent with theory.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples Comparison

**Example Set:** Compute key Flash ADC parameters for different resolutions, $V_{REF} = 5 \text{ V}$:

| Resolution $N$ | Comparators $2^N - 1$ | Resistors $2^N$ | LSB $\Delta V$ | Quantization Error Max | Conversion Time (typ.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 bits | 7 | 8 | 625 mV | 312.5 mV | ~5 ns |
| 4 bits | 15 | 16 | 312.5 mV | 156.25 mV | ~6 ns |
| 6 bits | 63 | 64 | 78.125 mV | 39.06 mV | ~10 ns |
| 8 bits | 255 | 256 | 19.53 mV | 9.77 mV | ~15 ns |
| 10 bits | 1023 | 1024 | 4.88 mV | 2.44 mV | ~25 ns |

**Observation:** Going from 8-bit to 10-bit **quadruples** the hardware (255 → 1023 comparators) for only 2 extra bits of resolution. This is the **bit-resolution vs. hardware complexity tradeoff** that fundamentally limits Flash ADC resolution.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Ladder Current Limitation and Power Consideration

**Problem Statement:** Why can't we simply increase $N$ to 16 bits and call it a day? Derive the power and bandwidth consequences.

**Step 1 — Ladder power dissipation:**
The ladder dissipates constant DC power as long as $V_{REF}$ is applied.

$$
P_{ladder} = \dfrac{V_{REF}^2}{2^N \cdot R}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparator input capacitance:**
Each comparator contributes a parasitic input capacitance $C_{comp}$. The total input capacitance seen by the analog source is:

$$
C_{in,total} = (2^N - 1) \cdot C_{comp}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth limitation:**
The analog source must drive this capacitance, creating a low-pass RC filter with the source resistance $R_s$.

$$
f_{-3\text{dB}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_s C_{in,total}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_s (2^N - 1) C_{comp}}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the limitation:**
For a 10-bit Flash ADC with $C_{comp} = 2 \text{ pF}$ and $R_s = 50 \Omega$:

$$
f_{-3\text{dB}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 1023 \times 2 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.56 \text{ GHz}
$$

While the bandwidth is still respectable, the **input capacitive loading reduces analog signal integrity** at high frequencies — a critical concern in high-speed PCB layout.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The KTU PCBMT403 syllabus specifically highlights that **exponentially increasing hardware with resolution** is the **primary disadvantage of Flash converters**. This is a high-weightage concept for the 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-bit Flash ADC

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a 3-bit Flash ADC, showing the interaction between the resistor ladder, the comparator bank, the thermometer-code generation, and the priority encoder.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Analog Input Voltage V_in]:::inputNode
    B[DC Reference Voltage V_REF 5V]:::refNode
    C1[Resistor Ladder 8 x 1kohm]:::ladderNode
    D1[Comparator C1 Ref 1V]:::compNode
    D2[Comparator C2 Ref 2V]:::compNode
    D3[Comparator C3 Ref 3V]:::compNode
    D4[Comparator C4 Ref 4V]:::compNode
    D5[Comparator C5 Ref 5V]:::compNode
    D6[Comparator C6 Ref 6V]:::compNode
    D7[Comparator C7 Ref 7V]:::compNode
    E[Thermometer Code 7-bit Output]:::thermNode
    F[Priority Encoder Logic]:::encoderNode
    G[3-bit Binary Output D2 D1 D0]:::binaryNode
    H[Bubble Error Corrector Optional]:::correctorNode

    A --> D1
    A --> D2
    A --> D3
    A --> D4
    A --> D5
    A --> D6
    A --> D7

    B --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    C1 --> D4
    C1 --> D5
    C1 --> D6
    C1 --> D7

    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E

    E --> H
    H --> F
    F --> G

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef refNode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ladderNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef compNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef thermNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef encoderNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef binaryNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef correctorNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow and Decision Logic Diagram

This Mermaid diagram explains the **decision-tree logic** that each comparator follows, leading to the priority encoder's role in binary conversion.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Apply V_in to all comparators]:::startNode
    B[Comparator C_k Decision]:::decisionNode
    C{V_in greater than V_ref k?}:::branchNode
    D[Output C_k equals 1 HIGH]:::highNode
    E[Output C_k equals 0 LOW]:::lowNode
    F[All C_k outputs form Thermometer Code]:::thermNode
    G[Priority Encoder Finds Highest 1]:::findNode
    H[Output N-bit Binary Code]:::outputNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- Yes --> D
    C -- No --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branchNode fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef highNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef thermNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef findNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture Diagram — Flash vs Other ADC Types

This diagram contextualizes where Flash ADCs sit in the broader ADC architecture family covered in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ADC Architectures KTU Module 3]:::rootNode
    B[Flash ADC Parallel]:::flashNode
    C[Successive Approximation ADC]:::sarNode
    D[Dual Slope Integrating ADC]:::dualNode
    E[Sigma Delta ADC]:::sigmaNode
    F[Pipeline ADC]:::pipelineNode

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F

    B --- B1[Speed: Fastest GSPS]:::flashProp
    B --- B2[Hardware: 2 to the N minus 1 comparators]:::flashProp
    B --- B3[Resolution: 6 to 8 bits typical]:::flashProp
    B --- B4[Used in Oscilloscopes Radar]:::flashProp

    C --- C1[Speed: Medium 1 MSPS]:::sarProp
    C --- C2[Hardware: 1 comparator plus SAR logic]:::sarProp

    D --- D1[Speed: Slow]:::dualProp
    D --- D2[Resolution: 16 plus bits high accuracy]:::dualProp

    classDef rootNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef flashNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sarNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dualNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sigmaNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pipelineNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flashProp fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef sarProp fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dualProp fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the Mermaid block diagrams above, each color-coded block represents a distinct sub-system of the Flash ADC. The **inputNode** (orange) is the analog signal source, **ladderNode** (purple) is the resistor network, **compNode** (pink) represents the parallel comparators, **thermNode** (yellow) holds the thermometer code, **encoderNode** (green) performs binary conversion, and **binaryNode** (blue) is the final N-bit output.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Flash ADC. State its main advantage and one major disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Flash Analog-to-Digital Converter** (also called a **Parallel ADC**) is a high-speed data conversion circuit that uses $2^N - 1$ comparators operating in parallel to directly convert an analog input voltage into an N-bit digital output within a single clock cycle.

**Main Advantage:** It is the **fastest** type of ADC, with conversion times in the nanosecond range and sampling rates up to several GSPS (Giga Samples Per Second).

**Major Disadvantage:** The number of comparators grows **exponentially** with resolution ($2^N - 1$), making the hardware complex, expensive, and power-hungry. Practical Flash ADCs are limited to **6 to 8 bits** of resolution.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "thermometer code" in a Flash ADC? How is it converted to binary form?**

**Model Answer:**

In a Flash ADC, the $2^N - 1$ comparator outputs form a unique pattern called the **thermometer code**. In this code, all comparators below a certain input voltage level output logic **1 (HIGH)**, and all comparators above that level output logic **0 (LOW)** — resembling the mercury column of a thermometer where all values up to the actual temperature are "filled".

For example, for a 3-bit Flash ADC with $V_{in} = 5.2 \text{ V}$: outputs are $C_7 C_6 C_5 C_4 C_3 C_2 C_1 = 0\ 0\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1$.

The thermometer code is converted to standard N-bit binary by a **Priority Encoder** circuit, which identifies the position of the highest-order "1" in the thermometer code and maps it to the corresponding binary value (e.g., the transition between $C_5$ and $C_6$ in the example yields binary $101_2 = 5_{10}$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Question:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a 3-bit Flash ADC**. Also derive the expressions for (i) number of comparators, (ii) LSB voltage, and (iii) maximum quantization error. Assume $V_{REF} = 8 \text{ V}$.

##### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Working of 3-bit Flash ADC with Block Diagram**

**Model Solution:**

A 3-bit Flash ADC consists of the following blocks:

**1. Resistor Ladder Network (8 resistors of value $R$ each):**
The ladder is connected between $V_{REF} = 8 \text{ V}$ (top) and ground (bottom). It generates 7 internal tap voltages at $1\text{ V}, 2\text{ V}, 3\text{ V}, 4\text{ V}, 5\text{ V}, 6\text{ V}, 7\text{ V}$. Total resistance = $8R$. Ladder current = $V_{REF} / 8R = 1 \text{ mA}$ (if $R = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$).

**2. Comparator Bank (7 Comparators $C_1$ to $C_7$):**
The analog input voltage $V_{in}$ is connected to the **non-inverting (+) input** of all 7 comparators simultaneously. The inverting (−) input of comparator $C_k$ is connected to the k-th tap on the resistor ladder.

**3. Priority Encoder:**
Accepts the 7-bit thermometer code from the comparators and produces a 3-bit binary output ($D_2 D_1 D_0$).

**Working Principle:** When $V_{in}$ is applied, every comparator simultaneously checks whether $V_{in}$ exceeds its specific reference voltage. The comparators with reference voltages below $V_{in}$ output HIGH (1), while those with reference voltages above $V_{in}$ output LOW (0). The resulting pattern is the thermometer code, which the priority encoder translates to a 3-bit binary number.

**Example:** For $V_{in} = 5.2 \text{ V}$, the outputs are $C_1=1, C_2=1, C_3=1, C_4=1, C_5=1, C_6=0, C_7=0$. The priority encoder detects the transition between $C_5$ and $C_6$ and outputs binary $101$.

##### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Derivations**

**(i) Number of Comparators:**

An N-bit Flash ADC requires $2^N - 1$ comparators because it must distinguish between $2^N$ possible output codes, requiring $2^N - 1$ decision thresholds (excluding the endpoints 0 V and $V_{REF}$). For N = 3:

$$
N_c = 2^3 - 1 = 7 \text{ comparators}
$$

**(ii) LSB Voltage:**

The reference voltage $V_{REF}$ is divided into $2^N$ equal steps, giving the LSB:

$$
\text{LSB} = \Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N} = \dfrac{8}{2^3} = 1 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

The maximum quantization error is half the LSB:

$$
\vert E_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N+1}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{3+1}} = \dfrac{8}{16} = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**Question:** Compare **Flash ADC** with **Successive Approximation ADC** and **Dual-Slope ADC** based on (a) conversion speed, (b) hardware complexity, (c) resolution, and (d) typical applications. Justify why Flash ADCs are preferred for digital oscilloscopes.

##### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Comparison Table**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Flash ADC | Successive Approximation ADC | Dual-Slope ADC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conversion Speed** | **Fastest** (single clock cycle, ~ns) | Medium (N clock cycles, ~µs) | **Slowest** (requires integration time, ~ms) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | **Very High** ($2^N - 1$ comparators + encoder) | Moderate (1 comparator + SAR register + DAC) | Low (1 integrator + 1 comparator + counter) |
| **Resolution** | Low (6–8 bits practical) | Medium-High (8–18 bits typical) | **Very High** (16–24 bits) |
| **Power Consumption** | **Very High** (all comparators active) | Moderate | Low |
| **Cost** | **High** | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Applications** | Digital oscilloscopes, radar, high-speed data acquisition, satellite receivers | Microcontroller ADCs, industrial process control, data loggers | Digital voltmeters, precision measurement, slow industrial sensors |

##### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Why Flash ADCs for Digital Oscilloscopes**

**Model Solution:**

Digital oscilloscopes (DSOs) require ADCs that can sample analog signals at extremely high rates, often in the **GSPS (Giga Samples Per Second)** range, to accurately capture fast transient events, high-frequency periodic signals, and single-shot pulses.

Flash ADCs are preferred for the following reasons:

1. **Single-Cycle Conversion:** A Flash ADC completes the entire conversion in essentially one clock cycle (limited only by comparator propagation delay), achieving sub-nanosecond conversion times. SAR ADCs would require N clock cycles (one per bit), making them too slow for GSPS sampling.

2. **Wide Analog Bandwidth:** The parallel architecture allows the input signal to be applied simultaneously to all comparators, maintaining high analog bandwidth (often > 1 GHz). This is critical for capturing fast-rising edges.

3. **No Iterative Settling Required:** Unlike SAR ADCs (which need DAC settling) or dual-slope ADCs (which need integration), Flash ADCs have no iterative feedback loop, eliminating accumulation of timing errors.

4. **Moderate Resolution is Sufficient:** Oscilloscopes primarily capture waveform shape and timing, not absolute amplitude precision. 6–8 bit resolution is usually adequate, perfectly matching Flash ADC's practical range.

5. **Real-Time Sampling:** Flash ADCs can sample truly in real-time (not requiring equivalent-time sampling techniques), enabling the capture of **non-repetitive, single-shot transient events** — a fundamental requirement for debugging digital circuits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the number of resistors and comparators.** Many students write "$2^N$ comparators" instead of "$2^N - 1$". The "minus 1" is because the endpoints (0 V and $V_{REF}$) do not require a comparator.
> 2. **Quantization error is $\pm \text{LSB}/2$, not $\pm \text{LSB}$.** This is a common 1-mark deduction error.
> 3. **Always state the unit** for LSB and quantization error (V or mV). Mark allocation in KTU strictly penalizes missing units.
> 4. **In comparison questions**, students often write only the disadvantage of Flash ADCs without explaining the **advantage** of the comparison ADC. The KTU marking scheme awards marks for **both sides** of the comparison.
> 5. **Priority encoder explanation:** Do NOT just say "it converts thermometer to binary." You MUST explain the **logic** — i.e., it identifies the highest-order 1 (the transition point) and maps it to binary. Examiners specifically look for this logic step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flash ADC (KTU 2024 PCBMT403, Module 3):**

- **Core Architecture:** Flash ADC = **Resistor Ladder** + **Parallel Comparator Bank** + **Priority Encoder**.
- **Hardware Formulas:**
   - Comparators = $2^N - 1$ (NOT $2^N$)
   - Resistors = $2^N$
   - LSB = $V_{REF} / 2^N$
   - Max Quantization Error = $\pm V_{REF} / 2^{N+1}$
- **Conversion Time:** Effectively **1 clock cycle** (single-step parallel conversion) — the fastest of all ADC architectures.
- **Output Format:** Internally generates a **thermometer code** (contiguous run of 1s transitioning to 0s), converted to N-bit binary by a **priority encoder**.
- **Tap Voltages:** The k-th tap (from ground) provides $V_{ref,k} = k \cdot V_{REF} / 2^N$.
- **Ladder Current:** $I_{ladder} = V_{REF} / (2^N \cdot R)$ — dissipated as DC power even when idle.
- **Major Advantage:** **Speed** (GSPS-class sampling rates possible).
- **Major Disadvantage:** **Exponentially growing hardware complexity** with resolution — limits practical resolution to 6–8 bits.
- **Other Limitations:** High power consumption, large input capacitance (loading the analog source), large silicon die area, expensive.
- **Bubble Errors:** Caused by metastable comparators in the thermometer code; mitigated by **Gray code encoding** or **bubble error correction circuits** between the comparator bank and the priority encoder.
- **Applications:** Digital storage oscilloscopes, radar/LIDAR systems, high-speed communication receivers, particle physics detectors, electronic warfare systems.
- **Industrial IC Examples:** AD9215 (10-bit, 105 MSPS), ADC08B2000 (8-bit, 2 GSPS), CA3308 (8-bit, 15 MSPS).
- **KTU 2024 High-Yield Equations to Memorize Verbatim:**
   - $N_c = 2^N - 1$
   - $\Delta V = V_{REF} / 2^N$
   - $\vert E_q \vert_{max} = V_{REF} / 2^{N+1}$
   - $I_{ladder} = V_{REF} / (2^N \cdot R)$
- **Comparison Quick-Reference:**
   - **Fastest:** Flash > Pipeline > SAR > Dual-Slope
   - **Highest Resolution:** Dual-Slope > SAR > Pipeline > Flash
   - **Most Complex Hardware:** Flash > Pipeline > SAR > Dual-Slope
   - **Lowest Power:** Dual-Slope > SAR > Pipeline > Flash
- **Common KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **Flash** with **Pipeline** ADCs. Flash uses a **single-stage** parallel array; Pipeline uses **multiple lower-resolution Flash stages** cascaded with sample-and-hold circuits between them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delta-Sigma Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Delta-Sigma Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Delta-Sigma (ΔΣ) Converter** (also called a *Sigma-Delta* converter) is a high-resolution, oversampling Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) architecture that uses **noise shaping** and **digital filtering/decimation** to achieve very high effective resolution (typically **16 to 24 bits**) from a low-resolution (often **1-bit**) quantizer. The KTU 2024 syllabus (PCBMT403, Module 3) categorises the ΔΣ converter as a key *oversampling converter* that combines the dual operations of **delta modulation (Δ)** and **summation/integration (Σ)** in a feedback loop, hence the name.

> [!NOTE]
> **Delta (Δ)**: Represents the *difference* (error) between the input analog signal and the predicted feedback signal.
> **Sigma (Σ)**: Represents the *summation/integration* of this error performed by an integrator in the forward path.

The ΔΣ modulator trades **speed for resolution**. By sampling the analog input at a rate **far greater** than the Nyquist rate (a concept called **oversampling**), it pushes the broadband quantization noise of the coarse quantizer out of the signal band of interest, where it is subsequently removed by a digital low-pass filter and decimated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Librarian and the Whisper" Intuition

Imagine a librarian who must accurately transcribe a soft-spoken story (low-amplitude analog signal) in a noisy room (quantization noise).

- **Standard ADC (Nyquist):** The librarian tries to record the story directly. Background chatter drowns out the voice, and the transcript is full of errors (limited resolution).
- **Delta-Sigma ADC:** The librarian hires an assistant whose *only* job is to listen constantly and shout, frame-by-frame, whether the storyteller is getting **louder (+1)** or **softer (-1)** than the previous instant. This is the **1-bit quantizer running at very high speed** (oversampling). Because the assistant listens so frequently, the "chatter noise" is pushed to very high frequencies, while the *story* (the slowly changing DC/low-frequency signal) is captured with astonishing fidelity. Later, an "editor" (**digital filter + decimator**) averages out the high-frequency chatter, leaving a clean, high-resolution transcript.

This is, in essence, the operating principle of every modern audio DAC chip, digital weighing scale, precision multimeter, and instrumentation sensor interface.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-critical parameters for a ΔΣ Converter:**
> - **Oversampling Ratio (OSR):** Ratio of sampling frequency to Nyquist frequency.
> - **Order of Modulator (L):** Number of integrators in the loop (1st order, 2nd order, etc.).
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Measured in **dB**, directly related to **Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)**.
> - **Decimation Factor (M):** The integer by which the oversampled data rate is reduced.

| Parameter | Typical Engineering Range | Physical Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sampling Rate (fs) | **MHz to GHz** | Far exceeds 2·B (Nyquist) |
| OSR | **64 to 1024+** | fs / (2·B) |
| Quantizer bits | **1 to 5 bits** | Often a single comparator |
| Effective Resolution | **16 to 24 bits** | After digital filtering |
| ENOB | **18 to 22 bits** | Practical audio/industrial |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks — Three Sub-Systems

A complete ΔΣ ADC is composed of three sequential stages, and KTU examiners expect students to be able to **identify and explain each stage independently**.

### 2.1.1 The ΔΣ Modulator (Analog Front-End)
A closed-loop negative-feedback system containing:
- **Subtractor (Δ):** Computes the difference between the input $x(t)$ and the DAC output.
- **Integrator (Σ):** A discrete-time (switched-capacitor) or continuous-time integrator that accumulates the error.
- **Quantizer:** A low-resolution (typically 1-bit) flash ADC producing a high-rate bitstream.
- **Feedback DAC:** Converts the quantized output back to analog and feeds it back to the subtractor.

### 2.1.2 The Digital Decimation Filter
A low-pass digital filter that:
- Suppresses the high-frequency quantization noise pushed out of the signal band by the modulator.
- Optionally removes out-of-band interferers.
- Typically implemented as a **Cascaded Integrator-Comb (CIC)** filter followed by half-band and FIR stages.

### 2.1.3 The Decimator (Down-Sampler)
Reduces the sample rate from the oversampled rate (e.g., **MHz**) to the desired Nyquist-rate output (e.g., **kHz**) by retaining every M-th sample.

## 2.2 Why Oversampling? — The Theoretical Justification

In a conventional Nyquist-rate ADC with a quantizer step size $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$, the **uniform white-noise assumption** gives a total rms quantization noise power of:

$$e_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}$$

This noise is **uniformly distributed** over the entire Nyquist band, from DC to $f_s/2$. When we **oversample** at $f_s = OSR \cdot 2B$ (where B is the signal bandwidth), the *same* total noise power is now smeared across a much wider frequency range. After the digital low-pass filter restricts us to the original signal band of width B, the **in-band noise power** becomes:

$$P_{n, in-band} = \frac{e_q^2}{OSR} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2N} \cdot OSR}$$

This alone yields a **3 dB SNR improvement per doubling of OSR** (equivalent to **+0.5 bits** of resolution). This modest gain is the *baseline oversampling* benefit, and is **insufficient** for high-resolution applications. The real magic happens when **noise shaping** is added on top.

## 2.3 Noise Shaping — The ΔΣ Magic

A noise-shaping feedback loop applies a **high-pass transfer function to the quantization noise** while leaving the **signal transfer function equal to unity** (ideally, at low frequencies). The result is that the in-band quantization noise is *spectrally sculpted* — pushed away from DC and concentrated near $f_s/2$, where it is harmlessly removed by the digital filter.

For a **first-order (L = 1) ΔΣ modulator**, the in-band noise becomes:

$$P_{n, in-band}^{(1)} = \frac{\pi^2 e_q^2}{36 \cdot OSR^3}$$

For a general **L-th order modulator**, the in-band noise is suppressed by a factor of $OSR^{(2L+1)}$:

$$P_{n, in-band}^{(L)} = \frac{\pi^{2L} e_q^2}{12(2L+1) \cdot OSR^{(2L+1)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of ΔΣ:** Every doubling of OSR yields **+3(2L+1) dB** of SNR improvement, equivalent to **+(2L+1)/2 bits** of additional resolution. For L = 1, this is **+1.5 bits per octave**; for L = 2, **+2.5 bits per octave**; for L = 3, **+3.5 bits per octave**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | LaTeX Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Oversampling Ratio | $OSR = \dfrac{f_s}{2 B}$ | Ratio of sample rate to twice signal bandwidth | dimensionless |
| Baseline Oversampling SNR Gain | $\Delta SNR = 10 \log_{10}(OSR)$ | SNR improvement from oversampling alone | **dB** |
| Quantization Step Size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | Smallest distinguishable voltage step | **Volts (V)** |
| Total Noise Power (White) | $e_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Uniformly distributed over $f_s/2$ | $V^2$ |
| In-band Noise (L-th order) | $P_n = \dfrac{\pi^{2L} \Delta^2}{12(2L+1) \cdot OSR^{2L+1}}$ | Noise power inside signal band B | $V^2$ |
| SNR (L-th order) | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76 + (2L+1) \cdot 10 \log_{10}(OSR)$ | Total signal-to-noise ratio in **dB** | **dB** |
| Effective Resolution (ENOB) | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Practical bit count after noise shaping | **bits** |
| Decimation Factor | $M = OSR$ | Output rate = $f_s / M$ | dimensionless |
| Stability Condition (1st order) | Loop gain $\vert H(f) \vert < 1$ at $f_s/2$ | Always stable for L = 1 | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute value expressions in this sheet are written as $\vert x \vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity. In handwritten KTU scripts, you may write the conventional $\vert x \vert$ or $|x|$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

ΔΣ converters dominate the following domains, and KTU examiners frequently connect theory to these industrial applications:

1. **Digital Audio (DSD, SACD):** Audio DACs in smartphones, soundbars, and Blu-ray players use **5th-order multi-bit ΔΣ modulators** with OSRs above 128 to achieve **>120 dB SNR**.
2. **Precision Weighing Scales & Industrial Sensors:** The 24-bit ΔΣ ADCs from Texas Instruments (ADS1256, ADS131M04) and Analog Devices (AD7195) are the workhorses of strain-gauge bridges, RTDs, and thermocouple readouts.
3. **Seismology & Geophysics:** Ultra-low-frequency ΔΣ ADCs (e.g., CS5371/72) digitize seismic signals with **>140 dB dynamic range** using OSRs exceeding 256.
4. **Power Quality Meters & Smart Grids:** Class-A energy meters rely on ΔΣ ADCs to monitor currents with **0.1% accuracy** over 50/60 Hz mains.
5. **Medical Instrumentation:** ECG, EEG, and pulse oximeter front-ends use ΔΣ ADCs to capture microvolt-level biopotentials in the presence of large DC offsets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Noise Power of a Uniform Mid-Tread Quantizer

Let the quantizer be an **N-bit mid-tread type** with full-scale range $V_{FS}$ and uniform step size $\Delta$.

**Step 1:** The total number of quantization levels is $2^N$. The full-scale range is mapped across these levels, giving:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

**Step 2:** Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ and is **statistically independent** of the input signal. The probability density function is:

$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for} \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The mean-square value of the quantization error (its variance) is computed as:

$$e_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ and integrating:

$$e_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluating the antiderivative at the bounds:

$$e_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta^3}{24} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 6:** Final result expressed in terms of $V_{FS}$ and N:

$$e_q^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}$$

This is the **total** noise power, uniformly spread across the entire Nyquist band $[0, f_s/2]$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Baseline Oversampling SNR Gain

**Step 1:** Let the total white noise power $e_q^2$ be uniformly distributed over the band $[0, f_s/2]$. The **two-sided noise power spectral density** is:

$$S_e(f) = \frac{e_q^2}{f_s/2} = \frac{2 e_q^2}{f_s} \quad \text{(units: } V^2/\text{Hz)}$$

**Step 2:** After digital low-pass filtering restricts us to the original signal band of width B, the in-band noise power is:

$$P_{n, in} = \int_{-B}^{+B} S_e(f) \, df = S_e(f) \cdot 2B = \frac{2 e_q^2}{f_s} \cdot 2B = \frac{4 B e_q^2}{f_s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the oversampling ratio $OSR = f_s / (2B)$, so $f_s = 2B \cdot OSR$:

$$P_{n, in} = \frac{4 B e_q^2}{2B \cdot OSR} = \frac{2 e_q^2}{OSR}$$

**Step 4:** The SNR for a full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$ is $P_{signal}/P_{n,in}$:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2 / 2}{e_q^2 / OSR} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Expanding using the standard N-bit result and isolating the OSR term:

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 + 10 \log_{10}(OSR)$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion — every doubling of OSR contributes exactly **3 dB** of SNR improvement, equivalent to **0.5 effective bits**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Noise Shaping Transfer Function (First-Order Modulator)

**Step 1:** Model the ΔΣ modulator in the Z-domain. Let $X(z)$ be the input, $Y(z)$ the output, $E(z)$ the additive quantization noise, and $H(z)$ the integrator transfer function.

A discrete-time integrator has the transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2:** Write the signal flow equations. The output $Y(z)$ is the sum of the integrator output and the noise:

$$Y(z) = H(z) \cdot [X(z) - Y(z)] + E(z)$$

This is because the subtractor feeds the difference $(X - Y)$ into the integrator, and the quantizer adds noise E.

**Step 3:** Solve for $Y(z)$:

$$Y(z) = H(z) X(z) - H(z) Y(z) + E(z)$$

$$Y(z) [1 + H(z)] = H(z) X(z) + E(z)$$

$$Y(z) = \frac{H(z)}{1 + H(z)} X(z) + \frac{1}{1 + H(z)} E(z)$$

**Step 4:** Define the **Signal Transfer Function (STF)** and **Noise Transfer Function (NTF)**:

$$STF(z) = \frac{H(z)}{1 + H(z)} \qquad NTF(z) = \frac{1}{1 + H(z)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the integrator $H(z) = z^{-1}/(1 - z^{-1})$ into the NTF:

$$NTF(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}} = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1} + z^{-1}} = 1 - z^{-1}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to frequency response by substituting $z = e^{j2\pi f/f_s}$:

$$NTF(f) = 1 - e^{-j2\pi f / f_s} = 2j \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right) \cdot e^{-j\pi f/f_s}$$

**Step 7:** The magnitude response is:

$$\vert NTF(f) \vert = 2 \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi f}{f_s} \right)$$

**Step 8:** For $f \ll f_s$ (the in-band region of interest), use the small-angle approximation $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$:

$$\vert NTF(f) \vert \approx \frac{2 \pi f}{f_s}$$

**Step 9:** The shaped noise power spectral density becomes:

$$S_{n,shaped}(f) = S_e(f) \cdot \vert NTF(f) \vert^2 \approx \frac{2 e_q^2}{f_s} \cdot \left( \frac{2 \pi f}{f_s} \right)^2$$

**Step 10:** Integrate the shaped noise over the signal band $[-B, +B]$ to get in-band noise:

$$P_{n, in}^{(1)} = \int_{-B}^{+B} \frac{2 e_q^2}{f_s} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^2 f^2}{f_s^2} \, df = \frac{8 \pi^2 e_q^2}{f_s^3} \int_{0}^{B} f^2 \, df = \frac{8 \pi^2 e_q^2 B^3}{3 f_s^3}$$

**Step 11:** Substitute $f_s = 2B \cdot OSR$ and simplify:

$$P_{n, in}^{(1)} = \frac{8 \pi^2 e_q^2 B^3}{3 (2B \cdot OSR)^3} = \frac{8 \pi^2 e_q^2 B^3}{3 \cdot 8 B^3 \cdot OSR^3} = \frac{\pi^2 e_q^2}{3 \cdot OSR^3}$$

**Step 12:** This is the **first-order in-band noise** after noise shaping. Compared to the unshaped value $P_{n, in} = 2 e_q^2 / OSR$, the suppression factor is $6 \pi^2 / OSR^2$ for the noise power. Every doubling of OSR now gives **9 dB** of SNR improvement (i.e., **+1.5 bits**).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SNR for an L-th Order Modulator (General Result)

By induction and standard ΔΣ analysis (Candy, 1974, with the assumption of uncorrelated white noise and stable loop), the in-band noise for an L-th order modulator is:

$$P_{n, in}^{(L)} = \frac{\pi^{2L} e_q^2}{12(2L+1) \cdot OSR^{2L+1}} \cdot (2B)$$

Plugging into the SNR expression for a full-scale sinusoid ($V_{p} = V_{FS}/2$):

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 + (2L+1) \cdot 10 \log_{10}(OSR) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi^{2L}}{12(2L+1)}\right)$$

For typical OSR > 16, the constant $\pi^{2L}/(12(2L+1))$ term is small and often neglected, leaving the elegant design rule of thumb:

$$\boxed{SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02N + 1.76 + (2L+1) \cdot 10 \log_{10}(OSR)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's Cheat Code:** To add **1 effective bit**, you can either: (a) double OSR (if L = 1, gives 1.5 bits, so you only need a $\times 1.6$ increase in OSR for 1 bit); (b) increase L from 1 to 2 (gives an extra 1 bit/octave of OSR); or (c) add 0.5 bits via the quantizer (use a 2-bit flash instead of 1-bit). Designers use a combination of all three.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A first-order ΔΣ ADC uses a 1-bit quantizer (N = 1) with $V_{FS} = 2$ V, sampling rate $f_s = 1$ MHz, and input signal bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz. Compute the in-band SNR and ENOB.

**Step 1 — OSR:**

$$OSR = \frac{f_s}{2B} = \frac{10^6}{2 \cdot 4 \times 10^3} = 125$$

**Step 2 — Quantization step and noise power:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ V}, \quad e_q^2 = \frac{1^2}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ V}^2$$

**Step 3 — In-band noise (first-order shaped):**

$$P_{n,in} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 0.0833}{3 \cdot (125)^3} = \frac{0.8225}{5.86 \times 10^6} = 1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}^2$$

**Step 4 — Signal power (full-scale sinusoid, $V_{p} = 1$ V):**

$$P_s = \frac{V_p^2}{2} = 0.5 \text{ V}^2$$

**Step 5 — SNR:**

$$SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1.40 \times 10^{-7}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3.57 \times 10^{6}) \approx 65.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — ENOB:**

$$ENOB = \frac{65.5 - 1.76}{6.02} \approx 10.6 \text{ bits}$$

> **Result:** A 1-bit quantizer, oversampled by 125×, achieves the equivalent of a **10.6-bit Nyquist-rate ADC** — a remarkable demonstration of the ΔΣ principle. With a 2nd-order modulator, ENOB would jump to ~12.1 bits, and with L = 3 it would reach ~13.6 bits, all at the same hardware cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — First-Order ΔΣ Modulator

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the closed-loop structure of a first-order ΔΣ modulator. Note the **strict alphanumeric node ID** convention and the absence of any reserved keywords (such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style`) as node names.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Analog Input x of t] --> nodeB[Summing Junction Subtract]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Integrator Sigma Block]
    nodeC --> nodeD[1 Bit Quantizer Comparator]
    nodeD --> nodeE[High Rate Bitstream Output Y]
    nodeD --> nodeF[1 Bit Feedback DAC]
    nodeF --> nodeB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Analog Input** $x(t)$ enters the summing junction (subtractor).
- The **1-bit feedback DAC** returns the previous quantized output $y[n]$ to the subtractor.
- The **Integrator (Σ block)** accumulates the difference error over time.
- The **1-bit quantizer** (essentially a comparator with thresholds at $\pm V_{ref}$) produces a high-rate bitstream.
- The output $Y$ is a 1-bit PDM (Pulse-Density Modulated) stream whose *local density* of 1s encodes the analog amplitude.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Complete ΔΣ ADC System

The full system adds the **digital decimation filter** and **decimator** to the modulator output, producing a high-resolution, low-rate digital word.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Analog Input Signal] --> nodeB[Delta Sigma Modulator 1st or 2nd Order]
    nodeB --> nodeC[High Rate Bitstream e.g. 1 MHz]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Digital Low Pass CIC Filter]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Half Band FIR Stages]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Decimator M equals OSR]
    nodeF --> nodeG[High Resolution Nyquist Rate Digital Output]
```

**Stage Functions:**
- **ΔΣ Modulator:** Noise-shaping and oversampling conversion to bitstream.
- **CIC Filter (Cascaded Integrator-Comb):** First-stage, multiplierless, computationally efficient low-pass filter.
- **Half-Band FIR Stages:** Sharpens the roll-off and suppresses out-of-band noise and aliases.
- **Decimator (↓M):** Reduces the sample rate by factor M, producing the final output.

## 4.3 Signal Flow — Quantization Noise Shaping

This diagram conceptually shows how noise shaping relocates the quantization noise power out of the signal band.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Quantization Noise Spectrum Flat over fs divided by 2] --> nodeB[After Noise Shaping High Pass NTF Applied]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Low Frequency Signal Band Preserved]
    nodeB --> nodeD[High Frequency Noise Pushed to fs divided by 2]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Digital LPF Removes Out of Band Noise]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Clean High Resolution Output]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — ΔΣ DAC (Inverse Topology)

Although the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises ADCs, examiners occasionally ask students to comment on the **dual** structure — the ΔΣ Digital-to-Analog Converter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[High Resolution Digital Input] --> nodeB[Digital Delta Sigma Modulator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[High Rate Low Resolution Bitstream]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Analog Low Pass Reconstruction Filter]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Clean Analog Output]
```

**Key Architectural Difference:**
- ADC: Oversampling happens in the **analog** domain at the front-end.
- DAC: Oversampling happens in the **digital** domain; the analog LPF is the only precision stage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Delta-Sigma (ΔΣ) converter. What are its three main functional blocks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Delta-Sigma (ΔΣ) converter is a high-resolution, oversampling ADC architecture that uses **noise shaping** and **digital decimation** to achieve effective resolutions of 16 to 24 bits. The name originates from the **delta (Δ)** operation (difference between input and feedback) and the **sigma (Σ)** operation (integration of the error).

The three main functional blocks are:
1. **ΔΣ Modulator** — A feedback loop containing a subtractor, integrator, low-resolution (typically 1-bit) quantizer, and a feedback DAC. It produces a high-rate bitstream while spectrally shaping the quantization noise out of the signal band.
2. **Digital Decimation Filter** — A low-pass filter (typically a CIC filter followed by half-band FIR stages) that removes the high-frequency shaped noise.
3. **Decimator** — Reduces the oversampled output rate by an integer factor M (equal to OSR) to produce the final Nyquist-rate, high-resolution digital output.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the correct definition and identification of Δ and Σ operations, and 2 marks for naming and explaining the three blocks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the concept of "noise shaping" in a Delta-Sigma converter. Why is it essential for high-resolution conversion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Noise shaping is the spectral manipulation of quantization noise by a ΔΣ feedback loop. The **Noise Transfer Function (NTF)** is designed as a **high-pass filter**, while the **Signal Transfer Function (STF)** is designed to be **unity (all-pass)** within the signal band of interest.

Consequences:
- The quantization noise power is *pushed away* from the low-frequency signal band and concentrated near $f_s/2$.
- After low-pass digital filtering, the in-band noise is dramatically suppressed — by a factor of $OSR^{(2L+1)}$ for an L-th order modulator.
- It is **essential** because a 1-bit quantizer, by itself, has only ~6 dB of SNR (≈1 effective bit). Without noise shaping, oversampling alone would require astronomically high OSRs to reach 16- or 20-bit resolution. Noise shaping allows a 1-bit quantizer to achieve >100 dB SNR at practical OSRs of 64–256.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for NTF = high-pass / STF = all-pass; 1 mark for explanation of spectral relocation; 1 mark for the practical SNR/ENOB justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)** with the **decimation factor (M)**. While they are numerically equal in single-stage designs, students must clarify that OSR is a *modulator parameter* (sampling) and M is a *digital filter parameter* (downsampling).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing the SNR formula as $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76 + 3(2L+1)\log_2(OSR)$ **without unit consistency**. The correct form uses $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios. Always state units in **dB**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Stating "ΔΣ is stable for any order" — in reality, **only first-order modulators are unconditionally stable**. Higher-order loops (L ≥ 3) require **careful coefficient scaling**, **reset mechanisms**, or **multi-stage (MASH) architectures** to prevent overload.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that the quantization noise of a 1-bit quantizer is **not white** in practice — the assumption of uncorrelated white noise holds only for *busy* input signals above the *idle tone threshold* (Lee's rule: $V_{p,min} \approx 2 \Delta / \pi \cdot \sqrt{OSR}$). Failure to address this loses 1–2 marks on higher-order problems.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Answer both sub-parts:**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a first-order Delta-Sigma modulator. Derive the Noise Transfer Function (NTF) and show that the in-band quantization noise power is reduced by a factor of $OSR^2$ compared to baseline oversampling.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. The first-order ΔΣ modulator consists of:
- A **summing junction** (subtractor) that computes the difference between the analog input $x(t)$ and the analog feedback $y_{DAC}(t)$.
- An **integrator** (the $\Sigma$ block) that accumulates this difference over time. In discrete time, $u[n] = u[n-1] + (x[n] - y_{DAC}[n])$.
- A **1-bit quantizer** (comparator) producing a 1-bit output $y[n] \in \{-1, +1\}$ (or $\{0, 1\}$ depending on convention).
- A **1-bit DAC** that converts $y[n]$ back to an analog voltage and feeds it to the subtractor.

**Operating Principle (1 mark):**

If $x[n]$ is greater than the previous output's analog equivalent, the integrator ramps up, eventually crossing the quantizer threshold, producing a 1. The feedback then pulls the integrator back down. The **density of 1s** in the output bitstream is **proportional to the input amplitude** — this is **Pulse Density Modulation (PDM)**.

**Derivation of NTF (3 marks):**

Using the Z-domain model from Section 3.3, Step 5:

$$NTF(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$$

Magnitude response on the unit circle:

$$\vert NTF(f) \vert = 2 \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi f}{f_s} \right)$$

For low frequencies ($f \ll f_s$): $\vert NTF(f) \vert \approx 2 \pi f / f_s$.

**In-Band Noise Reduction (1 mark):**

Baseline oversampling gives in-band noise $P_{n, baseline} = e_q^2 / OSR$.
After 1st-order noise shaping: $P_{n, shaped} = \pi^2 e_q^2 / (3 \cdot OSR^3)$.
Ratio: $P_{n, baseline} / P_{n, shaped} = 3 OSR^2 / \pi^2 \approx OSR^2$ (for large OSR).
Hence, noise power is suppressed by a factor proportional to $OSR^2$, i.e., by **+6 dB per octave of OSR** in addition to the baseline +3 dB/octave, totaling **+9 dB/octave** or **+1.5 bits/octave**.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A Delta-Sigma ADC uses a 1-bit quantizer (N = 1) in a 2nd-order (L = 2) modulator. The input signal has a bandwidth of 10 kHz, and the modulator sampling rate is 3.2 MHz. Calculate:
   (i) The Oversampling Ratio (OSR).
   (ii) The theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in dB.
   (iii) The Effective Number of Bits (ENOB).
   (iv) Comment on the practical stability considerations for L = 2 vs. L = 3 modulators.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) OSR (1 mark):**
$$OSR = \frac{f_s}{2B} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^4} = 160$$

**(ii) SNR (3 marks):**

Using the KTU 2024 formula:
$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 + (2L+1) \cdot 10 \log_{10}(OSR)$$

Substituting N = 1, L = 2, OSR = 160:
$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02(1) + 1.76 + (5) \cdot 10 \log_{10}(160)$$
$$= 6.02 + 1.76 + 5 \cdot 10 \cdot (2.204)$$
$$= 6.02 + 1.76 + 110.2$$
$$SNR \approx 117.98 \text{ dB} \approx 118 \text{ dB}$$

(If the small constant $\pi^{2L}/(12(2L+1))$ correction is included, the result reduces by ~3.5 dB, giving ~114.5 dB. Both answers are acceptable depending on the examiner's convention; students should clearly state the formula being used.)

**(iii) ENOB (1 mark):**
$$ENOB = \frac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{118 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{116.24}{6.02} \approx 19.3 \text{ bits}$$

**(iv) Stability Comment (2 marks):**

A 2nd-order (L = 2) ΔΣ modulator is **conditionally stable**. It remains stable for input signal levels up to approximately **70% of full scale** with properly chosen integrator gains. Above this threshold, the integrators can saturate and the loop becomes unstable. In practice, designers use **gain scaling** and **coefficient optimization** (e.g., the Lee stabilization rule, or the Boser-Wooley methodology) to extend stability.

In contrast, a 3rd-order (L = 3) modulator is **only marginally stable** and requires active stabilization techniques: (a) **reset circuits** that zero the integrators on overload detection; (b) **clipping/AGC** on the input to prevent overdrive; (c) **multi-stage cascaded (MASH) architectures** such as the 2-1 MASH or 2-2 MASH, which combine the output of multiple lower-order modulators using digital cancellation logic to achieve higher-order noise shaping *without* the stability penalty. Most practical industrial ΔΣ ADCs (e.g., the AD7768 from Analog Devices) use a 2nd-order or MASH-2-2 topology rather than a brute-force 3rd-order loop.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the Delta-Sigma ADC with the conventional Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC and the Dual-Slope integrating ADC. Discuss the relative merits in terms of: (i) resolution, (ii) speed, (iii) hardware complexity, and (iv) typical application areas.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | **SAR ADC** | **Dual-Slope ADC** | **ΔΣ ADC** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resolution** | 12–18 bits (typical 16) | 16–24 bits | 16–24 bits (typical 20) |
| **Speed** | Medium-Fast (100 kSps – 10 MSps) | Slow (≤ 100 Sps) | Slow-Medium (10 kSps max audio rate) |
| **Sampling Principle** | Binary search at Nyquist rate | Integration over fixed period | Oversampling + noise shaping |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Medium (DAC, comparator, SAR logic) | Low (op-amp integrator, counter) | High (modulator, digital filter) |
| **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | Strict, sharp analog LPF required | Inherent (integration = LPF) | Relaxed (digital filter handles it) |
| **Power Consumption** | Low-Medium | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Typical Applications** | Microcontrollers, data loggers, motor control | Digital multimeters (DMMs), panel meters | Audio DACs/ADCs, weighing scales, sensors, instrumentation |
| **Linearity** | Good, limited by internal DAC linearity | Excellent (ratiometric) | Excellent after calibration |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Medium (more silicon for digital filter) |

**Discussion (2 marks):**

- For **high-speed, moderate-resolution** applications (e.g., microcontrollers sampling audio at 48 kHz), **SAR ADCs** dominate due to their simplicity and deterministic latency.
- For **precision DC measurement** (e.g., benchtop multimeters measuring a 10 V reference with 6.5-digit accuracy), **dual-slope ADCs** are unmatched because their integration inherently rejects mains-frequency (50/60 Hz) noise.
- For **high-resolution, low-to-moderate speed sensor readout** (e.g., weighing scales, strain gauges, temperature sensors with 24-bit resolution), **ΔΣ ADCs** win because they combine inherent anti-aliasing, excellent linearity, and digital integration in a single chip.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and advantages of a **Multi-stAge noise SHaping (MASH)** Delta-Sigma modulator. How does it overcome the stability limitations of single-loop higher-order modulators?

**Model Solution:**

**MASH Architecture (3 marks):**

The MASH architecture, originally proposed by Hayashi et al. (1986) and refined by Boser and Wooley, uses **two or more low-order (typically 1st-order) modulators in cascade**, with a **digital cancellation network** that combines the outputs to achieve the noise-shaping performance of a higher-order modulator **without the stability penalty**.

A typical **2-1 MASH** structure:
- **Stage 1 (1st-order modulator):** Takes the analog input $x(t)$, produces a 1-bit output $y_1[n]$, and a residual error $e_1[n]$ (the quantization noise of stage 1).
- **Stage 2 (1st-order modulator):** Takes the residual $e_1[n]$ as its input, produces a 1-bit output $y_2[n]$.
- **Digital Cancellation Logic (DCL):** Combines $y_1$ and $y_2$ using the inverse NTF of stage 1 to cancel $e_1$ in the digital domain, while preserving the noise shaping of stage 2.

**Key Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **Inherent stability:** Each individual stage is a 1st-order loop, which is **unconditionally stable**. The MASH system as a whole inherits this stability.
2. **High-order noise shaping:** The combined output exhibits the NTF of an equivalent **L-th order** modulator (for an $n_1 - n_2 - \ldots$ MASH cascade, $L = n_1 + n_2 + \ldots$).
3. **Predictable behavior:** The architecture is fully linear in its digital cancellation path, simplifying the design and simulation cycle.
4. **No analog trimming required:** Higher-order performance is achieved purely through digital post-processing.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**

- **Sensitivity to analog component mismatch:** If the analog NTF of stage 1 deviates from its ideal model, the digital cancellation is incomplete and noise leaks into the output. This is mitigated by careful analog design, calibration, or the use of **self-calibrating MASH** architectures.
- **Higher digital complexity:** The digital cancellation network requires precise multi-bit arithmetic and adds to the silicon area of the digital filter.

**Practical Example (1 mark):**

The Texas Instruments ADS131M04 (24-bit, 32-kSps, 4-channel ΔΣ ADC) uses a 3rd-order MASH modulator architecture to achieve 115 dB SNR at 32 kSps, with ENOB > 18 bits, while remaining unconditionally stable across the full input range.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Memorize it. Reproduce it in the KTU exam hall.**

- [ ] **ΔΣ stands for Delta (difference) + Sigma (integration)** — coined from the feedback loop's two key operations.
- [ ] The **three blocks** of a ΔΣ ADC are: **Modulator → Digital Decimation Filter → Decimator**.
- [ ] **OSR = $f_s / (2B)$** is the single most important parameter. It is dimensionless.
- [ ] **Baseline oversampling** gives **+3 dB SNR per doubling of OSR** (≈ **+0.5 bit/octave**).
- [ ] **Noise shaping** (1st order) gives an *additional* **+6 dB SNR per doubling of OSR** (≈ **+1 bit/octave**), for a total of **+9 dB/octave** or **+1.5 bits/octave** for L = 1.
- [ ] **L-th order** modulator gives **+3(2L+1) dB SNR per OSR doubling** or **+(2L+1)/2 bits/octave**.
- [ ] **General SNR formula:** $SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 + (2L+1) \cdot 10 \log_{10}(OSR)$.
- [ ] **ENOB formula:** $ENOB = (SNR - 1.76) / 6.02$.
- [ ] **1st-order ΔΣ is unconditionally stable**; higher orders require care (MASH, gain scaling, reset).
- [ ] The **NTF of a 1st-order ΔΣ** is $1 - z^{-1}$, a digital differentiator (high-pass).
- [ ] The **STF** of a well-designed ΔΣ is approximately **unity** in the signal band.
- [ ] A **1-bit quantizer** + high OSR + noise shaping can outperform a 16-bit Nyquist ADC at audio frequencies.
- [ ] **CIC (Cascaded Integrator-Comb) filters** are preferred for the first decimation stage because they are multiplierless and computationally efficient.
- [ ] **Decimation factor M = OSR** (for single-stage designs) — this reduces the high-rate bitstream to Nyquist rate.
- [ ] **Real-world applications** to remember: digital audio (DSD, SACD), weighing scales, DMMs, ECG/EEG, smart meters, seismology.
- [ ] **SAR ADC** is best for fast, moderate resolution; **Dual-Slope** is best for precision DC; **ΔΣ** is best for high-resolution, low-speed sensor interfaces.
- [ ] **Pitfall — Idle Tones:** For DC or slowly varying inputs, 1st-order ΔΣ can produce audible "tones" (limit cycles) at the output. The remedy is to add **dither** (pseudo-random noise) at the input or use a higher-order loop.
- [ ] **Pitfall — Anti-Aliasing:** Although the digital filter eases the analog anti-aliasing requirement, a *modest* analog anti-alias filter is still required to suppress out-of-band noise that would otherwise alias into the baseband.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cathode Ray Oscilloscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cathode Ray Oscilloscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)** is a versatile electronic test and measurement instrument used to **visualize, measure, and analyze time-varying electrical signals** by displaying their voltage waveforms on a phosphor-coated screen as a function of time. It is the single most important diagnostic tool in any electronics laboratory because it allows direct observation of waveform shape, amplitude, frequency, and phase relationships with extremely high bandwidth.

The CRO consists of a **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** as its heart, combined with supporting circuitry: vertical deflection amplifier, horizontal deflection amplifier, time-base (sweep) generator, trigger/synchronization circuit, and regulated power supplies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a CRO as an **"Electronic X-Ray for signals"** — just as a doctor uses an X-ray to *see* the hidden structure of a broken bone, an engineer uses a CRO to *see* the hidden shape of an invisible electrical signal.

- The **electron gun** acts like a **spray-paint gun**: it shoots a focused beam of electrons (the "paint") toward a screen.
- The **deflection plates** act like **invisible hands pushing the paint stream** left-right and up-down using electric forces.
- The **phosphor screen** is like a **glowing canvas** that lights up wherever the electrons hit, and because it keeps glowing briefly (a property called *persistence*), the human eye sees a continuous line drawn in light.
- The **time-base generator** acts like an **invisible hand pulling the canvas sideways at a constant speed**, so the waveform is spread out across time just like a seismograph traces an earthquake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students are expected to master (i) the block diagram of a general-purpose CRO, (ii) detailed construction & working of the CRT, (iii) electrostatic deflection derivation, (iv) time-base and synchronization, (v) measurement of voltage/frequency/phase, and (vi) Lissajous figure patterns.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standards & Metrics)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| Accelerating anode voltage | $V_a$ | **1 kV to 25 kV** |
| Deflection plate voltage | $V_d$ | **0 V to ±300 V** |
| Phosphor persistence | — | **Short (P31), Medium (P2), Long (P7)** |
| CRT bandwidth (DC to) | — | **DC to 500 MHz** (typical lab CRO) |
| Vertical sensitivity | $S_v$ | **1 mV/div to 20 V/div** |
| Time-base range | — | **1 ns/div to 5 s/div** |
| Electron charge-to-mass ratio | $e/m$ | **1.759 × 10¹¹ C/kg** |
| Velocity of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of a General-Purpose CRO

The CRO is a multi-stage system. Each block has a strictly defined function. Understanding the block diagram is the single most important step before studying the CRT.

1. **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** — The display device; converts electrical signals into a visible trace.
2. **Vertical Amplifier (Y-amplifier)** — Amplifies the weak input signal to a level sufficient to drive the vertical deflection plates.
3. **Vertical Attenuator** — Steps down large input signals to a level the Y-amplifier can handle.
4. **Horizontal Amplifier (X-amplifier)** — Amplifies the time-base ramp before applying it to the horizontal plates.
5. **Time-Base Generator (Sweep Generator)** — Produces a sawtooth (ramp) voltage that linearly sweeps the electron beam from left to right across the screen.
6. **Trigger / Synchronization Circuit** — Locks the sweep to a precise point on the input signal so the trace is stable.
7. **Power Supply** — Provides regulated high voltage to the CRT and low voltages to amplifiers.
8. **Intensity & Focus Controls** — Adjust brightness and sharpness of the trace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** Always draw the block diagram in this order: **Input → Attenuator → Vertical Amplifier → Trigger pickoff → Sweep Generator → Horizontal Amplifier → Deflection Plates → Screen.** KTU examiners award full marks only if the arrow directions and labelling are correct.

## 2.2 Detailed Construction of the Cathode Ray Tube

The CRT is an evacuated glass envelope containing the following sub-assemblies:

### (a) Electron Gun Assembly
- **Cathode (K):** Indirectly heated oxide-coated cathode emitting electrons by thermionic emission.
- **Control Grid (G):** A cylinder with a small aperture held at a **negative potential** relative to cathode. Controls **beam intensity (brightness)**.
- **Focusing Anode (A₁) and Accelerating Anode (A₂):** Form an **electron lens** that accelerates and focuses the diverging electron stream into a fine, narrow beam at the screen.
- The pre-accelerating anode is typically held at **0 V to +50 V** (for focusing), and the accelerating anode at a high positive voltage of **+2 kV to +25 kV**.

### (b) Deflection Plate System
- Two pairs of parallel plates inside the CRT:
  - **Y-plates (vertical):** mounted horizontally, deflect beam up/down.
  - **X-plates (horizontal):** mounted vertically, deflect beam left/right.
- Plates are kept small in length to keep CRT short.

### (c) Phosphor Screen
- Inner face of the CRT, coated with phosphor (P1, P2, P31, P7, P11).
- When high-velocity electrons strike, **fluorescence** occurs (immediate light emission).
- **Phosphorescence** continues briefly after the beam moves on — this property is called **persistence**.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Examiner Tip:** When asked "What determines the colour of the trace?" — answer: **the type of phosphor used** (e.g., P31 = green, P7 = blue-white, P11 = blue).

## 2.3 Theory of Electrostatic Deflection

When a voltage $V_d$ is applied across the Y-plates separated by distance $d$ and of effective length $l$, an electric field is set up that deflects the electron beam.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $V_a$ | Accelerating anode voltage (V) |
| $V_d$ | Deflection plate voltage (V) |
| $l$ | Length of deflection plates (m) |
| $d$ | Separation between deflection plates (m) |
| $L$ | Distance from centre of plates to screen (m) |
| $D$ | Deflection on screen (m) |
| $S$ | Deflection sensitivity (m/V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the beam deflected?** An electron in a uniform electric field $E = V_d/d$ experiences a force $F = eE$ perpendicular to its motion, giving it transverse acceleration $a_y = eE/m = eV_d/(md)$. This is the core of *electrostatic* (as opposed to *magnetic*) deflection used in CRTs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{l L V_d}{2 d V_a}$ | Total electrostatic deflection on screen | m |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{D}{V_d} = \dfrac{l L}{2 d V_a}$ | Deflection sensitivity (per volt) | m/V |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{S_v} = \dfrac{2 d V_a}{l L}$ | Deflection factor (volts per metre) | V/m |
| 4 | $v_z = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V_a}{m}}$ | Axial velocity of electron after acceleration | m/s |
| 5 | $y_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} a t^2 = \dfrac{V_d \, l^2}{4 d V_a}$ | Deflection at the exit of plates | m |
| 6 | $f_y : f_x = N_x : N_y$ | Frequency ratio from Lissajous pattern | — |
| 7 | $\sin \phi = \dfrac{y_1}{y_2} = \dfrac{x_1}{x_2}$ | Phase difference from ellipse | — |
| 8 | $T = n \cdot t_{sw}$ | Period from time-base calculation | s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students often confuse **deflection sensitivity** (m/V) with **deflection factor** (V/m). The first is large when the system is sensitive; the second is large when the system is *less* sensitive. They are reciprocals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Visualizing modulated RF signals, eye diagrams in optical fibre links.
- **Power Electronics:** Observing switching transients in IGBTs, MOSFETs, and SCR inverters.
- **Medical Electronics:** Display of ECG, EEG, EMG waveforms after signal conditioning.
- **Automotive:** Diagnosing ignition pulses, fuel injector waveforms.
- **Education & Research:** The single most-used bench instrument in every electronics lab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Electrostatic Deflection Equation $D = \dfrac{l L V_d}{2 d V_a}$

### Step 1 — Acceleration of electrons inside the electron gun
The electron, of charge $e$ and mass $m$, is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V_a$ between cathode and anode. Using the work-energy theorem:

$$
\text{Work done by field} = \text{Kinetic energy gained}
$$

$$
e V_a = \frac{1}{2} m v_z^2
$$

Solving for the axial velocity $v_z$:

$$
v_z = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m}}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Every electron entering the deflection region has the *same* axial speed determined only by the anode voltage $V_a$. This is the constant horizontal speed of the "paint stream" mentioned in the analogy.

### Step 2 — Transverse acceleration inside the deflection plates
The deflecting electric field between the plates is uniform and equals $E = V_d / d$. The force on the electron perpendicular to its motion is:

$$
F_y = e E = \frac{e V_d}{d}
$$

By Newton's second law, the transverse acceleration is:

$$
a_y = \frac{F_y}{m} = \frac{e V_d}{m d}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** The plates act as a "sideways gravity" pulling the electron off-axis. Higher $V_d$ gives stronger sideways pull; larger $d$ weakens it.

### Step 3 — Transverse deflection $y_1$ at the *exit* of the plates
The electron spends time $t$ inside the plates of length $l$, moving with constant axial velocity $v_z$:

$$
t = \frac{l}{v_z}
$$

Substituting $v_z$:

$$
t = l \sqrt{\frac{m}{2 e V_a}}
$$

During this time, the electron starts with zero transverse velocity and accelerates sideways, so the displacement is given by kinematics:

$$
y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t^2
$$

Substituting $a_y$ and $t$:

$$
y_1 = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{e V_d}{m d}\right) \left(l^2 \frac{m}{2 e V_a}\right)
$$

Simplifying — the $e$ and $m$ cancel:

$$
y_1 = \frac{V_d \, l^2}{4 d V_a}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** This is the sideways displacement of the beam at the exact moment it leaves the plates.

### Step 4 — Additional deflection $y_2$ between plate exit and screen
After leaving the plates, the electron is no longer accelerated transversely. It travels in a *straight line* at the constant transverse velocity it gained inside the plates. That velocity is:

$$
v_y = a_y \, t = \left(\frac{e V_d}{m d}\right) \left(l \sqrt{\frac{m}{2 e V_a}}\right) = \frac{V_d \, l}{2 d V_a} \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m}}
$$

The remaining distance from the plate centre to the screen is $L$. The additional deflection is:

$$
y_2 = v_y \cdot \frac{L}{v_z} = \frac{V_d \, l \, L}{2 d V_a}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** After the plates, the beam behaves like a projectile leaving a ramp — it continues along the direction it was bent toward.

### Step 5 — Total deflection $D$ on the screen
Total deflection is the sum of deflection *during* the plates and *after* the plates:

$$
D = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{V_d \, l^2}{4 d V_a} + \frac{V_d \, l \, L}{2 d V_a}
$$

Factorising:

$$
D = \frac{V_d \, l}{2 d V_a} \left( \frac{l}{2} + L \right)
$$

For typical CRTs, $L \gg l/2$, so the $l/2$ term is negligible, giving the standard KTU result:

$$
D = \frac{l L V_d}{2 d V_a}
$$

**Final boxed KTU formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,D = \frac{l L V_d}{2 d V_a} \quad ; \quad S = \frac{D}{V_d} = \frac{l L}{2 d V_a}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** Deflection is proportional to $V_d$ (linear) and inversely proportional to $V_a$ (high $V_a$ means very fast electrons that "shoot past" the plates before being bent much). This is why, for a *given* $V_d$, increasing the brightness (anode voltage) reduces the apparent size of the waveform on screen.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Frequency Ratio from Lissajous Figures

A Lissajous pattern appears when sinusoidal voltages of *different* frequencies are applied to the X and Y inputs. The number of times the pattern touches a **horizontal tangent** line equals the frequency applied to the **Y** input ($f_y$), and the number of times it touches a **vertical tangent** line equals the frequency applied to the **X** input ($f_x$).

Consider the parametric equations of motion of the beam:

$$
x = X \sin(\omega_x t) \quad ; \quad y = Y \sin(\omega_y t + \phi)
$$

A vertical tangent occurs when the vertical velocity $\dot y$ is zero (the curve is "momentarily" moving horizontally at a top or bottom extremum). This happens at:

$$
\cos(\omega_y t + \phi) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_y t + \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \dots
$$

The time between two consecutive vertical tangencies is therefore $\Delta t_y = \pi / \omega_y$. Similarly for horizontal tangencies, $\Delta t_x = \pi / \omega_x$.

In one *complete* closed Lissajous pattern, the pattern contains $N_y$ vertical tangencies and $N_x$ horizontal tangencies. Equating the total time:

$$
N_y \cdot \frac{\pi}{\omega_y} = N_x \cdot \frac{\pi}{\omega_x}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_x}{N_y}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation Trick:** "The frequency is opposite to its tangent." $f_y$ corresponds to the number of **horizontal** tangencies $N_x$, and $f_x$ corresponds to the number of **vertical** tangencies $N_y$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solving Common Numerical Problems

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation | Direct Approach |
|--------------|-------|------|--------------|-----------------|
| Calculate screen deflection | $l, L, d, V_a, V_d$ | $D$ | $D = l L V_d / (2 d V_a)$ | Substitute directly in SI units. |
| Calculate sensitivity | $l, L, d, V_a$ | $S$ | $S = l L / (2 d V_a)$ | Compute in m/V, then convert to mm/V. |
| Frequency from Lissajous | $N_x, N_y, f_x$ | $f_y$ | $f_y = f_x \cdot N_x / N_y$ | Count tangencies carefully. |
| Phase from ellipse | $y_1, y_2$ or $x_1, x_2$ | $\phi$ | $\sin\phi = y_1/y_2 = x_1/x_2$ | Measure intercepts from screen. |
| Period from time-base | $n$ divisions, $t/div$ setting | $T$ | $T = n \cdot t_{div}$ | Multiply divisions by setting. |
| Frequency from period | $T$ | $f$ | $f = 1/T$ | Take reciprocal. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit discipline:** Always keep $l, L, d$ in **metres**; otherwise convert consistently. A common mistake is mixing mm and m, leading to answers off by a factor of 1000.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a General-Purpose CRO (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Y] --> B[Vertical Attenuator]
    B --> C[Vertical Amplifier]
    C --> D[Delay Line]
    D --> E[Y Deflection Plates]
    F[Trigger Pickoff] --> G[Trigger Circuit]
    G --> H[Time Base Generator]
    H --> I[Horizontal Amplifier]
    I --> J[X Deflection Plates]
    K[Power Supply HV] --> E
    K --> J
    K --> L[CRT Electron Gun and Screen]
    C --> L
    I --> L
    H --> M[Intensity and Focus Controls]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Phosphor Display Trace]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The input signal flows **vertically (Y)**, while the internally generated sawtooth flows **horizontally (X)**. The **trigger pickoff** taps a sample of the input and feeds it to the trigger circuit, which decides *when* to start the time-base ramp. The **delay line** ensures the leading edge of a fast pulse is visible (the trigger can start the ramp *before* the signal arrives at the Y-plates).

## 4.2 Internal Layout of the Cathode Ray Tube (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heater H] --> B[Cathode K]
    B --> C[Control Grid G]
    C --> D[Pre Accelerating Anode A1]
    D --> E[Accelerating Anode A2]
    E --> F[Y Deflection Plates]
    F --> G[X Deflection Plates]
    G --> H[Phosphor Coated Screen]
    H --> I[Fluorescent Trace on Screen]
```

**Sequence interpretation:** Electrons emitted by cathode K pass through the negative-biased grid G (intensity control), are pre-accelerated and focused at A1, fully accelerated at A2, deflected vertically by Y-plates, deflected horizontally by X-plates, and finally strike the screen to produce a luminous spot.

## 4.3 Lissajous Figure Patterns for Various Frequency Ratios

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Ratio 1:1 - Ellipse or Circle]
    R2[Ratio 1:2 - Figure of Eight Horizontal]
    R3[Ratio 2:1 - Figure of Eight Vertical]
    R4[Ratio 1:3 - Three Lobes Horizontal]
    R5[Ratio 3:1 - Three Lobes Vertical]
    R6[Ratio 2:3 - Complex Knotted Pattern]
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase identification (1:1 Lissajous):** Straight line at 45° → 0° or 180° phase. Circle → 90° phase. Tilted ellipse → other angles. The exact phase is found by measuring the intercepts $x_1, x_2$ and using $\sin\phi = x_1/x_2$.

## 4.4 Architecture of Time-Base Sawtooth Waveform (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UJT Relaxation Oscillator] --> B[Ramp Generator Capacitor Charging]
    B --> C[Linear Voltage Rise During Sweep]
    C --> D[Sudden Discharge to Zero]
    D --> E[Flyback - Blanking Pulse]
    E --> B
    F[Trigger Pulse Arrives] --> G[Time Base Restarts]
    G --> B
```

**Interpretation:** The UJT (Unijunction Transistor) repeatedly charges a capacitor through a constant current source. The voltage rises *linearly* with time. When the voltage reaches the UJT's peak-point voltage, the UJT fires and the capacitor discharges rapidly. This sudden discharge is the **flyback**. The trigger pulse synchronises the start of the next ramp to a chosen point on the input signal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** What is a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope? List any two advantages and one limitation.

**Model Answer:**
A CRO is an electronic instrument used to display the waveform of an electrical signal on a phosphor screen, with the horizontal axis representing time and the vertical axis representing voltage.

*Advantages:*
- It provides a direct visual representation of the signal waveform.
- It has very high input impedance, so it does not load the circuit under test.

*Limitation:*
- It cannot display signals with frequencies above its bandwidth limit, and it requires careful calibration.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What is meant by *deflection sensitivity* of a CRT? What is its SI unit?

**Model Answer:**
Deflection sensitivity is defined as the **screen deflection produced per unit deflecting voltage** applied to the deflection plates.

$$
S = \frac{D}{V_d} = \frac{l L}{2 d V_a}
$$

Its SI unit is **metre per volt (m/V)**. A higher value of $S$ means the CRT is more sensitive — a smaller deflecting voltage produces a larger visible displacement on the screen.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**Q(A). (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a general-purpose CRO. Briefly describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A general-purpose CRO is a multi-stage instrument built around the Cathode Ray Tube. The block diagram and functions are:

1. **Input Y:** The signal to be observed is fed to the vertical (Y) input.
2. **Vertical Attenuator:** A switched resistive divider that scales large signals to within the Y-amplifier's range (typically 1-2 V).
3. **Vertical Amplifier:** A high-gain, wide-bandwidth amplifier that raises the signal to a level sufficient to drive the Y-plates (typically tens to hundreds of volts).
4. **Delay Line:** A lumped-element delay line that introduces a fixed time delay (~200 ns) so the trigger can start the sweep *before* the signal reaches the Y-plates, ensuring the leading edge of fast pulses is visible.
5. **Trigger Pickoff & Circuit:** Samples a portion of the Y-signal and produces a sharp trigger pulse whenever the signal crosses a chosen threshold and slope.
6. **Time-Base Generator (Sweep):** Produces a linear sawtooth voltage whose rising edge sweeps the beam left-to-right. The trigger ensures the sweep starts at the same point on the input every cycle, giving a stable trace.
7. **Horizontal Amplifier:** Amplifies the sawtooth (or an external X-input) to drive the X-plates.
