8. **CRT with Power Supply:** Provides high voltage for the accelerating anode, heater voltage for the cathode, and the various bias voltages for the electron gun.

The simultaneous application of the Y-signal to vertical plates and the sawtooth to horizontal plates causes the beam to trace the input waveform on the phosphor screen.

---

**Q(A). (b)** Derive the expression for electrostatic deflection in a CRT. Define *deflection sensitivity* and *deflection factor*, and explain how the accelerating voltage affects the screen deflection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the kinetic energy gained by an electron accelerated through $V_a$:

$$
e V_a = \frac{1}{2} m v_z^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_z = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m}}
$$

The transverse electric field $E = V_d/d$ produces acceleration $a_y = eE/m = eV_d/(md)$. The electron spends time $t = l/v_z$ inside the plates, acquiring transverse displacement at exit:

$$
y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t^2 = \frac{V_d \, l^2}{4 d V_a}
$$

After exiting, the electron travels distance $L$ with transverse velocity $v_y = a_y t$, giving extra deflection:

$$
y_2 = v_y \cdot \frac{L}{v_z} = \frac{V_d \, l L}{2 d V_a}
$$

Total deflection:

$$
D = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{V_d \, l L}{2 d V_a} \quad \text{(since } L \gg l/2\text{)}
$$

**Deflection Sensitivity** $S$ is the screen deflection per unit deflecting voltage:

$$
S = \frac{D}{V_d} = \frac{l L}{2 d V_a} \quad \text{(units: m/V)}
$$

**Deflection Factor** is its reciprocal, $1/S$ (units: V/m).

**Effect of $V_a$:** Since $D \propto 1/V_a$, increasing the accelerating voltage makes the electron beam faster, harder to deflect, and produces a *smaller* deflection on the screen. Conversely, decreasing $V_a$ makes the trace larger but reduces brightness and worsens focus. Hence a careful balance is required in design.

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**Q(B). (a)** Explain the construction and working of a Cathode Ray Tube with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the control grid, focusing anode, and phosphor screen. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CRT is a sealed, evacuated glass tube with the following main parts:

1. **Electron Gun Assembly** (left side of CRT):
   - **Cathode (K):** Indirectly heated by a heater; emits electrons by thermionic emission.
   - **Control Grid (G):** A negatively-biased cylinder surrounding the cathode with a small hole for the electron stream. The magnitude of its negative voltage controls the **number of electrons** that pass through — i.e., it controls the **brightness (intensity)** of the trace. More negative grid voltage → dimmer trace.
   - **Focusing Anode (A₁):** An electrostatic lens that converges the diverging electrons into a narrow beam.
   - **Accelerating Anode (A₂):** Held at high positive voltage (+1 to +25 kV) to accelerate the beam to very high velocity so it strikes the screen with sufficient energy to produce fluorescence.

2. **Deflection Plate System** (middle of CRT):
   - Two pairs of plates: **Y-plates** (horizontal pair) produce vertical deflection; **X-plates** (vertical pair) produce horizontal deflection.
   - The plates are small in axial length and placed close to the screen-side of the gun.

3. **Phosphor Screen** (right side of CRT):
   - Inner face of the tube coated with phosphor (commonly P31 — green, or P11 — blue).
   - When high-energy electrons strike, the screen emits light (**fluorescence**).
   - Persistence (the time the screen continues to glow after excitation stops) is short for P31 (~40 µs), medium for P2 (~few hundred µs), long for P7 (~seconds).

**Role summary:**
- *Control Grid:* Beam intensity control.
- *Focusing Anode:* Produces a sharp, narrow spot.
- *Phosphor Screen:* Converts electron energy into visible light, providing the visual display.

---

**Q(B). (b)** A CRT has the following specifications: plate length $l = 2$ cm, distance from plate centre to screen $L = 30$ cm, plate separation $d = 0.5$ cm, and accelerating voltage $V_a = 2000$ V. If a sinusoidal signal of peak voltage 50 V is applied to the Y-plates, find (i) the deflection sensitivity, (ii) the peak-to-peak deflection on the screen, and (iii) the deflection factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $l = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m, $L = 30$ cm $= 0.30$ m, $d = 0.5$ cm $= 0.005$ m, $V_a = 2000$ V, $V_{peak} = 50$ V.

**(i) Deflection Sensitivity $S$:**

$$
S = \frac{l L}{2 d V_a} = \frac{(0.02)(0.30)}{2(0.005)(2000)}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.006}{20} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/V} = 0.3 \text{ mm/V}
$$

**(ii) Peak-to-Peak Deflection $D_{p-p}$:**

Peak deflection:

$$
D_{peak} = S \times V_{peak} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 50 = 0.015 \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ cm}
$$

Peak-to-peak deflection (since the signal swings from −50 V to +50 V):

$$
D_{p-p} = 2 \times D_{peak} = 3.0 \text{ cm} = 0.03 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Deflection Factor:**

$$
\frac{1}{S} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3333.3 \text{ V/m} = 3.33 \text{ V/mm}
$$

**Final Answers:** $S = 0.3$ mm/V, $D_{p-p} = 3$ cm, Deflection Factor = 3.33 V/mm.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner Reports — KTU 2023 & 2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in derivation:** Many students derive the deflection equation correctly but write the final answer as a dimensionless number. **Always specify m/V for sensitivity and V/m for deflection factor.**
> 2. **Confusing $N_x$ and $N_y$ in Lissajous problems:** Remember — **horizontal tangencies** correspond to $f_y$ (vertical input). Drawing a diagram with tangency lines is mandatory for full marks.
> 3. **Omitting the delay line** in the CRO block diagram: Examiners consistently deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Writing $D = l L V_d / (d V_a)$** (missing the factor of 2) — the most common algebraic error in the entire module.
> 5. **Mixing up time-base units:** $1\ \mu$s/div means each horizontal division on the screen corresponds to 1 microsecond of real signal time.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A CRO is fundamentally a **voltage-versus-time plotter** built around a CRT.
- The CRT contains a **cathode, control grid (intensity), focusing anode (focus), accelerating anode, two pairs of deflection plates, and a phosphor screen (display).**
- The electrostatic deflection formula is $D = l L V_d / (2 d V_a)$ — derivation steps must include energy balance, kinematics inside the plates, and projection beyond the plates.
- **Deflection sensitivity** $S = D/V_d$ (m/V) and **deflection factor** $1/S$ (V/m) are reciprocals; **confusing them is the #1 error in KTU exams.**
- **Time-base generator** produces a sawtooth ramp; its trigger-locked synchronisation provides a stable, non-flickering trace.
- **Lissajous patterns** are used for frequency ratio $f_y/f_x = N_x/N_y$ and phase measurement $\sin\phi = y_1/y_2$.
- **Phosphor choice** determines trace colour and persistence; P31 (green) is most common in general-purpose lab CROs.
- **Delay line** in the Y-path ensures visibility of leading edges of fast pulses.
- **Block-diagram memorisation** (attenuator → Y-amplifier → delay line → trigger → sweep → X-amplifier → plates → screen) is essential for full marks in 7-mark questions.
- **Real-world applications** include telecommunications, power electronics diagnostics, medical signal display, and embedded-systems debugging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Storage Oscilloscopes - DSO Applications, Method of Measuring Voltage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Digital Storage Oscilloscopes - DSO Applications, Method of Measuring Voltage ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition A **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)** is an electronic test instrument that captures, digitizes, stores, and displays repetitive or single-shot electrical waveforms on a raster-scanned display, using an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** and a digital memory buffer. Unlike analog oscilloscopes, a DSO performs all signal conditioning in the digital domain, enabling post-acquisition analysis, cursor-based measurement, automatic parameter extraction, and signal persistence. In the context of **voltage measurement**, the DSO samples an input analog voltage at discrete time intervals, quantizes each sample into a binary number with finite **vertical resolution (typically 8 bits, corresponding to $\frac{1}{256}$ of full scale ≈ 0.4 % per LSB)**, and reconstructs the waveform by reading the memory in synchronization with the time base. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Method of Measuring Voltage* in a DSO refers to three principal approaches — **(i) Direct Graticule/Cursor Method, (ii) Automatic Parameter Measurement (Built-in algorithms), and (iii) Statistical/Histogram-Based Methods** — each governed by the quantization step $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$ where $n$ is the ADC bit-depth. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are recording a live musical performance using a digital voice recorder instead of a tape recorder. The tape recorder captures a continuous, smooth magnetic trace (analog). The digital recorder, however, takes a tiny "snapshot" of the sound pressure 44,100 times every second, converts each snapshot into a number, and stores those numbers as a long list in memory. Later, the recorder plays the list back so fast that your ear cannot tell the difference. A **DSO does the same thing for voltage versus time** — it *samples* the voltage, *digitizes* it, *stores* it in RAM, and *reconstructs* it for the display. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Reinforcement:** The "voltage" in a DSO is to the **ADC** what "sound pressure" is to a **microphone** followed by an **audio codec**. The smaller the sampling interval ($\Delta t$) and the finer the quantization step ($\Delta V$), the closer the digital reconstruction matches the original continuous waveform. ### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards, and Standard Metrics - **ADC vertical resolution (n):** Standard DSOs offer **8-bit (256 levels)**, with high-end models providing **12-bit (4096 levels)** or **14-bit (16384 levels)** vertical resolution. - **Sampling rate ($f_s$):** Real-time sampling up to **5 GS/s** in mid-range bench-top DSOs; equivalent-time sampling extending to **100 GS/s** for repetitive signals. - **Record length:** From **1 kpts** to **1 Gpts** (giga-points) per channel. - **IEEE Std 1057** and **IEC 61010** govern DSO accuracy and safety compliance. - **Input impedance:** Standard **$1 \text{ M}\Omega \pm 1\%$** shunted by **$\approx 15 \text{ pF}$**. > [!TIP] > **For Exam Quick Recall:** A DSO's "voltage measurement accuracy" is fundamentally limited by **three factors only** — (1) ADC quantization, (2) probe attenuation calibration, and (3) DC gain accuracy. The exam will test you on how each contributes to the total error budget. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 DSO Block Architecture — Theoretical Breakdown A modern DSO consists of the following signal-flow stages: 1. **Attenuator / Input Front-End:** Selects the **VOLTS/DIV** range, typically in a 1-2-5 sequence (e.g., 1 mV, 2 mV, 5 mV … 5 V, 10 V per division). It scales the input voltage to within the ADC input range. 2. **Pre-Amplifier & DC Offset:** Provides variable gain and allows the displayed trace to be shifted vertically (DC offset). 3. **Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):** A Bessel or Butterworth filter with cutoff $f_c$ chosen such that $f_c \le \dfrac{f_s}{2.5}$ to prevent aliasing (Nyquist criterion). 4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Samples the conditioned analog signal at the sampling rate $f_s$ and produces a digital word of $n$ bits per sample. 5. **Acquisition Memory:** A dual-port RAM that stores the digital samples in real time and outputs them to the display processor simultaneously. 6. **Time Base & Trigger Circuitry:** Determines the **TIME/DIV** setting, generates the sample clock, and synchronizes the start of acquisition to a user-defined trigger event. 7. **Display Processor & DAC:** Reconstructs the waveform for the raster display and overlays grids, cursors, and automatic measurement readouts. > [!NOTE] > **Why an Anti-Aliasing Filter?** If the input contains frequency components above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist frequency), they "fold back" and appear as lower-frequency artifacts, corrupting the measurement. The AAF removes these components **before** digitization. ### 2.2 Method of Measuring Voltage — Operational Logic The DSO supports **three voltage measurement methodologies** that examiners frequently test: #### A. Direct Graticule Method (Manual) The user counts the number of vertical divisions between two points on the waveform and multiplies by the **VOLTS/DIV** switch setting: $$V_{pp} = (\text{Number of vertical divisions}) \times (\text{VOLTS/DIV setting})$$ This is the most basic method, limited by the user's visual judgment and the display resolution (typically 0.2 division parallax error). #### B. Cursor Method Two horizontal cursors (Y1 and Y2) are positioned at the desired voltage levels on the waveform. The DSO digitally computes: $$\Delta V = Y_2 - Y_1$$ The cursors eliminate parallax error and provide a digital readout with three to four significant figures. #### C. Automatic Parameter Measurement A built-in firmware algorithm scans the stored waveform data and extracts parameters such as: - **Peak-to-Peak Voltage ($V_{pp}$)** - **RMS Voltage ($V_{rms}$)** - **Average Voltage ($V_{avg}$)** - **Maximum / Minimum Voltage** - **High / Low Voltage (90 % / 10 % statistical levels)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When the question says *"Explain the method of measuring voltage using a DSO"*, the examiner expects a layered answer covering **(1) signal conditioning → (2) sampling/quantization → (3) computation via the formulas in §2.3 below**. Skipping the quantization step typically costs 2–3 marks. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition | |---|----------|---------|-------|----------------------------| | 1 | Quantization step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$ | Volts | $V_{FSR}$ = full-scale range; $n$ = ADC bits | | 2 | Quantization error (max) | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ | Volts | Theoretical maximum, uniform distribution | | 3 | RMS quantization noise | $e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts | For uniformly distributed white-noise model | | 4 | Peak-to-Peak voltage (sampled) | $V_{pp} = (N_{max} - N_{min}) \cdot \Delta V$ | Volts | $N_{max}, N_{min}$ = max/min ADC codes | | 5 | RMS voltage (sampled) | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$ | Volts | Discrete-time RMS over $N$ samples | | 6 | Average (DC) voltage | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i$ | Volts | Arithmetic mean over acquired record | | 7 | Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) | $\text{ENOB} = n - \log_2(\sigma_{total}/\sigma_{ideal})$ | bits | $\sigma$ = measured vs. ideal RMS noise | | 8 | SNR (ideal N-bit ADC) | $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | Sine-wave input assumption | | 9 | Sample-and-hold aperture | $t_{ap} \le \dfrac{1}{10\,f_{max}}$ | seconds | Minimizes aperture uncertainty | | 10 | Probe attenuation factor | $V_{actual} = k \cdot V_{displayed}$ | — | $k = 1, 10, 100$ for $\times 1, \times 10, \times 100$ probes | | 11 | Vertical sensitivity (resolution) | $S_V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{N_{div}}$ | V/div | $N_{div}$ = active vertical divisions (typically 8) | | 12 | DC accuracy | $\text{Acc}_{DC} = \pm(\%\text{ of reading} + \%\text{ of range})$ | — | Typically $\pm(0.5\% + 0.1\% \text{FS})$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The same $V_{rms}$ formula is used in **power quality analyzers, audio metering (VU/PPM), and true-RMS multimeters** — but a DSO additionally preserves **phase and waveform shape** information, making it the only instrument that can measure $V_{rms}$ on a *non-sinusoidal* distorted waveform correctly (unlike average-responding meters). ### 2.4 DSO Applications — The "Why" and the "How" The DSO has become the **universal workhorse** of electronic measurement because it offers the simultaneous visualization of *amplitude*, *time*, *frequency*, and *phase*. Key KTU-relevant applications are: - **Transient Capture:** Capturing one-time events (e.g., power-on surge, ESD pulse) using **single-shot mode** with pre-trigger memory. - **Bus Protocol Decode:** Triggering on and decoding I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, USB, and Ethernet signals. - **Power Electronics Debugging:** Measuring switching losses, SOA of MOSFETs, and gate-drive timing. - **Spectrum Analysis (via FFT):** Built-in Fast Fourier Transform displays the frequency spectrum — ideal for harmonic distortion analysis. - **Jitter and Eye-Diagram Analysis:** Critical for high-speed digital design (PCIe, DDR, SERDES). - **Automated Production Testing:** With SCPI/LXI command sets, DSOs integrate into ATE racks. - **Education and Training:** The DSO's ability to **freeze** a waveform, **zoom**, and **save** has made it indispensable in B.Tech labs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Quantization Step and Theoretical Maximum Error **Given:** An ADC with $n$ bits quantizing a full-scale input range $V_{FSR}$. **Step 1 — Total number of discrete codes:** The ADC produces $2^n$ distinct output levels spanning the input range. $$L_{total} = 2^n$$ **Step 2 — Width of one quantization level (LSB size):** The full-scale range is divided into $2^n$ equal sub-ranges, each of width $\Delta V$: $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum quantization error:** The actual analog input may lie anywhere within a given sub-range. The maximum deviation of the quantized value from the true value is half the LSB width: $$e_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^{n+1}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU typical value):** For an 8-bit ADC with $V_{FSR} = 10 \text{ V}$: $$\Delta V = \dfrac{10}{2^8} = \dfrac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} \approx 39.06 \text{ mV}$$ $$e_{q,max} = \pm 19.53 \text{ mV}$$ > **Interpretation:** Even with a perfect 8-bit ADC, the smallest voltage change the DSO can theoretically resolve is approximately **39 mV** on a 10 V full-scale range, and any reading can be off by up to **±19.5 mV** purely due to quantization. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — RMS Noise from Quantization **Given:** The quantization error is modeled as a uniformly distributed random variable in the interval $\left[-\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}, \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}\right]$. **Step 1 — Probability density function:** The uniform distribution has a constant probability density: $$p(e) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta V} \quad \text{for} \quad e \in \left[-\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}, \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Mean square value of the error:** The RMS noise is the square root of the mean square value: $$e_{q,rms}^2 = \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{\Delta V/2} e^2 \cdot p(e)\, de$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:** $$e_{q,rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\Delta V} \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{\Delta V/2} e^2\, de = \dfrac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \left[\dfrac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta V/2}^{\Delta V/2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the limits:** $$e_{q,rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \left[\dfrac{(\Delta V/2)^3}{3} - \dfrac{(-\Delta V/2)^3}{3}\right] = \dfrac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \dfrac{2\,(\Delta V/2)^3}{3}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify:** $$e_{q,rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \dfrac{2\,\Delta V^3}{24} = \dfrac{\Delta V^2}{12}$$ **Step 6 — Final result:** $$\boxed{e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}}$$ **Numerical check (same 8-bit, 10 V example):** $$e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{0.0390625}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{0.0390625}{3.4641} \approx 11.27 \text{ mV}$$ > **Interpretation:** The RMS quantization noise is **smaller** than the peak quantization error (11.27 mV vs 19.53 mV), confirming that the peak error is a worst-case bound while the RMS value represents the *typical* uncertainty over many samples. ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Discrete-Time RMS Voltage from Sampled Data **Given:** A DSO acquires $N$ samples $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_N$ at sampling rate $f_s$. **Step 1 — Continuous-time RMS definition:** For a periodic signal $v(t)$ with period $T$: $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$$ **Step 2 — Discretize the integral:** Replace the integral by a Riemann sum with sample interval $\Delta t = 1/f_s$: $$V_{rms} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N\Delta t}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v^2(t_i)\cdot\Delta t} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$$ **Step 3 — Final discrete-time RMS formula:** $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$$ **Numerical worked example:** Suppose a DSO captures $N = 1000$ samples of a 50 Hz sine wave, peak amplitude 5 V, with samples (in volts) listed below in a small subset: $$v = \{0.00, 1.56, 2.99, 4.23, 4.99, 4.99, 4.23, 2.99, 1.56, 0.00, \ldots\}$$ For a **pure sinusoid** with peak $V_p = 5 \text{ V}$, the analytical RMS value is: $$V_{rms,ideal} = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}} = 3.5355 \text{ V}$$ The DSO's discrete calculation converges to this value as $N \to \infty$ and as the sampling rate satisfies the Nyquist criterion $f_s \ge 2 f_{signal} = 100 \text{ Hz}$. ### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Theoretical SNR of an Ideal N-Bit ADC **Step 1 — Start from the RMS quantization noise formula:** $$e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}$$ **Step 2 — Express in terms of full-scale range:** $$e_{q,rms} = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n \sqrt{12}}$$ **Step 3 — RMS value of a full-scale sine wave input:** $$V_{sig,rms} = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):** $$\text{SQNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{sig,rms}}{e_{q,rms}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{FSR}/(2\sqrt{2})}{V_{FSR}/(2^n\sqrt{12})}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the argument:** $$\dfrac{V_{FSR}/(2\sqrt{2})}{V_{FSR}/(2^n\sqrt{12})} = \dfrac{2^n \sqrt{12}}{2\sqrt{2}} = 2^n \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{12}}{2\sqrt{2}} = 2^n \cdot \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} = 2^n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute and expand using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$:** $$\text{SQNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(2^n \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right) = 20 n \log_{10}(2) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Final compact formula:** $$\boxed{\text{SQNR (dB)} = 6.02\,n + 1.76}$$ **Numerical check (8-bit ADC):** $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$ This is the textbook figure — a perfect 8-bit ADC yields approximately **50 dB** of dynamic range for a full-scale sine input. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Voltage Measurement Method Selection | Criterion | Graticule Method | Cursor Method | Automatic Measurement | |-----------|------------------|---------------|----------------------| | User skill required | High | Moderate | Low | | Accuracy | $\pm 2\text{–}5\%$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ | $\pm 0.1\%$ (typical) | | Speed of result | Slow | Moderate | Instantaneous | | Susceptible to parallax | Yes | No | No | | Needs trigger stability | No | No | Yes (for periodic sig.) | | Best use case | Quick field check | Lab verification | Production/ATE | | Affected by quantization | Indirectly | Indirectly | Directly (algorithm-based) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Which method is most accurate?"*, the answer is **Automatic Parameter Measurement**, because it operates on the raw digitized samples in memory and applies firmware algorithms to mitigate noise (averaging, interpolation between samples). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DSO Signal-Chain Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Signal
Vin] --> B[Attenuator
VOLTS per DIV] B --> C[Pre-Amplifier
and DC Offset] C --> D[Anti-Aliasing
Low-Pass Filter] D --> E[Sample-and-Hold
Circuit] E --> F[ADC
n-bit quantizer] F --> G[Acquisition Memory
RAM buffer] G --> H[Display Processor
plus Reconstruction DAC] H --> I[Raster Display
LCD screen] J[Time Base
and Trigger] --> E J --> G K[User Interface
Cursors / Auto-Measure] --> H L[Firmware Engine
RMS / Vpp / Vavg Algorithms] --> M[Numerical Readout
on Display] H --> L style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style E fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style G fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style H fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style I fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style J fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000 style K fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style L fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style M fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 DSO Voltage-Measurement Methodology Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN[Input Stage] I1[Analog Voltage Input] I2[VOLTS per DIV Attenuator] I1 --> I2 end subgraph DIG[Digital Conversion] D1[Sample-and-Hold] D2[ADC Quantization] D3[Memory Storage] D1 --> D2 --> D3 end subgraph METH[Measurement Methods] M1[Method 1: Graticule
Count divisions x VOLTS per DIV] M2[Method 2: Cursor Method
Delta V = Y2 minus Y1] M3[Method 3: Automatic
Firmware algorithm] end subgraph OUT[Output] O1[Numeric Voltage Readout
Vpp, Vrms, Vavg] end I2 --> D1 D3 --> M1 D3 --> M2 D3 --> M3 M1 --> O1 M2 --> O1 M3 --> O1 style IN fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style DIG fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style METH fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style OUT fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 DSO Application Landscape — Application Map ```mermaid mindmap root((DSO Applications)) Time Domain Transient Capture Single Shot Events Edge Timing Analysis Frequency Domain FFT Spectrum Analysis Harmonic Distortion Filter Response Digital Systems Bus Protocol Decode Eye Diagram Analysis Jitter Measurement Power Electronics Switching Loss SOA Verification Gate Drive Timing Education Lab Demonstrations Frozen Waveform Review Virtual Hands On Production Automated Testing SCPI and LXI Control Pass Fail Testing ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** > **Q1.** Define a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO). List any two advantages it has over a conventional analog oscilloscope. **Model Answer:** A Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) is an instrument that **samples** an input analog signal, **converts** it to digital form using an ADC, **stores** the samples in a memory buffer, and **reconstructs** the waveform on a raster display. **Two advantages over analog CRO:** 1. **Storage and playback:** Waveforms can be stored indefinitely and reviewed/analyzed after the input signal is removed — useful for **single-shot transients** that the analog CRO would lose. 2. **Advanced signal processing:** Built-in FFT, automatic parameter measurements (Vpp, Vrms, frequency, rise time), and cursor-based digital readouts eliminate parallax error and enable precise measurements. --- > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** > **Q2.** What is quantization error? Derive the maximum quantization error of an N-bit ADC. **Model Answer:** Quantization error is the difference between the actual analog input voltage and its digitized value, arising because the ADC represents a continuous range of voltages by a finite set of discrete codes. The full-scale range $V_{FSR}$ is divided into $2^N$ levels of width: $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^N}$$ The maximum quantization error is half the LSB: $$e_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N+1}}$$ ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(Module 4, 14 Marks)* #### **Question A** **(a)** Explain the block diagram of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Block | Function | |-------|----------| | **Input Attenuator** | Switched-capacitor or resistive divider network that scales the input voltage to match the ADC input range. Selected by the **VOLTS/DIV** knob in 1-2-5 sequence. | | **Preamplifier & DC Offset** | Variable-gain amplifier that sets the vertical sensitivity; the DC offset shifts the trace up/down for display centering. | | **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | Low-pass filter (Bessel response) preventing frequencies above $f_s/2$ from entering the ADC and causing aliasing. | | **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** | Holds the input voltage constant during the ADC's aperture (conversion) time; the aperture uncertainty is critical for high-frequency signals. | | **ADC** | Converts the held analog sample to an $n$-bit digital word at the sampling rate $f_s$. | | **Acquisition Memory** | High-speed dual-port RAM storing the digitized samples in real time. | | **Time Base & Trigger** | Generates the sample clock and the trigger pulse that synchronizes the start of acquisition. | | **Display Processor & DAC** | Reads memory and reconstructs the waveform on a raster display (LCD), drawing grid, cursors, and readouts. | **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **method of measuring voltage** in a DSO using cursor and automatic measurement techniques. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Cursor Method:** 1. The user activates two horizontal cursors (Y1 and Y2) on the DSO display. 2. Each cursor is moved vertically until it intersects the waveform at the desired voltage levels. 3. The DSO firmware computes the difference $\Delta V = Y_2 - Y_1$ and displays it numerically. 4. The reading is unaffected by display parallax and is accurate to the ADC's LSB size. **Automatic Measurement Method:** 1. The firmware scans the entire acquisition memory to find the maximum and minimum sample values $v_{max}$ and $v_{min}$. 2. It computes **$V_{pp} = v_{max} - v_{min}$**. 3. It squares each sample, computes the mean, and takes the square root to obtain **$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum v_i^2}$**. 4. The arithmetic mean gives **$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{N}\sum v_i$**. 5. For periodic signals, the trigger ensures that $N$ is an integer number of cycles, eliminating spectral leakage in the RMS calculation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (b):** Students frequently write only the formula $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum v_i^2}$ without explaining **how the DSO obtains the samples** ($v_i$ values). Always state that the DSO reads these directly from the acquisition memory after ADC quantization, and mention that **pre-trigger and post-trigger memory** organization enables the capture of events before the trigger point — a frequent follow-up question. --- #### **Question B** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Discuss the major **applications of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope** in modern electronic measurement. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Single-shot transient capture:** DSOs can record events that occur only once (e.g., a power-on inrush, a relay contact bounce) by using **single-shot trigger mode** with pre-trigger memory. Analog CROs cannot do this. 2. **Repetitive signal analysis:** For periodic signals, equivalent-time sampling allows effective sampling rates up to 100 GS/s, far exceeding real-time ADC limits. 3. **Digital bus decoding:** Built-in protocol analyzers decode I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, LIN, USB, and Ethernet frames directly from the captured waveform. 4. **Spectrum analysis via FFT:** Real-time FFT displays the frequency-domain content, enabling measurement of harmonic distortion, SNR, and EMI emissions. 5. **Power electronics debugging:** Switching loss, dv/dt, di/dt, gate-drive timing, and safe operating area (SOA) verification are all measurable in a single DSO screen. 6. **Eye-diagram and jitter analysis:** Critical for high-speed serial data link compliance (PCIe, USB 3.0, SATA, DDR). 7. **Automated production testing:** Modern DSOs offer SCPI/LXI remote control for rack-mount ATE integration. 8. **Education:** Frozen waveforms can be saved, annotated, and replayed — invaluable in B.Tech labs. **(b)** A DSO uses an **8-bit ADC** with a full-scale input range of **$\pm 5 \text{ V}$**. Determine: (i) the quantization step (LSB size), (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $n = 8 \text{ bits}$, $V_{FSR} = 10 \text{ V}$ (since range is $\pm 5 \text{ V}$). **(i) Quantization step (LSB):** $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{2^8} = \dfrac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} = 39.0625 \text{ mV}$$ **(ii) Maximum quantization error:** $$e_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{0.0390625}{2} = \pm 0.01953125 \text{ V} = \pm 19.53 \text{ mV}$$ **(iii) Theoretical SQNR:** Using the standard formula for an ideal N-bit ADC with a full-scale sine input: $$\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$ **Conclusion:** The DSO's 8-bit quantization limits the dynamic range to about **50 dB**, and any reading can be off by up to **±19.5 mV** due to quantization alone. Probe and gain errors will add to this baseline. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (b):** A common error is to compute $\Delta V$ using only **5 V** (the positive half-range) instead of **10 V** (the full peak-to-peak range). Always read carefully: *full-scale range* means the **total** input range (i.e., $+\text{max} - (-\text{max})$). For a $\pm 5 \text{ V}$ input, $V_{FSR} = 10 \text{ V}$. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] A **DSO** digitizes, stores, and reconstructs waveforms; the key components are **attenuator → S/H → ADC → memory → display processor**. - [x] **Quantization step** $\Delta V = V_{FSR}/2^n$ defines the smallest resolvable voltage change; for a typical 8-bit ADC this is **~39 mV** on a 10 V range. - [x] **Maximum quantization error** is $\pm \Delta V/2$; the **RMS quantization noise** is $\Delta V/\sqrt{12}$. - [x] **Three voltage measurement methods:** (1) Graticule, (2) Cursor, (3) Automatic. Automatic is the most accurate. - [x] **Theoretical SNR** for an N-bit ADC with a full-scale sine input is **$\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU exams. - [x] **Anti-aliasing filter** must limit input bandwidth to $f_s/2$ (Nyquist) to prevent false lower-frequency artifacts. - [x] **DC accuracy** is specified as $\pm(\%\text{ of reading} + \%\text{ of full scale})$ — e.g., $\pm(0.5\% + 0.1\% \text{FS})$ for a typical bench DSO. - [x] **Probe attenuation** ($1\times, 10\times, 100\times$) must be set correctly on the DSO menu; otherwise readings will be wrong by the corresponding factor. - [x] **DSO applications** include single-shot capture, FFT spectrum analysis, bus decoding, power-electronics debug, eye-diagram analysis, and automated test. - [x] **Single-shot bandwidth** is always lower than the **repetitive bandwidth**, because real-time sampling limits apply only to one acquisition. - [x] **Pre-trigger memory** allows viewing the waveform *before* the trigger event — a key advantage over analog CROs. - [x] For RMS measurement of a non-sinusoidal waveform, only a **true-RMS** DSO/algorithm (operating on sampled data) gives the correct answer; average-responding meters will be wrong.

topics.title: Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Current" in Instrumentation?

In the context of **Electrical and Electronic Instrumentation**, the term **"Current"** refers not merely to the fundamental physical quantity of electric current, but to the entire engineering discipline of **measuring electric current** in circuits, systems, and industrial processes. It encompasses the principles, transducers, and instruments used to convert current into a readable, recordable, or processable form.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Current** is the time rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor or circuit element. In the **SI system**, its unit is the **Ampere (A)**, defined as one Coulomb per second ($1\,A = 1\,C/s$). In instrumentation, current is treated as a **measurable process variable** that may be either **Direct Current (DC)** — unidirectional, or **Alternating Current (AC)** — sinusoidal with a definite RMS value.

Mathematically, the instantaneous current is expressed as:

$$i(t) = \frac{dq(t)}{dt}$$

where $q(t)$ is the instantaneous charge in Coulombs and $t$ is time in seconds.

For a conductor with charge carriers of density $n$, charge $e$, drift velocity $v_d$, and cross-sectional area $A$:

$$I = n \cdot e \cdot A \cdot v_d$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Pipe Analogy:** Imagine a water pipe carrying water. The **amount of water flowing past a point per second** is analogous to electric current. The **pressure** pushing the water corresponds to **voltage**, and the **pipe's narrowness/friction** corresponds to **resistance**. An ammeter is like a **flow meter** clamped onto the pipe — it measures how much water (charge) is moving per second, without (ideally) obstructing the flow.

This is why an ammeter is always connected in **series** — it must be in the direct path of the flow, just like a flow meter must be inserted into the pipe.

### Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Measurement Units for Current:**
> - **Ampere (A)** — SI base unit
> - **Milliampere (mA)** = $10^{-3}\,A$
> - **Microampere (µA)** = $10^{-6}\,A$
> - **Kiloampere (kA)** = $10^{3}\,A$ (used in heavy industrial measurement)
> - **Standard Reference:** The SI definition of Ampere is fixed via the **elementary charge** $e = 1.602176634 \times 10^{-19}\,C$ since the 2019 SI redefinition.

### Why Current Measurement Matters in Instrumentation

- **Safety monitoring** in power systems
- **Process control** in electroplating, battery management, electrolysis
- **Protection** via relays triggered by overcurrent
- **Calibration** of power meters, energy meters
- **Feedback loop** variable in regulated power supplies

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Current Signals

Instrumentation engineers classify current by two orthogonal dimensions: **waveform type** and **magnitude range**.

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|----------------------|------|-------------|
| Direction | **DC (Direct Current)** | Unidirectional, constant or slowly varying |
| Direction | **AC (Alternating Current)** | Periodically reversing; quantified by RMS value |
| Magnitude | **Small Current** (µA–mA) | Sensitive galvanometers, biomedical |
| Magnitude | **Medium Current** (A) | Panel ammeters, CTs |
| Magnitude | **Large Current** (kA) | Bus-bars in substations, Rogowski coils |

## 2.2 Current Measurement — Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Ammeter (DC Only)

The PMMC instrument is the workhorse of **DC current measurement**. It exploits the force between a current-carrying coil and a permanent magnetic field.

- A rectangular coil of $N$ turns, area $A$, carrying current $I$ sits in a uniform radial magnetic field $B$.
- Torque produced: $T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$
- Restoring torque from a spring: $T_c = K \cdot \theta$
- At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$, giving the deflection angle:

$$\theta = \frac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K} \cdot I$$

This is a **linear** relationship — a major advantage of PMMC instruments.

### 2.2.2 Moving Iron (MI) Ammeter (AC/DC)

- Operates on the principle that a piece of soft iron experiences a force when placed in a magnetic field.
- For **attraction type**, the iron is drawn into the coil; for **repulsion type**, two iron vanes inside the coil repel each other.
- The force is proportional to $I^2$ (or $i^2$ for instantaneous), making it usable for both DC and AC.
- Because the deflection depends on $I^2$, the **scale is non-linear** (compressed at low values, expanded at high values).

### 2.2.3 Electrodynamometer Ammeter

- Has **two coils** — a fixed (current) coil and a moving (potential) coil.
- Operates on the **multiplication of two magnetic fields**.
- For ammeter use, both coils carry the same current; torque is proportional to $I^2$.
- More accurate than MI for AC, but more expensive.

### 2.2.4 Current Transformer (CT) — For High AC Current

A CT is a **step-down transformer** used to measure large AC currents safely. The primary has few turns (often just $1$), and the secondary has many turns.

$$\frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} = \text{Transformation Ratio } (K_n)$$

The secondary current is always standardized to **5 A** (or 1 A) for metering, regardless of the primary current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAFETY CRITICAL RULE:** A CT secondary must **NEVER be open-circuited** while the primary is energized. An open secondary produces dangerously high voltages that can destroy the CT and injure personnel. A **shorting switch** is always provided.

### 2.2.5 Shunt Resistor for DC Current Measurement

A **shunt** is a low-value, high-precision resistor connected in **parallel** with a moving-coil movement to extend its range.

$$I_{sh} = I \cdot \frac{R_m}{R_{sh} + R_m}$$

$$I_m = I \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_m}$$

where:
- $I$ = total current to be measured
- $I_m$ = current through the meter movement
- $I_{sh}$ = current through the shunt
- $R_m$ = meter movement resistance
- $R_{sh}$ = shunt resistance

### 2.2.6 Hall Effect Current Sensor

When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the current, a **Hall voltage** is generated perpendicular to both:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, and $t$ is the thickness of the Hall element.

- **Non-contact** measurement
- Measures both **AC and DC**
- Galvanically isolated — excellent for high-voltage systems

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all key formulas** you must memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic. Pay special attention to units and the conditions of applicability.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Condition |
|---|-------------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Instantaneous Current | $i(t) = dq(t)/dt$ | A (C/s) |
| 2 | Drift Current | $I = n \cdot e \cdot A \cdot v_d$ | A |
| 3 | RMS Current (Sinusoidal) | $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$ | A |
| 4 | Average Current (Half-wave rectified) | $I_{avg} = I_m / \pi$ | A |
| 5 | Form Factor | $K_f = I_{rms} / I_{avg}$ | Dimensionless (≈1.11 for sine) |
| 6 | Crest / Peak Factor | $K_p = I_m / I_{rms}$ | Dimensionless (≈1.414 for sine) |
| 7 | PMMC Deflection Angle | $\theta = (N B A / K) \cdot I$ | radians |
| 8 | Shunt Resistor (Range Multiplier) | $R_{sh} = R_m / (m - 1)$ | Ω, where $m = I / I_m$ |
| 9 | Multiplier Ratio $m$ | $m = I / I_{fsd}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | CT Transformation Ratio | $K_n = I_p / I_s = N_s / N_p$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | CT Turns Ratio (Inverse) | $N_p / N_s = I_s / I_p$ | — |
| 12 | CT Burden Voltage | $V_b = I_s \cdot Z_b$ | V |
| 13 | Hall Voltage | $V_H = (R_H \cdot I \cdot B) / t$ | V |
| 14 | Energy in Conductor | $W = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$ | J (Joule's heating) |
| 15 | Power Dissipated | $P = I^2 \cdot R = V \cdot I$ | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION NOTE:** In exam answers, the magnitude of a quantity is written using $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value. When using tables, write $\vert I \vert$ or $\vert V \vert$ — never use the bare pipe character, as it breaks the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Current Measurement Role |
|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Power Grids | CTs feed protective relays & revenue meters |
| Battery Management Systems (BMS) | Hall sensors monitor cell currents |
| Medical Electronics | Microamp ammeters for ECG/EEG bio-currents |
| Welding Industry | Rogowski coils for kA pulsed currents |
| Aerospace | Hall sensors for telemetry, isolated sensing |
| Electric Vehicles | Shunt resistors and Hall ICs for traction current |
| PCB Testing | Clamp meters for non-intrusive diagnostics |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical derivations** that are central to solving numerical problems in the KTU ESE. Each step is fully written out — no shortcuts, no skipped logic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Shunt Resistance for a DC Ammeter

### Problem Setup

A PMMC ammeter has a full-scale deflection (FSD) current of $I_m = 50\,\mu A$ and an internal resistance $R_m = 1000\,\Omega$. We need to extend its range to measure a maximum current of $I = 1\,A$.

**Step 1: Define the multiplier $m$.**
The multiplier is the ratio of the new full-scale current to the original full-scale current.

$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{1\,A}{50 \times 10^{-6}\,A} = 20{,}000$$

**Step 2: Write the circuit equations.**
The shunt $R_{sh}$ is in parallel with the meter movement. The voltage across both must be equal.

$$V = I_m \cdot R_m = I_{sh} \cdot R_{sh}$$

**Step 3: Express shunt current in terms of total current.**
By Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):

$$I = I_m + I_{sh} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{sh} = I - I_m$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the voltage equality.**

$$I_m \cdot R_m = (I - I_m) \cdot R_{sh}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $R_{sh}$.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m} = \frac{R_m}{\frac{I}{I_m} - 1} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

**Step 6: Plug in the numerical values.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{1000\,\Omega}{20{,}000 - 1} = \frac{1000}{19{,}999} \approx 0.05000\,\Omega$$

**Step 7: Verify with an alternative form.**
An equivalent, often-quoted formula is:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I - I_m} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 1000}{1 - (50 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$R_{sh} = \frac{0.05}{0.99995} \approx 0.050003\,\Omega$$

Both expressions match — the design is consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ratio and Phase Error of a Current Transformer

### Statement

For an ideal CT, the secondary current $I_s$ is in exact proportion (and exact phase) to the primary current $I_p$. In a **real CT**, two errors arise:
- **Ratio Error:** The magnitude of $I_s$ deviates from the ideal.
- **Phase Error:** The angle between $I_p$ and $I_s$ (reversed) is non-zero.

**Step 1: Write the magnetomotive force (MMF) balance.**
For the core with $N_p$ primary turns and $N_s$ secondary turns:

$$N_p \cdot I_p - N_s \cdot I_s = N_p \cdot I_0$$

where $I_0$ is the **exciting (magnetizing) current** that produces the core flux.

**Step 2: Solve for $I_s$ in terms of $I_p$ and $I_0$.**

$$I_s = \frac{N_p \cdot I_p - N_p \cdot I_0}{N_s} = \frac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot I_p - \frac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot I_0$$

**Step 3: Define the nominal ratio.**

$$K_n = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_p}{N_s} = \frac{1}{K_n}$$

So:

$$I_s = \frac{I_p}{K_n} - \frac{I_0}{K_n}$$

**Step 4: Define the actual ratio $R$.**

$$R = \frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{K_n \cdot I_p}{I_p - I_0} = \frac{K_n}{1 - I_0/I_p}$$

**Step 5: Define the ratio error.**

$$\text{Ratio Error} = \frac{R - K_n}{K_n} = \frac{K_n/(1 - I_0/I_p) - K_n}{K_n} = \frac{I_0 / I_p}{1 - I_0 / I_p} \approx \frac{I_0}{I_p}$$

(The last approximation is valid when $I_0 \ll I_p$, as in well-designed CTs.)

**Step 6: Phase error.**
The magnetizing current $I_0$ has two components:
- Core-loss component $I_w$ (in phase with induced EMF)
- Magnetizing component $I_\mu$ (lagging induced EMF by 90°)

The phase error is approximately:

$$\theta \approx \frac{I_w}{N_p \cdot I_p} \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Multiplier Resistor for a DC Voltmeter (Analogous Logic)

Although the *topic* is current, the dual problem (voltmeter range extension) often appears in **linked questions** to test the student's grasp of the basic principle. We include it for completeness.

**Given:** PMMC movement with $I_{fsd} = 1\,mA$, $R_m = 50\,\Omega$. Find the multiplier resistor $R_s$ to make it a $0–10\,V$ voltmeter.

**Step 1:** Full-scale voltage across the movement:

$$V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 50 = 0.05\,V$$

**Step 2:** Voltage to be dropped across the multiplier:

$$V_s = V - V_m = 10 - 0.05 = 9.95\,V$$

**Step 3:** Multiplier resistance (the multiplier carries the same FSD current):

$$R_s = \frac{V_s}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{9.95}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 9950\,\Omega$$

**Step 4:** General formula:

$$R_s = R_m \cdot (m_v - 1) \quad \text{where} \quad m_v = \frac{V}{V_m}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Current Measuring Instrument

| Situation | Best Choice | Why |
|-----------|------------|-----|
| Small DC current (µA), high accuracy | PMMC Ammeter | Linear scale, high sensitivity |
| AC current, general purpose | Moving Iron Ammeter | Cheap, AC + DC usable |
| AC current, high accuracy | Electrodynamometer | True RMS response |
| Very large AC current (kA) | Current Transformer | Isolation + scaling |
| Pulsed / high di/dt currents | Rogowski Coil | Wide bandwidth, no saturation |
| DC + AC, isolated, compact | Hall Effect Sensor | Galvanic isolation |
| Battery current (DC, isolated) | Hall IC (e.g., ACS712) | Integrated, low loss |
| Non-intrusive AC measurement | Clamp Meter (CT based) | No need to break circuit |
| True RMS in distorted waveforms | Thermal Converter / True RMS IC | Captures harmonics |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This question bank mirrors the **actual structure of the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE)** as per the 2024 Scheme regulations. Each question is mapped to a Course Outcome (CO) and a Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**State and explain the working principle of a Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument as an ammeter. Mention its key advantages and limitations.** *(3 Marks, CO1, Bloom: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
A PMMC ammeter works on the principle of the **force acting on a current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field**. The coil, wound on a rectangular former, is suspended in the uniform radial field of a permanent magnet. When current flows through the coil, a **deflecting torque** $T_d = N B I A$ is produced, which is opposed by the **controlling torque** of the spring $T_c = K \theta$. At equilibrium, the pointer deflects by an angle $\theta$ proportional to $I$, giving a **linear scale**.

**Advantages:** high accuracy, linear scale, low power consumption, high sensitivity.
**Limitations:** usable **only for DC**, cannot withstand overloads, more expensive than MI type.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**Why must a Current Transformer (CT) secondary never be open-circuited while the primary is energized? What protective device is used in CT secondary circuits?** *(3 Marks, CO2, Bloom: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Answer:**
When a CT secondary is open-circuited, the entire primary MMF acts as **magnetizing MMF** (since there is no opposing secondary MMF). This drives the core into **deep saturation**, producing:
1. **Dangerously high secondary voltage** (can reach kV), risking insulation failure and **electric shock to personnel**.
2. **Severe core heating** due to eddy and hysteresis losses.
3. **Permanent damage** to the core's magnetic properties.

**Protective device:** A **shorting switch (or shorting block)** is permanently installed across the secondary terminals. Before disconnecting the ammeter/burden, the CT secondary is first shorted.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Current Transformer (CT)** for AC current measurement. Derive expressions for **ratio error** and **phase angle error**. *(7 Marks, CO2, Bloom: Understand/Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A CT consists of a **high-permeability silicon-steel laminated core**, a **primary winding** (often a single turn — the line conductor itself), and a **secondary winding** of many turns wound uniformly on the core. The secondary is connected to a low-impedance **burden** (ammeter or relay coil).

**Working Principle (Faraday's Law):** The primary current $I_p$ produces an alternating flux in the core. This flux links the secondary, inducing an EMF, which drives a secondary current $I_s$ through the burden. The secondary current is set by the turns ratio and is largely independent of the burden value.

**Phasor Diagram:** With $I_p$ as reference, the secondary current $I_s$ (reversed, $-I_s$) ideally should be **180° out of phase** with $I_p$ and in magnitude equal to $I_p / K_n$.

**MMF Balance Equation:**

$$N_p I_p - N_s I_s = N_p I_0$$

where $I_0$ is the **exciting current**, having two components: $I_w$ (core-loss component, in phase with induced EMF) and $I_\mu$ (magnetizing component, lagging by 90°).

**Ratio Error Derivation:**

$$\text{Ratio } R = \frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{K_n}{1 - I_0 / I_p}$$

$$\text{Ratio Error} = \frac{R - K_n}{K_n} \times 100\% \approx \frac{I_0}{I_p} \times 100\%$$

**Phase Error Derivation:** The phase angle $\theta$ between $-I_s$ and $I_p$ is small and given by:

$$\theta \approx \frac{I_w}{N_p I_p} \text{ radians}$$

Reducing phase error requires: (i) high-permeability core, (ii) low flux density, (iii) high $N_p$, (iv) low burden impedance.

**Q. (b)** A **PMMC ammeter** has a full-scale current of $50\,\mu A$ and an internal resistance of $2000\,\Omega$. Calculate the value of the **shunt resistance** required to extend its range to measure a maximum current of $5\,A$. Also compute the **shunt multiplying power**. *(7 Marks, CO1, Bloom: Apply/Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Multiplier $m$.**

$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 100{,}000$$

**Step 2: Shunt Resistance Formula.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} = \frac{2000}{100{,}000 - 1} = \frac{2000}{99{,}999} \approx 0.02000\,\Omega$$

**Step 3: Verification by Voltage Equality.**

Voltage across meter movement at FSD:

$$V_m = I_m \cdot R_m = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 2000 = 0.1\,V$$

Shunt current at FSD:

$$I_{sh} = I - I_m = 5 - 50 \times 10^{-6} \approx 4.99995\,A$$

Shunt resistance:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{V_m}{I_{sh}} = \frac{0.1}{4.99995} \approx 0.02000\,\Omega \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Shunt Multiplying Power (SMP).**

$$SMP = m = 100{,}000$$

This means the shunt has extended the meter's range by a factor of one hundred thousand.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the construction, working, and characteristics of a **Hall Effect current sensor**. How is it used to measure DC and AC currents? List its advantages. *(7 Marks, CO3, Bloom: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (Hall Effect):** When a current-carrying semiconductor strip is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the current direction, a **transverse Hall voltage** appears across the strip:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I_c \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I_c$ is the control current, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, and $t$ is the strip thickness.

**Construction:** A thin semiconductor (GaAs, InSb, InAs) is the Hall element. It is placed in a **magnetic core** with a small air gap. The current-carrying conductor passes through the core, concentrating its magnetic flux through the Hall element in the gap.

**Working:**
1. The current $I$ to be measured flows through the conductor.
2. It produces a magnetic field $B \propto I$ in the core.
3. A constant **control current** $I_c$ is passed through the Hall element.
4. The Hall voltage $V_H \propto B \cdot I_c \propto I$ is generated.
5. After amplification, the output voltage is **linearly proportional** to the measured current.

**DC and AC Measurement:** Since the Hall voltage responds to the **instantaneous** magnetic field (and hence instantaneous current), Hall sensors measure **both DC and AC** — a major advantage over CTs (which only work for AC).

**Advantages:**
- Galvanic isolation (no direct electrical contact with the conductor)
- Measures DC and AC equally well
- Wide bandwidth
- Compact and reliable
- No saturation issues (with closed-loop designs)
- Excellent for battery, EV, and inverter applications

**Q. (b)** A **shunt-type DC ammeter** uses a moving-coil movement with $I_{fsd} = 10\,mA$ and $R_m = 75\,\Omega$. The shunt is constructed from **manganin strip** of resistivity $\rho = 4.85 \times 10^{-7}\,\Omega m$, length $L = 0.20\,m$, and thickness $t = 0.50\,mm$. Calculate:
  (i) The maximum current that can be measured.
  (ii) The width $w$ of the shunt strip required. *(7 Marks, CO1, CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Shunt Resistance from Strip Geometry.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t} = \frac{(4.85 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 0.20}{w \cdot (0.50 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$R_{sh} = \frac{9.70 \times 10^{-8}}{w \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.94 \times 10^{-4}}{w}$$

**Step 2: Use the Shunt Formula.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m - 1 = \frac{R_m}{R_{sh}}$$

$$m = 1 + \frac{R_m \cdot w}{1.94 \times 10^{-4}} = 1 + \frac{75 \cdot w}{1.94 \times 10^{-4}} = 1 + 3.866 \times 10^{5} \cdot w$$

We need an additional constraint. In a **design problem** the strip width $w$ is selected such that the **power dissipation** is acceptable or to match a desired maximum current. Suppose the design specifies $I_{max} = 50\,A$. Then:

$$m = \frac{I_{max}}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{50}{0.010} = 5{,}000$$

**Step 3: Solve for $w$.**

$$5{,}000 = 1 + 3.866 \times 10^{5} \cdot w$$

$$3.866 \times 10^{5} \cdot w = 4{,}999$$

$$w = \frac{4{,}999}{3.866 \times 10^{5}} \approx 0.01293\,m = 12.93\,mm$$

**Step 4: Verification.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{1.94 \times 10^{-4}}{0.01293} = 0.01500\,\Omega$$

Voltage across shunt at $I = 50\,A$:

$$V = I \cdot R_{sh} = 50 \cdot 0.015 = 0.75\,V$$

Current through meter:

$$I_m = \frac{V}{R_m} = \frac{0.75}{75} = 0.010\,A = 10\,mA \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Power Dissipated in Shunt.**

$$P_{sh} = I^2 \cdot R_{sh} = 50^2 \cdot 0.015 = 37.5\,W$$

(This is acceptable for a manganin strip with proper cooling.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing multiplier $m$ for voltmeters and ammeters:** Voltmeters use $R_s = R_m (m_v - 1)$ where $m_v = V/V_m$. Ammeters use $R_{sh} = R_m/(m - 1)$ where $m = I/I_m$. Students mix these up routinely — **lose 2 marks per error**.
> - **Forgetting the "minus 1" in shunt formula:** Writing $R_{sh} = R_m / m$ instead of $R_{sh} = R_m/(m-1)$ is a common mistake.
> - **CT direction of phasors:** In CT phasor diagrams, the *reversed* secondary current $-I_s$ is what aligns with $I_p$. Failing to reverse $I_s$ leads to wrong phase error derivations.
> - **Open CT secondary assumption:** Writing "CT secondary is open for safety" is **dangerous and will be marked wrong**. It must be **shorted**, not opened.
> - **Units of $R_H$:** Hall coefficient has units of $m^3/C$ in SI. Students often write $m^2/C$ or skip units — **lose 1 mark**.
> - **Temperature effects:** Shunt resistors use **manganin** (low temperature coefficient) precisely to minimize error — students often overlook this design point.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Current** in instrumentation is a **measurable process variable**, not just a circuit quantity.
- **PMMC** = DC only, linear scale, very sensitive.
- **MI Ammeter** = AC + DC, square-law scale, cheap.
- **Electrodynamometer** = AC + DC, true RMS, accurate, expensive.
- **CT** = AC only, **mandatory shorting of secondary**.
- **Shunt** = $R_{sh} = R_m/(m-1)$; manganin for stability.
- **Multiplier** = $m = I/I_{fsd}$ for ammeters, $m_v = V/V_m$ for voltmeters.
- **Hall Effect** = DC + AC, isolated, semiconductor-based, $V_H \propto I \cdot B$.
- **RMS vs Average vs Peak** are different — form factor $K_f \approx 1.11$ for sine.
- **CT errors** arise from magnetizing current $I_0$; reduced by high-permeability cores and low flux density.
- **Hall sensor** is the modern preference for **isolated DC current sensing** in EVs, BMS, and SMPS.
- **Rogowski coil** (extension topic) measures AC using $dI/dt$ integration — ideal for transients.
- **Always convert** to SI units (Amperes, Volts, Ohms) before substituting into formulas.
- **Use $K_n = N_s/N_p$** carefully — many students invert it.
- For an **AC waveform**, the current indicated by an MI meter is **RMS**, not average or peak.
- **Safety** is paramount: never break a CT secondary, never exceed the meter's FSD by more than 2× for short durations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
