## 3.2 Derivation: Vertical Resolution of a DSO

### Setup
A DSO uses an n-bit ADC with full-scale input range $V_{FS}$. The smallest distinguishable voltage (1 LSB) is the **vertical resolution**.

**Step 1 — Number of Discrete Levels**
An n-bit ADC produces:

$$
L = 2^n \text{ quantization levels}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage per LSB**
The full-scale range is divided into $2^n - 1$ intervals (excluding the zero state), giving:

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} \approx \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Quantization Noise (RMS)**
Quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-V_{LSB}/2, +V_{LSB}/2]$. Its RMS value is:

$$
E_{q, rms} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

**Step 4 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)**
For a full-scale sinusoidal input of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$:

$$
SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \, n + 1.76
$$

**Logical Comment:** Each additional bit of ADC resolution adds ~6 dB of dynamic range. KTU examiners love this formula.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Time Window vs. Sample Rate vs. Memory Depth

### Setup
A DSO with record length $N$ samples, sampling at $f_s$ Hz, displays a time window of duration $T_{window}$.

**Step 1 — Direct Relationship**

$$
T_{window} = N \cdot T_s = \dfrac{N}{f_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving for Required Sample Rate**
Given a desired time window $T_w$ and record length $N$:

$$
f_s = \dfrac{N}{T_w}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for Required Record Length**
Given a desired time window $T_w$ and sample rate $f_s$:

$$
N = T_w \cdot f_s
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style problem)**
A 1 kHz sine wave must be displayed over 5 full cycles. The DSO has 8-bit resolution and a record length of 10,000 samples.

Compute required parameters:
- $T_w = 5 / f_{sig} = 5 / 1000 = 5 \text{ ms}$
- $f_s = N / T_w = 10{,}000 / 0.005 = 2 \text{ MHz}$
- $T_{res} = 1 / f_s = 0.5 \, \mu s$ per sample
- If $V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}$, then $V_{LSB} = 10 / 256 \approx 39 \text{ mV}$

**Logical Comment:** This is exactly the kind of multi-part numerical problem KTU sets in Part B. Practice it.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Pen Frequency of a Strip Chart Recorder

### Setup
For a galvanometer-type recorder, the pen can be approximated as a second-order mass-spring-damper system. The pen must trace a sinusoid of amplitude $A$ at frequency $f_p$.

**Step 1 — Peak Pen Velocity**
The maximum velocity of a sinusoidal motion $y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_p t)$ is:

$$
v_{max} = 2 \pi f_p A
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Pen Velocity Constraint**
The galvanometer mechanism has a maximum permissible pen speed $v_{pen, max}$ (limited by mechanical inertia and pen-paper friction).

**Step 3 — Solve for Maximum Pen Frequency**

$$
f_{p, max} = \dfrac{v_{pen, max}}{2 \pi A}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**
A direct-writing galvanometer has $v_{pen, max} = 200 \text{ mm/s}$ and a full-scale deflection $A = 10 \text{ mm}$.

$$
f_{p, max} = \dfrac{200}{2 \pi \times 10} \approx 3.18 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Logical Comment:** Real recorders operate at 30–50% of this theoretical limit to keep distortion acceptable — so the practical bandwidth is ~1 Hz for a 10 mm deflection.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Time-Error on a Strip Chart Given Paper Speed

### Setup
Two events occur on a strip chart at paper positions $x_1$ and $x_2$ (mm). Paper speed is $v$ mm/s.

**Step 1 — Time Difference**

$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert}{v} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{v}
$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to Other Units**
If paper speed is given in mm/hr: $v_{mm/s} = v_{mm/hr} / 3600$.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example**
A pressure surge is marked at position 250 mm, and a relief valve opening at position 320 mm. The chart speed is 25 mm/s.

$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{320 - 250}{25} = \dfrac{70}{25} = 2.8 \text{ s}
$$

**Logical Comment:** This is a favourite 3-mark short numerical in KTU papers.

---

## 3.6 Servo-Type Recorder — Closed-Loop Position Transfer Function (Brief)

The error $e = V_{in} - \beta y_{pen}$ is amplified by $K_a$ and drives the motor (gain $K_m$ integrating to position $1/s$) with feedback $\beta$:

$$
\dfrac{Y_{pen}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{K_a K_m}{s + K_a K_m \beta}
$$

**Logical Comment:** The pole is at $s = -K_a K_m \beta$, so the bandwidth is directly set by loop gain. Increasing $K_a$ widens bandwidth but risks instability — the dominant reason for the servo recorder's slow ~10 Hz response versus the 100 Hz of a direct galvanometer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix. No markdown formatting (bold/italic/HTML) appears inside node labels. Raw uppercase alphanumeric text is used.

---

## 4.1 DSO — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigIn[A INPUT SIGNAL BNC] --> attNode[A ATTENUATOR 1X 10X 100X]
    attNode --> preAmpNode[B VERTICAL PREAMPLIFIER]
    preAmpNode --> clampNode[C OVERVOLTAGE CLAMP]
    clampNode --> adcNode[D ADC FLASH OR SAR]
    clkNode[H TIMEBASE CLOCK] --> adcNode
    trigNode[E TRIGGER CIRCUIT] --> clkNode
    trigNode --> memNode[F ACQUISITION MEMORY]
    adcNode --> memNode
    memNode --> procNode[G DISPLAY PROCESSOR]
    procNode --> dacNode[I DAC]
    dacNode --> lcdNode[J RASTER DISPLAY LCD]
    procNode --> usbNode[K USB ETHERNET INTERFACE]
    ctrlNode[L FRONT PANEL CONTROLS] --> procNode
```

**Explanation of the Flow:**

- **A → B → C → D:** The input signal is progressively conditioned (scaled, amplified, protected, and digitized).
- **H (Timebase Clock) and E (Trigger):** The trigger synchronizes the timebase to a meaningful point in the waveform; the clock drives the ADC.
- **F (Acquisition Memory):** Sequential storage of digitized samples.
- **G (Display Processor):** Reads the memory, applies interpolation, computes automated measurements.
- **I → J (DAC → Display):** Reconstructs the waveform for display on an LCD raster.
- **K and L:** Connectivity and operator interface for control/transfer.

---

## 4.2 DSO Acquisition Memory and Triggering — Detailed Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    analogIn[Q ANALOG INPUT] --> attNodeR[R INPUT ATTENUATOR]
    attNodeR --> ampNodeR[S VERTICAL AMPLIFIER]
    ampNodeR --> muxNodeR[T CHANNEL MUX]
    muxNodeR --> adcNodeR[U ADC]
    clkR[V SAMPLE CLOCK Fs] --> adcNodeR
    trigR[W TRIGGER DETECTOR] --> clkR
    trigR --> holdR[X ACQUISITION HOLD]
    adcNodeR --> memR[Y ACQUISITION MEMORY]
    holdR --> memR
    memR --> cpusigZ[Z DSP AND PROCESSING]
    cpusigZ --> dispR[AA LCD RASTER DISPLAY]
    cpusigZ --> measR[AB AUTO MEASUREMENTS]
    measR --> measListR[AC FREQ RMS RISE TIME DUTY]
```

---

## 4.3 Galvanometer-Type Strip Chart Recorder — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputG[BA PROCESS VARIABLE SENSOR] --> condG[BB SIGNAL CONDITIONER]
    condG --> ampG[BC DC AMPLIFIER]
    ampG --> coilG[BD D ARSONVAL MOVING COIL]
    coilG --> magG[BE PERMANENT MAGNET FIELD]
    coilG --> penG[BF PEN ASSEMBLY]
    penG --> paperG[BG CHART PAPER STRIP]
    motorG[BH SYNCHRONOUS MOTOR] --> gearG[BI GEAR TRAIN]
    gearG --> paperG
    speedG[BJ SPEED SELECTOR] --> gearG
    magG -.magnetic torque.-> coilG
```

**Logical Walk-through:**

- The sensor (e.g., thermocouple) generates a low-level signal.
- The DC amplifier boosts it to a level that drives the moving coil.
- The coil, in the permanent magnet field, experiences a torque $T = N B I A$ proportional to current.
- The pen, attached to the coil, deflects vertically.
- The paper moves horizontally at a constant rate — the trace is a plot of variable vs. time.
- The synchronous motor + gear train sets the paper speed.

---

## 4.4 Servo-Type (Null-Balance) Strip Chart Recorder — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inS[CA INPUT SIGNAL] --> compS[CB COMPARATOR]
    refS[CC REFERENCE VOLTAGE] --> compS
    compS --> errS[CD ERROR SIGNAL]
    errS --> ampS[CE SERVO AMPLIFIER]
    ampS --> motorS[CF SERVO MOTOR DC]
    motorS --> penS[CG PEN ASSEMBLY]
    penS --> paperS[CH CHART PAPER]
    penS --> fbS[CI FEEDBACK POTENTIOMETER OR LVDT]
    fbS --> compS
    speedS[CJ SPEED CONTROL GEAR] --> paperS
    motorS -.drives.-> penS
    motorS -.drives.-> fbS
```

**Logical Walk-through:**

- The input $V_{in}$ is continuously compared with a feedback voltage $\beta \cdot y_{pen}$.
- Any difference becomes the error signal.
- The servo amplifier drives a motor that simultaneously repositions the **pen** and the **feedback slider**.
- The loop is a closed *null-balance* — the system continuously drives the error to zero.
- This achieves higher accuracy than a direct galvanometer (no spring, no coil non-linearity).

---

## 4.5 Comparison Diagram — How a DSO and a Strip Chart Recorder Differ in Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCOPE[DA DIGITAL STORAGE OSCILLOSCOPE PATH]
        sIn[DA1 SIGNAL] --> sAdc[DA2 ADC] --> sMem[DA3 MEMORY] --> sDisp[DA4 LCD RASTER]
    end
    subgraph SCR[DB STRIP CHART RECORDER PATH]
        cIn[DB1 SIGNAL] --> cAmp[DB2 DC AMP] --> cPen[DB3 ELECTROMECHANICAL PEN] --> cPaper[DB4 PAPER STRIP]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A carries 3 marks (short answer). Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice (typically sub-parts a: 7 marks, b: 7 marks). Model solutions below follow the exact KTU valuation key style — stepwise, complete, and with marks-allocated key phrases highlighted.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between an analog CRO and a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Analog CRO | Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses electron beam in CRT to trace waveform | Uses ADC, digital memory, and raster display |
| 2 | Cannot capture single-shot transients | Captures and stores single-shot and pre-trigger events |
| 3 | No permanent storage | Stores waveforms in digital memory / external media |
| 4 | Limited automated measurements | Built-in automated frequency, RMS, rise-time measurements |
| 5 | Bandwidth limited by CRT phosphor and amp | Bandwidth defined by ADC and front-end amplifier |

(1 mark for the concept of digital vs analog; 1 mark each for two valid distinguishing points; 1 mark for any application/storage difference — total 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **three advantages** of a servo-type strip chart recorder over a galvanometer-type recorder.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher accuracy:** The null-balance feedback principle eliminates non-linearities of the moving-coil galvanometer. (1 mark)
2. **Greater pen-driving torque:** A servo motor can drive heavy multi-pen assemblies, while a galvanometer is limited to light pens. (1 mark)
3. **Better long-term stability:** No coil spring fatigue or thermal drift, since position is determined by a precision feedback potentiometer/LVDT. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the different **acquisition modes** of a DSO with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Attenuator** | Selects 1×, 10×, 100× scaling to bring signal within ADC range | 1 |
| **Vertical Pre-amplifier** | Amplifies the attenuated signal to ADC full scale | 1 |
| **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** | Samples the analog waveform at the timebase clock rate $f_s$ | 1 |
| **Acquisition Memory** | Stores each digitized sample in sequence (record length N) | 1 |
| **Trigger Circuit** | Detects a defined event (rising edge, pulse width) to start/synchronize the acquisition | 1 |
| **Display Processor** | Reads memory, interpolates, computes measurements, drives the display | 1 |
| **DAC & Raster Display** | Reconstructs the analog waveform for display on LCD/CRT | 1 |

(Examiner note: Marks are split 1+1+1+1+1+1+1 = 7 for correctly labeled blocks with brief functional descriptions.)

**Key Phrase to Write:** *The ADC, acquisition memory, and trigger circuit are the three defining subsystems of a DSO that distinguish it from an analog CRO.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Acquisition Mode | Working Principle | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Real-Time Sampling** | ADC samples the entire waveform in a single trigger event; $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) or $\geq 10 f_{max}$ in practice | 1.5 |
| **Equivalent-Time Sampling** | Builds the waveform over many triggers; samples a different point on each cycle, then assembles a composite waveform. Sub-types: *Sequential* and *Random* | 1.5 |
| **Roll Mode** | Continuous acquisition; samples scroll right-to-left like a strip chart; suitable for low-frequency slowly varying signals | 1 |
| **Peak Detect** | Stores min and max sample within each time bucket; catches narrow glitches between samples | 1 |
| **Averaging** | Averages N triggered acquisitions point-by-point; suppresses uncorrelated random noise by factor of $\sqrt{N}$ | 1 |
| **High-Resolution / Hi-Res** | Oversamples and averages within a single acquisition; effective vertical resolution can increase from 8 bits to ~12 bits | 1 |

(Examiner note: 7 marks are typically distributed as above. Always include a labelled diagram for *at least* real-time vs equivalent-time — that's a 2-mark item alone.)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **galvanometer-type strip chart recorder**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the working of a **servo-type (null-balance) strip chart recorder** and list its advantages over the galvanometer type. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Subsystem | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor / Input** | Transducer (e.g., thermocouple, strain gauge) converts the process variable to an electrical signal | 1 |
| **Signal Conditioner** | Filtering, cold-junction compensation, bridge excitation as required | 1 |
| **DC Amplifier** | Amplifies the low-level signal to a level that drives the moving coil | 1 |
| **D'Arsonval Moving Coil in Magnetic Field** | The coil in a permanent magnet field experiences a torque $T = BINA$ proportional to input current; this produces angular deflection | 1.5 |
| **Pen Assembly** | Rigidly attached to the coil, traces a vertical line whose height ∝ input signal | 1 |
| **Chart Paper Drive** | A synchronous motor with a gear train moves the paper at a selectable constant speed (mm/s or mm/hr) | 1 |
| **Working Summary** | "Time runs horizontally (paper motion), the variable runs vertically (pen deflection) — producing a permanent time-stamped plot." | 0.5 |

**Key Formula to Quote:** $T_{pen} = BINA$ (deflecting torque); $T_{control} = K_s \theta$ (control spring torque). At equilibrium: $\theta \propto I$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Working Principle [3 marks]:**
The servo recorder is a *closed-loop null-balance* instrument. The input signal $V_{in}$ is compared to a feedback voltage $\beta y_{pen}$ in a comparator. The error $e = V_{in} - \beta y_{pen}$ is amplified and drives a DC servo motor. The motor simultaneously moves the pen (vertically) and a feedback element (potentiometer slider or LVDT core). When the pen reaches the position where $\beta y_{pen} = V_{in}$, the error becomes zero, and the motor stops — this is the **null-balance** condition.

**Block Diagram Elements [2 marks]:**
Comparator → Error Amplifier → Servo Motor → Pen + Feedback Element → back to Comparator (closed loop).

**Advantages over Galvanometer Type [2 marks]:**
1. Higher accuracy (no spring non-linearity; precision feedback element).
2. Higher pen-driving torque (handles multi-pen and heavy stylus loads).
3. Better long-term stability and lower drift.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks — Topic: DSO & Strip Chart Recorder**
>
> 1. **Confusing Real-Time vs Equivalent-Time Sampling** — Examiners expect a *clear* mention of *when* each mode is used (single-shot transient → real-time; high-frequency repetitive signal beyond ADC bandwidth → equivalent-time). Skipping this loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Nyquist Criterion** — Whenever a DSO is discussed, the *Nyquist theorem* ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$) must appear. A complete DSO answer without it will be marked down 2 marks.
> 3. **Drawing the block diagram without labelling** — In Part B (a) questions, examiners require *labelled arrows showing signal flow direction*. Drawing boxes without arrows = 1–2 marks lost.
> 4. **Mixing up paper speed units (mm/s vs mm/hr)** — The strip chart paper speed is often given in mm/hr for slow trend recorders (e.g., temperature logs over 24 hours) and mm/s for fast process recorders. Always check the units before substituting.
> 5. **Omitting the "null-balance" concept** — When describing a servo recorder, the *key phrase* is *"the system drives the error to zero"* or *"null-balance principle."* Missing this phrase = at least 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Skipping the role of the trigger circuit in the DSO** — The trigger is *not* optional. It synchronizes the acquisition and is the *reason* a DSO can show pre-trigger data — a frequently tested concept.
> 7. **Forgetting the synchronization motor in strip chart recorder** — A common omission. The pen deflects vertically; *something* must move the paper horizontally. State: "A synchronous AC motor drives the paper at constant speed via a gear train."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 4: DSO & Strip Chart Recorder**

- [ ] **DSO Block Diagram (in order):** Input → Attenuator → Vertical Amplifier → ADC → Trigger → Acquisition Memory → Display Processor → DAC → Raster Display.
- [ ] **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Practical: $f_s \geq 10 \times f_{max}$ for waveform fidelity.
- [ ] **Acquisition Modes:** Real-Time, Equivalent-Time (Sequential/Random), Roll, Peak Detect, Averaging, Hi-Res.
- [ ] **Vertical Resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{FS} / 2^n$. SQNR $\approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- [ ] **Time Window:** $T_{window} = N / f_s$, where $N$ is record length.
- [ ] **Pre-Trigger Viewing** is a unique DSO feature — memory captures signal *before* the trigger event.
- [ ] **Strip Chart Recorder** plots variable vs. time on a moving paper strip. Horizontal axis = time (paper motion), Vertical axis = variable (pen deflection).
- [ ] **Two types:** Galvanometer (direct-writing, ~100 Hz) and Servo (null-balance, ~10 Hz, higher accuracy).
- [ ] **Galvanometer recorder** uses a D'Arsonval moving coil in a permanent magnet field; pen deflection $\theta \propto I_{in}$.
- [ ] **Servo recorder** uses a closed-loop error-driven DC motor; feedback via potentiometer or LVDT; the loop drives the error to zero.
- [ ] **Paper speeds:** mm/hr (slow trends) or mm/s (fast processes).
- [ ] **Time difference on chart:** $\Delta t = \Delta x / v_{paper}$.
- [ ] **Pen frequency limit:** $f_{p,max} = v_{pen,max} / (2\pi A)$.
- [ ] **Always quote the null-balance principle** for servo recorders.
- [ ] **Always label arrows and signal flow direction** in block diagrams — KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabeled diagrams.
- [ ] **DSO vs Strip Chart:** DSO is electronic, fast, and digital; Strip Chart is electromechanical, slow, and produces a permanent paper record for regulatory/audit purposes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-Y Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** X-Y Recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# X-Y RECORDER — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **X-Y Recorder** is a graphical recording instrument that directly plots the functional relationship between two mutually independent input variables by moving a recording pen or stylus along two perpendicular axes (designated X and Y) on a **stationary sheet of paper**. One input drives the pen horizontally (X-axis) while the other input drives the pen vertically (Y-axis), producing a Cartesian plot of $y = f(x)$ in real-time.

Unlike a strip-chart recorder (where paper moves at a constant rate and the pen deflects in only one axis), the **paper in an X-Y recorder is held stationary** on a flat platen (or platen table), and the pen position is independently controlled along both axes by two separate servo-driven position feedback systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot Artist"

Imagine you are teaching a friend to draw a graph. Instead of giving them pre-printed graph paper and asking them to plot one point at a time, you hand them a **blank sheet of paper and a pen mounted on a robot arm** that can move freely in two directions (left-right and up-down). Now, you give your friend **two electrical signals**:

- A voltage representing how far **right** to move → this controls the **X-axis** motor
- A voltage representing how far **up** to move → this controls the **Y-axis** motor

As the signals change in real time, the robot arm moves the pen across the paper, continuously **drawing the relationship** between the two signals. The trail left behind by the pen is your X-Y plot. This is exactly how an X-Y recorder works — except the "robot arm" is built using precision **DC servo motors** and **position feedback potentiometers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Stationary Paper"?**
> Because the user is interested in the *correlation* between X and Y at the *same instant in time*, not the *time history* of a single variable. Stationary paper lets the recorder visualize Lissajous patterns, frequency response curves, B-H loops, stress-strain curves, and transistor characteristic curves.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Metric | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| Input Signal Range | **0 – 10 mV to 0 – 50 V** (with attenuator) |
| Input Impedance | Typically **1 MΩ or higher** |
| Pen Speed (Slewing) | Up to **1.0 m/s** on each axis |
| Accuracy Class | Generally **± 0.25% of full scale** to **± 0.5% of full scale** |
| Paper Size | **A4 or A3** standard flat platen |
| Axes | Two independent orthogonal servos (X & Y) |
| Time Axis (if present) | Optional, via paper transport (Y-t mode) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The X-Y recorder operates on the **principle of a null-balance servo system** for each axis. Here is the precise operational chain:

**Step 1 — Signal Conditioning (Attenuation & Amplification)**
- The two input signals (one for X, one for Y) are typically small (millivolts to volts).
- They pass through **attenuator networks** (resistive voltage dividers) to bring them into a standard range.
- A **chopper-stabilized DC amplifier** (used to eliminate DC drift and zero offset) amplifies the conditioned signal to a level sufficient to drive the servo motor.

**Step 2 — Error Detection via Position Feedback Potentiometer**
- A **precision linear potentiometer** (the position-sensing element) is mechanically coupled to the pen carriage via a rack-and-pinion or lead screw.
- The wiper of the potentiometer slides linearly as the pen moves, producing a **DC feedback voltage** proportional to the pen's physical displacement from the origin (zero position).
- This feedback voltage is **opposed (subtracted) from the input signal** at the amplifier input stage, generating an **error voltage** $V_e$.

**Step 3 — Servo Motor Actuation**
- The error voltage $V_e$ is fed to a power amplifier that drives a **DC servo motor** (or in modern designs, a brushless DC / stepper motor).
- The motor turns in a direction that **reduces the error voltage toward zero** — this is the "null-balance" principle.
- When $V_e = 0$, the pen is stationary, and its position is an accurate representation of the input signal magnitude.

**Step 4 — Pen Movement and Plotting**
- The motor shaft drives a lead screw that moves the pen carriage along its axis.
- Two such systems (one for X, one for Y) operate simultaneously, creating a **2-D vector plot** of the input relationship.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Subsystem

| Subsystem | Why It Exists (Engineering Rationale) |
|---|---|
| **Chopper-stabilized amplifier** | Conventional DC amplifiers suffer from **drift** (slow output change with no input). Chopping the DC signal to AC, amplifying, then synchronously rectifying eliminates drift — critical for accurate plotting of slow-changing signals. |
| **Position feedback potentiometer** | Without feedback, the pen would simply deflect in proportion to input (like a voltmeter) — but a recorder must **track the input precisely at any position**, not just at zero. Feedback converts the system from an "open-loop meter" to a **closed-loop servo**. |
| **Lead screw + carriage** | Converts rotary motion of the servo motor into precise linear motion of the pen with mechanical advantage and high resolution. |
| **Stationary platen** | Eliminates the time-base, allowing the relationship between two independent variables to be visualized directly. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield for KTU board exams** — appearing in nearly every past paper on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_e = V_{in} - V_{fb}$ | Error voltage = input minus feedback | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\theta_m = K_a \cdot V_e$ | Motor shaft angle proportional to amplified error | Radians (rad) |
| 3 | $V_{fb} = K_p \cdot x$ | Feedback voltage proportional to pen displacement | V and mm |
| 4 | $x = K_s \cdot \theta_m$ | Linear pen displacement from motor rotation via lead screw | mm |
| 5 | $x_{steady} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p}$ | Steady-state pen position (null-balance condition $V_e = 0$) | mm |
| 6 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a \cdot K_s \cdot K_p}{J}}$ | Natural frequency of second-order servo loop | rad/s |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{K_a K_s K_p J}}$ | Damping ratio (determines overshoot/settling) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau s}$ | First-order approximation of pen response (for $\zeta \geq 1$) | transfer function |
| 9 | $\text{Error}_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 + K_{loop}}$ | Static position error of closed-loop system | mm or %FS |
| 10 | $f_{max} \leq \dfrac{v_{pen}}{2\pi A}$ | Maximum traceable input frequency (for sinusoidal inputs) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the variables are:
> - $K_a$ = amplifier gain (V/V)
> - $K_p$ = potentiometer sensitivity (V/mm)
> - $K_s$ = lead screw constant (mm/rad)
> - $J$ = moment of inertia of motor + load
> - $B$ = viscous damping coefficient
> - $K_{loop}$ = open-loop DC gain of the servo system
> - $v_{pen}$ = maximum pen slew velocity (mm/s)
> - $A$ = amplitude of the input signal excursion (mm)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | What is Plotted on the X-Y Recorder |
|---|---|
| **Electronics Lab** | Transistor/diode characteristic curves ($I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$, $I_D$ vs $V_D$) |
| **Magnetic Testing** | **B-H loop** of ferromagnetic materials (B on Y, H on X) |
| **Materials Science** | **Stress–strain curves** during tensile testing |
| **Vibration Analysis** | **Lissajous figures** to determine phase shift and frequency ratio |
| **Antenna / RF Engineering** | Radiation patterns, impedance loci (Smith chart overlay) |
| **Medical Instrumentation** | Pressure–Volume loops of the heart (cardiac catheterization) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Pen Position (Null-Balance Condition)

We now derive the relationship between the **input voltage** and the **final pen displacement** at equilibrium.

### Given System

The forward path consists of:
- Amplifier with gain $K_a$
- Servo motor (assumed ideal, no inertia at steady state)
- Lead screw (converts angle $\theta_m$ to linear displacement $x$ via $K_s$)

The feedback path consists of:
- Linear potentiometer with sensitivity $K_p$ (V per mm of pen travel)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The pen displacement at the output is generated by the motor angle times the lead-screw constant:

$$
x = K_s \cdot \theta_m
$$

The motor angle is driven by the amplified error voltage:

$$
\theta_m = K_a \cdot V_e
$$

Combining these two equations:

$$
x = K_s \cdot K_a \cdot V_e
$$

The error voltage is the difference between the input signal and the feedback voltage (set up at the summing junction of the amplifier):

$$
V_e = V_{in} - V_{fb}
$$

The feedback voltage is proportional to pen displacement:

$$
V_{fb} = K_p \cdot x
$$

Substituting this into the error equation:

$$
V_e = V_{in} - K_p \cdot x
$$

Now substitute this $V_e$ into the pen-displacement expression:

$$
x = K_s \cdot K_a \cdot (V_{in} - K_p \cdot x)
$$

Distribute the right-hand side:

$$
x = K_a K_s \cdot V_{in} - K_a K_s K_p \cdot x
$$

Move the term involving $x$ on the right to the left-hand side:

$$
x + K_a K_s K_p \cdot x = K_a K_s \cdot V_{in}
$$

Factor out $x$ on the left:

$$
x \cdot (1 + K_a K_s K_p) = K_a K_s \cdot V_{in}
$$

Solve for $x$:

$$
x = \dfrac{K_a K_s \cdot V_{in}}{1 + K_a K_s K_p}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $K_a K_s K_p$:

$$
x = \dfrac{V_{in} / K_p}{1 + \dfrac{1}{K_a K_s K_p}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Steady-State Pen Displacement:**
> $$\boxed{\;x_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{K_a K_s K_p}\right)}\;}$$
>
> When the **open-loop gain $K_a K_s K_p \gg 1$** (which is the practical case in a well-designed servo), the term $\dfrac{1}{K_a K_s K_p}$ becomes negligible, and we get the **ideal null-balance result**:
>
> $$\boxed{\;x_{ss} \approx \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p}\;}$$

This proves that the pen position depends **only on the input voltage and the potentiometer sensitivity** — it is **independent of the amplifier gain, motor characteristics, or lead-screw non-linearities**. This is the **fundamental advantage of the null-balance (servo) principle**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second-Order Transfer Function of the Servo

For dynamic analysis, the servo is modeled as a second-order system. The motor is governed by the torque equation:

$$
J \dfrac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d \theta_m}{dt} = K_t \cdot V_e
$$

where $K_t$ is the motor torque constant, $J$ is moment of inertia, and $B$ is the damping coefficient.

The pen position is:

$$
x = K_s \cdot \theta_m
$$

So the second derivative of pen position is:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = K_s \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2}
$$

The error voltage expression in terms of pen position (taking the Laplace transform):

$$
V_e(s) = V_{in}(s) - K_p \cdot X(s)
$$

Substituting into the torque equation (in Laplace domain) and solving for $X(s)/V_{in}(s)$:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{K_a K_s K_t}{J s^2 + B s + K_a K_s K_p K_t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**
> $$\boxed{\;\dfrac{X(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{K_{dc}}{1 + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} s + \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2} s^2}\;}$$
>
> where:
> $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a K_s K_p K_t}{J}}, \quad \zeta = \dfrac{B}{2 \sqrt{J \cdot K_a K_s K_p K_t}}, \quad K_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{K_p}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Position Error (Linearity Specification)

From the steady-state equation we derived earlier, the **static error** is the deviation from the ideal linear plot:

$$
\text{Error}_{ss} = x_{ideal} - x_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p} - \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p(1 + K_{loop})}
$$

Factor out $\dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p}$:

$$
\text{Error}_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{loop}}\right)
$$

Simplify the term in parentheses:

$$
\text{Error}_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{K_{loop}}{1 + K_{loop}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Static Error:**
> $$\boxed{\;\text{Error}_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot K_{loop}}{K_p \cdot (1 + K_{loop})}\;}$$
>
> For $K_{loop} \gg 1$, this reduces to $\text{Error}_{ss} \approx V_{in}/K_p \cdot (1 - 1/K_{loop})$, meaning the error is suppressed by a factor of $K_{loop}$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An X-Y recorder has a feedback potentiometer sensitivity $K_p = 0.1$ V/mm. The amplifier gain is $K_a = 1000$, the lead-screw constant is $K_s = 10$ mm/rad, and the open-loop DC gain $K_{loop} = K_a \cdot K_s \cdot K_p = 1000$. If the input voltage to the X-axis is $V_{in} = 5$ V, find the steady-state pen displacement.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the parameters: $K_p = 0.1$ V/mm, $K_{loop} = 1000$, $V_{in} = 5$ V.

Step 2 — Apply the steady-state pen position formula:

$$
x_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{K_{loop}}\right)}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute values:

$$
x_{ss} = \dfrac{5}{0.1 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{1000}\right)}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the denominator:

$$
0.1 \cdot \left(1 + 0.001\right) = 0.1 \cdot 1.001 = 0.1001 \text{ V/mm}
$$

Step 5 — Compute the result:

$$
x_{ss} = \dfrac{5}{0.1001} = 49.95 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pen moves approximately **49.95 mm** for an input of 5 V, which is essentially **50 mm** (the ideal value $V_{in}/K_p = 5/0.1 = 50$ mm). The error is only **0.05 mm** — a linearity better than **0.1%** thanks to the high open-loop gain. This is the engineering magic of the null-balance servo.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — X-Y Recorder Servo (X-Axis Subsystem)

The following block diagram shows the closed-loop servo for **one axis** (X or Y). The X-Y recorder contains two such identical servos operating simultaneously.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT SIGNAL V_in] --> S((SUMMING JUNCTION))
    S --> B[CHOPPER STABILIZED DC AMPLIFIER]
    B --> C[POWER AMPLIFIER]
    C --> D[DC SERVO MOTOR]
    D --> E[LEAD SCREW AND PEN CARRIAGE]
    E --> P[PEN WRITES ON STATIONARY PLATEN]
    E --> F[FEEDBACK POTENTIOMETER]
    F --> G[FEEDBACK VOLTAGE V_fb]
    G --> S
    F -.-> H[MECHANICAL COUPLING]
    H --> E
```

## 4.2 Complete X-Y Recorder Architecture (Two-Axis Block Diagram)

The diagram below shows the **complete X-Y recorder** with both axes, the time-base option, the platen, and the power supply.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_STAGE["INPUT STAGE"]
        XIN[INPUT X] --> XAT[X AXIS ATTENUATOR]
        YIN[INPUT Y] --> YAT[Y AXIS ATTENUATOR]
        TIN[TIME BASE OPTIONAL] --> TDR[TIME DRIVE MOTOR]
    end

    subgraph X_SERVO["X AXIS SERVO SYSTEM"]
        XAT --> XAMP[X CHOPPER AMPLIFIER]
        XAMP --> XSJ((SUMMING JUNCTION X))
        XSJ --> XPWR[X POWER AMPLIFIER]
        XPWR --> XMOT[X DC SERVO MOTOR]
        XMOT --> XSCREW[X LEAD SCREW]
        XSCREW --> XPEN[PEN CARRIAGE]
    end

    subgraph Y_SERVO["Y AXIS SERVO SYSTEM"]
        YAT --> YAMP[Y CHOPPER AMPLIFIER]
        YAMP --> YSJ((SUMMING JUNCTION Y))
        YSJ --> YPWR[Y POWER AMPLIFIER]
        YPWR --> YMOT[Y DC SERVO MOTOR]
        YMOT --> YSCREW[Y LEAD SCREW]
        YSCREW --> YPEN[PEN CARRIAGE]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK NETWORK"]
        XSCREW --> XPFB[X LINEAR POTENTIOMETER]
        YSCREW --> YPFB[Y LINEAR POTENTIOMETER]
        XPFB --> XSJ
        YPFB --> YSJ
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT"]
        XPEN --> PLATEN[STATIONARY PAPER PLATEN]
        YPEN --> PLATEN
    end

    TDR -.-> PLATEN
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow and Component Description

| Block | Function | Real Component |
|---|---|---|
| **X-IN / Y-IN** | External signal inputs | BNC connectors on rear panel |
| **X-AT / Y-AT** | Input range selection | Switched resistor divider network |
| **CHOPPER AMP** | Drift-free DC amplification | Mechanical/photonic chopper + AC amp + synchronous demodulator |
| **SUMMING JUNCTION** | Compares input with feedback | Resistor summing network at op-amp input |
| **POWER AMPLIFIER** | Drives the servo motor | Push-pull Class B transistor stage |
| **DC SERVO MOTOR** | Produces mechanical rotation | Permanent-magnet DC motor with low-inertia armature |
| **LEAD SCREW** | Converts rotation to linear pen motion | Precision-machined screw with anti-backlash nut |
| **LINEAR POTENTIOMETER** | Position sensor for feedback | Wire-wound or conductive-plastic linear pot |
| **PLATEN** | Holds the recording paper | Flat electrostatic or magnetic paper holder |
| **TIME DRIVE** | Optional Y-t mode (paper moves) | Synchronous motor driving paper roller |

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram — X-Y Recorder vs Strip-Chart Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XY["X-Y RECORDER"]
        X1[TWO INDEPENDENT INPUTS] --> X2[TWO SERVO SYSTEMS]
        X2 --> X3[STATIONARY PAPER]
        X3 --> X4[Y PLOTTED AGAINST X DIRECTLY]
    end

    subgraph SC["STRIP CHART RECORDER"]
        S1[ONE INPUT SIGNAL] --> S2[ONE SERVO OR GALVANOMETER]
        S2 --> S3[MOVING PAPER AT CONSTANT SPEED]
        S3 --> S4[Y PLOTTED AGAINST TIME]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Define an X-Y recorder. Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An X-Y recorder is an instrument that records the relationship between two variables by driving a pen along two perpendicular axes (X and Y) on a stationary sheet of paper, where one input signal controls the horizontal motion and another controls the vertical motion of the pen.

**Applications (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. Plotting **B-H (magnetization) curves** of ferromagnetic materials in electrical engineering labs.
2. Plotting **transistor/diode V-I characteristic curves** in electronics characterization.
3. Plotting **stress-strain curves** in mechanical materials testing.
4. Displaying **Lissajous figures** to determine phase difference and frequency ratio between two signals.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Why is a chopper-stabilized amplifier used in an X-Y recorder? What is the role of the feedback potentiometer?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Chopper-stabilized amplifier (1.5 marks):** A chopper-stabilized amplifier is used to **eliminate DC drift and zero offset errors** in the DC amplifier. Since X-Y recorders plot slowly varying DC signals, conventional DC amplifiers would introduce significant low-frequency drift, causing the pen to drift away from the true position. The chopper converts the DC input to AC, amplifies it (AC amplifiers are drift-free), and then synchronously rectifies it back to DC — producing a drift-free amplified output.

**Role of feedback potentiometer (1.5 marks):** The feedback potentiometer is a **linear position-sensing element** that converts the pen's mechanical displacement into a proportional DC voltage. This voltage is subtracted from the input signal at the summing junction to produce an error voltage, which drives the servo motor until the pen reaches a position where the error is zero. This implements the **null-balance principle** and ensures linearity independent of amplifier and motor non-linearities.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an X-Y recorder. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. Compare it with a strip-chart recorder.**

### **Part (a) — Block Diagram and Working (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The X-Y recorder consists of two identical servo systems, one for the X-axis and one for the Y-axis. Each servo system contains:
- Attenuator network (input range selector)
- Chopper-stabilized DC amplifier
- Summing junction
- Power amplifier
- DC servo motor
- Lead screw with pen carriage
- Linear feedback potentiometer

The paper is held **stationary on a flat platen**. There is no time-base drive unless operated in Y-t mode.

**Working (5 marks):**

1. **Signal input:** Two independent signals $V_x$ and $V_y$ are applied to the X and Y inputs respectively.

2. **Attenuation and amplification:** The signals are attenuated to standard range and amplified by chopper-stabilized DC amplifiers to eliminate drift.

3. **Error generation:** The amplified signal is compared with the feedback voltage from the linear potentiometer at the summing junction, producing an error voltage $V_e = V_{in} - V_{fb}$.

4. **Servo action:** The error voltage drives the DC servo motor through a power amplifier. The motor turns in the direction that reduces the error to zero.

5. **Pen movement:** The motor rotates a lead screw that moves the pen carriage linearly. The X-servo moves the pen horizontally, the Y-servo moves it vertically.

6. **Position sensing:** As the pen moves, the wiper of the linear potentiometer slides, generating a feedback voltage proportional to displacement.

7. **Null-balance condition:** When $V_e = 0$, the pen stops moving. The final pen position is given by $x_{ss} = V_{in}/K_p$, which is independent of motor and amplifier characteristics.

8. **Plotting:** As $V_x$ and $V_y$ change in real time, the pen traces the curve $y = f(x)$ on the stationary paper.

### **Part (b) — Advantages, Disadvantages, and Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Plots two independent variables directly, with **no time base distortion**.
2. **High accuracy** (± 0.25% FS) due to null-balance servo principle.
3. Suitable for **slowly varying DC and low-frequency signals** that cannot be plotted on oscilloscopes.
4. Can plot **complex curves** like B-H loops, transistor characteristics, and Lissajous patterns directly.
5. **Wide input range** — can handle millivolts to hundreds of volts using attenuators.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. **Low frequency response** — mechanical inertia limits slew rate.
2. **Mechanical wear** of lead screws, pen tips, and potentiometers requires regular maintenance.
3. **Slower than digital plotters** and cannot store data electronically.
4. **Bulky and expensive** compared to modern digital alternatives.
5. Pen friction and inertia introduce **hysteresis errors** at very slow pen speeds.

**Comparison with Strip-Chart Recorder (3 marks):**

| Parameter | X-Y Recorder | Strip-Chart Recorder |
|---|---|---|
| Paper | **Stationary** flat platen | **Moving** at constant rate |
| Variables plotted | Two independent variables (Y vs X) | One variable vs **time** |
| Drives | Two servo systems (X and Y) | One servo + one time-base drive |
| Application | B-H loops, V-I curves, Lissajous | Temperature, pressure vs time logs |
| Frequency response | Low (limited by pen inertia) | Depends on chart speed |
| Accuracy | ± 0.25% to ± 0.5% FS | ± 1% to ± 2% FS typically |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse an X-Y recorder with a **strip-chart recorder** — many students write "the paper moves at constant speed" for X-Y, which is the **strip-chart's** defining feature. For X-Y, the paper is **STATIONARY**.
> - Always mention the **null-balance / servo principle** in the working — a generic "the pen moves with the signal" answer will lose 2-3 marks.
> - Drawing the block diagram without **arrows showing the feedback loop** is a common mistake; the feedback path from the potentiometer back to the summing junction is **mandatory**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q: Derive the transfer function of the X-Y recorder servo system. Explain how the null-balance principle ensures linearity, with a numerical example.**

### **Part (a) — Derivation of the Transfer Function (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The X-Y recorder is a closed-loop servo system. Let us derive its transfer function $X(s)/V_{in}(s)$.

**Step 1 — Define system parameters:**
- Amplifier gain: $K_a$ (V/V)
- Motor torque constant: $K_t$ (Nm/V)
- Moment of inertia: $J$ (kg·m²)
- Viscous damping: $B$ (Nm·s/rad)
- Lead screw constant: $K_s$ (mm/rad)
- Potentiometer sensitivity: $K_p$ (V/mm)

**Step 2 — Write the motor torque equation:**

The motor torque is proportional to the error voltage:

$$
T_m = K_t \cdot V_e
$$

This torque accelerates the inertia and overcomes damping:

$$
J \dfrac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d \theta_m}{dt} = K_t \cdot V_e
$$

**Step 3 — Relate pen displacement to motor angle:**

$$
x = K_s \cdot \theta_m
$$

**Step 4 — Write the error voltage in terms of input and feedback:**

$$
V_e = V_{in} - K_p \cdot x
$$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace transform of the motor equation:**

$$
J s^2 \Theta_m(s) + B s \Theta_m(s) = K_t \left[ V_{in}(s) - K_p X(s) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $X(s) = K_s \Theta_m(s)$:**

$$
J s^2 \dfrac{X(s)}{K_s} + B s \dfrac{X(s)}{K_s} = K_t V_{in}(s) - K_t K_p X(s)
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply through by $K_s$ and collect $X(s)$ terms:**

$$
(J s^2 + B s) X(s) + K_t K_p K_s X(s) = K_t K_s V_{in}(s)
$$

$$
X(s) \left[ J s^2 + B s + K_t K_p K_s \right] = K_t K_s V_{in}(s)
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{K_t K_s}{J s^2 + B s + K_t K_p K_s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**
> $$\boxed{\;\dfrac{X(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{K_{dc}}{1 + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} s + \dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2}}\;}$$
>
> where:
> - DC gain: $K_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{K_p}$
> - Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t K_p K_s}{J}}$
> - Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2 \sqrt{J \cdot K_t K_p K_s}}$

### **Part (b) — Null-Balance Linearity and Numerical Example (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Null-Balance Linearity (3 marks):**

In a **null-balance system**, the pen is driven until the error voltage $V_e$ becomes zero. At this point, the feedback voltage exactly equals the input voltage:

$$
V_{fb} = V_{in} \implies K_p \cdot x_{ss} = V_{in}
$$

Therefore:

$$
x_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p}
$$

This result depends **only on the input voltage and the potentiometer sensitivity $K_p$** — it is **completely independent** of:
- The amplifier gain $K_a$ (which may drift or be non-linear)
- The motor characteristics (torque constant, friction, armature resistance)
- The lead-screw precision (as long as the potentiometer is mechanically coupled to the pen)

This is the key advantage of the null-balance principle: **calibration depends on a single, stable, linear element (the potentiometer)**, while the motor, amplifier, and lead-screw can be mediocre without degrading accuracy.

**Numerical Example (4 marks):**

**Problem:** An X-Y recorder has the following specifications: $K_p = 0.05$ V/mm, $K_a = 5000$, $K_s = 5$ mm/rad, $K_t = 0.02$ Nm/V, $J = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m², $B = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ Nm·s/rad. For an input voltage $V_{in} = 8$ V, find:
(i) The steady-state pen position
(ii) The natural frequency of the servo
(iii) The damping ratio

**Solution:**

**(i) Steady-state pen position:**

$$
x_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{K_p} = \dfrac{8}{0.05} = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Natural frequency:**

First, compute $K_{loop} = K_t \cdot K_p \cdot K_s = 0.02 \times 0.05 \times 5 = 0.005$ N·m/mm·rad. Converting units and computing:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t K_p K_s}{J}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.005}{2 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Converting to Hz:

$$
f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{50}{2\pi} \approx 7.96 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(iii) Damping ratio:**

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2 \sqrt{J \cdot K_t K_p K_s}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.005}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-8}}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.05
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Many students confuse **"null-balance"** with **"open-loop"** — it is a **closed-loop** principle where the error is actively driven to zero.
> - The damping ratio $\zeta = 0.05$ in our example is **highly underdamped**, meaning the pen will oscillate before settling. In a well-designed recorder, $\zeta \approx 0.7$ to 1.0 is desired (critically damped). This requires **additional compensation networks** (PID or lead-lag) not shown in the basic derivation.
> - When computing the natural frequency, ensure **unit consistency** — mixing mm and m, or N and kg·m/s², will lead to a wrong answer and full-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** X-Y recorder = two-variable plotter with **stationary paper**, two independent servo-driven axes (X and Y).
- [ ] **Block diagram must include:** Attenuator → Chopper amplifier → Summing junction → Power amplifier → Servo motor → Lead screw → Pen → Feedback potentiometer (loop back to summing junction).
- [ ] **Two key subsystems:** X-axis servo + Y-axis servo (operating simultaneously and independently).
- [ ] **Core principle:** **Null-balance / servo feedback** — pen is driven until $V_e = 0$.
- [ ] **Steady-state formula:** $x_{ss} = V_{in}/K_p$ (independent of $K_a$, motor, lead screw).
- [ ] **Why chopper amplifier?** Eliminates **DC drift** in slowly varying signals.
- [ ] **Why feedback potentiometer?** Converts pen position to voltage for **closed-loop control**.
- [ ] **Transfer function:** Second-order with $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ determined by loop gain and inertia.
- [ ] **Static error:** $\text{Error}_{ss} \approx 1/K_{loop}$ for large open-loop gain.
- [ ] **Top 5 applications:** (1) B-H loop, (2) V-I characteristics, (3) Stress-strain, (4) Lissajous figures, (5) Pressure-Volume loops.
- [ ] **Comparison with strip-chart:** X-Y has **stationary paper + two servos**; strip-chart has **moving paper + one servo + time base**.
- [ ] **Accuracy class:** ± 0.25% to ± 0.5% of full scale.
- [ ] **Key advantage:** Linearity depends only on $K_p$, not on motor/amplifier imperfections.
- [ ] **Key limitation:** Low slew rate due to mechanical inertia — cannot track high-frequency signals.
- [ ] **Modern alternative:** Digital plotters and DAQ systems (but the X-Y recorder remains the **textbook standard** for null-balance servo analysis).
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** Do NOT say "paper moves at constant speed" for X-Y — that is the **strip-chart** definition.
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** Do NOT forget the **feedback path arrow** in the block diagram — examiners deduct heavily for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plotter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plotter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plotter** is an electro-mechanical output device that translates digital commands from a computer into precise, hard-copy graphical representations on paper (or similar media) by precisely controlling the motion of one or more pens across a writing surface. Unlike printers that work in a raster (line-by-line) fashion, plotters are fundamentally **vector-based** output devices, drawing images by creating continuous lines, curves, and geometric primitives from a sequence of coordinate-based drawing commands.

In the context of **Electrical and Electronic Instrumentation (PCBMT403)**, a plotter is classified as a **recording instrument** (Section 4 — Recorders). It is the most accurate hard-copy device used to **permanently document** the time-varying or spatially-varying output of measurement systems, transducers, signal analyzers, and process controllers. Plotters bridge the gap between an analog/digital measurement signal and a permanent, scaled physical trace on paper.

### Key Industry-Standard Specifications

The following physical and operational parameters are universally used to characterize any plotter (per ISO and IEC drafting standards):

- **Resolution:** Expressed in **dots per inch (dpi)** or **steps per millimeter**. Modern plotters achieve **0.0254 mm (0.001 inch)** mechanical resolution, equivalent to **1016 dpi**.
- **Pen Speed:** Maximum linear velocity of the pen carriage, typically **10 cm/s to 100 cm/s**.
- **Acceleration:** The rate of change of pen velocity, critical for sharp corners (typically **0.5g to 2g**).
- **Accuracy / Repeatability:** The deviation between the commanded position and the actual plotted position, typically **±0.05% of full scale** or **±0.05 mm**, whichever is greater.
- **Pen Pressure:** Force applied by the pen tip on the media, typically **10 g to 50 g** for ink pens and **50 g to 200 g** for ballpoint pens.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Artist"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Think of a plotter as a robotic artist's hand.**
> Imagine a perfectly steady human hand holding a pen, capable of drawing any line, curve, or letter that a computer "tells" it to draw. The computer says, *"Move to coordinate (x₁, y₁) and put the pen down,"* then *"Move to (x₂, y₂),"* then *"Lift the pen,"* then *"Move to (x₃, y₃)…"* The plotter does exactly this thousands of times per second, but with mathematical perfection — no hand tremor, no ink blot, and with sub-millimeter precision. The "brain" of the system is the host computer, and the "muscles" are the **stepper motors** (or servo motors) that physically move the pen and the paper.

The crucial difference between a plotter and a printer can be summarized as:

- A **printer** draws the entire page as a grid of tiny dots (raster scan), like a pointillist painter.
- A **plotter** draws only the actual lines and curves of the image (vector scan), like a draftsman with a T-square and set-square.

This makes plotters inherently superior for **engineering drawings, contour maps, PCB layouts, and waveform plots** where long, continuous, mathematically defined lines are required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Plotters

Plotters can be classified along two primary axes: **(a) the way the media is held** and **(b) the actuation technology**.

### 2.1.1 Drum (Roller) Plotters

In a **drum plotter**, the paper is mechanically gripped between two rollers (one drive roller and one pinch roller) and is moved back-and-forth along one axis (conventionally the Y-axis) as the pen carriage moves left-right along the perpendicular axis (X-axis). The plot area is theoretically **unlimited in the Y-direction** — you simply feed a longer paper roll.

**Working Logic — Step by Step:**

- The **drive roller** is coupled to a **stepper motor (M₁)** through a precision lead screw or timing belt. Each pulse to M₁ rotates the roller by a fixed angular step Δθ, which translates into a linear paper advance Δy on the paper.
- A second **stepper motor (M₂)** moves the **pen carriage** along a horizontal lead screw, providing X-axis motion.
- A third actuator (often a solenoid) raises and lowers the pen to start/stop drawing.
- The host computer sends a stream of **G-code-like commands** (e.g., HP-GL or HPGL/2), which the plotter's microcontroller interprets as incremental (Δx, Δy) moves.

> [!NOTE]
> **HP-GL (Hewlett-Packard Graphics Language)** is the de-facto industry-standard command language for plotters. Common commands include `PU` (Pen Up), `PD` (Pen Down), `PA x,y` (Plot Absolute), `PR Δx,Δy` (Plot Relative), and `SP n` (Select Pen n).

### 2.1.2 Flatbed (Bed) Plotters

In a **flatbed plotter**, the paper is held stationary (flat) on a fixed bed, and the pen carriage moves in **both X and Y directions** over the paper. Two independent motors drive two perpendicular lead screws, and the pen is mounted on a gantry that spans the width of the bed.

**Working Logic — Step by Step:**

- A **gantry beam** spans the entire width of the paper (X-axis), supported by two rails along the left and right edges.
- Motor M₁ moves the gantry horizontally (X-direction) via a lead screw running along the gantry.
- Motor M₂ moves the pen carriage along the gantry (Y-direction) via a second lead screw.
- Multiple pens (typically 4–8) are stored in a rotating turret, and the appropriate pen is rotated into position before drawing.

**Key Advantage over Drum Plotter:** Because the paper is held flat, **paper distortion and slippage are eliminated**, giving superior geometric accuracy — critical for **architectural blueprints and PCB artwork**.

### 2.1.3 X-Y Recorders (Analog Plotters)

This is the most important sub-type from an **instrumentation** perspective. An **X-Y recorder** is essentially a **flatbed plotter with no digital computer** — instead, the X and Y pen positions are directly driven by **two independent analog input voltages**, V_x(t) and V_y(t).

The pen therefore traces a real-time plot of **Y as a function of X** on the paper, exactly as a CRO displays Y vs X on a phosphor screen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** X-Y recorders are explicitly covered in the syllabus and frequently appear in exams. The relationship between input voltage and pen position is governed by a **DC servo loop** with position feedback (usually via a linear potentiometer or LVDT).

## 2.2 Actuation Technology — Servo vs. Stepper Drive

| Drive Type | Working Principle | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| **Stepper Motor Drive** | Open-loop; each pulse = one fixed angular step. Position inferred from pulse count. | Simple, no feedback sensors needed, low cost. | Can lose steps under high load → accumulated error. Limited speed and torque. |
| **DC Servo Drive** | Closed-loop with optical encoder / LVDT / potentiometer feedback. Motor driven until feedback = command. | High accuracy, no lost steps, faster response. | More complex, expensive, requires feedback transducer. |

The position control equation for a closed-loop servo plotter is:

$$
V_{command}(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where $e(t) = x_{desired}(t) - x_{actual}(t)$ is the position error, $K_p$ is the proportional gain, and $K_d$ is the derivative gain. This is a classical **PD (Proportional-Derivative) controller**, identical in form to the control law used in industrial robot arms.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula you must memorize** for the Plotter topic in the PCBMT403 KTU exam. Master these and you will comfortably score full marks in any calculation sub-part.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Where Used |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | $\Delta y = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi \cdot D}{S \cdot n}$ | Paper advance per pulse | mm / step | Drum plotter resolution |
| 2 | $\Delta x = \dfrac{p_{lead}}{N_{steps/rev}}$ | Pen carriage advance per pulse | mm / step | Lead screw step size |
| 3 | $Resolution = \dfrac{25.4}{\Delta x}$ | Dots per inch | dpi | Spec comparison |
| 4 | $v_{pen} = f_{pulse} \cdot \Delta x$ | Pen linear velocity | mm/s | Speed calculations |
| 5 | $\%FS\;error = \dfrac{e_{max}}{FS} \times 100$ | Full-scale accuracy | % | Specification check |
| 6 | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{command}}{K_p \cdot K_{motor}}$ | Steady-state position error | mm | Servo analysis |
| 7 | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{total}}{J_{eq}}}$ | Natural frequency of pen | rad/s | Step response |
| 8 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B_{eq}}{2\sqrt{K_{total}\cdot J_{eq}}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | Stability |
| 9 | $P_{draw} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot F_{pen} \cdot v_{pen}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | Drawing power | Watts | Energy budget |
| 10 | $t_{plot} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{v_{pen}} \cdot k$ | Total plotting time | seconds | Time estimation |

> **Symbol legend:** $N$ = number of pulses, $D$ = roller diameter, $S$ = gear ratio, $n$ = number of pulses per revolution, $p_{lead}$ = lead screw pitch, $f_{pulse}$ = pulse frequency, $FS$ = full scale, $K$ = stiffness/gain, $J$ = moment of inertia, $B$ = damping coefficient, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $F$ = force, $\eta$ = efficiency, $L$ = total line length, $k$ = corner/acceleration penalty factor (typically 1.2–1.5).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Plotting stress-strain curves, S-N fatigue curves, and thermodynamic cycles (P-V, T-S diagrams).
- **Civil Engineering:** Plotting load-deflection curves of structural members, moment-curvature diagrams.
- **Electronics Engineering:** Generating **PCB artwork masters**, plotting Bode plots, Nyquist plots, and antenna radiation patterns.
- **Process Control:** Strip-chart recording of temperature, pressure, flow, and level over weeks/months.
- **Medical Instrumentation:** Plotting ECG, EEG, and EMG waveforms on calibrated paper.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains the **complete, exhaustive, line-by-line mathematical derivations** that KTU examiners love to ask as 7-mark sub-parts. Work through these slowly — they form the spine of every numerical question on Plotters.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolution of a Stepper-Driven Drum Plotter

**Problem Setup:** A drum plotter uses a stepper motor that produces **200 steps per revolution**. The motor drives the paper roller (diameter $D = 40$ mm) through a **gear reduction of $S = 5:1$** (motor turns 5 times for every 1 turn of the roller). The roller is directly coupled to the paper.

**Find:** The linear resolution (mm/step) of the paper advance.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the effective steps per revolution of the roller.

The motor makes 5 revolutions for every 1 revolution of the roller. Therefore, the number of motor steps that result in **one full revolution of the roller** is:

$$
N_{roller} = N_{motor} \times S = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \; \text{steps/rev}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the circumference of the roller.

The paper travels a distance equal to the roller's circumference for one full revolution:

$$
C = \pi \times D = \pi \times 40 = 125.664 \; \text{mm}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the linear advance per step.

Since the roller makes exactly $N_{roller} = 1000$ steps to advance the paper by one full circumference $C$:

$$
\Delta y = \frac{C}{N_{roller}} = \frac{125.664}{1000} = 0.12566 \; \text{mm/step}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to dpi for spec comparison.

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{25.4 \; \text{mm/inch}}{\Delta y} = \frac{25.4}{0.12566} = 202.1 \; \text{dpi}
$$

**Conclusion:** The paper advance resolution is **≈ 0.1257 mm/step (≈ 202 dpi)**. This means a vertical line drawn on the plot will have a "staircase" quantisation step of 0.1257 mm — invisible to the naked eye but quantifiable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pen Carriage Resolution of a Flatbed Plotter

**Problem Setup:** A flatbed plotter uses a stepper motor (200 steps/rev) coupled directly (no gears) to a lead screw of **pitch 4 mm**. The lead screw drives the pen carriage. A second identical lead screw drives the gantry in the Y-direction.

**Find:** (a) The X-axis resolution, (b) The Y-axis resolution, (c) The total number of steps required to draw a 100 mm × 100 mm square.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the linear advance per motor pulse.

For a lead screw, **one full revolution of the screw advances the carriage by exactly one pitch**:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p_{lead}}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{4 \; \text{mm}}{200} = 0.02 \; \text{mm/step}
$$

Step 2 — Since the Y-axis uses an identical lead screw:

$$
\Delta y = \frac{p_{lead}}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{4}{200} = 0.02 \; \text{mm/step}
$$

Step 3 — Express resolution in dpi:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{25.4}{0.02} = 1270 \; \text{dpi}
$$

This is a high-precision flatbed plotter — typical of PCB artwork generation systems.

Step 4 — Compute total steps for a 100 mm × 100 mm square.

A square has 4 sides. Each side is 100 mm long. Number of steps per side:

$$
N_{side} = \frac{100 \; \text{mm}}{0.02 \; \text{mm/step}} = 5000 \; \text{steps}
$$

Total steps for the 4 sides:

$$
N_{total} = 4 \times 5000 = 20{,}000 \; \text{steps}
$$

Plus 4 corner transitions (pen up, move, pen down) — each requiring 1 extra step pair — so practically:

$$
N_{practical} = 20{,}000 + 4 \times 2 = 20{,}008 \; \text{steps}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 mm square requires **20,008 motor steps** to be drawn on this flatbed plotter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Pen Speed from Stepper Pulse Frequency

**Problem Setup:** The stepper motor of the flatbed plotter in Derivation 2 can be pulsed at a **maximum rate of 5000 pulses per second (pps)**. Find the maximum pen carriage velocity.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Relate pulse frequency to linear speed.

The pen moves one step (0.02 mm) for every pulse. Therefore, the linear velocity is:

$$
v_{pen} = f_{pulse} \times \Delta x
$$

Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.

$$
v_{pen} = 5000 \; \text{pps} \times 0.02 \; \text{mm/step}
$$

Step 3 — Compute.

$$
v_{pen} = 100 \; \text{mm/s} = 10 \; \text{cm/s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum pen speed is **100 mm/s (10 cm/s)**. Above this, the stepper motor would "skip" pulses due to insufficient torque to overcome inertia, leading to lost steps and corrupted drawings.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Plotting Time Estimation

**Problem Setup:** A CAD drawing contains **L = 50 metres** of total line length. The plotter is a flatbed with $v_{pen} = 100$ mm/s, and on average each line segment ends with a corner that requires a **stop-accelerate-decelerate** cycle taking an additional **30%** of the straight-line time. Find the total plotting time.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert line length to consistent units.

$$
L = 50 \; \text{m} = 50{,}000 \; \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the theoretical straight-line plotting time.

$$
t_{straight} = \frac{L}{v_{pen}} = \frac{50{,}000}{100} = 500 \; \text{s}
$$

Step 3 — Add the corner penalty.

Using the penalty factor $k = 1.30$:

$$
t_{plot} = t_{straight} \times k = 500 \times 1.30 = 650 \; \text{s}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to minutes for convenience.

$$
t_{plot} = \frac{650}{60} = 10.83 \; \text{minutes} \approx 10 \; \text{min}\;50 \; \text{sec}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 50-metre drawing will take approximately **10 minutes 50 seconds** to plot.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Steady-State Position Error of Servo Plotter

**Problem Setup:** An X-Y servo plotter uses a DC servo motor with torque constant $K_m = 0.05 \; \text{N·m/A}$ and is connected through a gear train of ratio $N_g = 50$ to the pen carriage. The lead screw pitch is $p = 2$ mm. A PD controller is used with $K_p = 100$ (V/mm) and $K_d = 10$ (V·s/mm). The pen carriage mass is $m = 0.5$ kg and viscous friction is $B = 5$ N·s/m.

**Find:** The steady-state position error when a step command of 10 mm is applied.

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — The transfer function approach.

For a second-order mass-spring-damper system with PD control, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
\frac{X(s)}{X_d(s)} = \frac{K_p K_m N_g \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\right)}{m s^2 + B s + K_p K_m N_g \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\right) + K_d K_m N_g \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\right) s}
$$

Step 2 — For a **step input** of magnitude $X_d$, apply the **Final Value Theorem** to find the steady-state position:

$$
x_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot X(s) = X_d
$$

(For a Type-1 system with PD control, the steady-state error to a step input is theoretically **zero**.)

Step 3 — However, in **practical non-ideal systems**, factors like static friction ($F_s$) and motor dead-zone cause a small residual error. The error is given by:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{F_s}{K_{loop\;gain}} = \frac{F_s}{K_p \cdot K_m \cdot N_g \cdot \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{p}\right)}
$$

Step 4 — Suppose $F_s = 1$ N (typical coulomb friction). Compute:

$$
K_{loop} = 100 \times 0.05 \times 50 \times \frac{2\pi}{2} = 100 \times 0.05 \times 50 \times 3.1416
$$

Step 5 — Multiply stepwise:

$$
K_{loop} = 100 \times 0.05 = 5
$$
$$
K_{loop} = 5 \times 50 = 250
$$
$$
K_{loop} = 250 \times 3.1416 = 785.4
$$

Step 6 — Compute the steady-state error:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{1 \; \text{N}}{785.4 \; \text{N/mm}} = 1.273 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm} = 1.273 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The steady-state position error is **1.273 µm**, which is well below the resolution (20 µm) and therefore practically indistinguishable from perfect tracking. This is why **closed-loop servo plotters** are preferred for high-precision work like IC mask generation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete X-Y Plotter / Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Vx and Vy] --> B[Signal Conditioner and Attenuator]
    B --> C[Reference Voltage Source]
    C --> D[Error Detector and Comparator]
    D --> E[DC Servo Amplifier PD Controller]
    E --> F1[X axis Servo Motor Mx]
    E --> F2[Y axis Servo Motor My]
    F1 --> G1[Lead Screw X axis]
    F2 --> G2[Lead Screw Y axis]
    G1 --> H1[Pen Carriage X position]
    G2 --> H2[Pen Carriage Y position]
    H1 --> I1[Position Feedback LVDT or Potentiometer X]
    H2 --> I2[Position Feedback LVDT or Potentiometer Y]
    I1 --> D
    I2 --> D
    H1 --> J[Pen Tip on Paper]
    H2 --> J
    J --> K[Hard Copy Plot Y vs X]

    style I1 fill:#A5F3FC

```

**Reading the diagram:** Two analog input voltages $V_x$ and $V_y$ enter the system. After signal conditioning, they are compared against the feedback from LVDT/potentiometer position sensors. The error is amplified by a PD controller, which drives two independent DC servo motors. The motors drive lead screws that move the pen carriage in X and Y. The pen tip traces the curve on paper, producing a permanent hard-copy plot.
