# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Differentiate between a printer and a plotter. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Printer | Plotter |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Drawing method | Raster (line-by-line dots) | Vector (continuous lines) |
| Image quality | Quantised by dot size | Mathematically smooth curves |
| Cost per line | High (inkjet/laser toner) | Low (single pen stroke) |
| Best for | Text, photographs | CAD drawings, charts, PCB artwork |
| Color | Multiple cartridges | Multiple pens (turret) |
| Resolution | 300–2400 dpi | 1000+ dpi (effective) |

A plotter is preferred for engineering drawings because it draws continuous mathematically-defined lines, whereas a printer approximates lines using dots.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. List any three advantages of a flatbed plotter over a drum plotter. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher geometric accuracy** — paper is held flat and stationary, so there is no slippage or stretching distortion during plotting.
2. **Multi-pen capability** — the rotating pen turret allows quick switching between 4 to 8 pens of different colors and line widths without manual intervention.
3. **Better for thick or rigid media** — flatbed plotters can plot on cardboard, mylar, or even sheet metal, whereas drum plotters are limited to flexible paper rolls.
4. **Higher precision** — typically ±0.025 mm versus ±0.1 mm for drum plotters.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 Internal Choice]**

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **X-Y recorder**. Discuss the role of the servo amplifier and position feedback. (7 Marks)

**A(b)** A drum plotter uses a stepper motor of **1.8° per step** (i.e., 200 steps/rev) driving a paper roller of **diameter 60 mm** through a **gear ratio of 4:1**. The lead screw driving the pen carriage has a **pitch of 5 mm** and is driven by a similar stepper motor directly. Find:
- (i) Resolution of paper advance in mm/step
- (ii) Resolution of pen carriage in mm/step
- (iii) Maximum pen speed if the stepper is pulsed at **4000 pps**
- (iv) Time to draw a triangle with sides 80 mm, 100 mm, 80 mm. (7 Marks)

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**B(a)** Compare **drum plotters and flatbed plotters** in detail, with neat diagrams. Discuss the application areas where each is preferred. (7 Marks)

**B(b)** A closed-loop servo plotter has a PD controller with $K_p = 80$ V/mm and $K_d = 5$ V·s/mm. The motor torque constant is $K_m = 0.04$ N·m/A, gear ratio $N_g = 40$, lead screw pitch $p = 2$ mm, carriage mass $m = 0.4$ kg, viscous damping $B = 4$ N·s/m, and static friction $F_s = 0.8$ N. Find:
- (i) The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$
- (ii) The steady-state position error for a step input
- (iii) Comment on stability. (7 Marks)

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### ✅ Solution to Question A(a) — X-Y Recorder

An **X-Y recorder** is an analog recording instrument that plots one variable (Y) as a function of another variable (X) on a rectangular sheet of paper. The two input signals are analog voltages $V_x$ and $V_y$.

**Construction:**

1. **Chart bed** — a flat rectangular plate (A3 or A4 size) on which the recording paper is clamped.
2. **X-axis carriage** — moves horizontally (left-right) along precision guide rails.
3. **Y-axis carriage** — mounted on the X carriage, moves vertically (up-down) perpendicular to X.
4. **Pen assembly** — mounted on the Y carriage; uses a capillary/ink pen or heated stylus on thermal paper.
5. **Two DC servo motors** — one for each axis, coupled to the carriage through lead screws.
6. **Two position feedback transducers** — linear potentiometers (or LVDTs) provide a voltage proportional to actual carriage position.
7. **Reference voltage source** — provides a stable DC reference for comparison.
8. **Two servo amplifiers** — one for each axis, implementing the PD control law.

**Working:**

- The X input voltage $V_x$ is attenuated and compared with the X-feedback voltage $V_{fx}$ from the X-axis potentiometer.
- The error $e_x = V_x - V_{fx}$ is amplified by the X-servo amplifier and applied to the X-axis DC motor.
- The X motor rotates the X lead screw, which moves the X carriage (and with it, the Y carriage and pen) horizontally until $V_{fx} = V_x$ and the error becomes zero.
- Simultaneously, the Y input voltage $V_y$ drives the Y servo loop, moving the pen vertically.
- The pen therefore traces the curve $Y = f(X)$ on the paper in real time.

**Role of Servo Amplifier:** The servo amplifier performs three functions:
- **Error amplification** — multiplies the small error signal (often in mV) to a power level (several watts) capable of driving the motor.
- **Phase compensation** — provides the derivative term to prevent overshoot and oscillation.
- **Power amplification** — delivers the motor current needed to overcome friction and inertia.

**Role of Position Feedback:** The linear potentiometer (or LVDT) provides a voltage proportional to actual pen position. This closes the loop, ensuring:
- **Accuracy** — pen position always matches the input voltage within a small steady-state error.
- **Linearity** — the relationship between $V_x$ and pen position is linear, not dependent on motor characteristics.
- **Stability** — feedback prevents runaway behaviour and damps out oscillations.

**(7 marks — full credit)** 

---

### ✅ Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical

**Given data:**
- Stepper motor step angle: $1.8°/step$ → $N_{steps/rev} = 360/1.8 = 200$ steps/rev
- Roller diameter: $D = 60$ mm
- Gear ratio: $S = 4:1$ (motor turns 4 times per 1 roller turn)
- Lead screw pitch: $p = 5$ mm
- Pulse frequency: $f = 4000$ pps

**(i) Resolution of paper advance:**

Step 1 — Effective steps per roller revolution:

$$
N_{roller} = 200 \times 4 = 800 \; \text{steps/rev}
$$

Step 2 — Roller circumference:

$$
C = \pi D = \pi \times 60 = 188.496 \; \text{mm}
$$

Step 3 — Linear advance per step:

$$
\Delta y = \frac{C}{N_{roller}} = \frac{188.496}{800} = 0.2356 \; \text{mm/step}
$$

**(ii) Resolution of pen carriage:**

Step 1 — Lead screw directly coupled to stepper (no gears):

$$
\Delta x = \frac{p_{lead}}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{5}{200} = 0.025 \; \text{mm/step}
$$

**(iii) Maximum pen speed:**

$$
v_{pen} = f_{pulse} \times \Delta x = 4000 \times 0.025 = 100 \; \text{mm/s} = 10 \; \text{cm/s}
$$

**(iv) Time to draw the triangle:**

Step 1 — Total perimeter:

$$
L = 80 + 100 + 80 = 260 \; \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Straight-line time (no corner penalty assumed):

$$
t = \frac{L}{v_{pen}} = \frac{260}{100} = 2.6 \; \text{s}
$$

If a corner penalty of $k = 1.3$ is considered (3 corners):

$$
t = 2.6 \times 1.3 = 3.38 \; \text{s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\Delta y = 0.2356$ mm/step
- (ii) $\Delta x = 0.025$ mm/step
- (iii) $v_{pen,max} = 100$ mm/s
- (iv) $t \approx 2.6$ s (or 3.38 s with corner penalty)

**(7 marks — full credit)**

---

### ✅ Solution to Question B(a) — Comparison

| Parameter | Drum Plotter | Flatbed Plotter |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| Paper handling | Continuous roll gripped between two rollers | Stationary sheet clamped on flat bed |
| X-axis motion | Pen carriage slides on horizontal rail | Gantry beam spans the width |
| Y-axis motion | Paper rolls under carriage | Pen carriage slides along gantry |
| Plotting area in Y | Unlimited (limited by roll length) | Limited by bed size (A1 to A0 max) |
| Accuracy | Lower (paper slip and stretch) | Higher (no paper motion) |
| Pen system | Single or dual pen | Multi-pen rotating turret (4 to 8 pens) |
| Speed | Faster (paper and pen move together) | Slower (large masses to accelerate) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Footprint | Compact | Bulky |
| Best applications | Long seismic traces, strip charts, GIS maps | PCB artwork, IC masks, mechanical CAD |
| Media variety | Limited to flexible rolls | Can handle rigid sheets, mylar, cardboard |

**Application Preferences:**

- **Drum plotters are preferred for:** Seismograph recording, weather data strip charts, large GIS contour maps, where continuous long traces are needed.
- **Flatbed plotters are preferred for:** PCB master artwork, VLSI mask generation, architectural blueprints, mechanical engineering CAD drawings, where geometric accuracy is paramount.

**(7 marks — full credit)**

---

### ✅ Solution to Question B(b) — Servo Analysis

**Given data:** $K_p = 80$ V/mm, $K_d = 5$ V·s/mm, $K_m = 0.04$ N·m/A, $N_g = 40$, $p = 2$ mm, $m = 0.4$ kg, $B = 4$ N·s/m, $F_s = 0.8$ N.

Step 1 — Compute the equivalent motor-to-carriage gain:

$$
K_{mc} = K_m \cdot N_g \cdot \frac{2\pi}{p} = 0.04 \times 40 \times \frac{2\pi}{2} = 0.04 \times 40 \times 3.1416 = 5.027 \; \text{N/mm}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the equivalent stiffness:

$$
K_{total} = K_p \cdot K_{mc} = 80 \times 5.027 = 402.12 \; \text{N/mm} = 402120 \; \text{N/m}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the natural frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_{total}}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{402120}{0.4}} = \sqrt{1005300} = 1002.6 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

Convert to Hz:

$$
f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1002.6}{2\pi} = 159.6 \; \text{Hz}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the damping ratio.

The effective damping coefficient with PD control is:

$$
B_{eff} = B + K_d \cdot K_{mc} = 4 + 5 \times 5.027 = 4 + 25.13 = 29.13 \; \text{N·s/m}
$$

Then:

$$
\zeta = \frac{B_{eff}}{2\sqrt{K_{total} \cdot m}} = \frac{29.13}{2\sqrt{402120 \times 0.4}} = \frac{29.13}{2 \times 401.06} = \frac{29.13}{802.12} = 0.0363
$$

Step 5 — Steady-state error for step input (under static friction):

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{F_s}{K_{total}} = \frac{0.8}{402.12} = 1.989 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm} \approx 1.99 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

Step 6 — Comment on stability.

The damping ratio $\zeta = 0.0363 \ll 1$, indicating the system is **severely underdamped**. The step response will exhibit large overshoot and prolonged ringing oscillations. The system is **marginally stable** (oscillatory but bounded).

To improve damping, the derivative gain $K_d$ should be increased. For critical damping ($\zeta = 1$), we would need:

$$
B_{eff,crit} = 2\sqrt{K_{total} \cdot m} = 2 \times 401.06 = 802.12 \; \text{N·s/m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
K_{d,crit} = \frac{802.12 - 4}{5.027} = 158.6 \; \text{V·s/mm}
$$

This is a 32× increase from the current $K_d = 5$, which would dramatically improve transient response.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\omega_n = 1002.6$ rad/s (≈ 159.6 Hz), $\zeta = 0.0363$ (severely underdamped)
- (ii) $e_{ss} \approx 1.99$ µm
- (iii) System is underdamped and oscillatory; increase $K_d$ to ≈ 158.6 V·s/mm for critical damping.

**(7 marks — full credit)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–3 marks per question in Plotter problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the gear ratio in stepper-driven plotters.** If a gear ratio $S$ is given, remember that the roller/load makes only **1 revolution per S motor revolutions** — multiply the steps/rev by $S$, don't divide.
> 2. **Confusing roller diameter with roller radius.** The linear paper advance per revolution is $\pi D$, not $\pi r$. Many students write $\pi r$ and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring the units of $K_p$ in the damping formula.** $K_p$ is in V/mm, $K_m$ in N·m/A, $K_d$ in V·s/mm. You must convert all to consistent SI units (N, m, s) before computing $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without the feedback path.** A plotter block diagram WITHOUT the LVDT/potentiometer feedback line is marked incomplete. Always draw the feedback loop with an explicit arrow back to the comparator.
> 5. **Conflating "pen speed" with "pulse rate".** These are linked by the step size: $v = f \times \Delta x$. Students often write $v = f$ alone, losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Not specifying HP-GL/HPGL-2** when asked about plotter command languages — this is a favourite 1-mark direct question.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention PD control.** A servo plotter's amplifier is a **PD controller**, not just a P controller. The derivative term is essential for damping.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision: cover these 15 points and you are examination-ready.**

- ☐ A **plotter** is a vector-based electro-mechanical output device that produces hard-copy drawings by drawing continuous lines under computer control.
- ☐ Plotters are classified as **drum (roller)**, **flatbed (bed)**, and **X-Y recorder** types.
- ☐ **Drum plotter** — paper moves in Y, pen moves in X. Paper fed from a roll; unlimited Y length.
- ☐ **Flatbed plotter** — paper is stationary; both pen and gantry move. Higher accuracy, multi-pen turret.
- ☐ **X-Y recorder** — analog input plotter, drives the pen with two DC servo motors from voltages $V_x$ and $V_y$.
- ☐ **Stepper motor drive** = open-loop, no feedback, risk of lost steps.
- ☐ **DC servo drive** = closed-loop with LVDT/potentiometer feedback, high accuracy.
- ☐ **HP-GL / HPGL-2** is the standard plotter command language (`PU`, `PD`, `PA`, `PR`, `SP`).
- ☐ Resolution formula: $\Delta x = p_{lead}/N_{steps/rev}$ for lead screw; $\Delta y = \pi D \cdot S / N_{steps/rev}$ for drum roller.
- ☐ Pen speed: $v_{pen} = f_{pulse} \times \Delta x$.
- ☐ Plotting time: $t_{plot} = (L/v_{pen}) \times k$, where $k \approx 1.2-1.5$ accounts for corner penalties.
- ☐ Servo control law: $V_{command} = K_p \cdot e + K_d \cdot \dot{e}$ (PD controller).
- ☐ Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_{total}/m}$; Damping ratio: $\zeta = B_{eff}/(2\sqrt{K_{total} \cdot m})$.
- ☐ Steady-state error to step input (ideal): zero (Type-1 system); practical limit is set by static friction.
- ☐ Typical specs: resolution **20–100 µm**, accuracy **±0.05% FS**, pen speed **10–100 cm/s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** is a flat-panel, electronically modulated optical device that uses the light-modulating properties of **liquid crystals (LCs)** — a state of matter that exhibits properties intermediate between those of conventional isotropic liquids and solid crystalline materials — to produce images, alphanumeric characters, or graphical data. The liquid crystals themselves do not emit light; instead, they rely on either an external light source (**backlight**) or ambient light reflection to render information visible to the observer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LCD is classified under the *Electronic Display Devices* unit of PCBMT403. The board examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **passive matrix** (TN, STN) and **active matrix** (TFT) addressing, and articulate the **electro-optical effect** that drives light modulation.

### 1.2 The Four Fundamental Phases of Liquid Crystals

Liquid crystals are unique organic compounds that exist in a distinct mesophase (a phase between solid and liquid). The most important phases used in display technology are:

| Phase | Full Form | Optical Behavior | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smectic** | "Soapy" layered | Highly ordered, layered molecular structure | Smectic displays, ferroelectric LCDs |
| **Nematic** | "Thread-like" | Positional order absent, orientational order present | **TN, STN, IPS LCDs (most displays)** |
| **Cholesteric** | "Chiral nematic" | Helical structure with selective Bragg reflection | Reflective color displays, e-paper |
| **Isotropic** | True liquid | Random orientation | — (Non-display phase, occurs above clearing point) |

> [!NOTE]
> The phase transition temperature at which the LC becomes isotropic is called the **Clearing Point** (typically **80°C – 100°C** for standard mixtures like **E7** or **5CB**). Exceeding this temperature permanently destroys display function — a common failure mode in field instrumentation.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Venetian Blind" Effect

Imagine a row of Venetian blinds hanging in front of a window. When the slats are aligned (parallel to the floor), light passes through. When you twist the slats (rotate them by 90°), they block the light. Liquid crystal molecules work in a remarkably similar way:

- **OFF State (No Voltage):** The LC molecules are aligned in a helical (twisted) configuration. They *gradually rotate* the polarization of incoming polarized light, allowing it to pass through the second polarizer. → **Pixel appears BRIGHT**
- **ON State (Voltage Applied):** The electric field forces the LC molecules to align vertically (perpendicular to the glass). They *no longer twist* the light's polarization, so the second polarizer blocks it. → **Pixel appears DARK**

This is the heart of the **Twisted Nematic (TN) Effect**, the foundational technology of virtually all modern LCDs.

### 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

LCDs were first developed at the **RCA David Sarnoff Research Center** in **1963** by George Heilmeier. They revolutionized the display industry because of their inherent advantages over **Cathode Ray Tubes (CRTs)**: drastically lower power consumption (**1–10 mW per cm²** vs **100 mW per cm²** for CRT), slim form factor, and absence of geometric distortion. Today, LCDs dominate consumer electronics, industrial HMIs, medical instrumentation, and automotive dashboards.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Physics: The Electro-Optical Effect

The LCD operates on a **field-induced birefringence** principle. The key physical parameters governing its operation are:

1. **Dielectric Anisotropy ($\Delta\epsilon$)**: The difference between the dielectric constants measured parallel and perpendicular to the LC molecular long axis.
$$\Delta\epsilon = \epsilon_\parallel - \epsilon_\perp$$
For the commonly used nematic mixture **E7**: $\epsilon_\parallel = 19.0$, $\epsilon_\perp = 5.2$, giving $\Delta\epsilon = +13.8$ (positive dielectric anisotropy — required for parallel alignment to the field).

2. **Optical Birefringence ($\Delta n$)**: The difference between the extraordinary and ordinary refractive indices. This is the parameter that enables polarization rotation.
$$\Delta n = n_e - n_o$$
Typical values range from **0.06 to 0.20**. For the **Gooch-Tarry first minimum condition** of a TN cell, the product $\Delta n \cdot d$ is engineered to satisfy:
$$\Delta n \cdot d = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\lambda \approx 0.866\lambda$$
where $d$ is the cell gap (typically **3 µm – 10 µm**) and $\lambda$ is the design wavelength (typically **550 nm** for green light, to which the human eye is most sensitive).

3. **Frederiks Transition Voltage (Threshold Voltage $V_{th}$)**: The minimum RMS voltage at which the LC directors begin to reorient. It is given by:
$$V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{K}{\epsilon_0 \cdot \Delta\epsilon}}$$
where $K$ is the elastic constant of the LC (typically **$K \approx 10^{-11}$ N**). This yields a typical $V_{th} \approx 1.0$ – $1.5$ V RMS.

### 2.2 Construction: A Layer-by-Layer Anatomy

An LCD cell is fabricated as a sandwich of precisely aligned thin films:

1. **Substrates (Top & Bottom):** Two sheets of optically flat glass (or flexible plastic in modern variants), typically **0.5 mm – 1.1 mm** thick.
2. **Transparent Electrodes:** Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) is patterned on the inner surfaces of the glass. ITO is chosen because it offers a rare combination of $> 80\%$ optical transparency in the visible spectrum and electrical conductivity of approximately $\sigma \approx 10^4 \ \Omega^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$.
3. **Alignment Layers:** A thin polyimide layer (e.g., **SE-7492**) is rubbed unidirectionally with a velvet cloth. This produces microscopic grooves that anchor the LC directors in a defined orientation. The rubbing directions on the top and bottom plates are perpendicular, creating the **90° twist** characteristic of the TN cell.
4. **Spacers:** Micro-spheres or cylindrical glass fibers of precisely controlled diameter ($3$ – $10 \ \mu\text{m}$) maintain uniform cell gap.
5. **Liquid Crystal Layer:** Nematic material (e.g., **5CB, E7, ZLI-2293**) injected via capillary action in vacuum.
6. **Polarizers:** Two linear polarizer films oriented at **90°** to each other, one on each outer surface.
7. **Backlight Unit (BLU):** For transmissive displays, an edge-lit or direct-lit array of LEDs, often combined with a light guide plate (LGP), diffuser, and brightness enhancement films (BEFs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The optical **contrast ratio** of a TN-LCD is defined as the luminance ratio of the bright (off) state to the dark (on) state:
$$\text{CR} = \frac{L_{\text{on}}}{L_{\text{off}}}$$
A high-quality TN panel achieves CR $\geq 500:1$; premium IPS panels exceed **1500:1**.

### 2.3 Active Matrix vs. Passive Matrix Addressing

This is a critical KTU examination point. Displays are addressed by controlling the voltage across each pixel:

| Parameter | Passive Matrix (PM-LCD) | Active Matrix (AM-LCD / TFT-LCD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching Element** | None (pixels share row/column electrodes) | Thin-Film Transistor (TFT) per pixel |
| **Addressing** | Time-multiplexed (rows scanned sequentially) | Each pixel driven independently |
| **Capable Resolution** | Limited (typically $< 400 \times 400$ lines) | Very high ($4\text{K} = 3840 \times 2160$ and beyond) |
| **Refresh Rate Limitation** | Strong (cross-talk at high multiplexing) | Minimal (charge held on pixel capacitor) |
| **Viewing Angle** | Narrow ($\pm 45°$ typical) | Wide ($\pm 89°$ for IPS) |
| **Contrast Ratio** | $20:1$ – $50:1$ | $1000:1$ – $5000:1$ |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower (no TFT leakage) | Slightly higher |
| **Cost per Pixel** | Very low | Higher (complex fab process) |
| **Example Applications** | Calculators, digital watches, simple HMIs | Smartphones, monitors, TVs, medical displays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TFT Channel Material Evolution (KTU Trivia):**
> - **a-Si:H** (Amorphous Silicon) — dominant in mass-market displays, low mobility ($\mu \approx 0.5 \ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$).
> - **LTPS** (Low-Temperature Polycrystalline Silicon) — higher mobility ($\mu \approx 50 \ \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$), used in high-resolution smartphone displays.
> - **IGZO** (Indium Gallium Zinc Oxide) — emerging technology, ultra-low leakage current, ideal for 4K/8K TVs and OLED backplanes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Relation** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\epsilon_0 \Delta\epsilon}}$ | Frederiks threshold voltage | $1.0$ – $1.5$ V RMS |
| $\Delta\epsilon = \epsilon_\parallel - \epsilon_\perp$ | Dielectric anisotropy | $+10$ to $+15$ (E7) |
| $\Delta n = n_e - n_o$ | Optical birefringence | $0.06$ – $0.20$ |
| $\Delta n \cdot d = 0.866 \lambda$ | Mauguin condition (1st min) | $d \approx 4.7 \ \mu\text{m}$ at $\lambda = 550$ nm |
| $\text{CR} = L_{\text{on}} / L_{\text{off}}$ | Contrast ratio | $500:1$ (TN), $1500:1$ (IPS) |
| $\tau_{on} \propto \dfrac{\eta \cdot d^2}{K \cdot \pi^2}$ | LC response time (turn-on) | $10$ – $30$ ms (TN), $< 5$ ms (IPS/OLED) |
| $\eta_{lc} = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Display power efficiency | $5$ – $10\%$ (LCD w/ BLU) |
| $f_{refresh} \geq 60 \ \text{Hz}$ | Flicker-free refresh rate | $60$ – $144$ Hz (gaming) |
| $\theta_{view} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta n \cdot d}{\lambda}\right)$ | Half-angle viewing cone | $\pm 45°$ (TN), $\pm 89°$ (IPS) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Students:** In the formula sheet above, **do not confuse** the **dielectric anisotropy** $\Delta\epsilon$ (a property of the LC *electrical* response) with the **optical birefringence** $\Delta n$ (a property of the LC *optical* response). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial instrumentation, LCDs serve as:
- **Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs)** in PLCs, VFDs, and CNC controllers — where wide temperature tolerance ($-40°\text{C}$ to $+85°\text{C}$) and sunlight readability are paramount.
- **Medical Monitors** (patient vital signs, infusion pumps) — where IPS panels provide accurate grayscale rendering for diagnostic imaging.
- **Automotive Instrument Clusters** — where TN-TFT displays withstand high-vibration environments and offer fast response ($< 5$ ms) for real-time speed/RPM indication.
- **Avionics & Defense** — where ruggedized transflective LCDs (a hybrid of reflective and transmissive) operate reliably under direct sunlight without backlight power drain.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Mauguin Condition for Twisted Nematic Cells

We derive the relationship between LC birefringence, cell gap, and the wavelength of light that enables the half-wave plate behavior critical to TN-LCD operation.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Jones Matrix Problem.**

A plane-polarized light wave $\mathbf{E}_{in} = \begin{pmatrix} E_0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$ enters a TN cell whose optical axis twists linearly from $0$ to $\pi/2$ across the cell gap $d$. We seek the condition under which the wave emerges linearly polarized, having followed the local optical axis.

**Step 2 — Gooch-Tarry First Minimum Condition.**

For a twisted nematic cell to act as a perfect polarization rotator, the **Mauguin condition** must be satisfied:

$$\Delta n \cdot d \gg \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

The exact optimum, where the transmitted intensity reaches a *local maximum* (analyzer transmission peak), is given by the Gooch-Tarry condition:

$$\Delta n \cdot d = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation for Standard Design.**

Substituting $\lambda = 550 \ \text{nm} = 5.50 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m}$:

$$\Delta n \cdot d = 0.866 \times 5.50 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m} = 4.763 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m} = 476.3 \ \text{nm}$$

For a typical liquid crystal with $\Delta n = 0.10$:

$$d = \frac{4.763 \times 10^{-7}}{0.10} = 4.763 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} \approx 4.76 \ \mu\text{m}$$

This is the standard **cell gap** used in the vast majority of commercial TN-LCDs.

**Step 4 — Why the First Minimum?**

The Gooch-Tarry series $\Delta n \cdot d = \frac{\sqrt{4k^2 - 1}}{2}\lambda$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ gives transmission peaks. The $k=1$ first minimum is preferred because:
- Smaller $d$ → faster LC response time (since $\tau \propto d^2$).
- Lower required $\Delta n$ → broader material selection.
- Lower operating voltage (since $V_{th} \propto 1/d$).

### 3.2 Derivation: Response Time of an LCD Pixel

**Step 1 — The Governing Equation.**

The reorientation dynamics of the LC director under an applied field are governed by the **Ericksen-Leslie equation**. For a simplified small-angle approximation, the characteristic turn-on time is:

$$\tau_{on} = \frac{\eta}{\Delta\epsilon \cdot \epsilon_0 \cdot E^2 - \frac{\pi^2 K}{d^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Turn-On Condition.**

The driving RMS electric field $E$ must exceed the Frederiks threshold $E_{th} = \pi \sqrt{K / (\epsilon_0 \Delta\epsilon)} / d$. After algebraic simplification, in the *high-voltage limit* where $E \gg E_{th}$:

$$\tau_{on} \approx \frac{\eta \cdot d^2}{\Delta\epsilon \cdot \epsilon_0 \cdot E^2 \cdot d^2 - \pi^2 K} = \frac{\eta \cdot d^2}{\pi^2 K \left( \dfrac{V^2}{V_{th}^2} - 1 \right)}$$

**Step 3 — Turn-Off (Relaxation) Time.**

When the field is removed, the elastic restoring torque dominates, and the relaxation time is:

$$\tau_{off} = \frac{\eta \cdot d^2}{\pi^2 K}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Numerical Estimate.**

For E7 LC material with $\eta = 0.05 \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{N}$, $d = 5 \ \mu\text{m}$:

$$\tau_{off} = \frac{0.05 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\tau_{off} = \frac{0.05 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-11}}{1.184 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\tau_{off} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-12}}{1.184 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 0.0106 \ \text{s} \approx 10.6 \ \text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight.**

The $\tau \propto d^2$ dependence is the central reason modern displays use **thinner cell gaps** (down to $2$ – $3 \ \mu\text{m}$) to achieve sub-$5$ ms response times, critical for video playback and gaming applications.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: LCD Variants Comparison

| **Property** | **TN** | **STN** | **IPS (In-Plane Switching)** | **VA (Vertical Alignment)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LC Orientation (OFF)** | Twisted 90° | Twisted 180°–270° | Parallel to substrate | Perpendicular to substrate |
| **LC Orientation (ON)** | Vertical | Vertical | Slightly rotated (in-plane) | Slightly tilted |
| **Viewing Angle** | $\pm 45°$ (narrow) | $\pm 60°$ (narrow) | $\pm 89°$ (very wide) | $\pm 80°$ (wide) |
| **Contrast Ratio** | $500:1$ | $60:1$ | $1500:1$ | $3000:1$ – $5000:1$ |
| **Response Time** | Fast ($< 5$ ms) | Slow ($> 100$ ms) | Moderate ($5$ – $14$ ms) | Slow ($> 20$ ms, but improving) |
| **Color Shift** | Severe off-axis | Severe off-axis | Minimal | Moderate |
| **Backlight Efficiency** | High (low absorption) | Low (color filters needed) | Moderate | High |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Application** | Calculators, watches | Older PDAs, low-cost devices | Smartphones, pro monitors, medical | TVs, premium monitors |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Very low | Low | High (requires comb electrodes) | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to justify "Why IPS is preferred over TN for medical imaging displays," the answer is **color fidelity across wide viewing angles** combined with **stable grayscale reproduction** — both essential for diagnostic accuracy. IPS is also the standard in high-end KTU lab equipment displays.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: LCD Module Operation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the signal flow and functional block architecture of a complete LCD module — from electrical input to visible optical output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controller MCU or Video Source] --> B[Timing Controller TCON]
    B --> C[Source Driver Column IC]
    B --> D[Gate Driver Row IC]
    C --> E[Source Lines Columns on Glass]
    D --> F[Gate Lines Rows on Glass]
    E --> G[TFT Pixel Array Active Matrix Backplane]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Liquid Crystal Cell LC Layer 3 to 10 micrometers]
    H --> I[Color Filter Substrate RGB Mosaic]
    I --> J[Polarizer Film 1 Analyzer]
    H --> K[Polarizer Film 2 Polarizer]
    K --> L[Backlight Unit LED Array plus Light Guide]
    L --> K
    J --> M[Observer Eye]
    M --> N[Perceived Image]
    G --> O[DAC Gamma Reference Voltage Generator]
    O --> C
    G --> P[Temperature Sensor NTC]
    P --> B
    B --> Q[Display Power Supply DC DC Converters]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:**
- The **Controller (A)** delivers digital video (e.g., RGB888, LVDS, eDP, MIPI-DSI).
- The **TCON (B)** demultiplexes the data into column data and row scan signals.
- **Source Drivers (C)** buffer and apply the column voltages (one per pixel column).
- **Gate Drivers (D)** sequentially enable each row via the gate line.
- Each **TFT (G)** acts as a switch that charges a tiny storage capacitor at each pixel — holding the LC voltage stable until the next refresh.
- The **LC layer (H)** modulates light passing through based on the RMS voltage across it.
- The **backlight (L)** is the optical source; the **color filter (I)** provides the RGB components.
- The **observer (M)** perceives the modulated light as an image.

### 4.2 Layered Construction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stack[LCD Cell Cross-Section]
        A1[Top Polarizer Linear 0 deg] --> A2[Top Glass Substrate 0.5 to 1.1 mm]
        A2 --> A3[Top ITO Electrode Patterned]
        A3 --> A4[Top Alignment Layer Rubbed PI]
        A4 --> A5[Liquid Crystal Layer 3 to 10 micrometers]
        A5 --> A6[Bottom Alignment Layer Rubbed PI 90 deg offset]
        A6 --> A7[Bottom ITO Electrode Patterned]
        A7 --> A8[Bottom Glass Substrate]
        A8 --> A9[Bottom Polarizer Linear 90 deg Analyzer]
    end
    A9 --> A10[Backlight Unit LED plus LGP plus Diffuser]
```

### 4.3 TN Cell Operating States (Mermaid State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerOff
    PowerOff --> Idle: V_rms = 0
    Idle --> BrightState: No voltage applied
    BrightState --> Transition: V_rms greater than V_threshold
    Transition --> DarkState: LC molecules align vertically
    DarkState --> Relaxation: V_rms removed
    Relaxation --> BrightState: Elastic restoring torque returns twist
    BrightState --> DarkState: V_rms greater than V_threshold
```

**Reading Guide:** The "BrightState" and "DarkState" alternate based on the applied RMS voltage. In a *normally white* (NW) TN cell, the unpowered state is bright; the powered state is dark. This is the standard configuration for transmissive LCDs.
