## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCBMT403]**
*Define the term "Liquid Crystal" and list the three primary mesophases used in display applications.*

**Model Answer:**
A liquid crystal is an organic compound that exists in a state of matter intermediate between a solid crystal and an isotropic liquid, exhibiting both positional or orientational order (like a solid) and fluidity (like a liquid).
The three primary mesophases used in display technology are:
1. **Nematic** — orientational order only; the most widely used in TN, STN, and IPS displays.
2. **Smectic** — layered positional + orientational order; used in ferroelectric LCDs.
3. **Cholesteric (Chiral Nematic)** — helical structure; used in reflective displays and e-paper.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming phases, 1 mark for correct display association.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCBMT403]**
*Differentiate between Active Matrix LCD (AM-LCD) and Passive Matrix LCD (PM-LCD) based on switching element and resolution capability.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PM-LCD | AM-LCD (TFT-LCD) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Element | None — pixels share row/column electrodes | A thin-film transistor (TFT) per pixel |
| Resolution | Limited (< 400 × 400 lines) | Very high (up to 8K) |
| Refresh Limitations | Strong cross-talk at high multiplexing | Minimal — pixel charge held on capacitor |

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for switching element distinction, 1 mark for resolution, 1 mark for refresh/cross-talk understanding.)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When describing the TN effect, students frequently confuse *which state is bright* and *which is dark*. A **normally white (NW) TN cell** is BRIGHT when OFF and DARK when ON. Also, students often write "twist of 180°" — the standard TN geometry is **90°**, not 180°. 180° is the STN variant. **Losing 2 marks on this point is the #1 mistake.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a Twisted Nematic (TN) Liquid Crystal Display. Discuss the role of polarizers and the alignment layer.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the Mauguin condition for a TN cell and explain how it determines the cell gap for a given liquid crystal material. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of TN-LCD:**

The TN-LCD cell consists of the following layers (top to bottom):

1. **Top Polarizer:** A linear polarizer film oriented at 0° relative to the top LC alignment direction.
2. **Top Glass Substrate:** Coated with **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)** as the top transparent electrode.
3. **Top Alignment Layer:** A rubbed polyimide (e.g., SE-7492) film that anchors the LC directors parallel to the rubbing direction.
4. **Liquid Crystal Layer:** A thin layer (typically $3$ – $10 \ \mu\text{m}$) of nematic LC (e.g., 5CB or E7).
5. **Bottom Alignment Layer:** Rubbed perpendicular to the top layer, creating a **90° twist** in the LC director across the cell.
6. **Bottom Electrode (ITO):** On the bottom glass.
7. **Bottom Polarizer (Analyzer):** Oriented at 90° to the top polarizer.

**Working Principle:**
- **OFF State ($V_{rms} = 0$):** The LC molecules are twisted 90° between the two alignment layers. Linearly polarized light from the top polarizer "follows" the helical twist and rotates its polarization by 90°. It now aligns with the bottom analyzer (also at 90° to the top), so light passes through. **Pixel is BRIGHT.**
- **ON State ($V_{rms} > V_{th}$):** The applied electric field forces the LC molecules to align parallel to the field (perpendicular to the glass). The helical twist collapses. Polarized light now passes through the LC without rotation, hits the analyzer at 90° to its polarization, and is **BLOCKED**. **Pixel is DARK.**

**Role of Polarizers:** Polarizers convert the unpolarized backlight into linearly polarized light (input polarizer) and then determine whether the polarization-modulated light passes or is blocked (analyzer). Without the polarizers, the LC's polarization rotation would have no observable effect on intensity.

**Role of Alignment Layers:** The rubbed polyimide provides anisotropic anchoring energy that defines the initial orientation of LC molecules. The 90° orthogonal rubbing between top and bottom creates the characteristic twisted structure that gives the TN effect its name.

---

**(b) Derivation of the Mauguin Condition:**

The Mauguin condition describes the regime in which a TN cell acts as a perfect polarization rotator. We begin with the Jones matrix formulation.

For light of wavelength $\lambda$ propagating through a twisted nematic layer of thickness $d$ and birefringence $\Delta n$, the Jones matrix is:

$$J_{TN} = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ -b^* & a^* \end{pmatrix}$$

where (in the limit of large $\Delta n \cdot d$ relative to $\lambda$):

$$a = \cos\phi\sqrt{1+u^2} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{i\pi \Delta n d}{2\lambda}\right) \cdot \left[\text{sinc terms}\right]$$

For $\phi = \pi/2$ (the 90° twist), the transmission through the crossed polarizer is maximized when:

$$\Delta n \cdot d = \frac{\sqrt{4k^2 - 1}}{2}\lambda \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The **first minimum** ($k=1$) gives:

$$\Delta n \cdot d = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\lambda \approx 0.866\lambda$$

**Determination of Cell Gap:**

Given a typical LC with $\Delta n = 0.10$ and design wavelength $\lambda = 550$ nm:

$$d = \frac{0.866 \times 550 \ \text{nm}}{0.10} = \frac{476.3 \ \text{nm}}{0.10} = 4763 \ \text{nm} \approx 4.76 \ \mu\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The cell gap is fixed by the product $\Delta n \cdot d$ at the Mauguin condition. A thinner cell (smaller $d$) requires a higher birefringence material, while a thicker cell allows use of lower-$\Delta n$ LCs. The first minimum is preferred in commercial TN-LCDs because the cell gap is small enough to ensure fast response and low operating voltage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Compare TN, STN, IPS, and VA LCD technologies in terms of viewing angle, contrast ratio, response time, and typical applications. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Explain the operation of an Active Matrix TFT-LCD. Discuss the role of the storage capacitor and the addressing sequence. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of LCD Variants:**

| Technology | Viewing Angle | Contrast Ratio | Response Time | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TN (Twisted Nematic)** | $\pm 45°$ (narrow) | $500:1$ | $< 5$ ms (fast) | Calculators, watches, low-cost monitors |
| **STN (Super-Twisted Nematic)** | $\pm 60°$ (narrow) | $60:1$ (low) | $> 100$ ms (slow) | Older PDAs, low-end industrial displays |
| **IPS (In-Plane Switching)** | $\pm 89°$ (very wide) | $1500:1$ (high) | $5$ – $14$ ms | Smartphones, professional monitors, medical |
| **VA (Vertical Alignment)** | $\pm 80°$ (wide) | $3000:1$ – $5000:1$ (very high) | $> 20$ ms (improving) | TVs, premium monitors |

**Key Engineering Trade-offs:**
- **TN** offers speed and cost but suffers from poor off-axis color.
- **STN** boosts contrast in multiplexed addressing but at the cost of color and speed; often requires a **DSTN** double-layer compensation.
- **IPS** aligns the LC *in-plane* with the substrate using comb-shaped electrodes on the *same* glass — this rotation is visible from any angle, hence the wide viewing cone.
- **VA** starts with the LC vertical (perpendicular) — when voltage is applied, the LC tilts in a direction that varies across pixels, but the perpendicular dark state gives the highest native contrast.

---

**(b) Active Matrix TFT-LCD Operation:**

An AM-LCD contains a **thin-film transistor (TFT)** at every pixel. Each pixel is essentially a tiny analog memory cell.

**Pixel Circuit (simplified):**
- Gate line $G_i$ — controls the TFT switch (row selection).
- Source line $S_j$ — carries the analog column voltage.
- Storage capacitor $C_{st}$ — holds the charge during the frame time.
- LC capacitor $C_{lc}$ — represents the LC layer as a voltage-dependent dielectric.

**Addressing Sequence (Line-by-Line):**

1. The gate driver activates row $i$ by applying $V_g = +20$ V (ON-state of TFT).
2. All source drivers simultaneously output the column voltages for row $i$ — these voltages charge $C_{st}$ and $C_{lc}$ through the TFT channel.
3. The gate driver deactivates row $i$ ($V_g = -5$ V, OFF-state), isolating the pixel.
4. The stored charge on $C_{st}$ maintains the LC voltage for the **entire frame period** (typically $16.7$ ms at 60 Hz refresh).
5. This process repeats for each subsequent row until the full frame is written.

**Role of the Storage Capacitor:**

The LC itself has finite capacitance and significant leakage (especially at high temperature and at low frequency). Without $C_{st}$, the voltage on a pixel would droop before the next refresh, causing **flicker** and non-uniform display. The $C_{st}$ (typically $0.5$ – $2$ pF) acts as a charge reservoir that:
- Holds the RMS voltage constant across the LC layer.
- Suppresses voltage transients from capacitive coupling between adjacent gate/source lines.
- Ensures uniform gray-level reproduction across the panel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many KTU students write that the storage capacitor "stores the pixel color" — it does not. It stores the **electrical charge** that maintains the **voltage** across the LC, which in turn controls the **optical transmission** of the pixel. The color is determined by the wavelength-selective **color filter** positioned above the LC layer, not by the capacitor. **Losing 1–2 marks on this distinction is common.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Liquid crystals** are mesophase organic compounds exhibiting properties of both liquids and crystals. The **nematic phase** is the workhorse of LCD technology.
- The **TN (Twisted Nematic) effect** relies on field-induced unwinding of a 90° molecular twist. **OFF = bright (NW cell), ON = dark.**
- The **Mauguin condition** $\Delta n \cdot d = (\sqrt{3}/2)\lambda$ determines the optical cell gap. Standard design: $d \approx 4.7 \ \mu\text{m}$ at $\lambda = 550$ nm with $\Delta n \approx 0.10$.
- **Frederiks threshold voltage** $V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{K/(\epsilon_0 \Delta\epsilon)}$ defines the minimum RMS voltage to switch the pixel — typically **1.0 – 1.5 V RMS**.
- **Response time** scales as $\tau \propto d^2$; reducing cell gap is the primary engineering lever for speed.
- **AM-LCD (TFT-LCD)** is essential for high resolution (> 400 lines) and high refresh; each pixel has its own TFT + storage capacitor.
- **Polarizers** are essential — without them, the LC's polarization rotation would be invisible.
- **Alignment layers** (rubbed polyimide) define the LC director orientation and create the helical twist.
- **ITO** is the standard transparent electrode material due to its combination of optical transparency ($> 80\%$) and electrical conductivity.
- **IPS** offers the best viewing angle; **VA** offers the highest contrast; **TN** offers the fastest response and lowest cost; **STN** is legacy technology for low-multiplex displays.
- The **backlight** is required for transmissive LCDs since liquid crystals do **not emit light** — a frequently tested conceptual point in KTU viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Analysing Instruments - Distortion Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Waveform Analysing Instruments - Distortion Meter # 📘 MODULE 4: WAVEFORM ANALYSING INSTRUMENTS — DISTORTION METER ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!NOTE] > **Distortion Meter (Definition):** A **Distortion Meter** is an electronic instrument designed to measure the degree to which a periodic waveform deviates from a pure sinusoidal shape, by quantifying the amplitude of harmonic components present relative to the fundamental frequency, expressed as a percentage called **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**. In any practical AC signal, the output of oscillators, amplifiers, power systems, and communication circuits contains the **fundamental frequency (f)** plus unwanted **harmonic frequencies (2f, 3f, 4f … nf)**. The presence of these harmonics corrupts the ideal sine wave. The instrument that quantifies this impurity is the **Distortion Meter** (also called a **Harmonic Distortion Analyzer**). ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎵 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Musician's "Tuning Fork"** > Imagine a vocalist singing the musical note "A" (say, **440 Hz**). A *perfect* voice produces only **440 Hz** (the fundamental). A *cracked* or *strained* voice produces 440 Hz **plus** its overtones (880 Hz, 1320 Hz, 1760 Hz …). The distortion meter is essentially an *electronic tuner* that listens to the voice, removes the pure 440 Hz note, and tells you *how much* of what remains is "noise/unwanted overtones" — expressed as a percentage. > > - **0%** → A perfectly pure, angelic voice. > - **5%** → A slightly hoarse voice (acceptable for Hi-Fi audio). > - **50%** → A badly distorted electric guitar sound. ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Limit | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Total Harmonic Distortion | **THD** | **< 0.1%** (High-Fidelity) | Hi-Fi amplifiers | | Individual Harmonic Distortion | **D_n** | **< 3%** per harmonic | Power systems | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **SNR** | **> 90 dB** | Precision instruments | # 2️⃣ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Waveform Distortion A pure sine wave is mathematically expressed as: $$ v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) $$ In reality, due to non-linearities in transistors, transformers, and switching circuits, the output becomes: $$ v(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) + V_2 \sin(2\omega t) + V_3 \sin(3\omega t) + \dots + V_n \sin(n\omega t) $$ Where: - $V_1$ = amplitude of **fundamental component** - $V_2, V_3, \dots V_n$ = amplitudes of **2nd, 3rd, … nth harmonic components** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind distortion:** Every non-linear electronic device (diodes, BJTs in saturation, magnetic cores) clips, compresses, or otherwise reshapes the input waveform. Fourier's theorem guarantees this shape change manifests as the generation of new frequencies — the **harmonics**. ## 2.2 Classification of Distortion Meters KTU syllabus mandates the study of three principal architectures: 1. **Fundamental Suppression Type Distortion Meter** (Most common, used in laboratories) 2. **Heterodyne Type Harmonic Distortion Analyzer** (Heterodyne principle, very high selectivity) 3. **Tuned Harmonic Distortion Analyzer** (Direct frequency-selective measurement) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for the Distortion Meter topic. Memorize the first three rows — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as **%** | | 2 | **Total Harmonic Distortion (RMS Form)** | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Where $V_1$ is fundamental RMS | | 3 | **Individual Harmonic Distortion (n-th)** | $D_n = \dfrac{V_n}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Distortion contributed by *n-th* harmonic | | 4 | **THD + Noise (THD+N)** | $\text{THD+N} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2 + V_{noise}^2}}{V_1}$ | Used in audio analyzers | | 5 | **Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SDR)** | $\text{SDR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}\right)$ | Expressed in **dB** | | 6 | **R.M.S. Voltage of Distorted Wave** | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}$ | Sum of RMS of all harmonics | | 7 | **Distortion Factor (British Std.)** | $d = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}}{\sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + \dots + V_n^2}} \times 100\%$ | Uses total RMS as denominator | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** In formula (1) the denominator is **V₁ (fundamental only)**, but in formula (7) the denominator is **total RMS**. Students often interchange these and lose 1–2 marks. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Distortion Meters - **Audio Engineering:** Verifying Hi-Fi amplifier quality (THD < 0.05% for premium amplifiers). - **Power Quality Analysis:** Detecting harmonic pollution in industrial grids caused by VFDs, SMPS, and rectifiers. - **Telecommunications:** Ensuring oscillator purity in transmitters. - **Vibration Analysis:** Studying mechanical resonances. # 3️⃣ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) We start with a **distorted periodic signal** $v(t)$ decomposed by Fourier Series into its fundamental and harmonic components: $$ v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n) $$ For a typical AC signal, the DC offset $V_0 = 0$, giving: $$ v(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t + \phi_1) + V_2 \sin(2\omega t + \phi_2) + \dots + V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n) $$ **Step 1 — Mean Square Value of the Waveform** The total mean-square (RMS squared) of the signal, by Parseval's theorem, is the sum of the mean squares of all orthogonal sinusoidal components: $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt = V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2 $$ > *Why?* Because the time average of $\sin(m\omega t)\sin(n\omega t)$ over a complete period is **0** for $m \neq n$ (orthogonality principle of Fourier series). **Step 2 — Mean Square of the Fundamental** The fundamental component has its own mean-square value: $$ V_{1,rms}^2 = V_1^2 $$ **Step 3 — Mean Square of the Harmonics (Total)** Subtracting (Step 2) from (Step 1) isolates the harmonic energy: $$ V_{harm}^2 = V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2 = V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2 $$ **Step 4 — Definition of THD** By IUPAC and IEEE convention, THD is the ratio of the RMS of the harmonics to the RMS of the fundamental: $$ \text{THD} = \frac{V_{harm}}{V_1} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}}{V_1} $$ **Step 5 — Expressed as a Percentage** $$ \boxed{\text{THD (\%)} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%} $$ This is the **master formula** the KTU examiner expects you to derive. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style Problem]** > *A distorted sine wave has fundamental amplitude 10 V (RMS), 2nd harmonic 1.0 V, 3rd harmonic 0.5 V, 4th harmonic 0.3 V, and 5th harmonic 0.2 V. Calculate THD.* **Solution — Step by Step:** **Step A:** Identify all harmonic RMS amplitudes. $$ V_1 = 10\,\text{V},\quad V_2 = 1.0\,\text{V},\quad V_3 = 0.5\,\text{V},\quad V_4 = 0.3\,\text{V},\quad V_5 = 0.2\,\text{V} $$ **Step B:** Compute the sum of squares of all harmonics. $$ \sum V_n^2 = (1.0)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (0.3)^2 + (0.2)^2 $$ $$ \sum V_n^2 = 1.00 + 0.25 + 0.09 + 0.04 = 1.38\,\text{V}^2 $$ **Step C:** Take the square root to get total harmonic RMS. $$ V_{harm} = \sqrt{1.38} = 1.1747\,\text{V} $$ **Step D:** Divide by the fundamental. $$ \text{THD} = \frac{1.1747}{10} = 0.11747 $$ **Step E:** Convert to percentage. $$ \boxed{\text{THD} = 11.747\% \approx 11.75\%} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Working Principle of the Fundamental Suppression Type | Stage | Block | Function | Typical Component | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Input Attenuator** | Scales the input signal to a measurable level | Resistive ladder | | 2 | **Preamplifier** | Boosts the signal for noise immunity | Low-noise op-amp (e.g., NE5532) | | 3 | **Fundamental Rejection Filter** | Removes only the fundamental, passes all harmonics | **Wien Bridge Notch** or **Twin-T Filter** | | 4 | **RMS Meter** | Measures true RMS of the residual (harmonic) content | True-RMS converter (AD736) | | 5 | **Reference Channel** | Measures the total RMS via a switchable path | True-RMS detector | | 6 | **Ratio Circuit** | Computes $V_{harm}/V_1$ and displays THD% | Analog divider (e.g., AD534) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** The instrument actually performs **two measurements**: > - **Measurement 1:** Total RMS voltage (switch in *total* position). > - **Measurement 2:** Harmonic RMS only (switch in *harmonic* position, fundamental blocked). > - **THD = (Reading 2) / (Reading 1)** — properly calibrated to read directly as a percentage. ## 3.4 Derivation: Wien Bridge Notch Filter Frequency The fundamental suppression filter in the popular Wien-Bridge configuration rejects the fundamental at a tuned frequency: $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} $$ For $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 10\,\text{nF}$: $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi (10\times 10^3)(10\times 10^{-9})} \approx 1591.5\,\text{Hz} \approx 1\,\text{kHz} $$ > The notch filter must be **tunable** to match the unknown fundamental frequency of the input. In laboratory distortion meters (e.g., HP 334A, Tektronix AA501), this tuning is automated. # 4️⃣ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Fundamental Suppression Type Distortion Meter ```mermaid graph LR node1["Distorted Input
Signal v t"]:::in node2["Input
Attenuator"]:::blk node3["Pre
Amplifier"]:::blk node4["Tunable Notch
Filter 50Hz to 20kHz"]:::filt node5["RMS
Detector 1"]:::msr node6["Display in
Percentage"]:::out node7["RMS
Detector 2"]:::msr node8["Reference
Amplifier"]:::blk node9["Range
Selector SW"]:::ctrl node10["Calibration
Source"]:::cal node11["Ratio
Computation"]:::ctrl node1 --> node2 --> node3 node3 --> node9 node9 -->|"Total path"| node8 --> node7 node9 -->|"Harmonic path"| node4 --> node5 node7 --> node11 node5 --> node11 node10 -.-> node3 node11 --> node6 classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef blk fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef filt fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 classDef msr fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef out fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef cal fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` ### Block-by-Block Explanation | Block | Physical Role | |---|---| | **Input Attenuator** | Protects the instrument from high-voltage signals; provides range selection. | | **Preamplifier** | Raises signal to a level adequate for the filter stage; sets input impedance. | | **Tunable Notch Filter** | The heart of the meter — rejects the fundamental, allows harmonics to pass. | | **Range Selector Switch** | Routes the signal either *through* the filter (harmonic measurement) or *bypasses* it (total measurement). | | **Reference Amplifier** | Provides the denominator signal (total RMS) to the ratio circuit. | | **RMS Detectors** | Two identical true-RMS converters — essential for accurate THD calculation. | | **Ratio Computation** | Analog divider computing $V_{harm}/V_1 \times 100$. | | **Display** | Analog dB scale or digital % readout. | ## 4.2 Architecture Flow: Heterodyne Type Distortion Analyzer ```mermaid graph TD sigA["Input
Signal"]:::in mixA["Mixer
Stage"]:::blk loA["Local
Oscillator
Variable"]:::osc ifA["IF Amplifier
at Fixed
Frequency"]:::filt detA["Detector
and RMS
Meter"]:::msr outA["THD
Display"]:::out sigA --> mixA loA --> mixA mixA --> ifA --> detA --> outA classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef blk fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef osc fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef filt fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 classDef msr fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef out fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` ### Why Heterodyne? — The "Why" Behind It In the **Heterodyne principle**, the input signal is mixed with a tunable local oscillator. The mixer output contains two frequencies: $(f_{signal} + f_{LO})$ and $\vert f_{signal} - f_{LO} \vert$. A sharp **fixed-frequency IF (Intermediate Frequency) filter** at, say, **20 kHz** is then used. As the local oscillator is tuned, each harmonic of the input signal is *swept across* the IF passband and measured one by one. - **Advantage:** Extremely **high selectivity** (Q-factor > 100) since the IF filter is fixed and crystal-controlled. - **Disadvantage:** Complex, expensive; mostly used in lab-grade analyzers (e.g., HP 3581A). ## 4.3 Comparative Architecture Matrix | Feature | Fundamental Suppression | Heterodyne | Tuned Harmonic | |---|---|---|---| | **Selectivity (Q)** | Low–Medium | Very High | High | | **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | | **Measurement Speed** | Fast | Slow (swept) | Slow | | **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 5 Hz – 50 kHz | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | | **Typical Use** | Field & lab | Lab (precision) | Lab & R&D | | **Filter Type** | Wien / Twin-T | Crystal IF | LC tuned | # 5️⃣ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q1a]** **Q1.** Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of a waveform. State its mathematical expression in terms of the fundamental and harmonic RMS voltages. **✅ Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** THD is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all the harmonic components to the RMS value of the fundamental component of a distorted waveform, expressed as a percentage. > > **Mathematical Expression (1 mark):** > $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ > > **Explanation (1 mark):** Where $V_1$ is the RMS voltage of the fundamental frequency, and $V_2, V_3, \dots, V_n$ are the RMS voltages of the 2nd, 3rd, … n-th harmonics. A THD of 0% indicates a perfect sine wave, while higher values indicate greater waveform distortion. --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Q1b]** **Q2.** List any **three** types of distortion meters and explain the working principle of the **Fundamental Suppression Type** distortion meter in brief. **✅ Model Answer (3 marks):** **Three Types (1.5 marks):** 1. Fundamental Suppression Type Distortion Meter 2. Heterodyne Type Harmonic Distortion Analyzer 3. Tuned Harmonic Distortion Analyzer **Working of Fundamental Suppression Type (1.5 marks):** > The distorted input signal is first amplified and then passed through a **tunable notch filter** (typically Wien Bridge or Twin-T) that **rejects the fundamental frequency** and allows all the harmonics to pass. The RMS value of the residual harmonic signal is measured and divided by the RMS value of the total signal (with the filter bypassed), giving the THD as a percentage. Two RMS measurements and one ratio computation yield the final display. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]** > **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following (Q.A or Q.B). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** from the Fourier series representation of a distorted periodic waveform. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A non-sinusoidal waveform has the following harmonic content measured by a distortion meter: Fundamental (50 Hz) = **220 V RMS**, 2nd harmonic = **8 V**, 3rd harmonic = **5 V**, 4th harmonic = **3 V**, 5th harmonic = **2 V**. Calculate: **(i)** THD in %, **(ii)** Total RMS voltage, **(iii)** Distortion Factor (British Standard definition). **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a): **Step 1 — Representing the Distorted Wave (1 mark):** By Fourier's theorem, any periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform can be expressed as: $$ v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n) $$ For an AC waveform with no DC component, $V_0 = 0$: $$ v(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) + V_2 \sin(2\omega t) + V_3 \sin(3\omega t) + \dots + V_n \sin(n\omega t) $$ **Step 2 — Computing Mean Square Value (2 marks):** By Parseval's theorem (using orthogonality of sinusoids): $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt = V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2 $$ **Step 3 — Isolating Harmonic Energy (2 marks):** Subtracting the fundamental's mean-square contribution: $$ V_{harm}^2 = V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2 = V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2 $$ **Step 4 — Definition and Final Formula (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{\text{THD (\%)} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%} $$ **Step 5 — Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. The waveform is **periodic** and **continuous**. 2. **Ohmic (resistive) load** — no phase-shift-induced distortion. 3. The signal is **band-limited** (harmonics above n-th are negligible). 4. The **D.C. component is zero**. #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b): **Given:** $V_1 = 220$ V, $V_2 = 8$ V, $V_3 = 5$ V, $V_4 = 3$ V, $V_5 = 2$ V **(i) THD Calculation (3 marks):** $$ \sum V_n^2 = 8^2 + 5^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 = 64 + 25 + 9 + 4 = 102 $$ $$ V_{harm} = \sqrt{102} = 10.0995 \text{ V} $$ $$ \text{THD} = \frac{10.0995}{220} \times 100\% = \boxed{4.59\%} $$ **(ii) Total RMS Voltage (2 marks):** $$ V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + V_5^2} $$ $$ V_{rms} = \sqrt{220^2 + 102} = \sqrt{48400 + 102} = \sqrt{48502} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{rms} = 220.23 \text{ V}} $$ **(iii) Distortion Factor (British Standard) (2 marks):** $$ d = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_n^2}}{\sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + \dots + V_n^2}} \times 100\% $$ $$ d = \frac{10.0995}{220.23} \times 100\% = \boxed{4.586\%} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The IEEE definition gives **4.59%**, while the British standard gives **4.586%**. The values are very close, but the *denominators* differ. Always state the definition you are using. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Fundamental Suppression Type Harmonic Distortion Analyzer**. Discuss the role of the **Wien Bridge Notch Filter** in it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **Fundamental Suppression**, **Heterodyne**, and **Tuned Harmonic** types of distortion meters on the basis of: principle, selectivity, cost, and typical application. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a): **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The instrument consists of the following sequential stages: 1. **Input Attenuator** — sets the input to a safe, measurable level. 2. **Preamplifier** — boosts the signal. 3. **Range Selector Switch** — directs the signal through one of two paths: - *Path A (Total):* → Reference Amplifier → RMS Detector 2 - *Path B (Harmonic):* → Wien Bridge Notch Filter → RMS Detector 1 4. **Ratio Circuit** — computes $V_{harm} / V_{total} \times 100$. 5. **Display** — calibrated to read directly as % THD. **Working Principle (2 marks):** > The distorted input is first measured as **total RMS** ($V_T$). Then the same signal is passed through a **tunable notch filter** that removes the fundamental frequency. The remaining signal — containing *only harmonics* — is measured as $V_H$. The ratio $V_H / V_T \times 100$ is the THD. Calibration is done by first feeding a **pure reference sine wave** (THD ≈ 0) and adjusting to a zero reading. **Role of Wien Bridge Notch Filter (2 marks):** > The Wien Bridge filter is a **frequency-selective null network**. When its components are tuned such that $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the bridge becomes *balanced*, and the output at $f_0$ falls to **zero** — this is the notch. All other frequencies (the harmonics, 2f, 3f, …) pass through with minimal attenuation. It is the **heart of the analyzer**, since without precise fundamental rejection, no accurate THD measurement is possible. Practical Q-factor of a Wien notch is **8–10**, sufficient for general-purpose work. #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b): **Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | Fundamental Suppression | Heterodyne | Tuned Harmonic | |---|---|---|---| | **Operating Principle** | Notch filter removes fundamental; ratio of RMS values is THD | Mixer + tunable LO + fixed IF; each harmonic swept through IF filter | LC-tuned filter selects one harmonic at a time; measures amplitude | | **Selectivity (Q-factor)** | Medium (Q ≈ 8–10) | Very High (Q > 100, crystal IF) | High (Q ≈ 30–50) | | **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | | **Complexity** | Simple | Complex (LO, mixer, IF stages) | Moderate | | **Measurement Speed** | Fast (2 RMS readings) | Slow (sweep required) | Slow (manual tuning) | | **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz – 20 kHz (audio) | 5 Hz – 50 kHz (precision lab) | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | | **Typical Application** | Field testing, audio amplifiers | Lab-grade R&D, oscillators | Power-quality analysis | | **Limitation** | Notch depth limits min. measurable THD (~0.1%) | Expensive; not portable | Requires manual retuning per harmonic | > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:** > > 1. **Forgetting the denominator distinction:** THD uses $V_1$ as denominator, while Distortion Factor uses $V_{rms}$. Mixing these costs **2 marks**. > 2. **Skipping the block diagram in long answers:** A diagram is **compulsory** for the 7-mark "with neat block diagram" sub-question. Skipping it = losing up to **3 marks**. > 3. **Not stating units:** Always write **%** after THD and **dB** after SDR. Forgetting units costs **0.5 mark** per occurrence. > 4. **Confusing THD with SNR:** THD is *deterministic* (harmonics), while SNR involves *random noise*. KTU explicitly tests this distinction. > 5. **Missing the "calibration step":** Examiners award **1 mark** for mentioning the calibration using a pure sine wave reference. --- ## 🚀 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > **Before you enter the exam hall, ensure you can answer YES to each of these:** - ✅ THD is defined as the ratio of **RMS of harmonics** to **RMS of fundamental** × 100. - ✅ The master formula is $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$. - ✅ Three types: **Fundamental Suppression, Heterodyne, Tuned Harmonic**. - ✅ The **Wien Bridge Notch Filter** has a notch at $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. - ✅ **Heterodyne** offers the **highest selectivity** (Q > 100). - ✅ **Fundamental Suppression** is the **most common** laboratory distortion meter. - ✅ The instrument performs **two RMS measurements** (total and harmonic-only) and computes a **ratio**. - ✅ **THD + Noise (THD+N)** includes random noise; **THD** does not. - ✅ **SDR (dB)** is the logarithmic form of inverse THD: $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_{harm}}\right)$. - ✅ **High-fidelity audio amplifiers** require **THD < 0.1%** at full rated power. - ✅ British Standard uses **total RMS** in denominator; IEEE uses **fundamental RMS** in denominator. - ✅ Block diagram of Fundamental Suppression Type has: **Attenuator → Amplifier → Notch Filter → RMS Detector → Ratio Circuit → Display**. - ✅ The **heterodyne type** mixes the input with a **tunable local oscillator** and uses a **fixed IF filter** for high-Q measurement. - ✅ Calibration is performed using a **pure reference sine wave** before measurement. - ✅ Real-world applications: **Hi-Fi audio testing, power-quality analysis, oscillator purity, vibration analysis**. --- > 📚 **End of Module 4 — Distortion Meter Notes** > *Total Coverage: Definition → Fourier Derivation → Three Architectures → Block Diagrams → Numerical Problems → KTU Exam Bank*

topics.title: Spectrum Analyser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spectrum Analyser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Spectrum Analyser** is a calibrated electronic test and measurement instrument designed to display the **amplitude (magnitude) of an input signal as a function of frequency** within its specified **frequency range**. Unlike a time-domain oscilloscope that plots voltage versus time, the spectrum analyser operates in the **frequency domain**, decomposing a composite signal into its constituent sinusoidal components using **Fourier analysis** principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** A spectrum analyser is a superheterodyne or FFT-based instrument that measures the power spectral density (PSD) of a signal, displaying frequency on the horizontal (X) axis and amplitude (in dBm, dBV, or dBµV) on the vertical (Y) axis. It is the most fundamental instrument for **RF, microwave, and signal-integrity analysis**.

The instrument is formally classified by the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) as a "selective voltmeter" because it effectively scans a narrow bandpass filter across a frequency sweep and reports the amplitude of signals present at each discrete frequency step.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a noisy classroom where many students are talking simultaneously — you cannot understand anyone. If, instead, you had a magical tool that could **isolate each student's voice by its pitch** and tell you **"who is talking how loud"**, you would have a perfect real-world analogy for a spectrum analyser.

- The **classroom noise** = the composite time-domain signal (mix of many sine waves).
- **Each student** = one individual frequency component (one pure tone).
- **The pitch of the voice** = the frequency of the sine wave.
- **How loud the student is talking** = the amplitude (power) of that frequency.
- **Your magical pitch-isolating tool** = the **spectrum analyser**.

In simple words: a spectrum analyser answers the question, *"What frequencies exist inside my signal, and how strong is each one?"*

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (Industry-Standard Terminology)

The following **bolded** terms represent the primary performance parameters universally adopted by KTU examiners and the RF industry:

- **Frequency Range** — the minimum-to-maximum frequency the instrument can analyse (e.g., **9 kHz to 26.5 GHz**).
- **Frequency Span** — the total width of the displayed frequency window (Span = Stop − Start).
- **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** — the width of the narrowest bandpass filter the analyser can apply, determining the ability to resolve two closely spaced spectral lines.
- **Video Bandwidth (VBW)** — the post-detection low-pass filter cutoff that smooths the displayed trace.
- **Sweep Time** — the time required for the local oscillator to scan the full span.
- **Dynamic Range** — the ratio (in dB) between the largest and smallest signals that can be measured simultaneously without distortion.
- **Sensitivity / Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL)** — the lowest detectable signal power, typically expressed in **dBm**.
- **Phase Noise** — short-term frequency instability of the internal local oscillator, appearing as a noise skirt around strong carriers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PCBMT403 syllabus, Module 4 specifically emphasises the **block diagram, operating principle, and the distinction between Swept-Tuned and FFT (Real-Time) spectrum analysers** along with their respective applications. Examiners frequently test the **superheterodyne receiver concept** and the **trade-off between RBW, span, and sweep time**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Spectrum Analysers

There are two principal architectural families of spectrum analysers encountered in KTU 2024 assessments:

### A. Swept-Tuned Spectrum Analyser (Superheterodyne Type)

This is the **classical, most widely deployed** architecture. It employs a sweeping **local oscillator (LO)** that heterodynes (mixes) with the input signal, producing an **intermediate frequency (IF)** that is held constant while the LO frequency is varied. A narrowband IF filter then performs the actual frequency selection, and a detector produces the amplitude reading.

#### Operational Block Logic — Step by Step

1. **RF Input Attenuation** — A precision step attenuator (typically 0–70 dB in 10 dB steps) protects the mixer and sets the reference level.
2. **Low-Pass / Bandpass Pre-filter (Image Reject)** — A tracking filter rejects image-frequency responses, ensuring only the desired band enters the mixer.
3. **Mixer Stage** — The input signal $f_s$ mixes with the local oscillator signal $f_{LO}$, producing sum and difference frequencies. The difference frequency is selected as the IF.
4. **Local Oscillator (YIG-Tuned Oscillator)** — A YIG (Yttrium Iron Garnet) sphere-based oscillator whose frequency is linearly swept via a ramp generator; this is the heart of the swept-tuned analyser.
5. **IF Section (Resolution Filter)** — A crystal or LC bandpass filter centred at the IF (commonly **21.4 MHz, 30 MHz, or 322 MHz**) sets the **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**.
6. **Logarithmic Amplifier (Log Detector)** — Converts the IF signal into a logarithmic (dB) scale so both small and large signals can be displayed simultaneously.
7. **Video Filter (VBW)** — A post-detection low-pass filter smoothing the display; reduces noise on the trace.
8. **Display (CRT / LCD)** — Plots amplitude vs. frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixer Equation (Heterodyning Principle):** The mixer produces both sum and difference products. The intermediate frequency output is selected by the IF filter as:
> $$f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{s} \vert$$
> where $f_{LO}$ is swept across the chosen span, ensuring $f_{IF}$ remains constant at the filter centre frequency for every instant of the sweep.

### B. Real-Time / FFT Spectrum Analyser

This architecture samples the input signal using a high-speed **Analogue-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** and computes the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)**, typically via the optimised **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm**, entirely in the digital domain.

- The **entire span** is captured simultaneously — no sweeping is required.
- Speed is limited by the **ADC sampling rate** and the **FFT length** $N$.
- Ideal for capturing **transient, burst, or non-repetitive** signals that a swept-tuned analyser would miss.
- Maximum usable frequency is bounded by the **Nyquist criterion**: $f_{max} \le \dfrac{f_{s}}{2}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every governing equation needed for KTU 2024 ESE problems on spectrum analysers.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Governing Equation** | **Standard Unit** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heterodyne (mixer) output | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$ | Hz | Selected intermediate frequency |
| Frequency Span | $\text{Span} = f_{stop} - f_{start}$ | Hz | Displayed frequency window width |
| Image Frequency | $f_{img} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ | Hz | Spurious response to be rejected |
| Sweep Time (empirical) | $T_{sw} \approx \dfrac{k \cdot \text{Span}}{(\text{RBW})^2}$ | seconds | Time to complete one scan |
| Minimum RBW (FFT) | $\text{RBW}_{min} = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Hz | Frequency resolution of FFT bin |
| FFT Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_{sample}}{N}$ | Hz | Bin width in digital analyser |
| Nyquist Limit | $f_{max} = \dfrac{f_{sample}}{2}$ | Hz | Highest analysable frequency |
| Power Conversion (dBm) | $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\,\text{mW}}\right)$ | dBm | Logarithmic power ratio |
| Voltage Conversion (dBµV) | $V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V}{1\,\mu V}\right)$ | dBµV | Logarithmic voltage ratio |
| Power Spectral Density | $S_x(f) = \lim_{T\to\infty}\dfrac{\lvert X_T(f)\rvert^2}{T}$ | W/Hz | Energy distribution per Hz |
| Dynamic Range (1 dB compression) | $DR = P_{1dB} - DANL$ | dB | Maximum usable amplitude range |
| Amplitude Accuracy | $A_{err} = \pm (A_{log} + A_{linear} + A_{band})$ | dB | Total measurement uncertainty |
| Phase Noise Spectral Density | $\mathcal{L}(f) = \dfrac{P_{noise\,@\,offset}/1\text{Hz}}{P_{carrier}}$ | dBc/Hz | LO stability metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern **5G base-station testing, radar ECCM (Electronic Counter-Countermeasures) analysis, EMI/EMC compliance verification, and oscillator spectral purity testing**, the spectrum analyser is irreplaceable. The **swept-tuned** variant dominates high-frequency microwave work (up to THz with external mixers), while the **FFT** variant dominates audio, vibration, and baseband digital-signal analysis.

## 2.3 The Fundamental Trade-Off Triangle

Three parameters of the swept-tuned analyser form an irreducible engineering trade-off — improving any one worsens at least one other:

- **Smaller RBW** → Better frequency resolution, but **longer sweep time** and **lower sweep rate**.
- **Wider Span** → Broader visibility, but **longer sweep time** and **worse resolution per bin**.
- **Faster Sweep** → Captures fast transients, but **degraded amplitude accuracy** and **IF-filter distortion**.

The empirical relationship governing this triangle (instrument-specific constant $k$):
$$T_{sw} \approx \frac{k \cdot \text{Span}}{(\text{RBW})^2}$$

This is a **guaranteed high-yield KTU question**, either as a numerical problem or a conceptual "explain the trade-off" question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mixer Output and Image Frequency Rejection

A spectrum analyser mixer multiplies the input RF signal with the local oscillator signal. Let us derive the IF output and the image frequency that must be rejected.

**Step 1 — Define the input signals.**
Let the RF input be:
$$x_s(t) = A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t)$$

Let the local oscillator output be:
$$x_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal mixer (multiplier) operation.**
A mixer is fundamentally a multiplier. The output is:
$$x_{mix}(t) = x_s(t) \cdot x_{LO}(t) = A_s A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_s t)\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**
Using the identity:
$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\bigr]$$

we obtain:
$$x_{mix}(t) = \frac{A_s A_{LO}}{2}\bigl[\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{LO} - f_s)t\bigr) + \cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{LO} + f_s)t\bigr)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Filter out the sum component.**
The IF bandpass filter centred at $f_{IF}$ rejects the high-frequency sum term. The retained IF output is:
$$x_{IF}(t) = \frac{A_s A_{LO}}{2}\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_{LO} - f_s)t\bigr)$$

For the IF to remain constant at the filter centre, the LO must be tuned such that:
$$\vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert = f_{IF}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the image frequency.**
Two different RF frequencies can produce the same IF when mixed with a single $f_{LO}$:

- **Desired signal:** $f_{s1} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$
- **Image signal:** $f_{s2} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$

Subtracting:
$$f_{s2} - f_{s1} = 2 f_{IF}$$

Therefore, the image frequency is:
$$f_{img} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$$

This is the **exact image frequency** that the pre-mixer tracking filter must suppress — a high-yield KTU derivable.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sweep Time vs. RBW Relationship

The IF filter has a finite bandwidth, and the LO must dwell long enough at each frequency for the filter to fully charge to its final value (typically reaching the 90% settling level of a Gaussian-shaped filter). The empirical relationship is derived from filter-charging dynamics.

**Step 1 — Establish the filter charging time.**
For a Gaussian IF filter of bandwidth RBW, the impulse response is approximately Gaussian. The 90% settling time of such a filter is inversely proportional to the bandwidth:
$$\tau_{settle} \propto \frac{1}{\text{RBW}}$$

**Step 2 — Count the number of filter-widths to scan.**
The number of filter-widths fitting in the displayed span is:
$$N_{filters} = \frac{\text{Span}}{\text{RBW}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to obtain total sweep time.**
$$T_{sw} = k \cdot \tau_{settle} \cdot N_{filters} = k \cdot \frac{1}{\text{RBW}} \cdot \frac{\text{Span}}{\text{RBW}}$$

**Step 4 — Final empirical form.**
$$\boxed{T_{sw} \approx \frac{k \cdot \text{Span}}{(\text{RBW})^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style).**
Given: Span = **10 MHz**, RBW = **10 kHz**, instrument constant $k = 2.5$.

$$T_{sw} = \frac{2.5 \times 10 \times 10^6}{(10 \times 10^3)^2} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{10^8} = 0.25\,\text{s}$$

If the RBW is tightened to **1 kHz** (10× narrower) while keeping span constant:
$$T_{sw} = \frac{2.5 \times 10 \times 10^6}{(1 \times 10^3)^2} = 25\,\text{s}$$

This **100× increase** in sweep time for a 10× improvement in resolution is the classic engineering trade-off.

## 3.3 Derivation: FFT Resolution Bandwidth and Nyquist Limit

In an FFT-based analyser, the input is digitised at $f_{sample}$ and split into $N$-point segments. Each segment produces an $N$-point FFT.

**Step 1 — Total observation window per FFT block.**
The time duration represented by an $N$-sample block is:
$$T_{obs} = \frac{N}{f_{sample}}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency spacing between adjacent FFT bins.**
The FFT inherently produces frequency-domain samples spaced at the reciprocal of the observation window:
$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_{obs}} = \frac{f_{sample}}{N}$$

This $\Delta f$ **is** the effective resolution bandwidth of the FFT analyser.

**Step 3 — Maximum analysable frequency (Nyquist).**
To avoid aliasing, the input bandwidth must be less than half the sampling rate:
$$f_{max} \le \frac{f_{sample}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example.**
Given: $f_{sample} = 100\,\text{kHz}$, $N = 1024$ points.

$$\Delta f = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{1024} \approx 97.66\,\text{Hz}$$

$$f_{max} = 50\,\text{kHz}$$

A longer FFT (larger $N$) yields finer resolution at the cost of longer acquisition time per block — analogous to the swept-tuned trade-off but framed in the digital domain.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Swept-Tuned vs. FFT Analyser

| **Parameter** | **Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne)** | **FFT (Real-Time)** |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Heterodyne mixing + narrowband IF | ADC sampling + digital DFT |
| Frequency Range | Very wide (Hz to THz) | Limited by ADC (typically ≤ GHz) |
| Transient Capture | Misses fast transients | Captures simultaneous full span |
| Resolution | Set by analog IF filter | Set by FFT bin width $\Delta f$ |
| Sweep Time | Slow for narrow RBW | Independent of RBW choice |
| Cost & Complexity | Higher for mm-wave bands | Lower for audio/baseband |
| Phase Noise Impact | Dominant limitation | Less critical |
| Best Suited For | RF/microwave, EMC, oscillator tests | Vibration, audio, baseband DSP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Swept-Tuned Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RF Input Connector] --> B[Step Attenuator 0 to 70 dB]
    B --> C[Low Pass Tracking Filter]
    C --> D[Mixer Stage]
    E[YIG Tuned Local Oscillator] --> D
    D --> F[IF Amplifier at fIF 21.4 MHz]
    F --> G[Crystal IF Bandpass Filter Sets RBW]
    G --> H[Logarithmic Amplifier Detector]
    H --> I[Video Bandwidth Low Pass Filter VBW]
    I --> J[Display Unit X axis Frequency Y axis Amplitude]
    K[Sweep Ramp Generator] --> E
    K --> J
    G -.feeds back.-> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **sweep ramp generator** drives two loads simultaneously — it tunes the **YIG oscillator** (sweeping the LO) and deflects the **display's X-axis** (so the displayed position is locked to the instantaneous LO frequency). The IF filter is the resolution-defining element.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FFT (Real-Time) Spectrum Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    C --> D[Memory Buffer N Samples]
    D --> E[Windowing Function Hann or Blackman]
    E --> F[FFT Engine N Point DFT]
    F --> G[Power Spectrum Magnitude Squared]
    G --> H[Display Unit X axis Frequency Y axis Power dBm]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Heterodyne Down-Conversion Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "RF Domain"
        S[Input Signal fs at 2.5 GHz]
    end
    subgraph "Mixer Process"
        LO[Local Oscillator fLO Sweeping 2.5 to 3.5 GHz]
        M[Mixer Multiplier]
    end
    subgraph "IF Domain"
        IFF[IF Bandpass Filter at 321.4 MHz RBW 10 kHz]
        DET[Log Detector]
    end
    subgraph "Display Path"
        AMP[Logarithmic Amplifier]
        DISP[Spectrum Display]
    end
    S --> M
    LO --> M
    M --> IFF
    IFF --> DET
    DET --> AMP
    AMP --> DISP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Illustration:** If the input signal is at $f_s = 2.5$ GHz and the IF is fixed at $f_{IF} = 321.4$ MHz, then for a low-side LO the LO is tuned to $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 2.8214$ GHz. As the LO sweeps from 2.8214 GHz to 3.8214 GHz, the displayed span covers 2.5 GHz to 3.5 GHz, with the image at $f_{img} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 3.1428$ GHz suppressed by the pre-filter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define a Spectrum Analyser. List its two principal types. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A spectrum analyser is an electronic instrument that displays the amplitude of signal components as a function of frequency, operating in the frequency domain. It decomposes a composite signal into its constituent sinusoidal components and presents the resulting **power spectral density** on a calibrated display.

The two principal types are:
1. **Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne) Spectrum Analyser** — uses a sweeping local oscillator and a fixed IF filter.
2. **Real-Time (FFT) Spectrum Analyser** — uses an ADC and digital FFT computation to obtain the spectrum instantaneously.

**[3 Marks]** — Awarded for: (1 mark) accurate definition, (1 mark) both types named, (1 mark) one-line distinguishing principle.

---

### Question 2 — What is Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)? Why is it critical in spectrum analysis? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) is the **bandwidth of the narrowest bandpass filter** (typically the IF filter) through which a signal must pass in the spectrum analyser chain. It defines the smallest frequency difference between two signals at which they can still be displayed as two distinct spectral lines rather than merging into one.

It is critical because:
- **Smaller RBW** yields better frequency resolution and lower displayed noise floor (DANL improves).
- However, smaller RBW increases **sweep time** quadratically and reduces the rate of signal capture.

**[3 Marks]** — Awarded for: (1 mark) definition, (1 mark) resolution implication, (1 mark) sweep-time trade-off.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Q.A.(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **swept-tuned superheterodyne spectrum analyser**. Discuss the role of the local oscillator, mixer, IF filter, and log amplifier. `**[7 Marks]**` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** A swept-tuned spectrum analyser is a superheterodyne receiver that performs a narrowband sweep across a wide frequency range. It displays amplitude (in dBm) on the Y-axis and frequency on the X-axis.

2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):**
   - RF Attenuator → Pre-selector Filter → Mixer ← Sweeping LO
   - IF Amplifier → IF Bandpass Filter (defines RBW) → Log Detector
   - Video Filter → Display

3. **Functional Explanation (3 marks):**
   - **Local Oscillator (YIG-tuned):** Produces a linearly swept sinusoidal signal $f_{LO}(t)$ that drives both the mixer and the display's X-deflection, ensuring frequency-axis synchronisation.
   - **Mixer:** Multiplies the input $f_s$ with $f_{LO}$, producing $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$. The difference frequency is held constant by the closed-loop sweep.
   - **IF Filter:** A narrowband filter (centre at 21.4 MHz or 322 MHz) whose bandwidth defines the **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**. It determines the analyser's ability to separate closely spaced signals.
   - **Logarithmic Amplifier:** Compresses a wide dynamic range (often 80–100 dB) into a viewable display range using a logarithmic transfer function.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The swept-tuned analyser offers excellent sensitivity and very wide frequency coverage, making it the workhorse of RF/microwave laboratories.

---

**Q.A.(b)** Derive the relationship between **sweep time, span, and resolution bandwidth** in a swept-tuned analyser. A spectrum analyser is set to a span of **5 MHz** with RBW of **30 kHz** and instrument constant $k = 2.5$. Calculate the sweep time. If the RBW is reduced to **3 kHz**, what is the new sweep time? Comment on the result. `**[7 Marks]**` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Starting from the IF filter settling time proportional to $1/\text{RBW}$ and the number of filter-widths equal to $\text{Span}/\text{RBW}$, the total sweep time is derived as:

$$T_{sw} = k \cdot \frac{\text{Span}}{(\text{RBW})^2}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
For Span = 5 MHz, RBW = 30 kHz, $k = 2.5$:

$$T_{sw1} = \frac{2.5 \times 5 \times 10^6}{(30 \times 10^3)^2} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^6}{9 \times 10^8} \approx 0.0139\,\text{s} \approx 13.9\,\text{ms}$$

For Span = 5 MHz, RBW = 3 kHz, $k = 2.5$:

$$T_{sw2} = \frac{2.5 \times 5 \times 10^6}{(3 \times 10^3)^2} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^6}{9 \times 10^6} \approx 1.39\,\text{s}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
A tenfold reduction in RBW causes a **hundredfold increase in sweep time** (from 13.9 ms to 1.39 s), confirming the inverse-square trade-off. Narrower RBW improves resolution and lowers the noise floor but makes the analyser too slow to track fast-changing signals.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q.B.(a)** Explain the **FFT-based real-time spectrum analyser** with a neat block diagram. Compare its performance with the swept-tuned type. `**[7 Marks]**` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (1 mark):** An FFT analyser digitises the time-domain input using an ADC and applies the **Discrete Fourier Transform** (efficiently computed as the FFT) to obtain the frequency spectrum.

2. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Input → Anti-aliasing Filter → ADC → Memory Buffer → Window Function → FFT Engine → Power Calculation → Display.

3. **Key Features (2 marks):**
   - **Simultaneous capture** of the entire span — no sweeping, hence "real-time."
   - Resolution set by FFT bin width $\Delta f = f_{sample}/N$.
   - Maximum analysable frequency is $f_{sample}/2$ (Nyquist).
   - Excellent for **transient, burst, and non-repetitive** signals.

4. **Comparison with Swept-Tuned (2 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Swept-Tuned** | **FFT** |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Range | Hz to THz | Limited by ADC (≤ GHz) |
| Transient Capture | Misses fast events | Captures all simultaneously |
| Resolution | Analog IF filter | Digital bin width |

---

**Q.B.(b)** A spectrum analyser has an IF of **21.4 MHz** and is tuned to receive an input signal at **1 GHz**. Calculate **(i)** the LO frequency (assume high-side injection) and **(ii)** the image frequency. Briefly explain why image rejection is essential. `**[7 Marks]**` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{IF} = 21.4$ MHz, $f_s = 1000$ MHz.

**(i) Local Oscillator Frequency (3 marks):**
For high-side injection: $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$
$$f_{LO} = 1000 + 21.4 = 1021.4\,\text{MHz}$$

**(ii) Image Frequency (3 marks):**
$$f_{img} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1000 + 2(21.4) = 1042.8\,\text{MHz}$$

**Image Rejection Explanation (1 mark):**
If a real signal exists at 1042.8 MHz, it will also mix with the LO to produce the same 21.4 MHz IF output, creating a **spurious response** on the display. Without rejection, the analyser cannot distinguish the true signal from the image, leading to measurement error. A pre-mixer **tracking low-pass filter** is therefore essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Spectrum Analyser Questions**
>
> 1. **Mixer output confusion:** Many students write $f_{IF} = f_{LO} + f_s$ instead of the **difference frequency** $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$. The IF filter selects the difference, not the sum. **Lose 1 mark instantly.**
> 2. **Forgetting image frequency:** When asked "calculate the LO frequency," examiners expect you to also state the image frequency in the follow-up. Always pair both.
> 3. **Sweep-time units:** Sweep time is in **seconds**, not milliseconds — convert properly. Also note RBW and span must be in the **same** unit (Hz).
> 4. **Log vs. linear amplitude:** Spectrum analyser displays are almost always logarithmic (dBm). Don't write peak voltage directly; convert using $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P/1\text{mW})$.
> 5. **FFT vs. Swept-Tuned confusion:** Real-time (FFT) analysers do **not** have a sweeping local oscillator. If the question says "sweeping LO," it is the swept-tuned type by definition.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spectrum Analyser** = frequency-domain instrument displaying **amplitude vs. frequency** (not time).
- Two types: **Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne)** and **Real-Time (FFT)**.
- Swept-tuned core equation: $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert$.
- Image frequency formula: $f_{img} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ — must be rejected by the pre-selector.
- **Trade-off triangle:** RBW, Span, Sweep Time are interlinked by $T_{sw} = k \cdot \text{Span}/(\text{RBW})^2$.
- **RBW (Resolution Bandwidth)** = IF filter bandwidth; smaller RBW → better resolution + longer sweep + lower noise floor (DANL).
- **VBW (Video Bandwidth)** = post-detection low-pass filter; smooths the trace but does not improve resolution.
- **FFT resolution** = $\Delta f = f_{sample}/N$; **Nyquist limit** = $f_{max} = f_{sample}/2$.
- Power in dBm: $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P/1\,\text{mW})$; Voltage in dBµV: $V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}(V/1\,\mu V)$.
- **YIG-tuned oscillator** is the heart of the swept-tuned analyser — its ramp drives both the mixer and the display.
- **Logarithmic amplifier** compresses a wide dynamic range (80–100 dB) for a single viewable display.
- **Dynamic Range** = $P_{1dB compression} - \text{DANL}$ in dB.
- **DANL** (Displayed Average Noise Level) defines the analyser's sensitivity — the smaller (more negative), the better.
- **Phase noise** of the LO is the dominant limitation for measuring weak signals near strong carriers.
- **FFT analysers** capture transients, swept-tuned analysers cannot.
- KTU expects: neat block diagrams, mixer derivation, image frequency, sweep-time calculation, and clear comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Spectrum Analyser (DSA)** is an electronic test and measurement instrument that displays the **amplitude of a signal versus frequency** within a defined frequency span. Unlike traditional swept-tuned (analog/heterodyne) spectrum analysers that scan across frequencies sequentially using a tunable local oscillator, a digital spectrum analyser employs **digital signal processing (DSP) techniques**, primarily the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**, to compute and display the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** of a sampled time-domain input signal in near real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The core of a digital spectrum analyser is the **A/D converter + FFT processor** chain. Students must clearly understand why **anti-aliasing filters**, **sampling theorem**, and **windowing functions** are non-negotiable pre-requisites for accurate spectral display.

Formally, the DSA computes:

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}
$$

where $X[k]$ is the complex frequency-domain representation, $x[n]$ is the time-domain digitized signal, $N$ is the number of samples, and $k$ is the frequency bin index.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing beside a busy Indian railway station platform. Your ear hears a **single loud chaotic sound** — horns, announcements, footsteps, vendors shouting. To your ear, it is one mass of noise. But if you had a special "audio prism" that could **separate each sound source by its pitch**, you would suddenly see: *423 Hz = Train horn, 250 Hz = Vendor's voice, 110 Hz = Footsteps, 1.2 kHz = Announcement chime*.

A **Digital Spectrum Analyser is precisely this "electrical prism"** for any time-varying voltage or current signal. It decomposes a complex waveform into its constituent sine-wave frequencies, allowing the engineer to "see" signal purity, harmonics, distortion, noise, and interference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A digital spectrum analyser converts **time-domain data** (which humans understand intuitively via oscilloscopes) into **frequency-domain data** (which reveals hidden spectral content invisible in time domain). This is the central purpose of all Fourier-based instrumentation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the design and selection of a DSA:

- **Nyquist Frequency**: $f_{max} = f_s/2$ where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency.
- **Frequency Resolution**: $\Delta f = f_s/N$ where $N$ is the FFT length.
- **Span**: The total frequency range displayed on screen (e.g., 0 to 100 kHz).
- **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**: The minimum frequency spacing that the analyser can resolve between two spectral lines.
- **Dynamic Range**: Typically **80 dB to 120 dB** for modern digital analysers.
- **Standard FFT sizes**: $N = 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768$ points.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a DSA Works

The operational flow of a digital spectrum analyser can be broken into the following structured logic chain:

1. **Input Signal Conditioning**
   - The unknown analog input signal $x(t)$ enters through a calibrated **input attenuator** that protects the sensitive downstream electronics from over-voltage.
   - The attenuated signal is then passed through an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (LPF) whose cutoff is set slightly below $f_s/2$ to satisfy the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** and prevent spectral aliasing.

2. **Sampling and Quantization (ADC)**
   - The filtered analog signal is sampled at a rate $f_s$ and quantized into discrete digital words (typically 12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit resolution in commercial DSAs).
   - Output: Time-domain discrete sequence $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$.

3. **Windowing (Pre-FFT Processing)**
   - Because the FFT assumes the sampled block is **one period of a perfectly periodic signal**, any discontinuity at the block edges causes **spectral leakage**.
   - A window function $w[n]$ is multiplied with $x[n]$ to taper the ends to zero.
   - Common windows: **Hann, Hamming, Blackman-Harris, Flat-Top, Kaiser**.

4. **FFT Computation**
   - The windowed sequence is fed to an FFT processor (Cooley-Tukey radix-2 or radix-4 algorithm).
   - Output: Complex frequency bins $X[k]$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$.

5. **Magnitude Calculation & Display**
   - The magnitude spectrum $\vert X[k] \vert$ or power spectrum $\vert X[k] \vert^2$ is computed.
   - The result is displayed as **amplitude (dB or linear) versus frequency (Hz)** on a logarithmic or linear X-axis.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The Engineering "Why"

| Step | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| Attenuator | Protects ADC; sets full-scale input range. |
| Anti-aliasing LPF | Prevents high-frequency content from folding back into the analysis band as false signals. |
| ADC | Bridge between analog real-world and digital DSP domain. |
| Windowing | Reduces side-lobe leakage; trades main-lobe width for side-lobe suppression. |
| FFT | Computes $N$ frequency bins in $O(N \log N)$ operations — efficient for real-time use. |
| Display | Human-readable visualization of spectral content. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. |
| 2 | Maximum Analyzable Frequency | $f_{max} = f_s / 2$ | Hz | Nyquist limit. |
| 3 | Frequency Resolution (Bin Width) | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | Hz | Smaller $\Delta f$ = better resolution. |
| 4 | Total Acquisition Time | $T_{acq} = N / f_s$ | seconds | Time-domain capture window. |
| 5 | FFT Output Bin Frequency | $f_k = k \cdot \Delta f$ | Hz | $k$ = bin index (0 to $N/2$). |
| 6 | Magnitude Spectrum | $\vert X[k] \vert = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2 + \text{Im}^2}$ | linear | Often converted to dBFS or dBm. |
| 7 | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | $PSD[k] = \frac{\vert X[k] \vert^2}{f_s \cdot N}$ | W/Hz | Normalized for window energy. |
| 8 | Conversion to dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert X[k] \vert}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | dB | $V_{ref} = 1$ V (dBV) or 1 mW (dBm). |
| 9 | Spectral Leakage Trade-off | $\text{Window} \Rightarrow \text{Main-lobe} \uparrow ,\; \text{Side-lobe} \downarrow$ | — | Always a compromise. |
| 10 | Real-Time Bandwidth (RTBW) | $RTBW = 0.8 \cdot f_s$ | Hz | Max span for gap-free display. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU 14-mark derivations, memorize formulas (1), (3), and (4). Most calculation problems test the relationship between $N$, $f_s$, $\Delta f$, and $T_{acq}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Measuring carrier leakage, sidebands, and modulation fidelity in 4G/5G base stations.
- **EMI/EMC Compliance Testing:** Verifying that electronic products comply with CISPR/FCC conducted and radiated emission limits.
- **Audio & Acoustics Engineering:** THD+N (Total Harmonic Distortion + Noise) measurement of amplifiers and loudspeakers.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Diagnosing bearing faults in rotating machinery by inspecting vibration spectral peaks.
- **Radar & Defense:** Analysing chirp signals and pulse compression spectra.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** EEG/EMG frequency-content studies for clinical diagnosis.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Frequency Resolution, Sampling Rate, and FFT Length

This derivation is a **KTU favorite** for 7-mark sub-questions. We explicitly show every step.

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Definition of Frequency Bin Spacing**

In a digital spectrum analyser, the FFT algorithm partitions the entire sampled frequency range $[0, f_s]$ into exactly $N$ discrete frequency bins. The spacing between adjacent bins is, by definition, the **frequency resolution** $\Delta f$ of the analyser.

$$
\Delta f = \frac{\text{Total Frequency Range}}{\text{Number of Bins}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Total Frequency Range**

The total frequency range analyzed by the FFT extends from **DC (0 Hz)** up to the **sampling frequency $f_s$** (since the FFT inherently treats the input as periodic, and the spectrum is periodic with period $f_s$). Therefore:

$$
\text{Total Range} = f_s - 0 = f_s
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Number of Bins**

A standard $N$-point FFT produces exactly $N$ complex output bins. The unique, non-redundant bins lie between $k = 0$ and $k = N/2$ (the upper half is the complex-conjugate mirror). For resolution purposes, however, the bin spacing is computed using the full $N$:

$$
\text{Number of Bins} = N
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and Simplify**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Re-express Using Acquisition Time**

Since $T_{acq} = N / f_s$, we can rearrange $N = f_s \cdot T_{acq}$ and substitute:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{f_s \cdot T_{acq}} = \frac{1}{T_{acq}}
$$

This elegant result tells us: **the frequency resolution is the reciprocal of the time-domain capture window.**

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

To halve the resolution bandwidth (i.e., to see finer frequency detail), one must either **double the FFT size $N$** or **double the acquisition time $T_{acq}$**. This is the fundamental trade-off in digital spectrum analysis between **speed and resolution**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs in a Digital Spectrum Analyser

| Design Parameter | To Increase Resolution | To Increase Frequency Span | To Improve Dynamic Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| FFT Length $N$ | **Increase $N$** | No effect | Increase $N$ (averaging) |
| Sampling Rate $f_s$ | **Decrease $f_s$** (within Nyquist) | **Increase $f_s$** | Increase ADC bits |
| Acquisition Time $T_{acq}$ | **Increase $T_{acq}$** | Decrease $T_{acq}$ | Increase (more averages) |
| ADC Resolution | No effect | No effect | **Increase bits** |
| Window Type | Use **Blackman-Harris** | Use **Hann/Hamming** | Use **Flat-Top** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** The triangle of trade-offs is *Speed ↔ Resolution ↔ Bandwidth*. You cannot optimize all three simultaneously — pick two.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem:** A digital spectrum analyser uses a sampling frequency $f_s = 100$ kHz and an FFT size $N = 1024$. Determine:
(a) The frequency resolution $\Delta f$.
(b) The maximum analyzable input frequency.
(c) The time-domain acquisition window.
(d) The frequency of the 64th spectral bin.

**Solution (with every step shown):**

**(a) Frequency Resolution**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{1024}
$$

$$
\Delta f = 97.65625 \text{ Hz} \approx 97.66 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(b) Maximum Analyzable Frequency (Nyquist Limit)**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{100\,000}{2} = 50\,000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(c) Time-Domain Acquisition Window**

$$
T_{acq} = \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{1024}{100 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
T_{acq} = 0.01024 \text{ s} = 10.24 \text{ ms}
$$

**(d) Frequency of the 64th Bin**

$$
f_{64} = k \cdot \Delta f = 64 \times 97.65625
$$

$$
f_{64} = 6250 \text{ Hz} = 6.25 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\Delta f$ | **97.66 Hz** |
| $f_{max}$ | **50 kHz** |
| $T_{acq}$ | **10.24 ms** |
| $f_{64}$ | **6.25 kHz** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Digital Spectrum Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Programmable Input Attenuator]
    B --> C[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    C --> D[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Digital Memory Buffer N Samples]
    F --> G[Window Function Multiplier]
    G --> H[FFT Processor Engine]
    H --> I[Magnitude and Phase Calculator]
    I --> J[Logarithmic dB Converter]
    J --> K[Display Unit LCD or CRT]
    K --> L[Frequency Axis x]
    K --> M[Amplitude Axis y]

    subgraph controlLoop[Microcontroller Control Unit]
        N1[Span Selector]
        N2[RBW Selector]
        N3[Reference Level]
        N4[Trigger Settings]
    end

    N1 -.-> H
    N2 -.-> C
    N3 -.-> B
    N4 -.-> D

    classDef analog fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000000
    classDef digital fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000000
    classDef display fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000000
    classDef control fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000000

    class A,B,C,D analog
    class E,F,G,H,I,J digital
    class K,L,M display
    class N1,N2,N3,N4 control
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Block Diagram (KTU-style answer writing):** The **blue (analog)** blocks process the real-world continuous signal, the **green (digital)** blocks perform DSP, the **yellow** block is the user interface, and the **red (control)** blocks govern the analyser settings. Always draw the analog path first, then the digital path, and finally the control feedback in your KTU exam answers.
