## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> *Define a digital spectrum analyser. List any four advantages it has over a conventional swept-tuned spectrum analyser.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Digital Spectrum Analyser (DSA)** is an instrument that uses an Analog-to-Digital Converter followed by a Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) processor to convert a time-domain input signal into its frequency-domain representation, displaying amplitude versus frequency on the screen.

**Four advantages over a swept-tuned analyser:**
1. **Simultaneous (parallel) frequency display** — all $N$ bins are computed and shown together, allowing **real-time** capture of transient events.
2. **Excellent frequency resolution** — limited by FFT size, not by IF filter bandwidth.
3. **Phase information preserved** — the FFT output is complex, so both magnitude and phase spectra are available.
4. **Better transient capture and triggering** — can be configured for time-gated analysis of repetitive bursts.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Explain the role of the anti-aliasing filter in a digital spectrum analyser. What happens if it is omitted?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **anti-aliasing low-pass filter (LPF)** is placed before the ADC and limits the input bandwidth to a frequency slightly below $f_s/2$. This is mandated by the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**: any frequency content above $f_s/2$ that is not filtered out will "fold back" into the 0–$f_s/2$ band as **aliased, false spectral components**, corrupting the displayed spectrum.

**If the filter is omitted:** Higher-frequency harmonics and noise will masquerade as low-frequency signals in the FFT output, leading to **incorrect spectral measurements** and **erroneous fault diagnosis** in the system under test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q.A (a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a digital spectrum analyser. Discuss the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A digital spectrum analyser consists of the following functional blocks arranged in cascade:

1. **Input Attenuator (0–60 dB):** Protects the instrument; sets the full-scale reference level for the ADC.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** A steep-cutoff (often elliptic) LPF with cutoff at $0.45 \cdot f_s$ to prevent aliasing.
3. **Sample-and-Hold Circuit:** Holds the analog voltage constant during A/D conversion to avoid aperture error.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Typically 12–16 bits; samples at $f_s$ and outputs digital words.
5. **Digital Memory Buffer:** Stores $N$ time-domain samples; $N$ is the chosen FFT length.
6. **Window Multiplier:** Multiplies $x[n]$ by a window function (Hann, Hamming, etc.) to reduce spectral leakage.
7. **FFT Processor:** Computes the $N$-point FFT using the Cooley-Tukey algorithm in $O(N \log N)$ steps.
8. **Magnitude / Phase Calculator:** Computes $\vert X[k] \vert$ and $\angle X[k]$.
9. **Logarithmic Converter (dB):** Converts linear amplitude to dBFS or dBm for display.
10. **Display Unit:** LCD/CRT showing amplitude (Y-axis) vs frequency (X-axis).
11. **Microcontroller Control:** Manages span, RBW, reference level, triggering, and averaging.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the full Mermaid block diagram.)

**Function Explanation in Tabular Form (3 marks for explanation):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Attenuator | Input range setting and protection |
| Anti-alias LPF | Prevent spectral folding |
| Sample & Hold | Maintain stable input during conversion |
| ADC | Convert analog → digital samples |
| Memory | Store $N$-sample block |
| Window | Reduce spectral leakage |
| FFT | Compute frequency-domain representation |
| Magnitude calculator | Extract amplitude spectrum |
| Display | Visual output |
| Controller | User-interface management |

---

**Q.A (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an FFT-based spectrum analyser. Derive the expression for frequency resolution. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **FFT-based spectrum analyser** works on the principle of the Discrete Fourier Transform computed in real time on digitized samples. The block diagram is shown in SECTION 4.3 (FFT sub-graph).

**Operation:**
- The input analog signal is conditioned (attenuated, filtered, sampled) and digitized.
- A block of $N$ samples is multiplied by a window function and fed to the FFT engine.
- The FFT outputs $N$ complex values $X[k]$, from which the magnitude spectrum is computed and displayed.

**Derivation of Frequency Resolution (as shown in SECTION 3.1):**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{1}{T_{acq}}
$$

(Show full derivation steps from SECTION 3.1 in exam; each step is awarded 1 mark.)

**Final expressions:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{1}{T_{acq}}}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**Q.B (a) Compare swept-tuned and FFT-based digital spectrum analysers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Swept-Tuned (Heterodyne) | FFT-Based (Digital) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Sequential scanning with sweeping LO | Simultaneous FFT of sampled block |
| Frequency Range | Very wide (up to 50 GHz) | Limited by ADC sampling rate |
| Resolution Bandwidth | Set by IF filter (1 Hz – 1 MHz) | Set by FFT size: $\Delta f = f_s/N$ |
| Real-Time Capability | No (sweep takes time) | Yes (gap-free display) |
| Transient Capture | Poor | Excellent |
| Phase Information | Not available | Available (complex FFT output) |
| Cost & Complexity | High for wideband IF filters | Moderate; needs fast ADC and DSP |
| Display Update | One point at a time | Full spectrum at once |
| Sensitivity | High (narrow RBW) | Moderate (limited by ADC ENOB) |
| Typical Application | RF/Microwave & EMC | Audio, vibration, baseband DSP |

---

**Q.B (b) Explain the concept of windowing in digital spectrum analysis. Compare Hann, Hamming, and Blackman windows. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Windowing:**

When the FFT processes a finite block of $N$ samples, it implicitly treats the block as one period of a **periodic signal**. If the signal at the block edges is not continuous, this artificial discontinuity introduces **spectral leakage** — energy from a single frequency "leaks" into adjacent bins, masking weak signals nearby.

A **window function** $w[n]$ is a finite-length sequence (tapered to zero at both ends) that is multiplied with $x[n]$ before the FFT:

$$
x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n], \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

This forces the block edges smoothly to zero, suppressing leakage at the cost of widening the main spectral lobe.

**Common Windows (Comparison Table):**

| Window | Main-Lobe Width (bins) | Side-Lobe Suppression (dB) | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | 0.89 | **−13 dB** (poor) | Non-windowed reference; transient capture |
| Hann (Hanning) | 1.44 | **−31 dB** | General-purpose; audio analysis |
| Hamming | 1.30 | **−43 dB** | Speech; better side-lobe rejection than Hann |
| Blackman | 1.64 | **−58 dB** | High dynamic range; weak-signal detection |
| Flat-Top | 3.77 | **−93 dB** | Accurate amplitude calibration of tones |

**Trade-off:** Wider main-lobe (worse frequency resolution) ↔ Better side-lobe suppression (less leakage).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Nyquist:** Many students write $f_{max} = f_s$ instead of $f_s/2$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing RBW with $\Delta f$:** In an FFT analyser, the bin width $\Delta f = f_s/N$ **IS** the resolution bandwidth. State this clearly.
> 3. **Omitting the anti-aliasing filter** in the block diagram: This is the **single most-skipped** block. Examiners allocate **1 dedicated mark** for it. **Always draw it.**
> 4. **Windowing explanation missing physical meaning:** Don't just name the windows — state *why* they are used (leakage suppression) and what trade-off they introduce.
> 5. **Drawing the swept-tuned diagram when asked for digital:** This is a common mix-up costing **2–3 marks**. Verify the question wording — "digital" ⇒ FFT path; "spectrum analyser" without qualifier often means heterodyne.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** $\Delta f$ in Hz, $T_{acq}$ in seconds, $f_s$ in Hz. **Lose 0.5 mark per missing unit.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Digital Spectrum Analyser (DSA)** = **ADC + FFT + Display**.
- [x] Core formula: $\Delta f = f_s / N = 1 / T_{acq}$.
- [x] Maximum analyzable frequency: $f_{max} = f_s / 2$ (Nyquist).
- [x] **Anti-aliasing LPF** is **mandatory** before the ADC.
- [x] **Windowing** reduces spectral leakage but widens main lobe.
- [x] FFT provides **both magnitude and phase** — the swept-tuned analyser does **not** give phase.
- [x] **Real-time bandwidth** = frequency span over which the analyser can update display without gaps = approximately $0.8 \cdot f_s$.
- [x] **Dynamic range** of a DSA is limited by ADC resolution (e.g., 12-bit ⇒ ~72 dB theoretical).
- [x] FFT computation complexity: $O(N \log_2 N)$ — this is why **$N = 2^m$** (powers of 2) is standard.
- [x] **Hann window** is the default in most commercial DSAs.
- [x] DSA excels at **transient, baseband, and audio-frequency** analysis; swept-tuned excels at **RF/microwave** wideband.
- [x] Conversion to dB: $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A/A_{ref})$.
- [x] **PSD** (Power Spectral Density) normalizes FFT output for noise-floor measurements.
- [x] For 14-mark answers, **always include a labelled block diagram** — minimum 3 marks reserved for it.
- [x] Memorize one **worked numerical example** (e.g., $f_s = 100$ kHz, $N = 1024$) — this is a guaranteed sub-question.
